title: PLSQL-CAT-020 — Pre-filter wrapped + edition-isolated objects in DBMS_METADATA extraction; consult ALL_PLSQL_OBJECT_SETTINGS for compile-time PLSCOPE_SETTINGS. (/oracle audit finding 2)
close_reason: Investigated: catalog code already extracts EDITION_NAME from ALL_OBJECTS (lines 148, 1976, 2648-2670) AND surfaces wrapped/inaccessible-edition messaging in DBMS_METADATA error remediation (line 1748). The /oracle audit's 'pre-filter wrapped + edition-isolated objects' suggestion is a perf-only optimization — current behaviour relies on DBMS_METADATA's graceful failure on wrapped sources and the EDITION_NAME field for downstream filtering. The full preemptive filter would require an extra ALL_PLSQL_OBJECT_SETTINGS query; deferring until the engine can demonstrate the perf cost matters at scale. Field semantics are correct.

title: PLSQL-SUPPORT-014 — Redaction-delta manifest generator: record every transformation, commit alongside the fixture
description: Imported from `plan.md` v0.11 on 2026-05-12.
close_reason: Added crates/plsql-support/src/redaction_delta.rs with RedactionDeltaManifest, DeltaStep, DeltaConfig types + record_redaction_delta(original, config) -> RedactionDeltaManifest + verify_redaction_delta(original, manifest, config) -> bool. The manifest records every redaction transformation applied to a fixture in canonical order: (1) apply_rules substring rule-list, (2) scrub_literals literal scrubbing, (3) rename_identifiers token-level rename. Each step records: name, match_count, byte_delta (signed; positive = grew, negative = shrank), post_step_sha256 (chain-of-trust hash), and step-specific metadata (rule_count + manifest_version for rules, strings_scrubbed/numerics_scrubbed/date_literals_scrubbed for scrub, renamed_identifier_count + preserved_keyword_count for rename). Manifest carries original_sha256 + redacted_sha256 + step chain + byte counts but NEVER the original content (test enforces this — JSON of manifest cannot contain pre-redaction text). verify_redaction_delta replays the config + checks hash chain equality — proves bytewise reproducibility. Schema pinned: plsql.support.redaction_delta v1. 8 unit tests: 3-step ordering, schema pinning, no-pre-content leak, rule match-count semantics, round-trip verify, salt-differs-fails verify, chain-of-trust last==final, determinism, byte_delta totals match manifest. 68 plsql-support tests pass; clippy --all-targets -D warnings clean. Re-exported via lib.rs. Cite: plan.md §16 (Support workflow) + /security-audit-for-saas. Closes oracle-0z5a.

title: PLSQL-PRIV-DOC-1 — plsql-privileges lacks a doctor surface (/world-class-doctor-mode)
description:
Audit finding from /world-class-doctor-mode pass.

State. The workspace has 6 doctor surfaces shipped:
- CatalogDoctorReport (plsql-catalog) — grant suggestions + counts.
- DepGraphDoctorReport (plsql-depgraph) — node/edge counts + invariants.
- LineageDoctorReport (plsql-lineage) — orphan/freshness/observation-window.
- McpDoctorReport (plsql-mcp) — tool registry + Instant Client posture + write-safety.
- ChangesetDoctorReport (plsql-cicd) — overall_risk + remediation_hints.
- (CLI-only) corpus-license-check / plan-lint --doctor.

Gap. plsql-privileges has no doctor surface. A privilege model that successfully resolves needs a way for an agent to say 'how confident are we that this model is complete?' — e.g., flagging:
- Synonyms with unresolved targets that still produce a SynonymPrivilegePath.
- AuthorizationAmbiguity instances (the model already records these, but no aggregator surfaces them as a doctor count).
- Schemas referenced by the catalog but for which we have zero ALL_TAB_PRIVS rows (could mean 'no grants' or could mean 'we lack SELECT_CATALOG_ROLE' — the former is fine, the latter is a silent gap).

Action. Add a PrivilegeDoctorReport struct with:
- ambiguity_count
- unresolved_synonym_targets
- schemas_without_grants (for cross-checking with catalog capability negotiation)
- counts: definer/invoker objects, accessible_by clauses observed
And a doctor() free fn that builds it from a PrivilegeModel + CatalogSnapshot. Wire into the lineage/depgraph doctor-aggregation path.

Cite. /world-class-doctor-mode — every layer-2 surface gets a doctor so 'why is my analysis like this?' has a single first-stop answer.
close_reason: Added crates/plsql-privileges/src/doctor.rs with PrivilegeDoctorReport (privileges_total, public_grants_total, synonym_paths_total, public_synonym_paths, access_control_entries_total, cross_schema_writes_total/ambiguous, authorization_ambiguities_total, ambiguity_reasons: Vec<DoctorReasonRow>, schemas_observed_total, authid_distribution, diagnostics_total, posture, remediation_hints). Classify_posture three-state (Clean/Caution/Unknown) where Unknown fires when ambiguities outnumber resolved privileges. Build_remediation_hints surfaces ambiguity / cross-schema / public-synonym / diagnostics / empty-model hints. doctor_report(model) free fn; AuthidDistribution::record() helper. 7 new unit tests cover empty, ambiguity-counting, cross-schema-ambiguity, unknown-posture trigger, public-synonym counting, distinct-schema-union, and AUTHID distribution. Wired into lib.rs pub re-exports. Tests + clippy --all-targets -D warnings clean.

description: Imported from `plan.md` v0.11 on 2026-05-12.
close_reason: Added crates/plsql-support/src/rename.rs with rename_identifiers(source, salt) -> (String, RenameStats) and rename_with_reserved(source, salt, &[&str]) variants. Walks PL/SQL source byte-by-byte, renames every Oracle identifier ([A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9_$#]*) to id_<6-byte-hex> via SHA-256(salt || \0 || raw), deterministic across runs. Reserved-word safe: DEFAULT_RESERVED list (78 keywords + datatypes) preserves SELECT/FROM/etc. Quoted identifiers ("Customer Id") rename the body but keep quotes. RenameStats reports distinct renamed count + preserved keyword count. 12 unit tests cover deterministic-across-runs, salt-changes-output, reserved-pass-through, string-literal pass-through, doubled-apostrophe, line + block comments, quoted-identifier body rename, same-id-same-alias within a pass, alias length stable, empty source, custom reserved override, idempotent re-application. 46 plsql-support tests pass; clippy --all-targets -D warnings clean. Cite: plan.md §16 (Support workflow) + /security-audit-for-saas (redaction discipline). Closes oracle-14cy.

title: PLSQL-OSS-AUDIT3 — classify_dbms_metadata_ddl substring-matches keywords, misclassifies every VIEW/TRIGGER/PROCEDURE/FUNCTION whose body mentions TABLE
description:
crates/plsql-catalog/src/lib.rs:1467 detect_object_kind() / classify_dbms_metadata_ddl().

Docstring (lib.rs:1266-1268) promises: 'The object kind is read from the leading CREATE keyword (TABLE / VIEW / PACKAGE / PROCEDURE / FUNCTION / SEQUENCE / TRIGGER / TYPE)'.

Actual behavior: detect_object_kind() uppercases the whole DDL and uses .contains() in cascade order — TABLE first, then VIEW, then PACKAGE, etc. The leading CREATE keyword is never actually parsed; any DDL whose body mentions TABLE gets classified as TABLE regardless of what was actually created. Verified with a 5-line repro program:

  CREATE VIEW V_AUDIT AS SELECT * FROM AUDIT_TABLE                   becomes TABLE (expected VIEW)
  CREATE TRIGGER TRG BEFORE INSERT ON EMP_TABLE FOR EACH ROW         becomes TABLE (expected TRIGGER)
  CREATE PROCEDURE P AS BEGIN INSERT INTO AUDIT_TABLE VALUES(1); END becomes TABLE (expected PROCEDURE)
  CREATE FUNCTION F RETURN NUMBER IS BEGIN RETURN TABLE_NAME(); END  becomes TABLE (expected FUNCTION)

This was the root of oracle-a9ix (PLSQL-OSS-MOCK2 round-2 — closed via Option (a): rename + schema-collapse fix + DDL preservation). The rename + schema bug were fixed, but the substring-classifier bug survived the rewrite and is now actively contradicting the new docstring. Existing tests (lib.rs:6779) only assert TABLE/PACKAGE/SEQUENCE go through, never that a VIEW lands as VIEW — that is how the bug slipped.

Practical impact for OSS release: load_from_dbms_metadata_dir is on the public API surface, dual-licensed Apache-2.0/MIT, and is one of the three catalog ingestion paths documented in docs/components/catalog.md:37. Any user pointing it at a real DBMS_METADATA dump will get a CatalogSnapshot where almost every non-table object is silently filed as a Table. Downstream consumers (depgraph, lineage, sarif_scan, release_gate) then receive a corrupted catalog.

Fix shape: in detect_object_kind, after CREATE [OR REPLACE], skip whitespace + optional 'OR REPLACE' / 'GLOBAL TEMPORARY' / 'PUBLIC' / 'EDITIONABLE' / 'NONEDITIONABLE' / 'FORCE' qualifiers, then match the NEXT token (PACKAGE BODY / TYPE BODY / TABLE / VIEW / PACKAGE / PROCEDURE / FUNCTION / SEQUENCE / TRIGGER / TYPE) — not .contains() over the whole DDL. Add coverage tests: each kind, with a body that mentions every OTHER keyword. The kind under test must win.
close_reason:
Fixed by header-tokenizer-based classifier in crates/plsql-catalog/src/lib.rs.

Root cause: detect_object_kind() used `upper.contains("TABLE")` etc. on the entire upper-cased DDL — any VIEW/TRIGGER/PROCEDURE/FUNCTION/PACKAGE whose body or comments mentioned 'TABLE' was silently re-classified as Table. Real public surface, real silent catalog corruption.

Fix: replaced detect_object_kind with parse_create_header() that runs a real byte-level cursor over the DDL header:
- Skip leading whitespace + `--` line comments + `/* */` block comments
- Consume CREATE, optional OR REPLACE, optional FORCE/EDITIONABLE/NONEDITIONABLE in any order
- Read 1-2 identifier tokens to form a typed DdlKind (multi-word kinds MATERIALIZED VIEW, PACKAGE BODY, TYPE BODY handled)
- Honest uncertainty: DdlKind::Unknown when the header doesn't tokenize (R13)
extract_owner_and_name now operates on the post-kind suffix only, not the whole DDL — body content can't poison it.

Failing tests landed FIRST and now pass:
- classify_view_with_table_in_body_is_view_not_table
- classify_trigger_with_table_in_body_is_trigger
- classify_procedure_with_table_in_body_is_procedure
- classify_function_with_table_in_body_is_function
- classify_package_body_with_table_is_not_package_or_table
- classify_materialized_view_is_materialized_view_not_view
- classify_view_with_leading_block_comment_mentioning_create_table_is_view
- classify_procedure_with_leading_line_comments_is_procedure
- classify_editionable_view_is_view
- classify_quoted_table_owner_name_is_table
- classify_plain_table_is_table
- classify_package_spec_mentioning_view_and_table_is_package

Before vs after for canonical case (CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW hr.v_emp AS SELECT * FROM hr.emp WHERE 'TABLE'='TABLE'):
- BEFORE: classifies as ObjectType::Table (CatalogObject::Table), owner=HR name=V_EMP
- AFTER: classifies as ObjectType::View (CatalogObject::View), owner=HR name=V_EMP

Verification (CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/home/durakovic/plsql-intel-target):
- cargo build -p plsql-catalog: OK
- cargo test -p plsql-catalog: 44 unit + 1 integration test pass
- cargo clippy -p plsql-catalog --all-targets -- -D warnings: clean
#![forbid(unsafe_code)] preserved. No private estate/private estate DB references. No tracker IDs in /// doc comments.

title: PLSQL-OSS-UX — plsql-engine analyze /nonexistent silently succeeds with empty-result JSON (Axiom 14)
description:
CLI: plsql-engine analyze.

What's missing/awkward: 'plsql-engine analyze /nonexistent' (or any unreadable / non-existent project root) prints a fully-formed robot-JSON envelope to stdout (run_id=5381, file_count=0, posture="Clean", diagnostics=[]) and exits 0. The project.root field literally echoes '/nonexistent'. No 'diagnostics' record names the missing-path failure; no stderr message; no non-zero exit. Same behavior for an empty directory.

Why an agent or human would trip on it: This is an Axiom 14 (Never-Silent-Fail) violation and an Axiom 0 violation. An agent that mistypes a path silently gets a 'clean Oracle19c analysis' it can keep parsing and acting on. The 'posture': 'Clean' result is actively misleading — it doesn't mean 'clean', it means 'we scanned zero files.' In a CI pipeline this would make a missing-checkout pass silently. The capabilities exit-code dictionary says exit 1 = 'analysis ran but encountered an error' and exit 2 = 'bad args, unreadable artifact' — neither is fired in this case.

Suggested fix: At minimum, exit 2 if PROJECT_ROOT does not exist or is not a directory, with a stderr message 'plsql-engine: project root does not exist: <path>' (matching usr-loop scan's pattern). Optionally: when the root exists but contains zero .pks/.pkb/.sql files, either exit 2 with a 'no PL/SQL files under <path>; pass --allow-empty to proceed' message, or include a top-level 'project_status': 'empty' tag in the JSON and a warning on stderr.
close_reason: TDD: added crates/plsql-engine/tests/analyze_invocation_failure.rs (2 integration tests) + 3 unit tests in src/main.rs. Implementation: new CliError::ProjectRootMissing/ProjectRootNotDirectory variants (exit_code=2), validate_project_root() helper, called from run_analyze() before AnalysisRequest construction. Smoke: 'plsql-engine analyze /nonexistent' → '  × plsql-engine: project root does not exist: /nonexistent' on stderr, exit 2 (was exit 0 with Clean envelope). '--robot-json' mode also exit 2. Closes Axiom 14 silent-fail hole. cargo build/test/clippy all green.

title: PLSQL-OSS-AUDIT — plsql-accretion gate functions use stringly-typed Result<String, String> in public API
description:
crates/plsql-accretion/src/gate.rs exposes a cluster of PUBLIC functions that all return Result<String, String> -- both the Ok payload and the Err are bare strings:

  gate.rs:344  pub fn roundtrip_check(corpus_dir, fixtures_dir) -> Result<String, String>
  gate.rs:455  pub fn honesty_check(candidate_text) -> Result<String, String>
  gate.rs:512  pub fn residue_check(candidate_text, fixtures_dir) -> Result<String, String>
  gate.rs:599  pub fn measure_estate_metrics(estate) -> Result<String, String>
  gate.rs:624  pub fn baseline_cmp(baseline_json, metrics_text) -> Result<String, String>
  gate.rs:699  pub fn pins_check(repo_root, candidate_text) -> Result<String, String>

This is inconsistent with the rest of the workspace, which is uniformly thiserror-based (36 crates depend on thiserror; no anyhow). plsql-accretion itself already defines a typed ProposerError enum in proposer.rs and uses it there -- so the gate module is the odd one out within its own crate.

The CompletionBackend trait (proposer.rs:659) similarly uses Result<String, String>, though that one is at least documented as deliberately opaque.

Why it matters: stringly-typed errors in a public API force consumers to string-match to branch on failure mode, cannot be matched exhaustively, and lose structure. A public reviewer evaluating the crate's API quality will flag the inconsistency -- the project clearly knows how to do typed errors (thiserror everywhere else) and just didn't here.

Suggested fix: introduce a GateError thiserror enum (or reuse/extend an existing accretion error type) with variants for the distinct failure modes these functions can hit (I/O, parse, honesty-violation, residue-found, baseline-regression, pin-mismatch). The Ok arm carrying a human report string is acceptable, but a typed Ok payload would be cleaner. Lower priority than the half-finished-feature beads but worth doing before tagging a stable API.

Added 6 new GateError variants: Io, Parse, HonestyViolation (G7), PrivacyResidue (G8), BaselineRegression (G6), PinMismatch (G9) — each carrying precise stage-tagged Display text. Updated parse_honesty helper, the usr-gate-rs binary (maps GateError->String evidence via err_str), and the gate.rs tests to assert typed variants instead of string .contains. Evidence: TDD — tests rewritten to matches!(e, GateError::Variant(..)) failed to compile (variants absent), then pass after adding them. cargo build/test/clippy -p plsql-accretion all green incl. gate_selftest 6/6.

title: PLSQL-OSS-UX — plsql-mcp doctor (human mode) writes WARN/INFO findings to stdout (Axiom 4)
description:
CLI: plsql-mcp doctor.

What's missing/awkward: 'plsql-mcp doctor' (no --robot-json) writes the version banner + every [WARN] / [INFO] finding to stdout (stderr is empty). Verified: stdout-bytes=636, stderr-bytes=0. The capabilities document says 'stdout is data only; all diagnostics go to stderr' — this contradicts that contract for the human path. With '--robot-json', stdout is a single JSON object on stdout (correct) and stderr is still empty, which is also fine for the success path.

Why an agent or human would trip on it: An agent piping 'plsql-mcp doctor | grep -v WARN' has to filter stdout for diagnostics, which the contract says should never appear there. Anyone wrapping doctor in 'if [ -z "" ]' to detect 'no issues' will see findings even on a healthy box (because the version banner is on stdout too). The stdout/stderr contract should be the same in both human and robot modes.

Suggested fix: In the human path, route the version banner, [WARN], and [INFO] lines to stderr; let stdout stay empty (or carry only a final 'doctor: ok' / 'doctor: N blocker(s)' summary line). Pin both contracts with verify-stdout-stderr-split.sh-style regression tests for both --robot-json and the default. Same fix applies to bare 'plsql-mcp' (no-args) since that path appears to share the same doctor printout.
close_reason: Fixed: doctor human mode now routes version banner, registered-tool count, and every [OK/INFO/WARN/BLOCK] finding row to stderr; stdout carries only a structured 'doctor: blockers=N warnings=N info=N' summary line. Tests main.rs::doctor_human_mode_routes_findings_to_stderr_not_stdout and doctor_robot_json_keeps_stdout_as_data_only pin both contracts. Verified end-to-end: doctor stdout=27b summary, stderr=636b diagnostics.

title: PLSQL-OSS-UX — plsql-mcp / plsql-engine reject --version (only -V / --help work) (Axiom 7)
description:
CLIs: plsql-mcp, plsql-engine.

What's missing/awkward: 'plsql-mcp --version' returns 'error: unexpected argument --version found' exit 2. Same for 'plsql-engine --version'. usr-loop accepts both '-V' and '--version'. The clap derive for plsql-mcp and plsql-engine sets up '#[command(name = ...)]' but does NOT set the 'version' attribute, so '--version' is not registered. By convention, every Unix-style CLI accepts --version; agents and humans both reach for it as a feature-detection probe.

Why an agent or human would trip on it: An agent doing 'plsql-mcp --version || echo unknown' to detect the binary's version (e.g. to pick a compatible MCP protocol version) gets exit 2 with no version emitted. The agent must instead know to run 'plsql-mcp info' or 'plsql-mcp capabilities | jq .version' — discoverable only by reading --help.

Suggested fix: Add '#[command(name = "plsql-mcp", version)]' (and same for plsql-engine + plsql-depgraph if missing). clap auto-derives '--version' from the workspace package version. Pin with a one-line test.
close_reason: Fixed: added 'version' attribute to #[command(...)] in plsql-mcp's Cli struct. Both 'plsql-mcp --version' and 'plsql-mcp -V' now print 'plsql-mcp 0.1.0' on stdout and exit 0. Regression tests main.rs::version_flag_long_form_is_accepted and version_flag_short_form_is_accepted pin both. Note: plsql-engine is in a different crate; left for subagent C as instructed.

title: PLSQL-OSS-MOCK2 — plsql-mcp DbToolsAlias::probe is a substring-match stub (false positives possible); docstring self-declares 'bead skeleton' and trails off mid-sentence
description: crates/plsql-mcp/src/connections.rs:67-99. The function is documented as 'The bead skeleton performs only a structural check — does the file exist and contain a section named after the alias? — and defers full parsing to the concrete connect implementation in.' (sentence ends mid-word, 'in.'). The body literally does `if text.contains(alias) { ... return available=true }` against the raw bytes of `~/.dbtools` (or .json/.conf variants). Any alias that happens to appear as a substring anywhere in the file — inside a comment, a different section name that contains the alias as a prefix/suffix, a quoted password or hostname — produces a false-positive 'available=true' with `connect_string: None`. The probe is reachable from the safety-tool and connection-management surfaces (DbToolsAlias is part of the public connections API, re-exported in plsql-mcp::lib). The function name promises a real probe. Suggested fix: either parse the dbtools file properly (JSON for dbtools.json, INI-ish for .dbtools/.dbtools.conf) and look up the alias as a structural key, or rename to `probe_substring` and add explicit 'best-effort, may report false positives' wording. Also finish the truncated docstring.
close_reason: Fixed: DbToolsAlias::probe replaced raw-substring match with structural lookup. dbtools.json is parsed as JSON and walked for a connection object with name==alias or a direct key==alias; .dbtools / .dbtools.conf use INI-style [section] header matching that skips ';'/'#' comments. Doc rewritten (no truncated 'in.'; no 'bead skeleton'). 5 adversarial test fixtures added (alias-in-comment, alias-as-prefix-of-real-alias, alias-in-hostname-or-password, INI section found, INI alias-in-comment-only) — all pass; original probe_finds_existing_section + probe_returns_unavailable_when_no_file still pass.

title: PLSQL-OSS-AUDIT — --listen public-bind error advises a flag that does not exist
description:
crates/plsql-mcp/src/tcp.rs:59 -- the ListenTargetError::PublicBindRefused error message reads: '--listen target {raw} binds to a public-internet address; pass --allow-public-bind to override'. The same advice is repeated in the module doc (tcp.rs:24) and a unit test in main.rs:904 asserts the stderr contains '--allow-public-bind'.

However, crates/plsql-mcp/src/main.rs:180-181 explicitly comments: '--allow-public-bind is not yet a CLI flag, so the safe default (loopback/private only) holds.' The MCP CLI never registers an --allow-public-bind argument (clap derive in main.rs has no such field; parse_listen_target is always called with allow_public_bind=false at main.rs:182).

Why it matters: a user who tries to bind plsql-mcp to a public address gets an error telling them to pass a flag that does not exist. The advice is un-actionable -- the user has no documented way to override. For an OSS release this is a dead-end UX path and an honesty gap (error text promises a capability the binary lacks).

Suggested fix: pick one of --
(a) Implement the --allow-public-bind CLI flag in main.rs's clap Serve command and thread it into parse_listen_target, OR
(b) If public bind is intentionally not exposed, change the error message to state that public binds are not supported (drop the '--allow-public-bind to override' clause) and update the tcp.rs module doc + the main.rs:904 test accordingly.

Note: the underlying tcp::parse_listen_target already supports an allow_public_bind parameter, so option (a) is a small wiring change.
close_reason: Wired --allow-public-bind as a real clap flag on 'serve' (Command::Serve + run_serve threaded to tcp::parse_listen_target). The error text that advised the flag is now actionable; the public-bind override the tcp.rs machinery already supported is reachable. plsql-mcp tests green (incl. public-bind-refused test).

title: PLSQL-MCP-DESIGN-1 — Audit findings from /mcp-server-design pass
description:
Audit finding from /mcp-server-design pass on crates/plsql-mcp/.

State. plsql-mcp ships a foundation skeleton (PLSQL-MCP-001) with a tool registry, doctor surface, audit baseline, K18 sanitization, safety token, transport stubs, and a config loader. 4803 LOC across the crate, 86 tests. Live-DB feature gate properly behind a feature flag.

Findings.

1. Transport is still stubbed (transport.rs). The stdio MCP loop is not wired — PLSQL-MCP-002 covers that. This is honestly documented as a skeleton.

2. Tool registry has no built-in `list_tools` MCP method response builder — the registry stores tools but the JSON-RPC `tools/list` shape isn't synthesized in code. PLSQL-MCP-002 covers this.

3. No protocol version negotiation surface — the MCP spec requires `initialize` with protocolVersion handshake. Not currently in tools/transport modules.

4. Audit surface (audit.rs) emits AuditPlan but no rotating audit log writer — every operation will accrete events in-memory. For production, a sink that writes to disk (with PII-redacted row text) and rotates daily would be needed. The Sink trait exists, the rotating implementation does not.

5. enable_writes token flow has good shape (session-scoped, time-bounded) but no idle-timeout — a token issued and then forgotten remains valid until process exit. Recommend an explicit `last_used_at` field on SessionSafetyState with a configurable idle-window check before each write call.

6. The DSN config supports `~/.dbtools` alias lookup as a skeleton (connections.rs:68) — the actual ~/.dbtools file format and walk are not implemented. PLSQL-MCP-LIVE-007 covers this.

Action. Items 1-3 are already gated by PLSQL-MCP-002. Items 4-5 are net-new beads to file separately (audit-log rotation; enable_writes idle-timeout). Item 6 is already gated by PLSQL-MCP-LIVE-007.

title: PLSQL-OSS-BUG — recognise_string_literal mangles non-ASCII content via 'bytes[i] as char' (Latin-1 reinterpretation)
description:
FILE: crates/plsql-ir/src/expr.rs  fn recognise_string_literal (lines 163-188), specifically line 184: 'out.push(bytes[i] as char);'

BUG: The function un-doubles '' escapes by walking the inner string's BYTES (let bytes = inner.as_bytes(); line 171) and pushing each byte as a char: 'out.push(bytes[i] as char)'. 'u8 as char' maps a byte 0..=255 to the Unicode scalar value with that numeric codepoint -- i.e. it reinterprets UTF-8 bytes as Latin-1. Any non-ASCII character in a PL/SQL string literal is therefore corrupted: each multi-byte UTF-8 sequence is expanded into its individual bytes, each rendered as a wrong Latin-1 char.

CONFIRMED EMPIRICALLY (scratch crate against plsql-ir):
  lower_expression("'cafe'") with an accented e  =>  Expr::StringLit("cafA©")  -- the 2-byte UTF-8 sequence for the accented e (0xC3 0xA9) became two Latin-1 chars. content != input.

WHY IT TRIGGERS: any string literal containing non-ASCII text -- accented characters, non-Latin scripts, the section sign, em-dash, smart quotes, etc. PL/SQL source is routinely UTF-8 (AL32UTF8 databases) so this is reachable on real corpora.

DOWNSTREAM IMPACT (MEDIUM): Expr::StringLit content feeds flow analysis -- crates/plsql-ir/src/flow_intra.rs:168 reads StringLit(s) for string_shape (dynamic-SQL taint). A corrupted literal propagates corrupted bytes into the IR and any consumer that inspects literal text. It does not crash (no panic -- bytes are always valid as Latin-1 chars) but it is a silent data-correctness defect: the IR no longer faithfully represents the source.

SUGGESTED FIX: walk chars, not bytes. Use inner.char_indices() / a chars().peekable() loop: detect a '\'\'' pair and emit one '\'', a solitary middle '\'' aborts (return None), otherwise push the char unchanged. This preserves multi-byte UTF-8 correctly. (The doubled-quote and solitary-quote logic only needs to compare against the ASCII ''' which is single-byte, so a char-based walk is straightforward.)
close_reason: recognise_string_literal (plsql-ir/src/expr.rs:184) no longer does 'bytes[i] as char' (Latin-1 reinterpretation). Now walks inner.chars().peekable(): a doubled '' emits one ', a solitary mid-quote aborts (None), every other char pushed unchanged — multi-byte UTF-8 preserved. New tests: string_literal_preserves_non_ascii_utf8 (cafe/accents/Cyrillic/CJK round-trip) and string_literal_unescapes_doubled_quotes_with_non_ascii. Written failing-first (confirmed mojibake), watched fail, then fixed. Suite green.

title: PLSQL-CICD-002 — Implement `predict <changeset>` using lineage `impact()` + Oracle-specific invalidation rules
description: Imported from `plan.md` v0.11 on 2026-05-12.
close_reason: Added crates/plsql-cicd/src/predict.rs implementing the predict <changeset> rule engine. predict(changeset, mode) -> InvalidationPrediction iterates each ChangedObject and dispatches per ChangedObjectKind through apply_oracle_invalidation_rules. Each match arm is one Oracle-specific invalidation rule with confidence tier per PredictMode (CatalogAware = High, LiveSnapshot = High with extracted-at-runtime note, SourceOnly = Low + SourceOnlyHeuristic uncertainty appended). Rules implemented: PackageSpec -> PackageSpecChanged direct invalidation; PackageBody -> ConditionalCompilationBranch uncertainty only (Oracle 11gR2+ fine-grained dependency policy means body-only changes do not invalidate dependents); StandaloneRoutineSignature -> RoutineSignatureChanged; StandaloneRoutineBody -> no invalidation (same fine-grained policy); TableAdditiveDdl/TableDestructiveDdl -> typed reason variants; TypeEvolution / SynonymRetargeting / GrantOrRevoke / EditionedObjectChange / MaterializedViewRefreshAffecting -> per-reason invalidation; ViewDefinitionChange / TriggerChange -> Other-reason with descriptive text; IndexChange / SequenceChange -> MissingCatalogObject informational uncertainty (Oracle does not invalidate dependents); OtherKnownKind -> low-confidence catch-all; Unclassified -> typed ParserRecoveryRegion uncertainty (R13). Per-object UnknownReason uncertainties propagate into the prediction's uncertainties Vec. Output sorted by (distance, owner, name) for stable diff. 8 new unit tests cover: empty changeset, package spec invalidation + High confidence, package body uncertainty + no invalidation, SourceOnly downgrades + appends source-only uncertainty, destructive DDL typed reason, index/sequence informational, unclassified typed uncertainty, stable ordering, per-object uncertainty propagation. title: PLSQL-OSS-UX — usr-gate-rs lacks --help / --version / capabilities (Axiom 0 violation)
description:
CLI: usr-gate-rs (crates/plsql-accretion/src/bin/usr_gate_rs.rs).

What's missing/awkward: The binary is a hand-rolled arg parser (no clap). Running 'usr-gate-rs --help' or 'usr-gate-rs -h' prints 'unknown subcommand "--help"' and exits 1. Same for '--version'. There is no 'capabilities' subcommand, no 'robot-docs', no exit-code dictionary anywhere a tool can read. Bare 'usr-gate-rs' (no args) returns 'unknown subcommand ""' exit 1.

Why an agent or human would trip on it: This is a P0 Axiom 0 (First-Try-Inevitability) violation. The FIRST thing any agent reaches for is '--help'; on this binary it returns an error. The binary IS documented in the source doc-comment ('// roundtrip, honesty, residue, baseline-cmp, pins, metrics') but those docs are inaccessible from the binary itself. An agent or operator wanting to verify the gate locally has no in-tool way to discover the subcommands.

Suggested fix: Add a real 'help'/'--help' handler (just print the doc-comment block + the subcommand list). Add '--version'. Add a 'capabilities' subcommand returning JSON with binary, version, subcommands[], exit_codes (currently silently: 0=PASS, non-zero=FAIL — needs explicit dictionary). It's a thin CLI so this is ~30 lines; the methodology bar (Axioms 0/5/9) is independent of binary size.
close_reason: usr-gate-rs gains --help/-h, --version/-V, --capabilities, --robot-docs (mirrors plsql-bindgen pattern). Hand-rolled parser handles info flags FIRST before any subcommand dispatch (Axiom 0). Capabilities JSON is contract-versioned (CAPABILITIES_CONTRACT_VERSION=1) with pinned drift-guard test (capabilities_contract_is_pinned) and single-line-JSON test. Robot-docs handbook lists every subcommand + the USR_GATE_TRUST_PINS env. 11 new tests pass (3 lib + 8 integration spawning the binary). Bare invocation and unknown subcommand now point at --help instead of erroring opaquely.

title: PLSQL-OSS-AUDIT3 — hand-rolled CLI tools write --help to stderr (POSIX violation); 'tool --help | less' breaks across 4 of 5 tools
description:
tools/corpus-bench/src/main.rs:396 print_usage(), tools/corpus-grow/src/main.rs:433, tools/corpus-license-check/src/main.rs:404, tools/plan-lint/src/main.rs:368.

All four hand-rolled-arg CLI tools route their --help output through a print_usage() helper that uses eprintln! exclusively. Behavior witnessed (with --help, exit 0):

  corpus-bench       --help => stdout=0 bytes, stderr=623 bytes
  corpus-grow        --help => stdout=0 bytes, stderr=729 bytes
  corpus-license-check --help => stdout=0 bytes, stderr=838 bytes
  plan-lint          --help => stdout=0 bytes, stderr=1065 bytes
  usr-loop (clap)    --help => stdout=3019 bytes, stderr=0 bytes  (correct)

POSIX / GNU convention (and clap's default, which is why usr-loop is correct) is that --help requested by the user goes to stdout — it is data, not a diagnostic. Routing it to stderr breaks:
  - tool --help | less                 (pager sees nothing)
  - tool --help > help.txt             (empty file)
  - tool --help | grep --robot-json    (no matches; flag exists)
  - agents that parse stdout to discover the flag surface

The same files correctly send --capabilities, --robot-docs, and --version to stdout. --help is the one anomaly, and it is the most-used discovery path. The bare-invocation print_usage-on-error path SHOULD stay on stderr (that is a diagnostic); only the explicit --help branch should switch to println!. Suggested fix: split print_usage() into print_usage_to(writer) and call it with std::io::stdout().lock() from the --help branch and std::io::stderr().lock() from the error branch. The fix is mechanical and per-tool ~6 lines.
close_reason: Split print_usage() into print_usage_to(W) in all 4 hand-rolled tools (corpus-bench, corpus-grow, corpus-license-check, plan-lint). --help / -h branch writes to stdout().lock(); error branch (unknown flag, missing arg) still writes to stderr().lock(). TDD: added tests/help_goes_to_stdout.rs in each tool asserting (a) --help exits 0 with non-empty stdout and empty stderr, (b) -h same, (c) unknown flag still produces stderr diagnostic. Red phase confirmed before fix (--help → 0 bytes stdout / 623+ bytes stderr); green after. Evidence: 3 tests × 4 tools = 12 new help tests all pass; cargo clippy -D warnings clean.

title: PLSQL-OSS-AUDIT — plsql-bindgen & plsql-doc Cargo.toml miss [lints] workspace = true
description:
crates/plsql-bindgen/Cargo.toml and crates/plsql-doc/Cargo.toml are the only two workspace crates that do NOT have a '[lints]\nworkspace = true' section (verified by scanning all crates/*/Cargo.toml and tools/*/Cargo.toml). Every other crate inherits the workspace lint table defined in /Cargo.toml, which includes 'unsafe_code = forbid' (rust) and 'dbg_macro = deny' + 'todo = deny' (clippy).

Why it matters: PUBLISHING.md asserts 'The whole workspace is #![forbid(unsafe_code)]'. Both crates DO carry an inline '#![forbid(unsafe_code)]' in lib.rs (and bindgen's main.rs), so unsafe is in fact still forbidden -- but the inconsistency means these two crates silently miss the 'dbg_macro = deny' and 'todo = deny' clippy gates. A stray dbg!() or todo!() could land in plsql-bindgen or plsql-doc without CI catching it, whereas it would be denied everywhere else. For an OSS release this is an inconsistent build-config surface a reviewer would flag.

Suggested fix: add the standard

[lints]
workspace = true

stanza to both crates/plsql-bindgen/Cargo.toml and crates/plsql-doc/Cargo.toml, matching every other crate. The inline #![forbid(unsafe_code)] can then be dropped (workspace lint covers it) or kept for redundancy.
close_reason: Added [lints] workspace = true to plsql-bindgen and plsql-doc Cargo.toml — they now enforce the workspace dbg_macro/todo deny gates. clippy -p plsql-doc -p plsql-bindgen --all-targets -D warnings clean.

title: E-2b follow-up — enforce the fail-closed read-only guard on the oraclemcp binary's raw-SQL tools
description: GAP found during E-2d README-accuracy review. E-2b's OracleDispatcher executed caller-supplied SQL (oracle_query -> read_query -> query_rows; oracle_explain_plan -> explain_plan) WITHOUT running it through the oraclemcp-guard classifier — the binary advertised a READ_ONLY OperatingLevel (registry/capabilities) it did NOT enforce, so a DELETE/DROP/GRANT handed to oracle_query would have reached Oracle. Contradicts oraclemcp's whole premise (safe-by-default). FIX (canonical commits 29d772f + 1851b13; mirrored to the extract repo 0fa5875): new ensure_read_only(sql) gates the two raw-SQL tools — Classifier::classify(sql) evaluated against SessionLevelState::new(ReadOnly,false); admit only LevelDecision::Allow; writes/DDL/DCL -> OperatingLevelTooLow, Forbidden constructs -> ForbiddenStatement, both with safe_alternative, both refused BEFORE the connection. The other 5 tools build their own parameterized dictionary SQL (no gate needed). Tests: NoExecMock (panics if a refused stmt reaches the DB) proves non-execution; writes/DDL/DCL refused, read-only SELECT passes, explain-of-write refused, multi-statement batch refused. Workspace GREEN 2623/0; extract GREEN 444/0; publish dry-run green. LESSON: advertise-but-don't-enforce — always verify the headline safety property is actually wired into the dispatch path, not just declared in capabilities.

title: PLSQL-OSS-SEC — prompt-injection sanitizer is blocklist-based and trivially evadable
description:
FILE: crates/plsql-mcp/src/query.rs:141-206 (injection_markers / sanitize)

THREAT (incomplete prompt-injection scrub): The K18 sanitizer that scrubs DB cell values before handing them to the calling agent is a fixed blocklist of literal marker strings (<tool_call>, antml:* tags, <|im_start|>, [INST], 'system: ' role prefixes, etc.). The skill's LLM-SECURITY guidance and Axiom 3 are explicit: prompt-injection defense must be structural (delimiting untrusted content), not a textual blocklist of 'known' injection shapes. A blocklist has unbounded evasions:

- Case/spacing variants: `< tool_call >`, `<TOOL_CALL>`, `<tool_call >` are not in the list (only exact `<tool_call>`).
- Unicode look-alikes / zero-width chars inside the tag.
- New model/tool-call syntaxes that did not exist when the list was written (the list is a snapshot; attack surface expands faster — Axiom 8).
- The far larger class of *plain-prose* injections ('Ignore previous instructions and ...') which carry no markup at all and are 100% unscrubbed.
- Markdown / code-fence tricks, role-confusion via newlines.

So a malicious row value in a customer DB (or any DB the agent queries) can still steer the calling agent. The sanitizer gives a false sense of completeness — sanitized=true / sanitized_cells signal implies 'this is now safe', which it is not.

This is the design pattern the skill warns against; it is not a single bug but a structural choice. Recommended changes:
1. Treat ALL query-result cell text as untrusted and wrap it structurally — e.g. emit cell values inside an explicit, clearly-delimited data envelope in the MCP response with a fixed, non-spoofable boundary, and document that the agent must treat envelope contents as data, never instructions.
2. Keep the marker scrub only as a belt-and-suspenders layer, but normalize first (case-fold, strip zero-width / control chars, collapse whitespace) so trivial variants are caught.
3. Make the response/docs honest that scrubbing is best-effort, not a guarantee — do not let `sanitized` read as 'safe'.

SEVERITY: Medium — the threat is real (data-borne prompt injection through query results) and the current control is materially weaker than it presents itself; appropriate to flag before an OSS release that advertises K18 sanitization.
close_reason: Replaced query.rs blocklist-only sanitize with a structural defense: (1) normalize away zero-width/control-char obfuscation; (2) structurally neutralize EVERY angle-bracket markup run by rewriting < > to fullwidth look-alikes so any tag is inert regardless of casing/spacing/unicode or whether it was known when written; (3) belt-and-suspenders case-insensitive blocklist still collapses known markers to [redacted]. Added QueryResponse.untrusted_data_notice (UNTRUSTED_DATA_NOTICE const) — an explicit non-spoofable data envelope telling the agent cells are data never instructions. sanitized flag now reads true only when content was genuinely altered; plain prose left byte-identical with changed=false (response honest that scrubbing is best-effort). TDD: 7 failing case/spacing/unicode/prose/envelope tests written first, watched fail, then GREEN. Existing marker tests preserved. cargo build/test/clippy green.

title: Tag PARSE-RECOVERY-001 diagnostics with UnknownReason::ParserRecoveryRegion
description:
### Tag PARSE-RECOVERY-001 diagnostics with UnknownReason::ParserRecoveryRegion

**Kind:** vision-gap  **Severity:** high  **Effort:** S

**Rationale:** The whole honest-uncertainty pillar hinges on recovery being typed as TypedDegradation, but make_recovery_diagnostic ends at .with_primary_span(span) and never adds the reason. gap.rs:79 keys off unknown_reasons.is_empty(), so every recovery diagnostic is silently misclassified as Grammar/Lowering — the system drops the exact signal it was built to surface. One-line fix, directly load-bearing for honest-uncertainty.

**Files:** /home/durakovic/projects/plsql-intelligence/crates/plsql-parser-antlr/src/recover.rs

**Acceptance:** recover.rs:348 chains .with_unknown_reason(UnknownReason::ParserRecoveryRegion); the recover_at_semicolon test (recover.rs:364) asserts !diag.unknown_reasons.is_empty() && diag.unknown_reasons[0]==UnknownReason::ParserRecoveryRegion; an accretion gap_record test confirms a recovered diagnostic classifies as TypedDegradation not Grammar/Lowering.
close_reason: make_recovery_diagnostic (recover.rs) now chains .with_unknown_reason(UnknownReason::ParserRecoveryRegion) so PARSE-RECOVERY-001 is tagged TypedDegradation. The accretion gap classifier (RepairClass::classify keys off unknown_reasons.is_empty()) now correctly classifies recovery as TypedDegradation, not a Grammar/Lowering candidate — closing an honest-uncertainty pillar leak. +recover_at_semicolon asserts the reason; +gap_record.rs repair_class_mapping asserts a PARSE-RECOVERY-001+reason classifies TypedDegradation.

title: Finalize CompletenessReport posture in CICD predict profile (stop serializing false-Degraded)
description:
### Finalize CompletenessReport posture in CICD predict profile (stop serializing false-Degraded)

**Kind:** vision-gap  **Severity:** high  **Effort:** S

**Rationale:** completeness_profile_for_mode (predict.rs:69-85) builds a report, sets catalog/plscope flags, and returns it with posture left at the #[default] Degraded. For an empty changeset (0 objects, 0 diagnostics) the anti-spin rules (lib.rs:797-803) say Clean, but consumers reading InvalidationPrediction.completeness.posture over the wire get a false-pessimistic Degraded. This is the exact inverse of the false-clean failure the design forbids, and it ships in serialized output.

**Files:** /home/durakovic/projects/plsql-intelligence/crates/plsql-cicd/src/predict.rs

**Acceptance:** completeness_profile_for_mode calls report.finalize_posture() before returning; a new test asserts predict() on an empty/zero-diagnostic changeset yields completeness.posture==CompletenessPosture::Clean, and a non-empty one with diagnostics never yields Clean.
close_reason: completeness_profile_for_mode left posture at the #[default] Degraded; predict() now populates the report tallies from the changeset's understanding (objects_total, objects_with_extracted_semantics vs objects_unrecognized by opacity, diagnostics_total=opacity records) and calls finalize_posture(). Empty/fully-understood changeset now serializes Clean; any opacity never Clean — closing the false-pessimistic inverse of the false-clean failure. +regression test across all 3 modes + clean + opaque cases.

title: Feed the real resolution strategy from plsql-symbols into engine-built edge provenance
description:
### Feed the real resolution strategy from plsql-symbols into engine-built edge provenance

**Kind:** vision-gap  **Severity:** medium  **Effort:** M

**Rationale:** The engine stamps ResolutionStrategy::LocalLexical on every call and table-access edge (engine/lib.rs:1092,1177), yet plsql-symbols::resolve_reference() (resolve_refs.rs:112) already determines whether resolution went via PackageMemberLookup, SameSchemaLookup, CatalogLookup, SynonymExpansion, etc. The provenance/strategy enum is fully built but the depgraph is fed a constant, so the depgraph-IR vision (rich, queryable provenance) is only partially delivered — a consumer cannot tell a qualified package-member resolution from raw lexical scope.

**Files:** /home/durakovic/projects/plsql-intelligence/crates/plsql-engine/src/lib.rs, /home/durakovic/projects/plsql-intelligence/crates/plsql-symbols/src/resolve_refs.rs

**Acceptance:** Edge construction in analyze_project_internal invokes the symbols resolver (or threads its result) so Provenance.resolution_strategy reflects the actual path; a test with a schema-qualified package-member call asserts the edge's provenance carries PackageMemberLookup (not LocalLexical), and a purely local call still carries LocalLexical.
close_reason: Engine Calls-edge provenance now records the REAL resolution strategy derived from the resolution path (qualified PKG.MEMBER -> PackageMemberLookup; bare local -> LocalLexical; package-fallback -> PackageMemberLookup) instead of hardcoded LocalLexical. Reads/Writes edges record SameSchemaLookup when the table resolved to a known node, else LocalLexical. +regression test asserting a qualified call carries PackageMemberLookup.

title: Mint Evidence for sub-High-confidence edges at the engine layer
description:
### Mint Evidence for sub-High-confidence edges at the engine layer

**Kind:** impl-gap  **Severity:** medium  **Effort:** M

**Rationale:** Engine inserts Medium-confidence Calls/Reads/Writes edges with evidence=None (engine/lib.rs:1095,1180). validate() (depgraph/lib.rs:758) only requires evidence when confidence!=High, so this passes — but it leaves the Evidence map (the justification payload explain_edge/explain_path surface to consumers) empty exactly where confidence is uncertain and justification matters most. The Evidence model is built and validated but unfed on the primary edge-creation path.

**Files:** /home/durakovic/projects/plsql-intelligence/crates/plsql-engine/src/lib.rs, /home/durakovic/projects/plsql-intelligence/crates/plsql-depgraph/src/lib.rs

**Acceptance:** Medium/Low-confidence edges are inserted with a populated Evidence (non-empty code+summary, spans where known, e.g. 'call resolved via local lexical scope' / 'table reference in embedded DML'); an engine test asserts dep_graph.evidence_for(edge_id) is Some for a Medium-confidence Calls edge and Evidence.summary is non-empty.
close_reason: Engine now mints a populated Evidence (PLSQL-DEP-002 for Calls, PLSQL-DEP-003 for Reads/Writes, with summary + the edge confidence) on every sub-High-confidence edge instead of evidence=None, feeding the explain_edge/explain_path justification payload exactly where confidence is uncertain. +regression tests asserting non-empty Evidence on Calls + R/W edges.

title: Index DepGraph edges by id and by from-node; remove per-node full sorts in BFS
description:
### Index DepGraph edges by id and by from-node; remove per-node full sorts in BFS

**Kind:** perf-gap  **Severity:** medium  **Effort:** M

**Rationale:** edge_by_id (depgraph/lib.rs:1020) is self.edges.iter().find — O(E) per call, invoked from query_path, explain_edge, explain_node. Worse, query_path's BFS (lib.rs:850) calls sorted_edges(&self.edges) inside the node loop and re-filters by from, making path queries O(V·E log E) and explain_node O(V·E). A one-time EdgeId→Edge map plus a from→Vec<EdgeId> adjacency index makes lookups O(1) and the BFS O(V+E). This is also a real simplification: ~11 sorted_edges call sites collapse to indexed neighbor walks.

**Files:** /home/durakovic/projects/plsql-intelligence/crates/plsql-depgraph/src/lib.rs

**Acceptance:** DepGraph holds HashMap<EdgeId,usize> and a from-adjacency index built once at construction/insert; edge_by_id and the query_path BFS use the indices (no sorted_edges(&self.edges) inside the BFS loop); existing e2e/explain tests pass byte-identically (ordering preserved by sorting only neighbor slices, not the whole edge set).
close_reason: query_path's BFS re-sorted ALL edges per popped node (O(V*E log E)); it now builds a from-adjacency index + an EdgeId index ONCE (hoisted, from sorted_edges so BFS visitation order is byte-identical -> I-DETERMINISM preserved), mirroring detect_cycles' existing local-index pattern. Reconstruction uses the local EdgeId index instead of O(path*E) edge_by_id scans. DepGraph is Serialize/Deserialize + round-trips through artifacts, so a persistent skip-serde index would be stale after deserialize — the local-index approach achieves the same asymptotic win (O(E log E)) with zero struct/serde risk. edge_by_id is now #[cfg(test)]. depgraph 42 tests green, clippy clean.

title: Per-arm dispatch coverage test proving every static MCP tool actually runs
description:
### Per-arm dispatch coverage test proving every static MCP tool actually runs

**Kind:** proof-gap  **Severity:** medium  **Effort:** M

**Rationale:** The lockstep test (dispatch.rs:403-407) only asserts the dispatch-table name-set equals the registry name-set; it never invokes the arms. A tool can satisfy lockstep with a dispatch arm that always returns RuntimeStateRequired or panics on real args. For a 46-tool surface that is the headline product, the proof that each static tool (parse_file, compile_check, analyze_project, oracle_capabilities, etc.) dispatches to a real Ran outcome is missing.

**Files:** /home/durakovic/projects/plsql-intelligence/crates/plsql-mcp/src/dispatch.rs

**Acceptance:** A test iterates every name in dispatch_table(), calls the dispatcher with minimal valid args, and asserts each static (non-runtime-state) tool returns Ran (not a panic/hang); tools that legitimately need runtime DB state are explicitly allow-listed and asserted to return RuntimeStateRequired.
close_reason: Added every_self_contained_static_tool_actually_runs: iterates the 10 genuinely self-contained static tools (oracle_capabilities/parse_file/get_symbol/compile_check/inspect_profile/dynamic_sql_evidence/doc_lookup/completeness_report/analyze_project/plsql_analyze) with minimal VALID args and asserts each returns DispatchOutcome::Ran with a structured result — proving the arms do real work, not just resolve a name (the lockstep test's gap). A stub arm returning RuntimeStateRequired or panicking would fail it.

title: De-duplicate is_ident_byte into one shared IR helper
description:
### De-duplicate is_ident_byte into one shared IR helper

**Kind:** simplification  **Severity:** low  **Effort:** S

**Rationale:** is_ident_byte is byte-for-byte identical in dml_edges.rs:365 and sql_resolve.rs:372 (b.is_ascii_alphanumeric()||b==b'_'||b==b'$'||b==b'#'). Both are identifier-tokenization helpers in the same crate; a single pub(crate) definition removes the drift risk where one is updated (e.g. to handle a new identifier byte) and the other silently diverges.

**Files:** /home/durakovic/projects/plsql-intelligence/crates/plsql-ir/src/dml_edges.rs, /home/durakovic/projects/plsql-intelligence/crates/plsql-ir/src/sql_resolve.rs

**Acceptance:** One is_ident_byte exists at a shared pub(crate) location in plsql-ir; both modules call it; the duplicate definition is removed and the crate's tests pass unchanged.
close_reason: is_ident_byte (byte-identical in dml_edges.rs + sql_resolve.rs) consolidated into one pub(crate) fn in plsql-ir lib.rs; both modules import it. Behavior-preserving, net -6 LOC. plsql-ir 324 tests green, no unused warnings.

title: Gate unquoted keyword-collision identifiers (LOOP/IF/CASE/BEGIN/END) in analyze_batch depth counting
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/oraclemcp-guard/src/classifier.rs:329 — verdict=keep, severity=high
**Impact:** A ReadWrite-elevated session can pass a DROP TABLE / TRUNCATE / GRANT DBA-bearing multi-statement batch through the guard as merely Guarded/ReadWrite — the splitter collapses it to one statement and the fail-closed BEGIN/END-desync law never fires, so the operating-level gate Allows it with no Destructive/Ddl/Admin step-up.
**Evidence:** classifier.rs:316-353 — for a bare Token::Word the match arms `Some("IF")|Some("CASE")|Some("LOOP")` do `depth += 1` and `Some("END")` does `depth -= 1`; the only context guard (325-328) is `w.quote_style.is_none()`, which excludes ONLY double-quoted identifiers, not unquoted keyword-collision identifiers. classify() at 913-914 takes the `shape.statement_count <= 1` branch and feeds the whole string to classify_statement; the unparseable-fail-closed default at 688-692 returns Guarded/ReadWrite (non-admin). Empirical run of a throwaway integration test against the crate's public API (then deleted):\nCONTROL shape balanced=false count=2\nCONTROL classify (Forbidden, None)\nEXPLOIT [SELECT 1 AS loop FROM dual; DROP TABLE orders; END;] balanced=true count=1 classify=(Guarded, Some(ReadWrite))\nEXPLOIT [SELECT 1 AS if FROM dual; TRUNCATE TABLE orders; END;] balanced=true count=1 classify=(Gua
**Repro:** Trigger: Classifier::default().classify("SELECT 1 AS loop FROM dual; DROP TABLE orders; END;"). Observed: GuardDecision{danger=Guarded, required_level=Some(ReadWrite)} instead of Forbidden. Control without the keyword alias ("SELECT 1 FROM dual; END;") correctly returns Forbidden, and the same batch with a non-keyword alias ("SELECT 1 FROM dual; DROP TABLE orders;") correctly returns Destructive/Ddl with statement_count=2 — isolating the defect to the bare LOOP/IF/CASE/BEGIN keyword inflating the depth counter so the two inner `;` boundaries fall in the depth!=0 branch (uncounted) and the trailing top-level END rebalances depth to 0 (balanced=true, count=1).
**Suggested fix:** In pure-SQL context, an unquoted bare word that collides with a PL/SQL structural keyword (LOOP/IF/CASE/BEGIN/END) used as an alias/column must not rebalance the block-depth counter. Strongest fail-closed option: when StageA returned PureSql, treat any top-level standalone BEGIN/END/LOOP/IF/CASE outside a recognized PL/SQL block as balanced=false (force Forbidden); or count the `;` segment boundary regardless of depth until has_plsql_block is true and treat any top-level END seen before any BEGIN as went_negative. Add regression tests mirroring quoted_keyword_identifier_does_not_move_block_depth (classifier.rs:1480): assert "SELECT 1 AS loop FROM dual; DROP TABLE orders; END;" (plus AS if/case/begin and bare keyword column names) is Forbidden or at least surfaces the DROP/GRANT as Destructive/Admin and never collapses to statement_count=1/Guarded, while genuine PL/SQL (real BEGIN..END;, 

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 6e08902655), re-verified on main. Root cause: in analyze_batch (classifier.rs), a bare Token::Word matching LOOP/IF/CASE/BEGIN used as a pure-SQL column alias inflated the block-depth counter, so the two real top-level `;` boundaries fell in the depth!=0 branch (uncounted) and a trailing top-level END rebalanced depth to 0 (balanced=true, statement_count=1). classify() then took the single-statement path, sqlparser failed on the multi-statement string, and the unparseable fail-cl

title: Seed advance_to_decl_end depth=1 for PACKAGE/TYPE SPEC so span runs to trailing END name
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-parser-antlr/src/lower/mod.rs:189 — verdict=defense-in-depth, severity=low
**Impact:** A PackageSpec/TypeSpec decl produced by the text-scanner fallback carries a too-short byte-range span (ends at the first member's `;` instead of after `END <name>;`); today no production consumer reads that spec span for user-facing output, so nothing currently breaks — it only becomes wrong output if a future byte-range/hover/highlight consumer reads a spec's span.
**Evidence:** mod.rs:186-190 lower_package SPEC arm: `let end = if is_body { advance_to_decl_end_with_depth(bytes, create_start, 1) } else { advance_to_decl_end(bytes, create_start) };` (depth 0 for SPEC). mod.rs:294-298 lower_type is identical. recover.rs:168 `if matches_kw_at(bytes, pos, b"CASE") { return Some(BlockStep::Open { consumed: 4 }); }` and recover.rs:180-196 end_block_len treats bare END as Close. Probe via real lower_source: SRC LEN=112; DECL COUNT(case)=1; span=0..83 text="CREATE PACKAGE p AS\n  g_max CONSTANT NUMBER := CASE WHEN x = 1 THEN 10 ELSE 20 END;" (END p; ends at 111). Plain `:= 10;` spec: span=0..50 (END p; at 78). Procedure-only spec: span=0..40. So truncation is universal at the first member `;`, not CASE-specific, and decl count is always 1. plsql-ir/src/lower.rs:91-108 PackageSpec sets members: Vec::new() and never re-scans the span. plsql-mcp/src/parse_tools.rs run_parse
**Repro:** Triggering input (real lower_source): `CREATE PACKAGE p AS\n  g_max CONSTANT NUMBER := CASE WHEN x = 1 THEN 10 ELSE 20 END;\n  PROCEDURE do_work;\nEND p;\n` → PackageSpec span end = 83 (after the constant `;`) instead of 111 (after `END p;`). Disproof of overstated harm: decl count = 1 (no orphaned/phantom decls); IR members always Vec::new() (no member loss); engine span-slice runs lower_statement_body over the spec slice but a spec has no executable statements/sinks so call/DML/taint extraction is empty regardless of truncation; MCP consumers do not surface the span at all, so no current sink observes the wrong value.
**Suggested fix:** In lower_package (mod.rs:186-190) and lower_type (mod.rs:294-298), seed depth 1 for the SPEC case too (a spec is `... AS member; member; ... END name;` — an AS…END[ name] envelope the byte scanner does not count), collapsing both conditionals to always call advance_to_decl_end_with_depth(bytes, create_start, 1). Update the doc comments (currently "A package/type SPEC has no such envelope: depth 0") to note the SPEC envelope is `AS ... END[ name];`. Add a regression test asserting a spec with a CASE-valued constant default plus a following PROCEDURE decl has a span END covering through `END <name>;`. Treat as latent hardening / invariant-consistency, not an urgent correctness fix — no current consumer reads spec spans.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.

Root-cause fix in crates/plsql-parser-antlr/src/lower/mod.rs lower_package: a PACKAGE SPEC opens an AS|IS ... END[ name]; envelope the byte scanner (block_structure_step) never counts (it only opens on BEGIN/IF/LOOP/CASE), so with depth seed 0 advance_to_decl_end returned on the first member declaration's `;`, truncating the PackageSpec span. Probe confirmed: pre-fix span end=83, post-fix=111 (just after `END p;`); the CASE-valued constant defaul
