A newline — settable via the untrusted --package CLI override / deserialized BindingPlan — terminated the comment and smuggled top-level fn/mod/use/`}` items into the generated module (denial-of-buil

title: Make EXECUTE IMMEDIATE USING-clause split string-literal-aware in dynamic_sql recogniser
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-symbols/src/dynamic_sql.rs:183 — verdict=keep, severity=medium
**Impact:** A literal-only MERGE/JOIN-USING dynamic statement (common in the ETL/staging PL/SQL this engine targets) loses ALL its referenced-object dependency edges in lineage/symbol resolution, is falsely labeled bind-parameterised, and is simultaneously stamped at the highest (High) confidence tier — a silent completeness gap plus confidence inflation that directly undermines the crate's honest-edge guarantee.
**Evidence:** crates/plsql-symbols/src/dynamic_sql.rs:182-189 (exact code): `let rest = &text[after_kw + "EXECUTE IMMEDIATE".len()..]; let using_pos = rest.to_ascii_uppercase().find(" USING "); let body = if let Some(p) = using_pos { &rest[..p] } else { rest };` — a literal-blind substring search, run BEFORE the string-aware char-walker (lines 217-256). Line 259: `let uses_binds = using_pos.is_some();`.

Empirical reproduction (temporary test added, compiled+run via `cargo test -p plsql-symbols ... --nocapture`, then reverted; git diff --stat now empty):
  MERGE fragments=[]
  MERGE candidate_objects=[]
  MERGE uses_binds=true
  MERGE opacity_reason=LiteralOnly
  MERGE confidence=Confidence { level: High, explanation: Some("literal-only dynamic SQL — assembled statement is a compile-time constant") }
  JOIN  fragments=[]  candidate_objects=[]  uses_binds=true
  CONTROL ('UPDATE t SET x = :1' USING v_x
**Repro:** Input: recognise_dynamic_sql("EXECUTE IMMEDIATE 'MERGE INTO target t USING staging s ON (t.id=s.id) WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET t.val = s.val';", site). The ` USING ` at byte offset 21 lies INSIDE the quoted literal, so using_pos=Some(21); body=&rest[..21] truncates to `'MERGE INTO target t` (unterminated literal); the char-walker reaches EOF with in_string==true and never flushes current_lit, yielding fragments=[] → candidate_objects=[] (target/staging edges dropped) → opacity_reason=LiteralOnly → confidence=High, while uses_binds is falsely true. Control with USING outside the literal ('UPDATE t SET x = :1' USING v_x) still parses correctly, proving the defect is specifically the literal-
**Suggested fix:** Replace the literal-blind `rest.to_ascii_uppercase().find(" USING ")` at line 183 with a top-level (outside-any-`'...'`) scan that reuses the same doubled-`''` escape rule the walker already applies (lines 219-231), so a ` USING ` keyword inside the dynamic SQL text literal is never mistaken for the PL/SQL bind clause. The native-dynamic-SQL USING always appears after the text expression is fully closed, so a top_level_using() helper preserves the legitimate trailing-bind case while leaving MERGE/JOIN/CREATE-INDEX literals intact. Add regression tests: MERGE-USING and JOIN-USING literals must yield non-empty candidate_objects and uses_binds==false, alongside the existing trailing-bind test at line 518.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 6070824fd7), re-verified on main. Root-caused and fixed the literal-blind EXECUTE IMMEDIATE USING split in crates/plsql-symbols/src/dynamic_sql.rs. The old line 183 `rest.to_ascii_uppercase().find(" USING ")` matched a ` USING ` keyword INSIDE the dynamic-SQL text literal (MERGE...USING, JOIN...USING (col)), truncating the body mid-literal; the char-walker then hit EOF still in_string and never flushed current_lit, so fragments==[] and candidate_objects==[] (all referenced-object

title: Sanitize CR/LF (and control chars) in BindingDiagnostic fields before emitting `//` comments in plsql-bindgen
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-bindgen/src/emit.rs:840 — verdict=keep, severity=medium
**Impact:** A hostile or upstream-buggy BindingPlan whose diagnostic message/code/routine/suggested_workaround contains a newline makes the plsql-bindgen CLI write uncommented, attacker-controlled text as live top-level Rust into the generated module — breaking the project's fail-closed "always emit compilable/safe Rust" invariant (corrupting/mis-compiling the generated bindings, or smuggling code that executes once the emitted module is compiled into a consumer).
**Evidence:** emit.rs:834-848 emit_diagnostic_comment raw-interpolates all four untrusted fields with no escaping:
  840  let _ = writeln!(out, "// bindgen-diag {scope}({}): {}", diag.code, diag.message);
  845-846  if let Some(workaround) = &diag.suggested_workaround { writeln!(out, "//   workaround: {workaround}"); }
  (scope is format!("[{r}] ") from diag.routine)
All four are Deserialize fields on BindingDiagnostic (lib.rs:126-145). Production path: main.rs:212 `serde_json::from_str` of an UNTRUSTED BindingPlan from --input/stdin, then main.rs:228 `emit_wrappers(&plan)` written verbatim at main.rs:242 (no sanitization — rg of main.rs shows no replace/control/comment_safe over generated output). `rg emit_wrappers crates/` shows the only non-test caller is main.rs:228.

End-to-end repro through the REAL built CLI (target/debug/plsql-bindgen, built clean):
  $ ./target/debug/plsql-bindgen --input /tm
**Repro:** Minimal triggering input (verified against the real CLI binary): a BindingPlan JSON with `"diagnostics":[{"routine":"foo","code":"BG_REF_CURSOR","message":"benign text\npub fn pwned() { std::process::exit(0); }","suggested_workaround":"do this\n*/ pub fn also_pwned() {}","severity":"skip"}]` piped through `plsql-bindgen --input plan.json`. The `\n` in `message` terminates the `//` comment so `pub fn pwned() {...}` is emitted as a live top-level Rust item; `suggested_workaround` is equally injectable. (Note severity serde-renames to lowercase: "skip"/"warn"/"info".) I also compiled the standalone writeln! pattern and the emitted text to confirm the injected line is valid Rust source, not iner
**Suggested fix:** Add a `comment_safe(&str)->String` helper that maps '\n'/'\r' (ideally all control chars) to spaces, and route diag.routine, diag.code, diag.message, and diag.suggested_workaround through it inside emit_diagnostic_comment (emit.rs:834-848). Apply the same treatment to plan.package_name at emit.rs:57-58, which has the identical raw-interpolation flaw (and is also settable via the untrusted --package override at main.rs:225). Add a regression test mirroring diagnostics_appear_as_trailing_comments with message/code/suggested_workaround containing "\n pub fn evil() {}" asserting every emitted line trim_start().starts_with("//").

### crates/plsql-bindgen/src/emit.rs:842 — verdict=keep, severity=medium
**Impact:** A hostile or compromised BindingPlan JSON fed to `plsql-bindgen` (a generator input deserialized with zero validation) can use a `\n` in any diagnostic field (routine/code/message/suggested_workaround) to terminate the `//` comment and inject arbitrary top-level Rust into the generated bindings module, which then compiles cleanly into the developer's crate.
**Evidence:** emit.rs:834-848 emit_diagnostic_comment writes raw: `writeln!(out, "// bindgen-diag {scope}({}): {}", diag.code, diag.message)` and `writeln!(out, "//   workaround: {workaround}")`, with scope = `format!("[{r}] ")` from diag.routine — no newline/control-char escaping. emit.rs:73-74: `for diag in &plan.diagnostics { emit_diagnostic_comment(&mut out, diag); }` runs for EVERY diagnostic, ungated. lib.rs:127-137: routine/code/message are plain String, suggested_workaround is Option<String>, derive-only Deserialize, no validation. main.rs:212: `let mut plan: BindingPlan = serde_json::from_str(&json)` from untrusted stdin/--input. emit.rs:332-347 documents the BindingPlan as UNTRUSTED and the fail-closed identifier gate; emit.rs:348 shows invalid_identifier_reason guards only routines, never the diagnostic path. Existing test emit.rs:1021 uses only single-line strings so the gap was never exer
**Repro:** Plan with diagnostic { routine:null, code:"X\npub fn another_injected() -> u32 { 42 }\n// ", message:"boom\npub struct AlsoInjected;\n// ", suggested_workaround:"wk\nimpl AlsoInjected { pub fn via_workaround() -> u32 { 7 } }\n// ", severity:"warn" } => CLI exit 0, generated file contains those three items at top level (outside any `//`), and the file compiles with rustc exit 0 producing the injected symbols. (Note: severity must be one of skip/warn/info — `error` is rejected by serde, but that does not gate the injection.) The routine field injects identically via the `[{r}] ` scope prefix.
**Suggested fix:** In emit_diagnostic_comment (emit.rs:834-848) sanitize every interpolated field before writing it into a `//` line so no field can terminate the comment. Add `fn one_line(s: &str) -> String { s.chars().map(|c| if c.is_control() { ' ' } else { c }).collect() }` and apply it to diag.routine, diag.code, diag.message, and suggested_workaround. Add a regression test that pushes a diagnostic whose code/message/routine/suggested_workaround each contain "\n<rust item>" and asserts no top-level item leaks outside a `//` comment, mirroring the invalid-identifier compile-level tests. The routine-in-comment at emit.rs:356 (`problem` embedding r.name) is lower risk since r.name has already passed the identifier gate, but applying the same one_line helper there keeps the convention uniform.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit ca093c9ecc), re-verified on main. Root cause: emit_diagnostic_comment (emit.rs:834-848) raw-interpolated all four untrusted BindingDiagnostic fields (routine via the `[{r}] ` scope, code, message, suggested_workaround) into single-line `//` comments emitted for EVERY diagnostic, with no escaping. A newline in any field terminated the comment so the remainder was emitted as live top-level Rust (compiles into the consumer). Fix: routed diag.routine, diag.code, diag.message and sugg

title: Fail closed on buried-semicolon SQL CASE/IF/LOOP desync (report Forbidden, not Guarded)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/oraclemcp-guard/src/classifier.rs:339 — verdict=defense-in-depth, severity=low
**Impact:** A malformed batch with an unbalanced SQL CASE that hides a top-level ';' boundary (e.g. "SELECT CASE WHEN 1=1 THEN 1 FROM dual ; DROP TABLE t END") is reported balanced/single-statement and downgraded to Guarded/ReadWrite instead of the documented Forbidden, violating the classifier's fail-closed desync law. No directly-executable destructive bypass: the payload is invalid single-statement SQL that Oracle rejects with a syntax error, and the executor runs the full string as one statement with no re-split, so the hidden DROP/GRANT/TRUNCATE cannot execute. Pure invariant-hygiene / defense-in-depth gap, not a live injection.
**Evidence:** classifier.rs:339 `Some("IF") | Some("CASE") | Some("LOOP") => { if !expecting_close { depth += 1; } ... }` increments the same `depth` counter used by BEGIN/END; classifier.rs:358 counts a ';' boundary only when `depth == 0`; classifier.rs:345-351 END decrements depth (rebalancing to 0); classifier.rs:913 takes the single-statement path when `statement_count <= 1`; classifier.rs:688-692 returns Guarded/ReadWrite on parse failure; classifier.rs:681 `starts_with_admin_verb` is leading-verb only (SELECT skips it). Module doc lines 9-11/23-26 and BatchShape doc line 284 state a desync must make the whole batch Forbidden.

Empirical run (temp integration test, default Classifier, pristine HEAD):
[P1-DROP] balanced=true has_plsql_block=false count=1 | danger=Guarded req=Some(ReadWrite)
[P2-GRANT] balanced=true has_plsql_block=false count=1 | danger=Guarded req=Some(ReadWrite)
[P3-TRUNCATE] ba
**Repro:** Trigger (mechanism): Classifier::default().classify("SELECT CASE WHEN 1=1 THEN 1 FROM dual ; DROP TABLE t END") returns danger=Guarded, required=ReadWrite (verified above) — should be Forbidden per the fail-closed law. Same for the GRANT and TRUNCATE variants.
Disproof of security impact: the first CASE segment is unbalanced (no END before FROM), so the full string is syntactically invalid single-statement Oracle SQL. It is delivered verbatim to StatementExecutor::execute (query_execute.rs:132) as one statement; Oracle's single-statement API does not statement-split on ';', so it raises a syntax error (ORA-00911/00936-class) rather than executing the hidden DROP. Control proof: a VALID balan
**Suggested fix:** Add a `saw_buried_semicolon` flag set in the `Token::SemiColon` arm when `depth > 0`, and in the BatchShape result force `balanced = false` when `saw_buried_semicolon && !has_plsql_block` — a ';' nested inside a pure-SQL CASE/IF/LOOP (no BEGIN/DECLARE seen) is illegitimate and signals a hidden top-level boundary, so the batch must route to the desync→Forbidden branch at classifier.rs:904. Add a regression test asserting the three crafted payloads classify as Forbidden, plus the balanced-CASE control still classifies Destructive so legitimate multi-statement detection does not regress. Treat as latent invariant-hygiene hardening (no live exploit) to be landed before any real StatementExecutor is bound.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 6e08902655), re-verified on main. Same root cause as oracle-73t1.1: classifier.rs increments the same block-depth counter for SQL CASE/IF/LOOP, so an unbalanced pure-SQL CASE that buries a top-level `;` (e.g. `SELECT CASE WHEN 1=1 THEN 1 FROM dual ; DROP TABLE t END`) reported balanced=true/count=1 and downgraded to Guarded/ReadWrite instead of the documented Forbidden, violating the fail-closed desync law. Fixed by the same saw_buried_semicolon mechanism — the buried `;` in a St

title: Reject unparseable custom-tool declared_level as a LoadError instead of silently dropping it
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/oraclemcp-core/src/custom_tools.rs:326 — verdict=keep, severity=low
**Impact:** An operator who pins a custom tool with a typo'd declared_level (e.g. "DLL" for "DDL") has that safety restriction silently discarded at load time; the tool loads at the looser classifier-derived level (e.g. READ_ONLY) and becomes reachable on a lower-ceiling profile than the operator intended, with no error.
**Evidence:** custom_tools.rs:326-333 (classify_at_load): `let effective = match def.declared_level.as_deref().and_then(OperatingLevel::parse) { Some(declared) => derived.max(declared), None => derived };` — `.and_then(OperatingLevel::parse)` yields None for any unrecognized token, and `None => derived` drops the declared floor.
levels.rs:57-65 (OperatingLevel::parse): matches only the exact trimmed/uppercased tokens READ_ONLY | READ_WRITE | DDL | ADMIN; `_ => None`. So "DLL", "read only", "rw" all → None.
custom_tools.rs:199-235 (validate): read in full — checks name, prefix, description, body, params only; never inspects declared_level. classify_at_load calls validate() first (line 316), so a typo passes structural validation.
Asymmetry confirmed — query_execute.rs:44-58 (parse_level): an unparseable requested_level on the EXECUTE path is surfaced as `ErrorClass::InvalidArguments`. Only the declared
**Repro:** Trigger: def_sql("sel", "SELECT 1 FROM dual", Some("DLL")) with max_level = OperatingLevel::ReadOnly. Trace: derived = ReadOnly (SELECT). Line 326-330: declared_level Some("DLL") → parse("DLL") → None → line 332 `None => derived` → effective = ReadOnly. Line 334: ReadOnly > ReadOnly is false → returns Ok(LoadedTool{ required_level: ReadOnly }). Contrast: the valid Some("DDL") variant (existing passing test, line 883-891) yields LoadError::OverCeiling{ required: Ddl } on the same ReadOnly ceiling. So input Some("DLL") → wrong output (silent load at ReadOnly) instead of the operator's intended DDL pin / a LoadError. NOT a privilege-escalation fail-open: derived is always the floor (effective =
**Suggested fix:** In CustomToolDef::validate() (custom_tools.rs:199-235), add a structural check near the other validations: `if let Some(lvl) = self.declared_level.as_deref() { if OperatingLevel::parse(lvl).is_none() { return Err(invalid(&format!("declared_level '{lvl}' is not a known operating level (READ_ONLY | READ_WRITE | DDL | ADMIN)"))); } }`. Because classify_at_load (line 316) and parse_tools_file both call validate() first, this rejects the typo at file-load time on every tools.d path. Add a unit test asserting def_sql("x","SELECT 1 FROM dual",Some("DLL")) → LoadError::Invalid.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit c9584c0aab), re-verified on main. Root cause: classify_at_load (crates/oraclemcp-core/src/custom_tools.rs:326) used `def.declared_level.as_deref().and_then(OperatingLevel::parse)` with `None => derived`, so any unrecognized declared_level token (e.g. typo 'DLL' for 'DDL', or 'read only', 'rw') silently parsed to None and the operator's intended safety pin was discarded — the tool loaded at the looser classifier-derived level (ReadOnly) on a lower-ceiling profile than intended, wi

title: Fix body_has_dml to scan all DML keyword occurrences (latent; land when QUAL007/008/CFL facts get wired into the engine)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-ir/src/fact_emit.rs:346 — verdict=defense-in-depth, severity=low
**Impact:** None today: the body_has_dml logic flaw is real but unreachable in production — the scanners/emit functions that feed QUAL007/QUAL008/cursor-FOR-loop facts are only ever called from the #[cfg(test)] module, so those rules emit zero findings in any real run regardless of this bug. It would become a fail-open under-report only if/when the QUAL007/008/CFL fact-emission path is wired into the engine.
**Evidence:** crates/plsql-ir/src/fact_emit.rs:346-355 (current code, first-hit-only, no retry loop):
346 fn body_has_dml(body: &str) -> bool {
347     for kw in ["insert ", "update ", "delete ", "merge "] {
348         if let Some(at) = body.find(kw)
349             && keyword_boundary_before(body, at)
350         { return true; }
351     }
352     false
353 }
keyword_boundary_before (217-221) returns false when char before `at` is alphanumeric or `_`. mask_string_literals (511-538) only blanks single-quoted contents, so `v_last_update DATE` survives.

Empirical reproduction (standalone rustc --edition 2024 of the exact logic):
  current=false fixed=true  | decoy update local then real update  <-- DIVERGES
  current=false fixed=true  | decoy insert local then real insert  <-- DIVERGES
  current=false fixed=true  | decoy delete local then real delete  <-- DIVERGES
  current=true  fixed=true  | genuine
**Repro:** Logic trigger (the defect is real in isolation): body_has_dml("function f return number is v_last_update date; begin update t set c=1 where id=p; return 1; end;") returns FALSE — first `update ` hit lands inside `v_last_update `, preceded by `_`, boundary check false, no retry to the genuine `update t`. The fix `["insert ","update ","delete ","merge "].iter().any(|kw| body.match_indices(kw).any(|(at,_)| keyword_boundary_before(body,at)))` returns TRUE. Verified by standalone compile above.
Impossibility-of-current-impact proof: body_has_dml is called only by scan_cursor_for_loops (403), scan_dml_in_function (695), scan_deterministic_misuse (825). None of those three is invoked outside `#[cfg
**Suggested fix:** Replace the first-hit-only loop with match_indices over every occurrence, mirroring the sibling scanners (scan_dml_in_function lines 692-694, scan_deterministic_misuse lines 812-814):
fn body_has_dml(body: &str) -> bool {
    ["insert ", "update ", "delete ", "merge "]
        .iter()
        .any(|kw| body.match_indices(kw).any(|(at, _)| keyword_boundary_before(body, at)))
}
Add a regression test covering a body that declares a `_update`/`_delete`/`_insert`-suffixed local before the real DML. PRIORITY: this is latent — schedule it to land together with the (currently missing) engine wiring that emits DmlInFunction/DeterministicMisuse/CursorForLoop facts, since neither the bug nor the fix affects production output until that path is wired.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit cdc46d4b48), re-verified on main. Root-cause fix in crates/plsql-ir/src/fact_emit.rs body_has_dml: replaced first-hit-only `body.find(kw) + keyword_boundary_before` loop with `match_indices(kw).any(|(at,_)| keyword_boundary_before(body,at))` over EVERY occurrence of each DML keyword (insert/update/delete/merge). The old code under-reported (fail-open) when a DML keyword first appeared as the tail of a declared local (e.g. v_last_update, deleted_flag, last_inserted), preceded by '

title: Drain stdout/stderr concurrently with stdin write in SubprocessBackend::complete to avoid pipe deadlock
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-accretion/src/proposer.rs:794 — verdict=keep, severity=low
**Impact:** An external integrator who wires SubprocessBackend to a real model CLI and sends a prompt larger than the ~64KB stdin pipe buffer to a child that streams substantial stdout/stderr before draining stdin will hang the proposer thread indefinitely (pipe deadlock); no shipped binary reaches this path today, so the impact is latent.
**Evidence:** crates/plsql-accretion/src/proposer.rs:781-810 — single-threaded write then drain:
  let mut child = Command::new(&self.program) .args(&self.args) .stdin(Stdio::piped()).stdout(Stdio::piped()).stderr(Stdio::piped()).spawn()...
  child.stdin.take()...write_all(prompt.as_bytes()).map_err(...)?;   // line 794
  let out = child.wait_with_output()...;                              // line 797
No spawned writer thread / no concurrent drain. Doc line 762: "so an arbitrarily long prompt is safe" — contradicted by the impl.

Empirical repro (standalone binary mirroring complete(): write_all then wait_with_output, piped stdin/stdout/stderr) fed a 400000-byte prompt to `cat`:
  prompt size = 400000 bytes
  DEADLOCKED: write_all/wait_with_output never returned in 6s (thread still running)
  exit=7

Reachability (rg over crates/, worktrees excluded): SubprocessBackend referenced only in proposer.rs (d
**Repro:** Triggering input: SubprocessBackend::new("<streaming model CLI that interleaves stdin reads with stdout writes, e.g. cat>", []).complete(&"x\n".repeat(200_000)) → prompt (~400KB) exceeds the ~64KB stdin pipe buffer; the child fills its own stdout pipe (parent isn't draining, since wait_with_output is not yet called) and blocks on write() while the parent blocks in write_all() → deadlock (observed: thread never finishes within 6s; exit code 7). Cannot trigger via any in-tree path: build_prompt emits a few hundred bytes and no shipped binary instantiates SubprocessBackend; the only SubprocessBackend tests pass <20-byte inputs or use printf (which ignores stdin), so write_all returns before the
**Suggested fix:** Move the prompt write into a spawned std::thread (which drops stdin on completion to send EOF) and call wait_with_output() on the main thread so stdout/stderr are drained concurrently — std-only, no new deps. Join the writer thread after wait_with_output and propagate its error. Also fix the doc at line 762 ("an arbitrarily long prompt is safe") to be honest, or make it true via this change. The finding's suggested_fix snippet is correct; consider tolerating BrokenPipe on the stdin write when the child exits successfully (some CLIs read only a prefix). A regression test feeding a >64KB prompt to `cat` (which deadlocks today) would lock this in.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit ce33bb0c7e), re-verified on main. Root-cause fix for the stdin/stdout pipe deadlock in SubprocessBackend::complete (crates/plsql-accretion/src/proposer.rs). The old code did a single-threaded write_all(prompt) THEN wait_with_output(), so a prompt larger than the ~64KB stdin pipe buffer against a CLI that streams stdout before draining stdin (e.g. `cat`) deadlocked both processes. Fix: move the stdin write into a dedicated std::thread (std-only, no new deps) that writes prompt byt

title: Route run_get_errors DB-text fields through K18 sanitize() before the get_errors/compile_with_warnings dispatch arms land
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-mcp/src/source.rs:190 — verdict=defense-in-depth, severity=low
**Impact:** None today: no JSON-RPC tools/call path reaches run_get_errors, so no hostile DB cell can currently surface unsanitized markup to an LLM. The gap becomes a real prompt-injection vector (medium) only if/when a get_errors or compile_with_warnings dispatch arm is added that returns these rows to an agent — at which point a compromised schema owner could embed tool-call markup in an identifier name that Oracle echoes into ALL_ERRORS.TEXT, bypassing the K18 neutralization every other DB-text path enforces.
**Evidence:** source.rs:174-192 — run_get_errors copies every string field verbatim, NO sanitize():
  owner: row.text("OWNER").unwrap_or("").to_string(),
  object_name: row.text("NAME").unwrap_or("").to_string(),
  object_type: row.text("TYPE").unwrap_or("").to_string(),
  attribute: row.text("ATTRIBUTE").unwrap_or("").to_string(),
  text: row.text("TEXT").unwrap_or("").to_string(),
Sibling run_get_object_source (source.rs:84) `let (scrubbed, was_sanitized) = sanitize(line);` + pushes UnknownReason::ResponseSanitized (97). run_get_clob (source.rs:127) `let (scrubbed, was_sanitized) = sanitize(&raw);` + same reason (138). Module header (source.rs:3-5) documents the two sanitize, omits get_errors. sanitize() (query.rs:254-318) structurally neutralizes EVERY `<`/`>` run to fullwidth look-alikes — get_errors never invokes it.
compile.rs:124 `let errors_response = run_get_errors(conn, owner, object_name)?;
**Repro:** Disproof of current exploitability: There is no JSON-RPC tools/call route to run_get_errors. dispatch_table() (dispatch.rs:122-165) is the lockstep-enforced single source of truth (test dispatch_table_matches_default_registry, dispatch.rs:360) and does not contain get_errors/compile_with_warnings/get_object_source/get_clob; default_tool_registry() (lib.rs:245-263) registers none of them. The only callers are compile.rs (internal Rust), lib.rs re-exports, and #[cfg(test)] integration tests (hero_demo_live_xe.rs:447 etc.) that call the function as a Rust API — the "get_errors" string there is a record!() transcript label, not a dispatch key. Hypothetical future trigger (once a dispatch arm lan
**Suggested fix:** In run_get_errors, sanitize the free-text fields (text, attribute, owner, object_name, object_type) via query::sanitize() exactly as run_get_object_source/run_get_clob do, OR-ing each field's `changed` flag into a per-response indicator; the numeric line/position/message_number fields stay as parsed integers. Add `unknown_reasons: Vec<UnknownReason>` to GetErrorsResponse and push UnknownReason::ResponseSanitized when any field was scrubbed (mirroring GetObjectSourceResponse). Then in compile.rs::run_compile_with_warnings, propagate errors_response.unknown_reasons into CompileWithWarningsResponse.unknown_reasons. Add a unit test planting a runtime-assembled angle-bracket tool-call shape in the TEXT/ATTRIBUTE columns of a stub error row, asserting no surviving '<'/'>' and that ResponseSanitized is emitted. Low cost now (functions not yet dispatched); closes the divergent-coverage gap so th

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit ab6f1ffe95), re-verified on main. Root-cause fix: run_get_errors (crates/plsql-mcp/src/source.rs) now routes every free-text field (owner, object_name, object_type, attribute, text) through query::sanitize() exactly as the sibling run_get_object_source/run_get_clob paths do, via a closure that ORs each cell's `changed` flag into a per-response indicator; numeric line/position/message_number fields stay as parsed integers. Added `unknown_reasons: Vec<UnknownReason>` to GetErrorsRe

title: PLSQL-MCP-LIVE-007 — Implement `get_object_source`, `get_clob` (with K18 sanitization), `get_errors` (structured `USER_ERRORS` / `ALL_ERRORS`)
description: Imported from `plan.md` v0.11 on 2026-05-12.
close_reason: Added crates/plsql-mcp/src/source.rs with three read-only tools: (1) run_get_object_source(conn, owner, object_name, object_type) reads ALL_SOURCE ordered by LINE, reassembles the body, sanitizes each line via the K18 sanitize() from PLSQL-MCP-LIVE-004, ensures lines end in newline for round-trip stability. Returns GetObjectSourceResponse with source + sanitized_lines counter + UnknownReason::ResponseSanitized when scrubbed. (2) run_get_clob(conn, sql, params, max_chars) one-row SELECT that projects a CLOB; first non-null cell is the payload; K18 sanitization + optional Unicode char-count truncation with ellipsis. Empty result is safe (returns default response). (3) run_get_errors(conn, owner, object_name) reads ALL_ERRORS (when owner non-empty) or USER_ERRORS (when owner blank) for structured ObjectError rows: owner / object_name / object_type / line / position / attribute / message_number / text — numeric line+position+message_number parsed; agents can reason about them programmatically rather than parsing free text. title: PLSQL-OSS-AUDIT — plsql-parser-java is a non-functional stub crate with zero reverse deps
description:
crates/plsql-parser-java is a workspace member and publishable crate (dual-licensed, has a description) but is a non-functional stub:

- Its own lib.rs:18-24 documents that 'output decoding is deferred to PARSE-000D' and that 'for now *any* run' returns a degraded ParseResult with an empty AST/CST plus a PARSE-JAVA-UNAVAILABLE diagnostic. lib.rs:191-194 confirms: even when the Java worker runs successfully, the result is discarded because 'structured decoding is not yet wired'.
- So the JavaAntlrBackend NEVER produces a real parse, by design, regardless of input.
- It has zero reverse dependencies: a scan of all Cargo.toml + *.rs files shows nothing in the workspace depends on plsql-parser-java except the top-level workspace members list. The engine, MCP, accretion, etc. all use plsql-parser-antlr instead.

Why it matters: shipping a publishable crate in an OSS release whose sole exported backend is permanently non-functional, and which nothing in the tree consumes, is confusing. A reviewer browsing the workspace will spend time on a crate that does nothing. The crate's docs are honest about it (good), but the crate itself is dead weight.

Suggested fix: decide explicitly -- either (a) move plsql-parser-java out of the workspace members list and into a 'future-work' / unpublished state until PARSE-000D lands, or (b) if it must stay, set 'publish = false' in its Cargo.toml and add a top-of-README/crate note that it is an in-progress backend not yet usable. Same consideration applies more weakly to plsql-bindgen (also zero reverse deps) but bindgen at least ships a working CLI binary, so this bead is scoped to plsql-parser-java.
close_reason: Chose option B (keep in workspace, make status unambiguous + publish=false): preserves CI coverage of the panic-free degradation contract and wire-protocol sketch in wire.rs that the spike still demonstrates. Added publish=false plus an explicit 'inert historical spike — parser-backend tournament loser' note in Cargo.toml description and TOML comment. Rewrote crate-level docs in src/lib.rs with a 'Status: not a usable crate' section stating it never produces a real parse by design, has zero reverse deps, and that plsql-parser-antlr is the live backend. Updated JavaAntlrBackend struct doc to match. Verified: cargo build --workspace exit 0; cargo test -p plsql-parser-java: 14 pass.

title: PLSQL-OSS-AUDIT4 — internal 'bead' / 'bead skeleton' vocabulary leaks into 45+ public rustdoc comments across 27 crates
description:
Closed bead audit3-4wyh / oss-005 / etc. de-slopified README + AGENTS.md, but the internal-process word 'bead' (the project's local issue-tracker term) still appears in 45 `///` or `//!` doc-comments across 27 source files. A few representative samples:

- crates/plsql-ir/src/decl.rs:11 — //! Subsequent beads will refine the placeholder [TypeRef] payloads once …
- crates/plsql-ir/src/decl.rs:8 — //! The actual lowering from parser AST to these declarations lands in (top-level) and later beads (sentence broken: a parenthetical with no referent at end of line)
- crates/plsql-mcp/src/safety.rs:106 — /// defined; the bead skeleton uses a URL-safe random-looking string …
- crates/plsql-sast/src/rules.rs:132 — /// Load a policy from a TOML string. The CLI bead …
- crates/plsql-cicd/src/gate.rs:664 — /// **Saturating cast regression (oracle-kxb3).** …  ← internal bead ID exposed in public doc
- crates/plsql-accretion/src/proposer.rs:411 — /// rich templating belongs to a future bead (`010`).
- crates/plsql-mcp/src/patch.rs:142 — /// quarantined bead (spec §7) …

Repro:
  rg -nI '^\s*///.*\bbead\b|^//!.*\bbead\b' --type rust crates | wc -l   ← 45 hits
  rg -lI '^\s*///.*\bbead\b|^//!.*\bbead\b' --type rust crates | wc -l   ← 27 files
  rg -nI '^\s*///.*\boracle-[a-z0-9]{4}\b' --type rust crates             ← 1 real bead-ID hit (gate.rs:664)

Reviewer impact: anyone browsing `cargo doc` on docs.rs / a published version sees phrases like 'The CLI bead', 'the bead skeleton', 'a future bead', and one bare internal tracker ID (oracle-kxb3). That is internal-process vocabulary the OSS user has no context for — same class of finding the round-3 de-slopify pass cleaned out of the README. The text reads as 'half-translated from a private workflow' and is exactly what an OSS reviewer would flag as 'tracker chatter left in the API docs'.

Suggested fix: a single sweep replacing 'bead' → 'pass'/'step'/'milestone'/'follow-up' (whichever fits the local context) in all `///` and `//!` comments, plus the one bead-ID reference at gate.rs:664 collapsed to describe the regression without the tracker handle. The comment at decl.rs:8 also needs the dangling '(top-level) and later beads' sentence repaired — it currently reads as if the parenthetical was supposed to name a bead ID that was deleted.
close_reason: Swept 65 'bead'/'beads' occurrences from /// and //! doc-comments across 39 source files: rewrote each per-site for sentence fluency (bead → pass / step / module / quarantine record / contract / later work as fit). Three explicit spot fixes landed: gate.rs:664 drops (oracle-kxb3); safety.rs:106 reframes 'bead skeleton' as 'reference implementation expects URL-safe random-looking string'; decl.rs:8 rewrites the gn1v-broken 'lands in (top-level) and later beads' / 'registration pass lands in.' sentence cleanly. Final 'git grep -nE (///|//!).*beads?' over crates/tools = 0 hits. Verified: cargo build --workspace clean; cargo test --workspace passes 1905/1905; cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings clean.

title: PLSQL-OSS-UX — plsql-depgraph 'doctor' (no --graph) prints miette pretty error but exits 2 (Axiom 5 OK, but message is unhelpful)
description:
CLI: plsql-depgraph doctor.

What's missing/awkward: 'plsql-depgraph doctor' (no --graph) prints '  × missing required `--graph <PATH|->` argument' (miette-styled with a leading × glyph and indent) to stderr and exits 2. The exit code is correct, but the message doesn't give an example invocation, doesn't say where a serialized DepGraph artifact typically lives, and doesn't suggest 'plsql-engine analyze | jq .payload.depgraph' (or wherever the artifact comes from). Same for 'plsql-depgraph query' / 'explain' without --graph.

Why an agent or human would trip on it: An agent that knows it has a depgraph problem won't know how to PRODUCE a serialized DepGraph JSON. The error stops at 'missing arg' instead of teaching 'how do I get this arg?'. Per Axiom 6 (Error-Teaches), the error should name (a) what failed, (b) where, (c) the exact command that produces the missing input.

Suggested fix: Augment the missing-arg error: '× missing required --graph <PATH|->; produce one with: plsql-engine analyze <project-root> > run.json && jq .payload.depgraph run.json > depgraph.json; then: plsql-depgraph doctor --graph depgraph.json'. If a 'serialize-depgraph' subcommand exists elsewhere, name it. Pin with a regression test.
close_reason: TDD-fixed: added a CLI smoke test (missing_graph_error_points_at_plsql_engine_analyze) that runs 'plsql-depgraph' against doctor / query cycle-detect / explain --node-id 1 without --graph, asserts exit code 2 and that stderr mentions --graph + 'plsql-engine analyze' + --out (verified RED before, GREEN after). Augmented CliError::MissingGraph with a miette #[diagnostic(help(...))] that teaches the canonical recovery: plsql-engine analyze <root> --out run.json && jq .payload.dep_graph run.json > depgraph.json && plsql-depgraph --graph depgraph.json <subcommand>, plus the stdin variant. Verified: cargo build -p plsql-depgraph OK; cargo test -p plsql-depgraph all green; cargo clippy -p plsql-depgraph --all-targets -- -D warnings OK. Visual confirmation: 'plsql-depgraph doctor' now prints the help text under the error and still exits 2 (Axiom 5 preserved, Axiom 6 satisfied).

title: PLSQL-PROJECT — split_script has no string/comment state: a lone '/' line OR a trailing ';' INSIDE a multi-line string literal falsely splits the statement mid-string (corrupts unit boundaries)
description: splitter.rs split_script (line-oriented) decides boundaries via `trimmed == "/"` and `!in_plsql && trimmed.ends_with(';')` with NO tracking of open single-quote / q-quote string literals or block comments across lines. Repro 1 (false split on '/' inside string): a SQL statement containing a multi-line string literal where one interior line is exactly `/` -> split as if a SQL*Plus terminator. Repro 2 (false split on ';'): plain-SQL (not in_plsql) multi-line string literal whose first physical line ends with `;` e.g. INSERT INTO t VALUES ('line one;<NL>line two'); -> line 1 trimmed ends ';' and !in_plsql -> emitted as a complete statement, truncating the string and leaving `line two');` as a bogus second statement. Also block comments containing `/` on their own line or a line ending `;`. Fix: add cross-line lexer state (single-quote parity with '' escape, q'X..X' / nq, /* */ block comments) and suppress the '/' and ';' split triggers while any are open; mirror recover_to_statement_boundary (oracle-1wsf) and scrub_literals q-quote handling. Needs TDD with multi-line-string + lone-/-in-string + ;-in-string + block-comment cases; behavior-sensitive (rest of pipeline depends on unit boundaries) so do it as a focused dedicated change, not a drive-by. Same string-blindness class as 1wsf (parser-antlr) and 5sdv (looks_wrapped).
close_reason: Fixed PLSQL-PROJECT split_script boundary-corruption bug (commit 23978e9). Root cause: line-by-line scan with no lexical state => a lone '/' line OR trailing ';' inside a multi-line string literal / block comment falsely terminated, corrupting unit boundaries. Fix: persistent cross-line in_string/in_block_comment state via consume_line() (handles Oracle '' escaped-quote, -- line comments rest-inert, multi-line /* */; returns ends-with-top-level-';'). Lone-'/' terminator now gated on at_top_level_at_line_start (a '/' inside an open string is content); ';' SQL terminator uses the top-level result not a buried ';'; a real terminator resets lexical state. Behaviour-preserving for all non-pathological cases — 50 pre-existing splitter tests still green + 4 new regression tests (';' in multi-line string, lone '/' in multi-line string, ';'/'/'in block comment, '' escaped quote). 54 plsql-project tests pass; cargo clippy --no-deps -D warnings clean. My plsql-project lane, collision-free with cc_1's plsql-sast.

title: PLSQL-OSS-MOCK2 — plsql-mcp doctor remediation text 'this is expected for the bead skeleton' references closed beads as if still open
description: crates/plsql-mcp/src/doctor.rs:222-232. When the ToolRegistry is empty, the doctor emits a Warning with remediation: 'Per-tool beads (PLSQL-MCP-002..PLSQL-MCP-LIVE-018) populate the registry; this is expected for the bead skeleton.' Two problems for an OSS user reading this output: (1) PLSQL-MCP-002..PLSQL-MCP-LIVE-018 are internal bead references that mean nothing to an external user, (2) 'this is expected for the bead skeleton' frames the empty-registry state as developer-in-progress when it's actually a configuration / startup error in the OSS deliverable (default_tool_registry() is full; an empty registry means a caller built one with ToolRegistry::new() and forgot to register anything). Suggested fix: change the remediation to a user-actionable line like: 'Build the registry via `plsql_mcp::default_tool_registry()` (the canonical full surface) — `ToolRegistry::new()` is a bare container and only used in tests.' No bead references.
close_reason: Fixed: rewrote crates/plsql-mcp/src/doctor.rs:228 empty-registry remediation. Removed 'Per-tool beads (PLSQL-MCP-002..PLSQL-MCP-LIVE-018) populate the registry; this is expected for the bead skeleton.' New text: 'Build the registry via plsql_mcp::default_tool_registry() — the canonical full surface. ToolRegistry::new() is a bare container intended only for tests; production code should never expose it directly.' No bead refs; frames the state as a config bug, not 'expected'.

title: PLSQL-OSS-DEAD — USR ledger append() has multi-process TOCTOU between iter() and append (Class 7)
description:
File: crates/plsql-accretion/src/ledger.rs:254-291 (Ledger::append).

Hazard: append() reads the tip via iter() (line 255), computes the new entry_id from parent=tip.entry_id (line 273), then opens the file with OpenOptions{create,append} and writes the new line (lines 281-289). There is no file lock and no atomic check-and-write. Two concurrent usr-loop invocations against the same cwd .usr/ledger/ledger.jsonl will:

  T1: iter() -> tip = E_N
  T2: iter() -> tip = E_N
  T1: write line (parent=E_N, entry_id=X1)  -> file line N+1
  T2: write line (parent=E_N, entry_id=X2)  -> file line N+2

Result: line N+2 stores parent_entry_id=E_N but the real previous tip is now E_(N+1)=X1. verify_chain() then fails with ChainBroken at line N+2 ('parent_entry_id mismatch'), permanently bricking the ledger for future appends.

Reachability: The ledger lives at <cwd>/.usr/ledger/ledger.jsonl. Nothing in the README, AGENTS.md, plan.md, the tool's --help/--robot-docs, or the ledger module asserts single-instance usage. CI uses concurrency: cancel-in-progress so GH Actions cannot race itself, but local dev and NTM-style agent swarms (which the project explicitly encourages — AGENTS.md describes parallel execution tracks) can invoke 'usr-loop scan' or 'usr-loop ledger append' twice concurrently from two panes against the same repo cwd.

Severity: detected corruption (verify_chain catches it), not silent — but the ledger then refuses every future append until the operator manually truncates and rebuilds. P3 robustness, not data-loss.

Suggested fix (one of):
  1. Wrap the entire iter()->compute->write critical section in a POSIX advisory lock on the ledger file (fs2 / fd-lock crate; #![forbid(unsafe_code)] is preserved by both). Drop the lock on Drop / on the function return.
  2. Use a sidecar .lock file next to ledger.jsonl with the same advisory-lock pattern (matches the install-precommit-guard pattern used elsewhere in the agent-mail skill).
  3. Document loudly in the ledger module docstring and the usr-loop --help that concurrent invocations against the same repo are unsupported; add a startup check that the .usr/ledger/ledger.jsonl mtime hasn't changed since iter() returned (a poor-man's optimistic-concurrency-control fallback).

Same hazard also applies to AccretionLedger::append (same file, ~lines 495-630) — fix once, apply to both.

Round-3 deadlock-finder-and-fixer audit (sibling agents are running other lenses concurrently).
close_reason: Added fs2 = 0.4 to workspace deps and plsql-accretion deps. Introduced LedgerLockGuard (RAII): acquires fs2::FileExt::lock_exclusive on sidecar <ledger>.lock file, releases on Drop. Held across the entire iter() → compute parent → write critical section in both Ledger::append and AccretionLedger::append. Cross-thread AND cross-process safe (flock(2) is per-OFD on Unix). TDD: tests/ledger_concurrent_append.rs runs 8 threads through a Barrier, each opening its own Ledger handle and racing append() on the same dir. Red phase confirmed: pre-fix produced 'Parse trailing characters' on interleaved writes (= ChainBroken in disguise). Green phase: all 8 writes land, all signatures distinct, verify_chain returns Ok. Also asserts the sidecar lock file lives at <ledger>.jsonl.lock. Same fix applied to AccretionLedger::append. forbid(unsafe_code) preserved (fs2 is safe Rust). Evidence: 3 new tests pass; full plsql-accretion suite green; cargo clippy -D warnings clean.

title: PLSQL-OSS-MOCK2 — plsql-catalog: classify_ddl_stub is a real stub (production fn with 'stub' in its name); load_from_dbms_metadata_dir public docstring self-declares 'This is a skeleton implementation'
description: crates/plsql-catalog/src/lib.rs:1268-1519. The public `load_from_dbms_metadata_dir` is documented as 'This is a skeleton implementation — full DDL parsing will be added when the parser (Layer 1) is complete'. It calls `classify_ddl_stub` (production code with 'stub' in the name). The classifier (1) collapses every object's schema to `SchemaName::new(SymbolId::new(0))` so multi-schema DBMS_METADATA dumps silently collide on one schema bucket, and (2) constructs every CatalogObject with `..ObjectCommon::default()` / `..TableMetadata::default()` / `..RoutineSignature::default()` (no columns, no constraints, no signatures, no grants). The docstring also lies about behaviour: 'Currently records file contents as raw DDL text in DbmsMetadataDdl fields' — but the function does no such thing, it discards the DDL text after a name extract. Suggested fix: either implement real DDL classification (parse the .sql files via plsql-parser + lower them to fully-populated CatalogObjects with the real schema), or mark the function `pub(crate)` and delete from the lib's pub use list until it's real. At minimum, rename `classify_ddl_stub` to something honest and rewrite the public docstring to describe actual behaviour, not aspirational behaviour.
close_reason: Option (a) implemented. crates/plsql-catalog/src/lib.rs: (1) renamed classify_ddl_stub → classify_dbms_metadata_ddl (honest scope); (2) replaced SymbolId::new(0) schema collapse with real OWNER.NAME parsing via new extract_owner_and_name + detect_object_kind + object_type_for_keyword helpers — owner schemas are interned through interner.intern_schema_name, unqualified DDL files under a stable named PUBLIC schema (UNQUALIFIED_DDL_SCHEMA const); (3) every CatalogObject now carries the raw DDL bytes on ObjectCommon.ddl as DbmsMetadataDdl{ddl_text, normalized_ddl=normalize_dbms_metadata_ddl(text), xml_text:None} — delivering the docstring promise the old code lied about; (4) rewrote public docstring on load_from_dbms_metadata_dir to describe actual behavior + honest limits (keyword-shaped, no column/signature parsing) with no 'skeleton implementation' / 'follow-up bead' language; (5) made directory traversal deterministic via sorted PathBuf collection. 3 new TDD tests: load_from_dbms_metadata_dir_records_real_schema_owner (HR + BILLING land in distinct buckets), load_from_dbms_metadata_dir_uses_named_default_schema_for_unqualified_ddl (PUBLIC bucket), load_from_dbms_metadata_dir_records_raw_ddl_text_on_object (round-trips raw DDL). All 6 dbms_metadata tests pass. cargo build/test/clippy all green.

title: Stop gating UDF detection on sqlparser keyword table — non-reserved Oracle names (purge/merge/refresh/...) fail-open SELECT to Safe
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/oraclemcp-guard/src/classifier.rs:386 — verdict=keep, severity=high
**Impact:** A custom tool defined as PackageCall("billing.purge()") (or any UDF whose name collides with a non-reserved sqlparser keyword) loads at the ReadOnly privilege floor / classifies Safe instead of Guarded, so an agent can invoke a side-effecting (row-deleting) routine inside a SELECT with no step-up gate — a fail-open in the guard's safety spine that defeats R15.
**Evidence:** classifier.rs:385-388 (verbatim, current committed code):
  if let Token::Word(name) = toks[i - 1] {
      if name.keyword != Keyword::NoKeyword {
          continue; // a keyword like VALUES( / IN( etc.
      }
SELECT path classifier.rs:553-585: calls.is_empty() → all_proven vacuously true; UnknownOracle.statement_purity returns Unknown (purity.rs:99-101) so stmt_side_effecting=false; stmt_pure=true → DangerLevel::Safe / OperatingLevel::ReadOnly.
purity.rs:68-69: permits_safe() == matches!(self, Purity::ProvenReadOnly); purity.rs:78-80 UnknownOracle.routine_purity → Purity::Unknown (so a DETECTED keyword-named UDF would correctly be Guarded — the keyword skip is the sole cause).
Production caller: custom_tools.rs:302  ToolBody::PackageCall(c) => format!("SELECT {c} FROM dual"); comment lines 300-301 assert the intended invariant "fail-closed to Guarded — never silently Safe".
Empirical 
**Repro:** Trigger X→Y: input X = `SELECT billing.purge() FROM dual` (a UDF named after the non-reserved Oracle keyword PURGE), classified by Classifier::default() (the live UnknownOracle, since the real engine oracle binding is deferred per classifier.rs:566-571). Output Y = DangerLevel::Safe / OperatingLevel::ReadOnly, udf_calls empty. Expected: Guarded/ReadWrite (the control `billing.purge_old_rows()` correctly yields Guarded). Mechanism: tokenizer tags the `purge` Word token with a non-NoKeyword value (PURGE is in sqlparser 0.62 ALL_KEYWORDS), so the `if name.keyword != Keyword::NoKeyword { continue; }` at line 386 drops it from the candidate set before is_builtin_function is ever consulted; calls 
**Suggested fix:** Replace the blanket `if name.keyword != Keyword::NoKeyword { continue; }` at classifier.rs:386 with an explicit small skip-set of the genuinely clause-introducing words that can legally precede `(` but are never UDF calls (e.g. VALUES, IN, CASE — match the lowercased name.value against a tiny literal set), and let the existing `if !is_builtin_function(&fname)` filter handle real built-ins (COUNT/MAX/etc.). Every other ident( / schema.ident( becomes a UDF candidate routed through the oracle consult (defaulting to Guarded under UnknownOracle). Add a regression test asserting `SELECT billing.purge() FROM dual` plus the other keyword-collision names classify as Guarded/ReadWrite, mirroring select_calling_udf_is_guarded_not_safe.

### crates/oraclemcp-guard/src/classifier.rs:386 — verdict=keep, severity=high
**Impact:** A side-effecting Oracle routine (e.g. PRAGMA AUTONOMOUS_TRANSACTION DML) whose name collides with any sqlparser keyword — billing.purge(), app.merge(), app.delete(), app.comment(), app.refresh(), etc. — is classified Safe/ReadOnly when called in a SELECT, so the guard never forces a step-up and the DML executes undetected; the agent/attacker controls the routine name, so this is a trivially-reachable bypass of the classifier's headline fail-closed contract.
**Evidence:** classifier.rs:386 (HEAD == disk, md5 da9448e971b074909c698d3f1ccb49d6 both): `if name.keyword != Keyword::NoKeyword { continue; }`. Then classify_statement (lines 553-584): `let calls = user_defined_calls(sql); ... let stmt_pure = (calls.is_empty() || all_proven) && !stmt_side_effecting; if stmt_pure { danger: Safe, ReadOnly }`. sqlparser-0.62.0/src/keywords.rs lines 117/238/323/407/504/591/635/822/844/1133/1134: ANALYZE, COMMENT, DELETE, EXPORT, IMPORT, LOAD, MERGE, PURGE, REFRESH, VALUE, VALUES are all Keyword variants. LIVE repro via real public API Classifier::default().classify on HEAD (temp integration test, since removed): `SELECT billing.purge() FROM dual` -> danger=Safe level=ReadOnly objects=[]; `app.comment()`/`app.merge(x)`/`app.refresh()`/`app.delete()` -> Safe; vs non-colliding `billing.purge_old_rows()` -> Guarded/ReadWrite objects=["purge_old_rows"] and `app.recalc(id)` -
**Repro:** Trigger: Classifier::default().classify("SELECT billing.purge() FROM dual") returns DangerLevel::Safe / OperatingLevel::ReadOnly with objects_affected=[], whereas the spec-required output is Guarded/ReadWrite. Mechanism: at line 386 the schema-qualified UDF `purge` is tokenized with name.keyword=PURGE != NoKeyword, so `continue` drops it before it is pushed to `calls`; calls.is_empty()==true makes all_proven vacuously true and routine_purity() is never invoked (a bound engine oracle cannot recover); query_base_objects yields only [dual], and statement_purity under the default UnknownOracle returns Unknown (not ProvenSideEffecting), so stmt_side_effecting==false; therefore stmt_pure==true => 
**Suggested fix:** In user_defined_calls() replace the blanket `if name.keyword != Keyword::NoKeyword { continue; }` skip with: (1) never skip a schema-qualified `schema.name(` form (SQL constructs like VALUES/IN/CAST/CASE are never schema-qualified, so a qualified keyword-named identifier is unambiguously a routine call); (2) for a bare `name(`, skip only the small allow-list of genuine SQL-construct keywords that can legally precede `(` and are never UDFs — VALUES, VALUE, IN, CAST, CASE, EXISTS, ALL, ANY, SOME, ARRAY — and treat every other keyword-colliding identifier (PURGE, MERGE, DELETE, COMMENT, ANALYZE, REFRESH, ...) as a candidate UDF that falls through to is_builtin_function(). This stays fail-closed (over-detection only adds Guarded). Add keyword-named variants (billing.purge(), app.comment(), app.merge()) to select_calling_udf_is_guarded_not_safe and adversarial_corpus.rs asserting Guarded. NOT

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit fcb67103bb), re-verified on main. Root cause: classifier.rs user_defined_calls() had a blanket `if name.keyword != Keyword::NoKeyword { continue; }` that fail-OPENed every keyword-colliding name (purge/merge/delete/comment/analyze/refresh/...) before the purity consult, so `SELECT billing.purge() FROM dual` -> Safe/ReadOnly. Fix: (1) a schema-qualified `schema.name(` is NEVER skipped (it is unambiguously a routine call — SQL constructs are never schema-qualified), closing the headline billing.purge() attack; (2) a bare `name(` is skipped only when its keyword is a structural/clause keyword (AS/IN/VALUES/OVER/OR/JOIN/...) that

title: Merge `=` into the relational precedence tier so `<=`/`>=`/`!=` aren't mis-split on the embedded `=`
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-ir/src/expr.rs:290 — verdict=keep, severity=medium
**Impact:** When a function call or tainted local sits on the LHS of a `<=`, `>=`, or `!=` comparison (e.g. `IF compute_total(x) <= limit`, `IF v_count >= p_limit`), the LHS is corrupted into an Expr::Raw node and silently dropped, producing Calls-edge false negatives in the dependency graph and intra-proc taint false negatives in the SAST engine. It under-reports (misses edges/flows); it does not fabricate a safe verdict on a dangerous sink.
**Evidence:** crates/plsql-ir/src/expr.rs:287-295 precedence table puts `=` in its own tier BEFORE the relational tier:
    let precedence: &[&[&str]] = &[
        &["OR"], &["AND"], &["="],
        &["<>", "!=", "<=", ">=", "<", ">"], &["||"], ...];
recognise_top_level_binary iterates tiers in order (expr.rs:297) and returns on first match; find_top_level_op scans left-to-right and matches the bare `=` byte (single-char op, alpha word-boundary guard at expr.rs:375-387 does not apply). Downstream blindness confirmed: collect_calls (calls.rs:207-225) only descends Call/Binary/Unary, Raw hits `_ => {}`; collect_expr_flow (flow_intra.rs:183-281) drops Raw at `_ => {}`.

Empirical probe (compiled against the crate, `cargo test -p plsql-ir`, output via --nocapture):
        a <= b  =>  Binary { op: "=", lhs: Raw { text: "a <", reason: UnrecognizedShape }, rhs: Name(b) }
   x >= p_user  =>  Binary { op: "="
**Repro:** Trigger X -> wrong output Y: lower_expression("a <= b") yields Binary{op:"=", lhs: Raw{text:"a <"}, rhs: Name(b)} instead of Binary{op:"<=", lhs: Name(a), rhs: Name(b)}. Concretely, extract_call_sites on body `BEGIN IF compute_total(x) <= 10 THEN log_it('y'); END IF; END;` returns ["log_it"] (compute_total dropped); the identical body with `<` returns ["compute_total","log_it"]. Affects exactly `<=`, `>=`, `!=`; `<>`, `<`, `>`, `=` are unaffected.
**Suggested fix:** Replace the two tiers `&["="]` and `&["<>", "!=", "<=", ">=", "<", ">"]` (expr.rs:290-291) with a single tier `&["<>", "!=", "<=", ">=", "=", "<", ">"]`. Multi-char ops stay ahead of single-char ones, so scanning `a <= b` reaches the `<` byte first and the tier tries `<>`, `!=`, then `<=` (match) — splitting correctly into lhs="a"/rhs="b". `=`, `<`, `>` continue to work. Add regression tests asserting lower_expression of "a <= b"/"a >= b"/"a != b" produce Binary{op:"<="/">="/"!="} with both operands lowered (not Raw), plus an extract_call_sites test that `IF f(x) <= 10 THEN` yields a call site for f.

### crates/plsql-parser-antlr/src/lower/mod.rs:803 — verdict=keep, severity=medium
**Impact:** lower_expression_text mislabels every top-level <=, >=, != comparison as op "=" with a corrupted LHS slice (e.g. "a <"); today the only callers are the fuzz target and the crate's own tests (the MCP production path uses lower_source, which never reaches this function), so no shipping analysis output is corrupted yet, but the pub API silently violates its documented shape-correct contract and is a latent trap for any future consumer that reads Binary.op/lhs_text.
**Evidence:** crates/plsql-parser-antlr/src/lower/mod.rs:800-808 tiers array has `&["="]` (line 803) BEFORE `&["<>", "!=", "<=", ">=", "<", ">"]` (line 804). split_top_level_bin (lines 866-922) is a position-driven left-to-right byte scan that tests each op of the current tier at every depth-0 byte. Empirical run of the REAL function via a throwaway integration test (since removed):
        a <= b => Binary { op: "=", lhs_text: "a <", rhs_text: "b", ... }
        a >= b => Binary { op: "=", lhs_text: "a >", rhs_text: "b", ... }
        a != b => Binary { op: "=", lhs_text: "a !", rhs_text: "b", ... }
        a <> b => Binary { op: "<>", lhs_text: "a", rhs_text: "b", ... }  (correct, no '=' byte)
         a < b => Binary { op: "<", lhs_text: "a", rhs_text: "b", ... }   (correct)
         a = b => Binary { op: "=", lhs_text: "a", rhs_text: "b", ... }   (correct)
  x <= y AND z => Binary { op: "AND", lhs
**Repro:** Trigger: lower_expression_text("a <= b", FileId::new(1), 0) returns AstExpr::Binary { op: "=", lhs_text: "a <", rhs_text: "b" } instead of op:"<=", lhs:"a". Mechanism: the " OR "/" AND " tiers return None on a bare comparison, then the &["="] tier (line 803) scans left-to-right and matches the '=' byte at index 3 of "a <= b" before the relational tier (line 804) is ever reached. Same bug for ">=" and "!=" (both contain '='); "<>", "<", ">", "=" are unaffected. Nesting under a lower-precedence operator (x <= y AND z) splits on AND at this level so the corruption only surfaces if "x <= y" is later re-lowered. NOT "every such expression in production": currently no production code path calls th
**Suggested fix:** Split the relational tier so the 2-char ops precede bare '='/'<'/'>': replace lines 803-804 `&["="], &["<>", "!=", "<=", ">=", "<", ">"],` with `&["<>", "!=", "<=", ">="], &["=", "<", ">"],`. This makes the 2-char forms match whole before the single-char tier can break them. Apply the identical reordering to the sibling at crates/plsql-ir/src/expr.rs:287-295 (where it is worse, since operands ARE recursively lowered there per expr.rs:300). Add regression tests asserting lower_expression_text("a <= b") yields Binary{op:"<=", lhs:"a", rhs:"b"} (and >=, !=) in both crates.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit a9ab5913d6), re-verified on main. Merged the `=` tier into the relational tier at BOTH sites. expr.rs:287-295 and lower/mod.rs:800-808 now use a single tier &["<>", "!=", "<=", ">=", "=", "<", ">"] (multi-char ops first). Root cause: a separate higher &["="] tier matched the embedded `=` byte in <=/>=/!= before the relational tier, corrupting the LHS into Raw/a-corrupted-slice and op into `=`, causing Calls-edge and intra-proc taint false negatives. Within-tier left-to-right matching reaches the `<`/`>`/`!` byte first and matches the 2-char op whole.

title: Make G2 roundtrip_check (and G8 residue_check) fail closed on unreadable collected files instead of vacuous-passing over 0 verified inputs
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-accretion/src/gate.rs:419 — verdict=defense-in-depth, severity=medium
**Impact:** If the G2 round-trip gate is pointed at a corpus where every collected source file is unreadable (non-UTF-8 legacy PL/SQL, or a file removed/chmod'd between listing and read), it reports "lossless round-trip 100%" and exits 0 — a vacuous PASS over zero verified files, directly violating the stage's own fail-closed invariant. Under the default repo-controlled ASCII corpus it never triggers, so impact is latent.
**Evidence:** crates/plsql-accretion/src/gate.rs:392-427 — the empty-files guard (line 392 `if files.is_empty()`) only fails-closed for files-NOT-FOUND; the loop body at line 404 `let Ok(src) = std::fs::read_to_string(f) else { continue; };` silently skips unreadable files, `checked` (line 417) counts only successful reads, and there is NO post-loop `if checked == 0` re-check before the unconditional `Ok(format!("lossless round-trip 100% over {checked} inputs ({} corpus + {} prior MinFixtures)", collect_sources(corpus_dir).len(), ...))` at line 419-427 (the parenthetical re-counts files FOUND, not files VERIFIED).
Docstring contract it violates, lines 393-394: "A round-trip stage with zero inputs cannot make a real claim — fail-closed (never a vacuous pass)."
Same continue-on-read-error pattern confirmed in residue_check at line 592-594.
Crate builds clean: `cargo build -p plsql-accretion --bin usr-ga
**Repro:** TRIGGER (confirmed live): mkdir a corpus dir, write one file with a gate ext and non-UTF-8 bytes: `printf '\xff\xfe\x00\x80 SELECT 1 FROM dual;' > corpus/legacy.sql`; run `usr-gate-rs roundtrip <corpus> <nonexistent-fixtures>`. Observed: stdout `lossless round-trip 100% over 0 inputs (1 corpus + 0 prior MinFixtures)`, exit 0 — vacuous PASS. TEMPERING (why defense-in-depth not keep): the default corpus `corpus/synthetic/l1` and the entire committed `corpus/` tree are ASCII/UTF-8 (verified with file(1)), so under normal operation collected files always read successfully and checked==files.len(); the bug only manifests under a non-default USR_GATE_CORPUS pointed at non-UTF-8 files or a TOCTOU r
**Suggested fix:** In roundtrip_check, treat an unreadable collected file as a hard gate failure (it is a found input the stage cannot verify): replace `let Ok(src) = std::fs::read_to_string(f) else { continue; };` with `let src = std::fs::read_to_string(f).map_err(|e| GateError::Io(format!("round-trip input {} could not be read ({e}) — cannot prove lossless over a file that exists; fail-closed", f.display())))?;`. As defense-in-depth also add a post-loop `if checked == 0 { return Err(GateError::Io("0 round-trip inputs verified — vacuous claim refused".into())); }` and make the evidence line report the verified `checked` count consistently (carry separate verified-corpus/verified-fixture counters rather than re-calling collect_sources(...).len()). Apply the identical read-failure-fails-closed treatment to residue_check at line 592 so an unreadable fixture fails the privacy stage rather than being silently 

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 7450e1f63a), re-verified on main. Root-cause fix in crates/plsql-accretion/src/gate.rs. roundtrip_check: replaced the silent `let Ok(src) = std::fs::read_to_string(f) else { continue; }` with a hard GateError::Io failure naming the unreadable file (a found input the stage cannot verify must not vacuous-pass); added separate verified corpus/fixture counters so the evidence line reports the VERIFIED split instead of re-calling collect_sources().len() (fixes the self-inconsistent 'over 0 inputs (1 corpus + ...)' line); added a post-loop `if checked == 0` vacuous-claim guard. residue_check: an unreadable fixture now fails closed a

title: Pass case-preserving connect string to wallet_param in ADB descriptor branch (oci.rs:168)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/oraclemcp-db/src/oci.rs:168 — verdict=defense-in-depth, severity=low
**Impact:** None today: the case-folding bug is in a public API (validate_adb_connect_string) that has no production caller, so no operator-visible failure occurs. If a future caller wired AdbConnectInfo.wallet_location into wallet discovery, a descriptor connect-string containing an uppercase path segment (e.g. wallet_location=/home/Oracle/wallet) would resolve to /home/oracle/wallet and mTLS auto-login would fail on case-sensitive Linux while the tcps:// form of the same wallet still worked.
**Evidence:** crates/oraclemcp-db/src/oci.rs:156 `let lower = t.to_ascii_lowercase();`
oci.rs:168 (descriptor branch) `wallet_location: wallet_param(&lower),`  <- case-folded input
oci.rs:178 (tcps:// branch)   `wallet_location: wallet_param(t),`        <- case-preserving input
oci.rs:208-214 wallet_param: `let idx = s.to_ascii_lowercase().find(needle)? + needle.len(); let rest = &s[idx..]; ...` -> lowercases ONLY for find(), but slices the VALUE out of the passed-in string, so &lower yields a folded path. The asymmetry + folding are exactly as claimed.
Workspace grep (rg 'validate_adb_connect_string|AdbConnectInfo|wallet_param' crates/): only hits are lib.rs:50-51 (re-export) and oci.rs:138/151/166/168/176/178/193/208 (definitions) plus oci.rs:321,328,334,341,345,349 — all six call sites sit inside `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` which begins at oci.rs:273.
Cross-crate grep (rg 'AdbConnectInfo|validate_adb'
**Repro:** Disproof of live impact: there is no production code path from AdbConnectInfo.wallet_location to wallet discovery. The defect would only manifest if validate_adb_connect_string were called and its wallet_location consumed; today the sole callers are unit tests (oci.rs lines 321-349, all under #[cfg(test)] mod tests at line 273), and connection.rs:81-99 sources the real wallet dir from opts.wallet_location instead. Latent trigger (would-be repro if wired up): validate_adb_connect_string("(description=(address=(protocol=tcps)(host=h)(port=1522))(connect_data=(service_name=svc))(wallet_location=/home/Oracle/wallet))").unwrap().wallet_location == Some("/home/oracle/wallet") (folded) instead of "
**Suggested fix:** Change oci.rs:168 from `wallet_location: wallet_param(&lower),` to `wallet_location: wallet_param(t),`, matching the sibling tcps:// branch at line 178. wallet_param already lowercases internally (line 210) only to locate the needle, so case-insensitive matching of `wallet_location=` is preserved while the extracted path value keeps its original case. Add a regression test (the existing descriptor test at oci.rs:328 omits wallet_location and asserts nothing on it): validate a descriptor containing wallet_location=/home/Oracle/wallet and assert i.wallet_location.as_deref() == Some("/home/Oracle/wallet"). Defense-in-depth: fix it before any caller wires AdbConnectInfo.wallet_location into wallet discovery.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 1c93a3299e), re-verified on main. Root-cause fix in crates/oraclemcp-db/src/oci.rs descriptor branch: changed wallet_location: wallet_param(&lower) to wallet_param(t) so the extracted path keeps its original case (case-sensitive Linux mTLS auto-login), matching the sibling tcps:// branch. wallet_param still lowercases internally only to locate the wallet_location= needle, so case-insensitive matching of the key is preserved. Regression test descriptor_wallet_location_preserves_case validates a descriptor with wallet_location=/home/Oracle/Wallet and asserts the value round-trips with mixed case; it FAILED before the fix (got fo

title: Make ADB descriptor TLS check and wallet_location parse whitespace/quote-tolerant in oraclemcp-db oci.rs
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/oraclemcp-db/src/oci.rs:160 — verdict=keep, severity=low
**Impact:** An operator who supplies a hand-written or tooling-generated full Oracle Net ADB descriptor with conventional spacing — (DESCRIPTION = (ADDRESS = (PROTOCOL = TCPS)...)) — has their legitimate mTLS connection profile rejected at config-load with "ADB descriptor must use PROTOCOL=TCPS (TLS)", and a quoted wallet_location path containing a space is silently truncated to the first space. Both are fail-closed availability/robustness defects (never a plaintext-accept security hole); currently latent because the validator has no live caller.
**Evidence:** oci.rs:158-164 (verbatim):
  if lower.starts_with("(description") || lower.contains("(address") {
      let uses_tls = lower.contains("protocol=tcps");
      if !uses_tls { return Err(OciError::InvalidAdbConnectString("ADB descriptor must use PROTOCOL=TCPS (TLS)".to_owned())); }
Only preprocessing: t = s.trim() (152), lower = t.to_ascii_lowercase() (156) — internal whitespace preserved.
oci.rs:212 (verbatim): let end = rest.find(['&', ')', '?', ' ']).unwrap_or(rest.len());  // space is a terminator

Standalone repro (rustc -O, exact logic copied from oci.rs):
  spaced descriptor: is_desc=true uses_tls=false -> REJECTED as non-TLS
  compact descriptor: is_desc=true uses_tls=true -> accepted
  wallet_param quoted-with-space -> "/opt/My"
Fail-closed proof:
  plaintext "(DESCRIPTION = (ADDRESS = (PROTOCOL = TCP)...))": is_desc=true uses_tls=false -> REJECTED (correct)
  plaintext "(descripti
**Repro:** Trigger 1 (TLS over-rejection): validate_adb_connect_string("(DESCRIPTION = (ADDRESS = (PROTOCOL = TCPS)(HOST=adb)(PORT=1522)))") returns Err(InvalidAdbConnectString("ADB descriptor must use PROTOCOL=TCPS (TLS)")) — proven above: is_desc=true, uses_tls=false. The same string with no spaces is accepted, proving spacing is the sole cause.
Trigger 2 (wallet truncation): wallet_param on "...?wallet_location=\"/opt/My Wallet/dir\"" returns "/opt/My" instead of "/opt/My Wallet/dir" — the ' ' terminator at line 212 fires before trim_matches strips the quotes.
Disproof of any security angle: a plaintext descriptor (spaced "PROTOCOL = TCP" or compact "protocol=tcp") still yields uses_tls=false and is
**Suggested fix:** Make the TLS check whitespace-insensitive: compute let compact: String = lower.chars().filter(|c| !c.is_whitespace()).collect(); then test compact.contains("protocol=tcps") (and use compact.contains("protocol=tcp") to emit the precise plaintext-rejection message). For wallet_param (line 212), make it quote-aware: if the char after wallet_location= is a quote, read to the matching closing quote (allowing interior spaces); otherwise terminate on ['&', ')', '?'] and whitespace. Add tests: spaced (PROTOCOL = TCPS) accepted as TLS, spaced (PROTOCOL = TCP) rejected as plaintext, and a quoted wallet_location path with a space round-tripping intact.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
