title: Cap expression-lowering recursion depth in lower_expression (flat binary chain stack-overflow DoS)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-ir/src/expr.rs:310 — verdict=keep, severity=high
**Impact:** A crafted ~40 KB PL/SQL file containing one assignment like `v := a OR a OR ... OR a;` (≈8000 operands on an 8 MiB main-thread stack, fewer on 2 MiB tokio worker stacks) drives lower_expression into linear-depth recursion that overflows the stack and aborts the whole analyzer process with SIGABRT — an unrecoverable DoS on this SAST product whose stated input is untrusted PL/SQL.
**Evidence:** expr.rs:101 `pub fn lower_expression(source: &str) -> Expr` — no depth param. expr.rs:288 recognise_top_level_binary; lines 310-311 `let lhs = lower_expression(lhs_text); let rhs = lower_expression(rhs_text);`. find_top_level_op (expr.rs:322) scans left-to-right, leftmost match wins (comment line 330 "Scan left-to-right; the leftmost match wins"), returns at line 409. recursion_guard.rs: MAX_RELOWER_DEPTH=128 doc states it bounds ONLY the re-lowering walks (extract_call_sites/extract_table_accesses) and explicitly notes "a real stack-overflow / SIGABRT seen on the bundled public fixture corpus/synthetic/l1". calls.rs:111 `walk_call_sites(&lowered, depth+1, ...)` is the guarded recursion; calls.rs:118 `collect_calls(&lower_expression(rhs_text), ...)` is UNGUARDED. plsql-engine/src/lib.rs:838 `std::fs::read_to_string(&abs)`; lib.rs:964 `extract_call_sites_bounded(&body_stmts)`. No source/s
**Repro:** Trigger: feed the engine (or call plsql_ir::lower_expression directly) a single statement `v := a OR a OR a OR ... OR a;` with ~8000+ operands (≈40 KB) on the default 8 MiB main-thread stack, or ~2000+ operands on a 2 MiB tokio worker stack. Empirically verified via a throwaway example binary linking the real lower_expression: recursion spine depth == operand count − 1; process aborts with SIGABRT (exit 134) at n=8000. Secondary tree-walk consumers re-walk the same Box<Expr> chain to identical depth: calls.rs:207 collect_calls (Expr::Binary line 221-222), flow_intra.rs:224 collect_expr_flow (Expr::Binary line 317), canonical.rs:65 canonicalize_expr (Expr::Binary line 148).
**Suggested fix:** Thread a depth budget through expression lowering, mirroring the existing MAX_RELOWER_DEPTH / honest-degradation pattern. Add `MAX_EXPR_DEPTH` (~256) and an internal `lower_expression_depth(source, depth)`; keep the public `lower_expression` signature stable. At depth >= cap return Expr::Raw with a typed reason (e.g. add UnknownExprReason::ExprDepthLimit) so the cap is honestly surfaced as a Diagnostic, not silently swallowed. Pass depth+1 at every internal recursion site: recognise_top_level_binary (expr.rs:310-311), recognise_call arg map (expr.rs:428), recognise_unary operands (expr.rs:473/480). Better still for legitimately-wide left-associative chains: build the Expr::Binary spine iteratively (left-fold) for the binary tier so operand count no longer drives recursion depth, with the depth cap backstopping nested parens. Add a regression test feeding a 1M-operand `a OR a OR ...` chai

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 8db70c5fde), re-verified on main. Root-cause fix for the flat-binary-chain stack-overflow DoS in lower_expression. Added pub const MAX_EXPR_DEPTH=256 and UnknownExprReason::ExprDepthLimit (new variant; only re-exported, no downstream exhaustive matches break). Public lower_expression signature kept stable; recursion budget threaded internally via new lower_expression_depth(source, depth). recognise_top_level_binary now builds the

title: Route `problem` through comment_safe in emit_routine invalid-identifier fail-closed comment (emit.rs:361)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-bindgen/src/emit.rs:356 — verdict=keep, severity=high
**Impact:** An operator who feeds the plsql-bindgen CLI an untrusted or upstream-buggy BindingPlan whose routine/parameter name contains a newline gets attacker-controlled `pub fn` items injected as live top-level Rust into the generated wrapper source, which then compiles into the consuming crate — defeating the project's documented fail-closed "always emit compilable, side-effect-free Rust" invariant.
**Evidence:** emit.rs:353-367 fail-closed comment site interpolates problem raw at line 361 (`problem = problem,`) with NO comment_safe, unlike package_name (emit.rs:58) and diagnostic fields (emit.rs:858-867). comment_safe (emit.rs:848-852) maps control chars (incl \n/\r) to spaces. invalid_identifier_reason (emit.rs:278-291) embeds raw r.name/p.name (lines 283/287); rust_ident_problem (lines 168-171) treats a newline as merely "a character outside [A-Za-z0-9_]". Reachability: main.rs:212 `serde_json::from_str` -> BindingPlan, emit_wrappers at main.rs:228; RoutineBinding.name lib.rs:63 and ParameterBinding.name lib.rs:86 are unvalidated String. EMPIRICAL: a throwaway test driving real emit_wrappers with name "ev\n    Ok(())\n}\npub fn injected_via_routine_name() -> i32 { 42 }\n//il" produced generated source containing, at a line-leading top-level position:\n    pub fn injected_via_routine_name() -> 
**Repro:** Construct a BindingPlan with one RoutineBinding whose name is "ev\n    Ok(())\n}\npub fn injected_via_routine_name() -> i32 { 42 }\n//il" and call emit_wrappers. Output contains `pub fn injected_via_routine_name() -> i32 { 42 }` at a top-level line-leading position (X=newline-bearing routine name -> Y=live injected top-level Rust). Disproof of the fix: wrapping `problem` in comment_safe at emit.rs:361 collapses the newline to a space, keeping the whole `// bindgen:` diagnostic on one line, and the probe passes.
**Suggested fix:** At emit.rs:361 change `problem = problem,` to `problem = comment_safe(&problem),`. The `message: {msg:?}` literal (line 360) is already safe via Debug-escaping. For defense-in-depth add a regression test mirroring diagnostic_fields_with_newlines_cannot_escape_comment (emit.rs:1103) that drives a single invalid routine with a newline-bearing name (and one with a newline-bearing parameter name) and asserts no injected `pub fn` reaches a line-leading top-level position.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 7bd652b199), re-verified on main. Root cause: emit_routine's BINDING_INVALID_IDENTIFIER fail-closed body interpolated `problem` RAW into a single-line `//` comment (emit.rs ~line 361). `problem` embeds the untrusted routine/parameter name, so a `\n` terminated the comment and emitted the tail as live top-level Rust. Fix: route it through comment_safe (`comment_problem = comment_safe(&problem)`), which folds control chars to spaces

title: Gate and escape untrusted rust_type.path in bindgen emitter (signature + UNSUPPORTED_MARSHALLING body)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-bindgen/src/emit.rs:416 — verdict=keep, severity=medium
**Impact:** An operator (or upstream tool) feeding the plsql-bindgen CLI a BindingPlan whose rust_type.path contains a double-quote or newline (any non-allowlisted type triggers the path) gets exit 0 plus a generated Rust module that does not compile (denial-of-build) and can have attacker-controlled tokens placed in live Rust positions — defeating the emitter's documented fail-closed "always emit compilable/safe Rust on hostile BindingPlans" invariant.
**Evidence:** RustTypeRef is unconstrained: lib.rs:109-114 `pub struct RustTypeRef { pub path: String, pub nullable: bool }` with derived Deserialize. CLI deserializes untrusted plan: main.rs:212 `let mut plan: BindingPlan = serde_json::from_str(&json)`, then main.rs:228 `let generated = emit_wrappers(&plan)`. No type-path gate exists: `invalid_identifier_reason` (emit.rs:278-291) only checks package_id, routine name, parameter NAMES via `rust_ident_problem`; `rg` across crates/plsql-bindgen/src found no `type_path_problem`/path validator. Raw interpolation sites: emit.rs:378 `write!(out, ", {}: {}", p.name, rust_type_sig(p))` → rust_type_in (emit.rs:563-568) interpolates `p.rust_type.path` raw; emit.rs:382 return type via compute_return_type→rust_type_str (emit.rs:826-832) raw; emit.rs:410-419 BINDING_UNSUPPORTED_MARSHALLING body interpolates `why` (built from raw path at emit.rs:135-138/144) into a 
**Repro:** Build: `CARGO_TARGET_DIR=$PWD/target cargo build -p plsql-bindgen --bin plsql-bindgen`. Input /tmp/evil_plan.json = BindingPlan with one procedure `do_thing`, one IN param `p_x` whose rust_type.path = `String" ; pub fn pwned(){}`. Run `target/debug/plsql-bindgen --input /tmp/evil_plan.json` → EXIT=0, emits uncompilable Rust (signature `p_x: String" ; pub fn pwned(){}` and a message literal closed early by the embedded `"`). rustc on the emitted module errors out (`prefix String is unknown`, `unknown start of token`). A function variant with return_type.path = `Bogus".to_string() }); } pub fn pwned() -> i32 { 42 } fn _x() { let _ = ("` reproduces the same raw interpolation in the `Result<...>
**Suggested fix:** Two independent fixes, both needed: (1) Signature-position leak — add a type-path validator (allowlist of marshallable paths plus Option<...> forms, or restrict to [A-Za-z0-9_:<>, ] with balanced brackets and no quotes/newlines) and call it for every parameter rust_type.path and the return type inside invalid_identifier_reason so an unsafe path diverts to a fail-closed BINDING_INVALID_IDENTIFIER/BINDING_INVALID_TYPE_PATH stub BEFORE the `pub fn` signature is written (closes emit.rs:378/382). (2) UNSUPPORTED_MARSHALLING body — stop raw-interpolating `why`: route the path through comment_safe for the `//` comment at emit.rs:414, and emit the message via Debug formatting like the BINDING_INVALID_IDENTIFIER body (build the full string and write `message: {msg:?}.to_string()` instead of `message: "...{why}...".to_string()`) so any `"`/`\n` is escaped. Add a regression test mirroring package_n

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 7bd652b199), re-verified on main. Root cause: RustTypeRef.path is unconstrained `String` on the wire; no type-path gate existed. A hostile path (e.g. `String" ; pub fn pwned(){}`) was interpolated raw into the `pub fn` signature (rust_type_sig->rust_type_in, emit.rs:378) and the `Result<...>` return position (compute_return_type->rust_type_str, line 382), plus the UNSUPPORTED_MARSHALLING body raw-interpolated `why` (built from the

title: Fix inverted PLW-06xxx/07xxx warning categories in categorize_error (compile.rs)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-mcp/src/compile.rs:46 — verdict=keep, severity=low
**Impact:** Any consumer of compile_with_warnings receives every PLW-06xxx informational warning (e.g. PLW-06005 implicit truncation) labeled "performance" and every PLW-07xxx performance hint (e.g. PLW-07203 NOCOPY) labeled "informational", which can misdirect an agent's remediation priority — but the tool is not currently wired into the live MCP dispatch table, so no agent is misled through the server today; only the public library re-export and tests reach the code.
**Evidence:** crates/plsql-mcp/src/compile.rs:44-52 (actual code):
  let code = error.message_number;
  if (5000..6000).contains(&code) { WarningCategory::Severe }
  else if (6000..7000).contains(&code) { WarningCategory::Performance }   // WRONG: 6xxx is Oracle INFORMATIONAL
  else if (7000..8000).contains(&code) { WarningCategory::Informational }  // WRONG: 7xxx is Oracle PERFORMANCE
  else { WarningCategory::Other }

message_number is the bare numeric column (source.rs:210-213): row.text("MESSAGE_NUMBER").and_then(|t| t.parse().ok()).unwrap_or(0) selected from USER_ERRORS/ALL_ERRORS — so 7203 maps to PLW-07203, 6005 to PLW-06005.

Routing (compile.rs:151-155): WarningCategory::Performance => response.performance.push(error); WarningCategory::Informational => response.informational.push(error); — returned verbatim.

Inversion baked into doc (line 12: "PLW-06xxx = performance, PLW-07xxx = information
**Repro:** Trigger (real input -> wrong output): an ALTER...COMPILE under PLSQL_WARNINGS=ENABLE:ALL that emits PLW-07203 ("parameter may benefit from NOCOPY"). Oracle writes MESSAGE_NUMBER=7203, ATTRIBUTE=WARNING into USER_ERRORS. categorize_error: 7203 ∈ 7000..8000 -> WarningCategory::Informational -> pushed to response.informational. But PLW-07203 is documented PERFORMANCE, so the agent sees a performance hint mislabeled "informational". Symmetrically PLW-06005 (MESSAGE_NUMBER=6005, documented INFORMATIONAL) -> 6000..7000 -> Performance -> response.performance. Oracle doc contract: SEVERE=PLW-05xxx(5000-5999), INFORMATIONAL=PLW-06xxx(6000-6249), PERFORMANCE=PLW-07xxx(7000-7249); the 6xxx/7xxx buckets
**Suggested fix:** Swap the two arms in categorize_error so (6000..7000)->Informational and (7000..8000)->Performance, matching Oracle's documented ranges (SEVERE 5000-5999, INFORMATIONAL 6000-6249, PERFORMANCE 7000-7249). Then correct the now-inverted module doc (line 12), the enum variant doc comments (lines 27-30, swap Performance<->Informational descriptions), and update the two tests: categorize_error_buckets_by_plw_range (assert 6010->Informational, 7203->Performance) and compile_returns_categorized_errors_when_present (the 6005 row now lands in informational, the 7203 row in performance — keep the len()==1 asserts but pin each specific row to its correct bucket to lock the mapping). The 5000..6000->Severe branch and the ERROR-attribute short-circuit are correct and unchanged. Consider also wiring compile_with_warnings into dispatch_table()/dispatch_tool if it is intended to be a live tool — currentl

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.

Root-cause fix for inverted PLW warning categories in categorize_error (crates/plsql-mcp/src/compile.rs). Oracle's documented PLW ranges are SEVERE=PLW-05xxx (5000-5999), INFORMATIONAL=PLW-06xxx (6000-6249), PERFORMANCE=PLW-07xxx (7000-7249). The code had 6xxx->Performance and 7xxx->Informational (swapped). Swapped the two arms so (6000..7000)->Informational and (7000..8000)->Performance. Also cor

title: Seed decl-section depth in standalone PROCEDURE/FUNCTION/TRIGGER text-scanner spans (recovery-path span truncation)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-parser-antlr/src/lower/mod.rs:216 — verdict=defense-in-depth, severity=low
**Impact:** In the shipped engine build (which pins the antlr-codegen feature), the truncation never reaches dependency-graph/taint extraction: the codegen path derives full parse-tree spans and populates body_statements, so the engine's span-slicing fallback is bypassed and the INSERT/Calls edges are captured. The truncated lower_source span only bites on a degraded ANTLR-recovery path (whole-file zero-decl text-scan fallback, or the lower_create_object_fallback for a routine the typed handler missed), so the claimed broad SAST fail-open does not occur in normal operation; the MCP parse_tools that call lower_source never slice the span at all.
**Evidence:** mod.rs:216/234/252 confirmed calling advance_to_decl_end (=advance_to_decl_end_with_depth(..,0), mod.rs:1222-1224); recover.rs:149-172 block_structure_step opens depth ONLY on BEGIN/IF/LOOP/CASE (never IS/AS/DECLARE); the depth-0 ';' early-return is mod.rs:1285-1288.
Throwaway test via lower_source (text scanner): PROC src.len()=89 span=0..35 text="CREATE PROCEDURE p IS\n  v_x NUMBER;"; FUNC 0..48 of 116; TRIG 0..72 of 126; control (no decl section) 0..63 of 63 correct — truncation reproduced exactly.
Throwaway test via the actual engine backend (parse_with_backend + Antlr4RustBackend, --features antlr-codegen): "PROC[0] src.len()=89 span=0..88 ... text=...INSERT INTO audit VALUES(v_x)\nEND"; "body_statements[0] has 2 stmts" — codegen path yields a FULL span and a populated body, NOT the truncated 0..35.
Cargo wiring: crates/plsql-engine/Cargo.toml:16 `plsql-parser-antlr = { ..., feature
**Repro:** Mechanism trigger (real): lower_source("CREATE PROCEDURE p IS\n  v_x NUMBER;\nBEGIN\n  v_x:=1;\n  INSERT INTO audit VALUES(v_x);\nEND;") -> AstDecl::Procedure span end.offset=35 (of 89), body excised. Same for FUNCTION (decl-section var) and TRIGGER (DECLARE section).
Impact disproof (normal build): parse_with_backend(src, fid, &Antlr4RustBackend, default) with --features antlr-codegen on the SAME input yields Procedure span 0..88 and body_statements[0] = 2 statements (the assignment + the INSERT) — so the engine keys body_by_span successfully, never slices the truncated span, and the Write edge to `audit` is emitted. The high-severity fail-open is only reachable when ANTLR fails to lower th
**Suggested fix:** Keep the finding's BEGIN-anchored fix (NOT naive depth-1, which over-runs into the next declaration as the verifier rationale documents). Before calling advance_to_decl_end in lower_procedure/lower_function/lower_trigger, scan forward (string/comment-safe) from `pos` to the first top-level BEGIN and pass that offset as the scan start with depth 0; the matching END returns the depth to 0 so the routine's own trailing ';' terminates correctly without swallowing a following CREATE. Add span-asserting regression tests: (1) each of PROCEDURE/FUNCTION/TRIGGER with a decl/DECLARE section asserting end.offset == src.len(); (2) each followed by a second CREATE, asserting the first span ends at its own END; and the second survives as a distinct decl (guards the over-run). Severity is low because the only production span consumer (engine lib.rs:960) uses the codegen full span in the shipped build; 

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 19ec513ec3), re-verified on main. Root cause: lower_procedure/lower_function/lower_trigger in crates/plsql-parser-antlr/src/lower/mod.rs called advance_to_decl_end (=depth 0) from create_start. A standalone routine has shape `… IS|AS <decls> BEGIN <body> END[ name];`; the IS|AS envelope is NOT counted by recover::block_structure_step (opens only on BEGIN/IF/LOOP/CASE), so a declaration-section `;` (e.g. `v_x NUMBER;`) was the firs

title: PLSQL-OSS-AUDIT — plsql-doc serve_preview_blocking has no public-bind guard, unlike plsql-mcp
description:
crates/plsql-doc/src/serve.rs:38-43 -- the public, re-exported (lib.rs:20 'pub use serve::serve_preview_blocking') function serve_preview_blocking<A: ToSocketAddrs>(addr, set, project_label) binds whatever address the caller passes directly via TcpListener::bind(addr), with no validation.

The module doc (serve.rs:6) states the server 'binds 127.0.0.1 by default ... SHOULD NOT be' exposed -- but that is only a convention; the function imposes no guard. If a caller passes 0.0.0.0 or a routable address, the preview server -- which serves rendered PL/SQL source, schema docs, and dependency graphs over plain HTTP with NO authentication -- is exposed on all interfaces.

Contrast with crates/plsql-mcp/src/tcp.rs:parse_listen_target, which deliberately REFUSES public-internet binds (0.0.0.0 / ::) unless explicitly overridden. The two TCP-serving crates in the same workspace have inconsistent bind-safety postures.

Why it matters: defense-in-depth. For an OSS release, a public API that can trivially expose unauthenticated source/schema content on a public interface, while a sibling crate guards against exactly that, is an inconsistent and leaky abstraction a security-minded reviewer will flag. The 'SHOULD NOT' doc is not a control.

Suggested fix: mirror the plsql-mcp pattern -- have serve_preview_blocking (or a wrapper) resolve the addr and refuse non-loopback/non-private binds by default, with an explicit opt-in parameter to override. At minimum, resolve the addr and emit a loud warning when binding a non-loopback address.
close_reason: TDD-implemented public-bind guard in crates/plsql-doc/src/serve.rs mirroring plsql-mcp/src/tcp.rs (is_safe_bind + guard_bind). serve_preview_blocking now takes allow_public_bind: bool; refuses 0.0.0.0/:: and routable public addrs by default with BindGuardError -> PermissionDenied io::Error. 13 new bind-guard tests (loopback v4/v6, RFC1918, link-local, ULA v6, unspecified v4/v6 refusal, routable v4 refusal, override unlocks, IO error conversion, end-to-end refusal). cargo test -p plsql-doc: 90+4 pass; cargo clippy -D warnings: clean.

title: Fail closed on no-semicolon pure-SQL batch with a dangerous verb buried after a benign prefix (parse-failure branch interior scan)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 11 — convergence).

### crates/oraclemcp-guard/src/classifier.rs:875 — verdict=defense-in-depth, severity=medium
**Impact:** A no-semicolon pure-SQL batch that buries a dangerous verb after a benign SELECT prefix (e.g. "SELECT 1 FROM dual\nDROP TABLE orders") is mis-classified Guarded/ReadWrite and gates to Allow on a ReadWrite-stepped-up session with no Admin/Ddl step-up, while the semicolon-delimited equivalent correctly fails closed to Forbidden. Today this changes no real outcome (no executor runs the buried verb), but it is a fail-closed-invariant regression that becomes a live under-gated execution the moment a per-statement/line-splitting transport or the deferred savepoint-preview executor lands.
**Evidence:** classifier.rs:505-519 analyze_batch counts statements only at top-level `;` (Token::SemiColon at depth==0). classifier.rs:1129-1130: `if shape.statement_count <= 1 { vec![classify_statement(sql, ...)] }` — whole string classified as one. classifier.rs:851-880 parse-failure branch: only `starts_with_admin_verb(&upper)` / `starts_with_ddl_verb(&upper)` then falls through to Guarded/ReadWrite default at 875-879. classifier.rs:311-316: `starts_with_admin_verb` does `leading.starts_with(v)` — LEADING token only. levels.rs:278-281: `if required <= self.effective_level() { Allow }`.

Probe test driven through the real crate (CARGO_TARGET_DIR=$PWD/target cargo test -p oraclemcp-guard), session stepped up to ReadWrite via set_current_level(ReadWrite):
  "SELECT 1 FROM dual GRANT DBA TO scott": stmt_count=1 parse=Err("Expected: end of statement, found: DBA") danger=Guarded req=Some(ReadWrite) gate
**Repro:** Trigger (classifier level): Classifier::default().classify("SELECT 1 FROM dual\nDROP TABLE orders") returns danger=Guarded, required_level=Some(ReadWrite); on a SessionLevelState at effective ReadWrite this gate()s to LevelDecision::Allow — whereas "SELECT 1 FROM dual; DROP TABLE orders" returns Forbidden/Blocked. Asymmetric `;`-vs-no-`;` fail-open reproduced for all 5 cited inputs.

Why it cannot escalate privileges TODAY (disproof of high/critical): the only StatementExecutor in the workspace is the test-only MockExecutor; the production oracle_query_execute handler submits the entire combined string as a single OCI statement (query_execute.rs:132), which Oracle rejects with a syntax error
**Suggested fix:** In the parse-failure `_` arm of classify_statement (classifier.rs:851-880), after the leading admin/DDL scans and before the Guarded/ReadWrite default at 875-879, run an INTERIOR scan over canonical_marker_scan(&upper): if the token stream contains any LEADING_ADMIN_VERBS / LEADING_DDL_VERBS sequence OR a top-level DML/DCL keyword (GRANT/REVOKE/DROP/TRUNCATE/ALTER/UPDATE/DELETE/INSERT/MERGE/CREATE) at a position other than the leading verb, return StatementClass::forbidden() — a buried dangerous verb in an unparseable single SQL segment is a desync, symmetric with the buried-`;` (saw_buried_semicolon, 1120) and trailing-after-END (saw_top_level_after_block_close, 1081) arms. Genuinely benign unparseable single SQL keeps the ReadWrite fail-closed default. Add regression tests asserting Forbidden for the 5 cited inputs plus a benign-unparseable control that stays Guarded/ReadWrite. (Defens

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: Parse-failure branch now fails closed (Forbidden) on a no-semicolon pure-SQL segment that buries a dangerous DCL/DDL/DML verb at a NON-leading position (has_buried_dangerous_verb over canonical_marker_scan, interior-only so a legitimate single unparseable UPDATE/MERGE is not over-restricted). Symmetric with saw_buried_semicolon / saw_top_level_after_block_close. +regression test (5 buried-verb inputs Forbidden, ;-form control, update_ts-identifier control stays Safe).

title: Skip builtin filter for schema-qualified calls in user_defined_calls (qualified-builtin-name fail-open)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 11 — convergence).

### crates/oraclemcp-guard/src/classifier.rs:711 — verdict=keep, severity=high
**Impact:** A side-effecting user-defined routine whose bare name collides with an Oracle SQL builtin (e.g. a package member named replace/round/trunc/mod/user/length/extract) called schema-qualified inside a SELECT — `SELECT billing.replace(x) FROM dual` — is classified Safe/ReadOnly and Allowed at the lowest READ_ONLY operating level with no step-up and no purity consult, so its DML/autonomous-transaction side effects execute unguarded.
**Evidence:** classifier.rs:711 (inside user_defined_calls): `if !is_builtin_function(&fname) { calls.push(ObjectRef::new(schema, fname)); }` — the builtin filter is applied to the bare `fname` even when `is_qualified==true` (schema is Some). BUILTINS (classifier.rs:595-637) contains replace(605), round(606), trunc(607), mod(610), user(623), length(597), extract(617), cardinality(636). This contradicts the module's own comment at lines 689-697: "A schema-qualified `schema.name(` is unambiguously a routine call ... so it is NEVER skipped". classify_statement Query arm (classifier.rs:896-934): `let calls = user_defined_calls(sql);` then `stmt_pure = (calls.is_empty() || all_proven) && !stmt_side_effecting && !has_row_lock;` → empty calls + UnknownOracle (returns Unknown, so stmt_side_effecting=false) + no FOR UPDATE → Safe/ReadOnly. routine_purity (line 899) only iterates `calls`, so the dropped routine
**Repro:** Trigger: `Classifier::default().classify("SELECT billing.replace(x) FROM dual")` returns danger=Safe, required_level=Some(ReadOnly), objects=[] (verified by running it). Expected (per the module's own invariant and the non-colliding control billing.purge_old): Guarded/ReadWrite. The bare-name control path is unaffected: COUNT(*)/CAST(...) are bare (is_qualified==false) so the proposed `is_qualified ||` guard short-circuits to the existing behavior and they stay Safe; genuine SQL builtins (REPLACE/ROUND/USER) are syntactically never schema-qualified in Oracle, so the fix cannot mis-flag a real read.
**Suggested fix:** At classifier.rs:711 change `if !is_builtin_function(&fname) {` to `if is_qualified || !is_builtin_function(&fname) {` so a schema-qualified `schema.name(` is always collected as a routine call (a genuine SQL builtin REPLACE/ROUND/TRUNC/MOD/USER/LENGTH/EXTRACT is never schema-qualified). Add a regression test mirroring select_calling_keyword_named_udf_is_guarded_not_safe but with builtin-named qualified members (billing.replace / app.trunc / app.round / app.user / app.mod / billing.length / app.extract) asserting Guarded + ReadWrite under the default UnknownOracle, while keeping select_with_builtin_only_is_safe (bare COUNT(*)/MAX) and genuine_sql_constructs_are_not_treated_as_udf_calls green.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: user_defined_calls now keeps a schema-qualified call even when its bare name collides with a SQL builtin (is_qualified || !is_builtin_function): billing.replace(x) is a package member, not REPLACE. Closes the Safe/ReadOnly fail-open. +regression test (7 builtin-named qualified UDFs Guarded; bare builtin control stays Safe).

title: Fix list_objects no-schema cursor seek to bind owner_index (placeholder collides with fetch bind on page 2+)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 11 — convergence).

### crates/plsql-mcp/src/list_objects.rs:188 — verdict=keep, severity=high
**Impact:** An agent that lists objects without a schema filter (the documented USER_OBJECTS / current-schema default) and pages past the first page sends a query whose seek placeholder collides with the fetch placeholder, so the second-and-later pages either panic in rust-oracle's positional bind path or run a nonsensical predicate (object_name > the numeric page-size), breaking pagination of the connected schema.
**Evidence:** crates/plsql-mcp/src/list_objects.rs:177-190 — cursor block computes owner_index=params.len()+1, name_index=params.len()+2; DBA branch pushes 2 binds and uses both :owner_index/:name_index, but the else branch emits `object_name > :{name_index}` while pushing only state.name (which lands at owner_index). Final clause (line 213-218) uses fetch_index=params.len()+1.

Deterministic repro via throwaway test driving run_list_objects with schema:None over an overflowing page, then feeding next_cursor back (DB-free StubConn). Captured SECOND-PAGE SQL:
  "... from user_objects where object_name > :2 order by object_name fetch first :2 rows only"
Assertions: has_seek_:2=true has_fetch_:2=true has_seek_:1=false  => the seek placeholder :2 collides with the fetch placeholder :2 and :1 (where state.name actually binds) is never referenced. (Test removed after running; tree clean.)

rust-oracle 0.6.3
**Repro:** Input X: run_list_objects(conn, ListObjectsRequest{ schema:None, page_size:Some(2), cursor:None }) over >2 rows returns next_cursor "BILLING\u{1}B"; then run_list_objects(conn, ListObjectsRequest{ schema:None, page_size:Some(2), cursor:Some("BILLING\u{1}B") }) -> issued_sql contains BOTH `object_name > :2` and `fetch first :2 rows only` (duplicate placeholder, :1 orphaned). Wrong output Y: against rust-oracle this binds 2 positional params into a SQL with a single distinct :2 placeholder -> panic (bind_values[1] OOB) or predicate object_name > 3. Reachability confirmed: schema:None selects `user as owner` (line 202) so the cursor's owner is non-empty and decode_cursor (133-149) accepts it; u
**Suggested fix:** In the else branch at line 188 reference owner_index (params.len()+1, where state.name actually lands) instead of name_index: `where_clauses.push(format!("object_name > :{owner_index}"));`. After the single push, fetch_index becomes params.len()+1 = owner_index+1 = the next distinct slot, so no collision. Add a regression test on the USER_OBJECTS+cursor path (schema:None, small page_size, overflowing rows, feed next_cursor back) asserting issued_sql contains `object_name > :1` and `fetch first :2 rows only` — every existing cursor test sets schema:Some(...), leaving this branch uncovered.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: list_objects user_objects cursor seek now binds owner_index (the slot its single pushed name lands at) instead of name_index, which had aliased the fetch-count bind and broke pagination. +regression test on build_query asserting seek :1 / fetch :2 distinct.

title: Fix inverted edge-direction in plsql_lineage::dependencies()/impact() and the self-masking test fixtures
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 11 — convergence).

### crates/plsql-lineage/src/lib.rs:885 — verdict=keep, severity=medium
**Impact:** Once dependencies()/impact() are wired into any consumer (e.g. the planned plsql-cicd transitive blast-radius walker in predict.rs, or a lineage CLI/MCP tool), "what does X depend on?" will return X's callers and "what breaks if I change X?" will return what X itself depends on — both flagship answers reversed on real engine-produced graphs. Today no production path calls these two functions, so no shipping user is affected yet; the danger is a latent, test-green correctness inversion that will silently mislead the moment it is consumed.
**Evidence:** ENGINE (producer, authoritative) crates/plsql-engine/src/lib.rs:1081 `Edge::new(EdgeId::new(next_edge_id), caller_node_id, callee_node_id, EdgeKind::Calls, ...)` and :1166 `Edge::new(..., caller_node_id, table_node_id, edge_kind, ...)` => every edge is from=dependent(caller/accessor) -> to=dependency(callee/table). Edge::new signature confirmed crates/plsql-depgraph/src/lib.rs:598 `pub fn new(id, from, to, kind, confidence)`.
DEPGRAPH semantics crates/plsql-depgraph/src/lib.rs:804 query_neighbors filters `edge.from == node.id` (FORWARD); :822 query_reverse_neighbors filters `edge.to == node.id` (REVERSE).
MCP contract (production consumer, passing tests) crates/plsql-mcp/src/graph_tools.rs:82 run_get_dependencies uses query_neighbors + collects `e.to.logical_id` (line 92) = forward=dependencies; :59 run_find_callers uses query_reverse_neighbors = reverse=callers. Fixture :182 builds Edge
**Repro:** Build a DepGraph with the engine's actual edge direction (from=accessor -> to=table): node1=APP.PROC (PackageProcedure), node2=APP.T (Table), edge Edge::new(EdgeId(1), NodeId(1)=PROC, NodeId(2)=T, EdgeKind::Reads, High). Then call plsql_lineage::dependencies(&g, &NodeId(1), None) and plsql_lineage::impact(&g, &NodeId(2), None). Observed (run as a cargo example): dependencies(PROC)=[] though docstring promises [APP.T]; impact(T)=[] though docstring promises [APP.PROC]; and the transposed pair dependencies(T) returns 1 edge (PROC) while impact(PROC) returns [APP.T] — i.e. each returns the other's answer. This is the X->wrong-Y proof. Reuse caveat (limits severity to medium): rg across crates s
**Suggested fix:** Swap the traversal directions to match the engine/graph_tools convention (from=dependent -> to=dependency). (1) dependencies(X) "what does X depend on?" must walk FORWARD: lib.rs:909 use graph.query_neighbors(...) and advance/collect the dependency side (recurse into edge.to, enqueue target_id), keeping LineageEdge source=edge.from/target=edge.to and LineageDirection::Upstream. (2) impact(X) "what breaks if I change X?" must walk REVERSE: lib.rs:1458 use graph.query_reverse_neighbors(...) and advance the BFS to edge.from (use edge.from.id at :1466 for best_confidence + queue), keeping LineageDirection::Downstream. (3) Correct impact()'s self-contradictory docstring at lib.rs:1422-1424 (downstream/blast-radius = incoming edges where edge.to==node). (4) Rebuild the test fixtures to the engine convention so they actually guard direction: in dependency_fixture (lib.rs:3273-3308) reverse all 

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: Fixed the inverted edge-direction convention in plsql-lineage. ENGINE builds from=dependent->to=dependency (caller->callee; Oracle ALL_DEPENDENCIES NAME->REFERENCED_NAME). dependencies() now walks FORWARD (collect edge.to), impact() walks REVERSE (collect edge.from) — matching graph_tools::run_get_dependencies/find_callers and the same crate's already-correct callers()/column_access(). SCOPE EXPANSION beyond the finding: recompile_order() used the same backwards convention (dependents before dependencies) and is also fixed (dependency/callee compiled first). Reversed the 4 unit fixtures (dependency_fixture/linear_chain_graph/branching_graph) + the e2e_50_packages fixture (Reads/Calls/view/trigger edges) to engine convention; explain_path test args swapped. Full lineage suite GREEN (98 lib + 6 e2e). /oracle audit: ALL_DEPENDENCIES (NAME depends on REFERENCED_NAME) per LOW-LEVEL-CATALOGS.md confirms dependencies=forward.

title: PLSQL-OSS-UX — plsql-bindgen reports 'unknown flag --version' from --robot-docs handler instead of clean --version
description:
CLI: plsql-bindgen (crates/plsql-bindgen/src/main.rs).

What's missing/awkward: 'plsql-bindgen --version' returns 'error: unknown flag "--version"; valid flags: -h, --capabilities, --help, --input, --output, --package, --robot-docs, --robot-json, --target' on stderr exit 1. The error message is structurally good (it lists valid flags — nice) but '--version' is so universally expected that returning an error is a friction point. The capabilities document includes a 'version: "0.1.0"' field, so the data is available.

Why an agent or human would trip on it: Same class as the plsql-mcp/plsql-engine --version finding (oracle-2rf6), but for the hand-rolled parser path. An agent doing 'plsql-bindgen --version' to feature-detect must instead call 'plsql-bindgen --capabilities | jq -r .version'. A trivial '/bin/zsh --version' shell idiom doesn't work here.

Suggested fix: Add a '--version' flag to the hand-rolled parser that prints 'plsql-bindgen <version>' to stdout and exits 0. Add it to the 'valid flags' list. Also: the error message's 'valid flags' list does not include '--version' (correctly), so the listing infrastructure is in place — just add the handler.
close_reason: Added --version/-V handler to hand-rolled parser in crates/plsql-bindgen/src/main.rs. Emits 'plsql-bindgen 0.1.0' (CARGO_PKG_VERSION) to stdout and exits 0. Both --version and -V added to VALID_FLAGS list. Smoke test: 'plsql-bindgen --version' → 'plsql-bindgen 0.1.0' (exit 0); 'plsql-bindgen -V' → same. New unit tests: version_flag_parses_long_and_short, version_flags_are_in_valid_flags_list (both passing). cargo build/test/clippy all green.

title: PLSQL-ENGINE real-estate gap: clean completeness report contradicts empty dep_graph + 53K diagnostics on a private Oracle estate
description: First run of `plsql-engine analyze` against a real Oracle estate (a private Oracle estate: 4251 files, 329MB, 713 .pkg + 3847 .trg + APEX exports). HONEST RESULT: (+) ran clean, exit 0, ZERO panics across the whole estate (tolerant contract + this session's UTF-8 fixes hold on real input — genuine robustness win). (−) but the SEMANTIC output is hollow on real code: dep_graph = 0 nodes / 0 edges, fact_store = 0 facts, dynamic_sql_sites = 0, unresolved_references = 0, db_link_edges = 0, semantic_model = just declaration_count, flow_summary all 0 — WHILE emitting 53,164 diagnostics. ROOT CAUSE: the deep ParseBackend is not wired (parser_backend reports 'Antlr4Rust' but the real path is the text-scanning pre-parser; the ANTLR backend is Java-gated/un-wired per PLSQL-PARSE-000A), so no semantic structure is extracted from real PL/SQL — the product's core 'know what breaks' value (dep graph / facts / lineage) cannot be delivered on a real estate today. HONESTY BUG (the actionable, in-scope part, plan §1.5 Evidence-UX commitment): the CompletenessReport headline says files_parsed_cleanly=4251 / files_recovered=0 / unresolved_references=0 / skipped_token_ratio=0.0 — a misleadingly PRISTINE picture that directly contradicts 53,164 diagnostics and an empty dep_graph/fact_store. The Trust Block must reflect 'we extracted ~nothing semantically here' (high uncertainty), not '0 unknowns'. Repro: plsql-engine analyze <real estate> --out x.json; jq .payload.completeness vs .payload.diagnostics|length vs .payload.dep_graph. Acceptance: completeness/Trust Block honestly reflects diagnostic volume + empty semantic subsystems (no false-clean); + a discovered-from child for wiring the deep backend (PLSQL-PARSE-000A scope).
close_reason:
Honest CompletenessReport/Trust Block landed in 02eb049. RE-RUN EVIDENCE (cargo run -q -p plsql-engine -- analyze ~/projects/private-estate --out /tmp/xc_p2.json; jq .payload.completeness):

BEFORE (false-clean): files_parsed_cleanly:4224, files_recovered:27, skipped_token_ratio:0.0, dynamic_sql_sites:0, opaque_dynamic_sql_sites:0, db_link_edges:0, unresolved_references:0, wrapped_units:0, missing_package_bodies:0 — a pristine all-zeros picture WHILE 6784 diagnostics / 6609 IR_UNCLASSIFIED_DECL.

AFTER (honest): posture:'LowConfidence', objects_unrecognized:6609, diagnostics_total:6784, objects_with_extracted_semantics:4123, extracted_semantics_ratio:0.38417816; wrapped_units/missing_package_bodies/dynamic_sql_sites/opaque_dynamic_sql_sites/db_link_edges/unresolved_references all now {"unmeasured":true} (NOT a misleading 0); schema_version 1.1.0 (additive bump).

Honesty cuts both ways (asserted in tests): clean synthetic corpus still reads Clean/Partial with ratio ~1.0; demo-no-db passes. Gates: cargo test --workspace 1621 passed / 0 suite failures (one TCP-socket flake green on isolated re-run, unrelated — zero plsql-mcp diff); cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings clean. Tests failing-first then green: plsql-core low_extraction_run_is_never_clean / clean_input_still_reads_clean / unmeasured serialization / degraded_when_nothing_understood; plsql-engine bh4p_low_extraction_run_reports_honest_non_clean_completeness + bh4p_clean_corpus_stays_honest_but_healthy. Deep-backend wiring remains separate scope (PLSQL-PARSE-000A); this fix is purely the reporting-layer honesty.

title: PLSQL-CAT-NEW-2 — Add ALL_POLICIES (VPD) loader for fine-grained access security (/oracle audit)
description:
Audit finding from /oracle skill applied to crates/plsql-catalog/src/lib.rs.

Gap. Catalog has no VPD/RLS coverage. ALL_POLICIES (and ALL_POLICY_CONTEXTS, ALL_POLICY_GROUPS) define DBMS_RLS predicates that change the effective table contents per session — a SELECT against a VPD-protected table reads through a generated WHERE clause. Lineage that ignores VPD will report the table as having only the columns/rows we see; auditors see the policy as silent.

Source. /oracle skill — DATABASE-REFERENCE.md routing for security options + SECURITY-OPTIONS-REFERENCE.md (VPD). Local catalog file: crates/plsql-catalog/src/lib.rs around the load_catalog_grants block at 2571.

Proposed shape. load_catalog_policies(conn, scope) -> Vec<VpdPolicy> over ALL_POLICIES (OBJECT_OWNER, OBJECT_NAME, POLICY_GROUP, POLICY_NAME, PF_OWNER, PACKAGE, FUNCTION, SEL/INS/UPD/DEL/IDX flags, CHK_OPTION, ENABLE, STATIC_POLICY, POLICY_TYPE). Plumbed into CatalogSnapshot::vpd_policies. Lineage flags VPD-protected objects with UnknownReason::VpdPolicyApplied so downstream consumers know the read surface is dynamic.

Cite. Oracle Database Reference 26, ALL_POLICIES view + Database Security Guide chapter on VPD.
close_reason: Added VpdPolicy {object_owner, object_name, policy_group, policy_name, function_owner, function_package, function_name, on_select, on_insert, on_update, on_delete, enabled} struct + SchemaCatalog::vpd_policies (behind #[serde(default)]). load_catalog_vpd_policies queries ALL_POLICIES filtered by object_owner. apply_vpd_policy_row decodes YN/YES/NO mix from dictionary text. Wired between load_catalog_editioning_views and load_catalog_dependencies. 3 orchestration fixtures updated. Test verifies BILLING_TENANT_ISOLATION policy on INVOICES routed through SECURITY.RLS_PKG.TENANT_PREDICATE with SELECT-only flags + enabled correctly decoded. 28 catalog tests pass; clippy --all-targets -D warnings clean. Cite: /oracle skill audit, SECURITY-OPTIONS-REFERENCE.md (VPD chapter); Oracle Database Reference 26 ALL_POLICIES view.

title: PLSQL-OSS-SEC — audit_insert_sql interpolates unvalidated table_name into SQL (identifier injection)
description:
FILE: crates/plsql-mcp/src/audit.rs:100-104 (with_audit_table), 155-165 (audit_insert_sql)

THREAT (SQL identifier injection): audit_insert_sql builds the audit-row INSERT by string interpolation:
  format!("insert into {table} (tool_name, agent_program, agent_model, session_id, recorded_at) values (:1,:2,:3,:4, systimestamp)")
'table' is self.sink.table_name. The doc comments CLAIM the value is validated — line 60 says 'the AuditPlan builder verifies the value is non-empty + matches a simple OWNER.NAME or NAME shape' — but with_audit_table (line 102) does NO validation whatsoever; it just stores the string. audit_insert_sql only trims and checks non-empty. So whatever ends up in table_name (a connections.toml field, a config value, or an agent-supplied argument once the tool is wired) is spliced verbatim into executable SQL.

A table_name carrying extra columns + a statement terminator + a second DDL/DML statement + a line comment, or a DBLINK suffix (X@SOMELINK), or an embedded subquery, escapes the intended INSERT shape. The VALUES are correctly bound (:1..:4) but the table IDENTIFIER itself is not — identifier positions cannot be bind-parameterised in Oracle, which is exactly why the doc demanded a SIMPLE_SQL_NAME-style validation that was never implemented. patch.rs::is_simple_sql_name is the existing, correct validator for this in the same crate.

FIX: Validate table_name in with_audit_table (or a builder finalisation step) before it can reach audit_insert_sql. For an OWNER.NAME shape: split on exactly one '.', run each half through is_simple_sql_name (reuse patch.rs's, or lift to a shared module). Reject anything else. Make audit_insert_sql return None/error for an unvalidated name. Fix the false doc claim either way.

SEVERITY: Medium — DDL/DML injection into the audit path; the audit table is operator-configured today (lower attacker control) but the doc-vs-code divergence means a future wiring that lets an agent set it would be directly exploitable, and the gap silently contradicts a stated invariant.
close_reason: audit.rs with_audit_table now validates table_name (strict OWNER.NAME/NAME identifier shape, each segment a DBMS_ASSERT.SIMPLE_SQL_NAME) and returns Option<Self>, rejecting None on injection. is_valid_audit_table_name + is_simple_sql_name added; false doc claim on AuditSink::table_name corrected. New tests with_audit_table_accepts_valid_identifiers and with_audit_table_rejects_identifier_injection (13 attack vectors: terminators, @DBLINK, extra columns, subquery, double-dot, etc). All green.

title: PLSQL-OSS-AUDIT — README over-promises the MCP server's tool-execution capability
description:
README.md:270-272 states: 'The MCP server (plsql-mcp) is a single crate exposing the full tool surface: foundation static-analysis tools, change-impact and release-gate tools, and the feature-gated live-DB surface.' README.md:333-339 documents 'plsql-mcp serve' / 'serve --listen ADDR' as 'stdio MCP loop' / 'TCP MCP loop' with no caveat.

But the MCP tool-execution path is NOT implemented: crates/plsql-mcp/src/mcp_protocol.rs:206-236 handle_tools_call validates the tool name against the registry and then returns a STATIC placeholder ('tool {name} is registered but execution is gated on the per-tool MCP-LIVE wiring') for EVERY tool. main.rs:4 confirms 'serve reaches into the transport layer once the per-tool beads land'. So an MCP client can connect, run initialize and tools/list, but every tools/call returns a non-executing stub.

(NOTE: the handle_tools_call placeholder itself is already tracked as a known issue and is NOT being re-filed here. This bead is specifically about the README/docs over-claiming.)

Why it matters: the README presents plsql-mcp as a working MCP server 'exposing the full tool surface'. A user who installs it and wires it into Cursor/Claude Desktop will find tool discovery works but no tool actually runs. For an OSS release this is a documentation-honesty gap -- the prose promises a capability the binary does not yet have.

Suggested fix: until the per-tool dispatch is wired, the README should state plainly that the MCP server currently supports the discovery handshake (initialize / tools/list) and that tool *execution* is in progress / not yet wired. Match the honest framing already used elsewhere in the tree (e.g. plsql-mcp/src/lib.rs:8 'Per-tool engine integration lands in subsequent beads', and the honest SKIP language in PUBLISHING.md).
close_reason: README MCP claim corrected: now states the server completes the handshake + advertises the full surface over tools/list, with per-tool tools/call wiring in progress; matching note added to Limitations. No longer overclaims.

title: PLSQL-OSS-MOCK2 — plsql-support::minimize_repro is a publicly-exported skeleton module; plan_minimize emits hardcoded 5-step boilerplate, never minimises
description: crates/plsql-support/src/minimize_repro.rs:1-21 (module-level docstring) and :89-148 (plan_minimize / plan_for_blob). The module docstring explicitly self-declares: 'plsql support minimize-repro skeleton' and 'the actual ddmin / delta-debug loop lands in a follow-up bead' and 'The actual byte-level minimisation isn't implemented here'. plan_for_blob always returns the exact same hardcoded vec of 5 MinimizationStep entries regardless of input bytes — it's not a plan, it's a static template echoed for every blob. The module is reexported (`pub use minimize_repro::{...}`) in crates/plsql-support/src/lib.rs so it appears in the crate's public surface. No CLI or MCP tool calls plan_minimize in this workspace, but the module is publicly available and shows up in rustdoc as 'minimize_repro'. An OSS reviewer auditing plsql-support would find a 260-line module whose docs say 'follow-up bead' and whose code is a fixed 5-step template — a textbook placeholder. Suggested fix: either land the real ddmin/delta-debug loop now, or downgrade visibility to `pub(crate)` / remove from `pub use` and add a `#[doc(hidden)]` until the worker lands. Also strip the 'skeleton' framing from the docstring or replace with explicit 'planning-only' wording that doesn't promise more than the function delivers.
close_reason: Picked option (b): no external consumers of plan_minimize exist in the workspace (grep clean), so renamed plan_minimize -> minimize_repro_plan, dropped the pub use minimize_repro::{...} re-export from crate root (keeping the module pub for in-workspace opt-in), and rewrote the module-level + function-level + helper docs to honestly describe the limited scope: a content-independent structural plan template (canonical strategy ordering) emitted for downstream consumers; no byte-level minimisation is performed by this module. All 'skeleton' / 'follow-up bead' language removed. Verified: cargo build -p plsql-support OK; cargo test -p plsql-support 95 passed; cargo clippy -p plsql-support --all-targets -- -D warnings OK.
