title: Fix UTF-8 char-boundary panic in classify_if: replace hardcoded &body[3..] with boundary-safe IF-keyword strip
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-ir/src/stmt.rs:616 — verdict=keep, severity=high
**Impact:** A PL/SQL routine body containing a multibyte UTF-8 character immediately abutting an IF keyword (e.g. `IFé THEN ...`) aborts the entire analysis process via panic, a denial-of-service on attacker/customer-controlled source slices lowered at plsql-engine/src/lib.rs:961.
**Evidence:** crates/plsql-ir/src/stmt.rs:522 `if starts_with_keyword(trimmed, "IF") { return classify_if(text); }`; the gate at stmt.rs:465-472 strips "IF" then accepts any char that is NOT [A-Za-z0-9_$#] (so a multibyte char's leading byte passes). stmt.rs:614-616: `let body = &text[..end_pos]; ... let after_if = &body[3..];` — hardcoded 3-byte slice for a 2-byte keyword, assuming a trailing ASCII space. classify is fed already-trimmed text (stmt.rs:147 `.trim()`, stmt.rs:163 `classify(&stripped)`), so no whitespace divergence. Empirical repro via a standalone crate calling the public `plsql_ir::lower_statement_body("IFé THEN x := 1; END IF;")`: `thread 'main' panicked at /home/durakovic/projects/plsql-intelligence/crates/plsql-ir/src/stmt.rs:616:25: start byte index 3 is not a char boundary; it is inside 'é' (bytes 2..4 of string)`. Sibling arms verified SAFE in the same run: RAISé/RETURNé/EXITé/EX
**Repro:** Triggering input: `plsql_ir::lower_statement_body("IFé THEN x := 1; END IF;")` → panic at stmt.rs:616:25 ("start byte index 3 is not a char boundary; it is inside 'é' (bytes 2..4)"). Mechanism: dispatch gate (stmt.rs:469-472) lets the leading byte of `é` (0xC3 at byte index 2) satisfy "non-identifier char after IF", routing to classify_if; line 616 then slices `&body[3..]`, landing on the 0xA9 continuation byte mid-codepoint. Disproof of broader scope: RAISE/RETURN/EXIT/EXECUTE IMMEDIATE do NOT panic on the analogous `<KW>é` because starts_with_keyword requires the full ASCII keyword as prefix, so their slice offset equals the keyword byte length (= the boundary at the start of `é`), confirm
**Suggested fix:** Replace the hardcoded `let after_if = &body[3..];` with a char-boundary-safe strip. Cleanest: gate the dispatch so IF must be followed by whitespace/`(` (mirroring the FOR/WHILE `"FOR "` gate at stmt.rs:660), or strip the 2-byte keyword safely: `let after_if = body.get(2..).unwrap_or("");` (since `starts_with_keyword(..,"IF")` already guaranteed an ASCII "IF" prefix, byte 2 is always a valid boundary), then `.trim_start()`. Add a regression test asserting `lower_statement_body("IFé THEN x := 1; END IF;")` returns without panic. The sibling RAISE/RETURN/EXIT/EXECUTE-IMMEDIATE arms are currently safe (offset == keyword byte length) but converting them to `.get(..).unwrap_or("")` would be defense-in-depth uniformity, not required for this fix.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit a634b473a4), re-verified on main. UTF-8 char-boundary panic in classify_if (crates/plsql-ir/src/stmt.rs). Root cause: hardcoded `let after_if = &body[3..]` assumed a trailing ASCII space after the 2-byte `IF` keyword; when a multibyte char (e.g. `IFé THEN ...`) abutted the keyword, byte index 3 landed mid-codepoint inside `é` and panicked (process abort under release `panic=abort`). The dispatch gate `starts_with_keyword(trimmed,"IF")` accepts a non-ASCII-alnum char right after `IF`, so this was reachable from adversarial/non-La

title: Tighten host_only() IPv6-bracket parsing to reject trailing-garbage authorities
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/oraclemcp-auth/src/http_guard.rs:54 — verdict=defense-in-depth, severity=low
**Impact:** No live request is mis-authorized today: the parser bug is real but the function that uses it (HttpGuardPolicy::check / host_only / authority_is_loopback) has zero production callers — the actual HTTP transport delegates Host/Origin/DNS-rebinding enforcement to rmcp's StreamableHttpServerConfig, not to this module. Impact is a latent correctness bug in a pub-exported security primitive plus a violated doc contract; if a future transport (bead P1-9a) ever wires check() in, crafted authorities like [::1].attacker.example would be mis-classified as loopback.
**Evidence:** host_only repro (rustc -O, exact function copied from http_guard.rs:54-64):
  host_only("[::1]") = "::1"  loopback=true
  host_only("[::1]:443") = "::1"  loopback=true
  host_only("[::1].attacker.example") = "::1"  loopback=true
  host_only("[::1]@attacker.example") = "::1"  loopback=true
  host_only("[::1]evil") = "::1"  loopback=true
  host_only("[2001:db8::1]:8080") = "2001:db8::1"  loopback=false
  host_only("attacker.example") = "attacker.example"  loopback=false
=> the parser defect is confirmed exactly as claimed.

Caller search (whole repo): the ONLY non-test references to HttpGuardPolicy/authority_is_loopback/HttpGuardError are crates/oraclemcp-auth/src/lib.rs:19 (`pub use http_guard::{HttpGuardError, HttpGuardPolicy, authority_is_loopback};`). All `.check(` and host_only/authority_is_loopback call sites are inside http_guard.rs itself (lines 96,109,122) and its #[cfg(test)] tes
**Repro:** Triggering input for the parser defect (in isolation): authority_is_loopback("[::1].attacker.example") returns true (proven above) though it is not loopback. BUT no production code calls this: the live transport (oraclemcp-core/src/http.rs) hands allowed_hosts/allowed_origins to rmcp's StreamableHttpServerConfig and never invokes HttpGuardPolicy::check, host_only, or authority_is_loopback. The finding's own why_real concedes "the transport that calls check() (bead P1-9a) is not yet wired up in this checkout." So there is no X->wrong-response Y on any live request today; the defect is latent until/unless this module is wired into a request path.
**Suggested fix:** After extracting the bracketed inner literal, require the remainder after ']' to be empty or `:port`; otherwise return the authority unchanged so it cannot match the loopback set. Replace the IPv6 branch at lines 56-59 with split_once(']') and an after_ok check (empty or `:` + all-ASCII-digit port), per the finding. Add regression tests asserting host_only("[::1].attacker.example") / "[::1]@attacker.example" / "[::1]evil" do NOT resolve to "::1" while "[::1]" and "[::1]:443" still do. Severity is low/defense-in-depth: this primitive is a pub API with no live caller (rmcp enforces the real transport guards), so this is contract-correctness hardening, not a live bypass fix.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 55363d7af3), re-verified on main. Root-caused and fixed the IPv6-bracket parsing defect in host_only() at crates/oraclemcp-auth/src/http_guard.rs:54. The old code `rest.split(']').next()` took the inner literal and silently dropped any trailing garbage, so authorities like `[::1].attacker.example`, `[::1]@attacker.example`, `[::1]evil` all reduced to `::1` and were mis-classified as loopback by authority_is_loopback()/check(). New code uses split_once(']') and validates the remainder after the closing bracket: it must be empty O

title: Validate/escape scratch-user password before interpolating into CREATE USER DDL
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-cicd/src/verify.rs:543 — verdict=defense-in-depth, severity=low
**Impact:** A scratch-user password containing a double-quote silently corrupts the CREATE USER statement and produces a confusing ORA error instead of a clear rejection; today the value is operator/test-supplied (never untrusted input) and runs only inside this crate's tests, so there is no live exploit — purely a robustness/defense-in-depth gap.
**Evidence:** verify.rs:542-544 — conn.execute(&format!("CREATE USER {schema} IDENTIFIED BY \"{password}\""), &[]) — schema is guarded by is_scratch_schema (line 535) but the password is not. effective_password() (verify.rs:362-366) clones scratch_user_password verbatim or returns the hardcoded default "V3r1fyTmp#2026"; no validation/escaping anywhere.

Production-surface grep (rg 'VerifyOptions|create_scratch_schema|scratch_user_password|effective_password' crates/): scratch_user_password is set only in crates/plsql-cicd/tests/{verify_live_xe.rs,cicd_cycle_live_xe.rs} and the pub struct; create_scratch_schema (cicd) is called only at verify.rs:616 inside verify(); verify() is called only from this crate's own #[test]/live-XE tests. The create_scratch_schema hits in plsql-mcp tests are a separate 2-arg local test helper, not this fn.

No binary wires it: 8 main.rs/bin targets exist; none construct Ver
**Repro:** Cannot exhibit the claimed X→Y exploit. Disproof of the headline: input password `pw" ; GRANT DBA TO PUBLIC --` yields CREATE USER ... IDENTIFIED BY "pw" ; GRANT DBA TO PUBLIC --" sent as ONE string to oracle 0.6.3 Connection::execute (OCI prepareStmt); OCI does not treat ';' as a separator and rejects the trailing statement with ORA-00911 — no second DDL runs. The only reproducible defect is benign: any password containing `"` (e.g. `a"b`) closes the quoted password early and corrupts the single CREATE USER statement, surfacing as a confusing ORA error rather than a clean rejection. Reachability of even that requires a caller to set VerifyOptions.scratch_user_password to such a value AND ho
**Suggested fix:** Add a validate_scratch_password(&str) -> Result<(), VerifyError> helper (new InvalidScratchPassword variant) that rejects empty/over-length and any char outside a conservative allowlist (e.g. [A-Za-z0-9#_$.+-]), and call it in create_scratch_schema before the format! at line 542 (or inside effective_password). A "\"" in an operator-chosen config password almost certainly signals a mistake, so explicit rejection is preferable to escaping. Add a unit test asserting `a"b` (or `pw" ; GRANT DBA TO PUBLIC --`) is rejected. Low priority — no live caller passes untrusted input today.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 55020d92c9), re-verified on main. Root-cause fix in /home/durakovic/projects/plsql-intelligence/.claude/worktrees/wf_2522fe96-bb7-10/crates/plsql-cicd/src/verify.rs. Added pub fn validate_scratch_password(&str) -> Result<(), VerifyError> (allowlist [A-Za-z0-9#_$.+-], rejects empty + over-length >128 bytes) plus new VerifyError::InvalidScratchPassword { reason } variant, and call validate_scratch_password(password)? inside create_scratch_schema BEFORE the format!("CREATE USER {schema} IDENTIFIED BY \"{password}\"") at the former

title: Add idle-entry reclamation to AdmissionController.agents map (mirror subscriptions registry)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/oraclemcp-core/src/admission.rs:47 — verdict=defense-in-depth, severity=low
**Impact:** None today: the AdmissionController is not wired into any request path (no production caller exists), so the unbounded map never grows from real traffic. The leak would only materialize if a future server both wires the controller AND keys it on an attacker-influenced/high-cardinality identity (e.g. a raw OAuth token subject) — neither condition exists in the tree. A worthwhile cheap hardening item, not a present failure.
**Evidence:** admission.rs:31  `agents: Mutex<HashMap<String, Arc<Semaphore>>>` ; lines 47-54 `agent_semaphore` does `agents.entry(agent.to_owned()).or_insert_with(|| Arc::new(Semaphore::new(self.per_agent_cap)))` — insert-only, no removal anywhere in the file (no remove/retain/clear/Drop/TTL).
Caller grep (`rg 'try_admit|AdmissionController|agent_semaphore' crates/`): only `tests/chaos.rs:18-35` (fixed literal "agent-a"), the module's own `#[cfg(test)]` block, and `lib.rs:45` (re-export). 
`rg 'admission|Admission|try_admit' server.rs http.rs query_execute.rs session_tool.rs` → EXIT 1 (no matches): not wired into any request path.
Sibling reclamation confirmed in subscriptions.rs:35-52 (`set.remove(client)`, `map.remove(uri)`, `map.retain(...)`). NOTE: the claim/verifier cite `stepup.rs` as a second reclaiming sibling, but `crates/oraclemcp-core/src/stepup.rs` does not exist — that comparison is wron
**Repro:** Impossibility (today): the only way to grow the map is `try_admit(agent)`; the sole non-test caller is tests/chaos.rs with a constant "agent-a", and no server/http/query/session code references the controller at all (grep EXIT 1). Therefore no runtime path supplies distinct agent strings → map cannot grow in any deployed configuration. Hypothetical future trigger: a server wiring try_admit per request with a per-request unique identity X (e.g. token subject) would add one resident HashMap entry per distinct X with no reclamation → unbounded growth. That wiring and that keying decision do not exist in the current tree.
**Suggested fix:** Latent hardening, not urgent. When the controller is eventually wired, either (a) prune in agent_semaphore() before insert via `agents.retain(|_, sem| sem.available_permits() < self.per_agent_cap || Arc::strong_count(sem) > 1)`, or (b) add a Drop-based reclamation on AdmissionPermit. Add a regression test asserting the map returns to baseline after a churned set of distinct agent strings are all dropped. Most importantly, document on `new`/`try_admit` that `agent` MUST be a low-cardinality server-controlled principal and MUST NOT be a raw client-supplied/per-request token subject — that contract alone closes the only path to the hypothetical DoS.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 4867a864a2), re-verified on main. Root-caused the unbounded growth of AdmissionController.agents in crates/oraclemcp-core/src/admission.rs. agent_semaphore() was insert-only with no removal anywhere. Added idle-entry reclamation: before inserting, agents.retain keeps an entry iff it is the currently-keyed agent OR Arc::strong_count(sem) > 1 (an outstanding permit/clone still references it) OR sem.available_permits() < per_agent_cap (some permits are held). An entry is dropped only when fully idle AND unreferenced, which is behav

title: Fix enforcement.rs doc: classifier does not "block" SET ROLE / non-allowlisted ALTER SESSION (Guarded/ReadWrite, not Forbidden)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/oraclemcp-guard/src/enforcement.rs:24 — verdict=defense-in-depth, severity=low
**Impact:** A developer/operator reading enforcement.rs's module doc would wrongly believe the classifier (layer C) blocks post-connect SET ROLE and non-allowlisted ALTER SESSION; in fact both classify Guarded/ReadWrite and a session already stepped up to ReadWrite Allows `SET ROLE DBA`. No write-path bypass on a correctly-provisioned (layer A) deployment — the documented hard boundary (least-privilege DB user) still holds — but the prose misrepresents the layering, eroding defense-in-depth reasoning.
**Evidence:** enforcement.rs:22-26 doc: "`SET ROLE` and non-allowlisted `ALTER SESSION` are blocked by the classifier (layer C), so a session cannot enable a write-bearing role post-connect."
classifier.rs:636-725 classify_statement has arms for Query/Insert/Update/Delete/Merge/Explain/DDL/Grant/Revoke/Commit/Rollback/Savepoint, then `_ => guarded_rw(Vec::new())` (line 724). No arm for sqlparser Statement::Set/SetRole or Statement::AlterSession → both fall through to Guarded/ReadWrite.
ClassifierConfig::new()==default() → empty block_patterns (classifier.rs:78-82), so Stage A (stage_a, line 197-201) does not block them either.
is_allowed_alter_session is called ONLY at oraclemcp-core/src/session_tool.rs:241 (the oracle_session SetSession action), never in classify() — confirmed by `rg 'is_allowed_alter_session' crates/`.
Live run of the real Classifier::default().classify(...):
  CLASSIFY "SET ROLE DB
**Repro:** Triggering input X → wrong output Y: A session stepped up to OperatingLevel::ReadWrite (legitimate for DML) calls classify("SET ROLE DBA") → Guarded/ReadWrite, then SessionLevelState{effective=ReadWrite}.evaluate(Some(ReadWrite)) → LevelDecision::Allow. The doc says this is "blocked by the classifier"; it is Allowed. Same for `ALTER SESSION SET sql_trace=true` (doc claims non-allowlisted ALTER SESSION is blocked by the classifier; allowlist is never consulted on the classify path). Why impact is bounded (not high): on a correctly-provisioned deployment the read-only Oracle user (layer A, the doc's stated "only hard boundary") has no DBA role to enable, and a default READ_ONLY session returns
**Suggested fix:** Correct the doc at lines 22-26 to describe actual behavior: SET ROLE and non-allowlisted ALTER SESSION fall through classify_statement's default arm to Guarded/ReadWrite (so they require step-up from a READ_ONLY session but ARE allowed once a session is at ReadWrite); the ALTER SESSION allowlist (is_allowed_alter_session) is enforced only on the dedicated oracle_session SetSession action (oraclemcp-core::session_tool.rs:241), not in classify(); and the hard guarantee against enabling a write-bearing role post-connect rests on layer A (the least-privilege Oracle user), not on layer C. If a stronger guarantee is actually intended, back it with code: add explicit classify_statement arms mapping Statement::SetRole to Admin/Destructive (or Forbidden) and consult is_allowed_alter_session inside a Statement::AlterSession arm so non-allowlisted params map to Forbidden, plus adversarial-corpus te

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 0c30cde88c), re-verified on main. Corrected the misleading module doc at crates/oraclemcp-guard/src/enforcement.rs (read_only_setup_statements) AND backed the strongest claim with code. The old prose claimed the classifier 'blocks' SET ROLE / non-allowlisted ALTER SESSION; it did not (both classified Guarded/ReadWrite and were Allowed at ReadWrite). New doc describes the code-true picture: SET ROLE now maps to Destructive/Admin (via the oracle-clgt.3 work), so it is never Allowed below ADMIN (READ_ONLY/READ_WRITE sessions are fo

title: Route describe.rs DB free-text through query::sanitize() before wiring describe into dispatch
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-mcp/src/describe.rs:153 — verdict=defense-in-depth, severity=low
**Impact:** None today — the describe tools are not advertised in the registry nor in the dispatch table, so no agent-reachable path exists. The moment describe_table/view/trigger/index are wired into dispatch_table()/default_tool_registry(), a schema owner who can set a column comment, CHECK condition, or view body could embed tool-call markup that would reach the agent unredacted, defeating the product's "treat every DB-read cell as untrusted/neutralized" defense.
**Evidence:** crates/plsql-mcp/src/query.rs:99-101 routes every DB-read cell through sanitize: `let (scrubbed, was_sanitized) = sanitize(text);` and sets QueryResponse.sanitized_cells + pushes UnknownReason::ResponseSanitized (lines 122-126). sanitize() is defined at query.rs:254. By contrast crates/plsql-mcp/src/describe.rs returns DB free-text VERBATIM with no sanitize call: line 153 `let text = row.text("TEXT_VC").map(String::from);` (view body), line 285 `comment: row.text("COMMENTS").map(String::from)`, line 305 table COMMENTS, line 349 `search_condition: row.text("SEARCH_CONDITION_VC").map(String::from)`, line 280 DEFAULT_EXPRESSION, lines 199/203 trigger TRIGGERING_EVENT / WHEN_CLAUSE.

Reachability (confirmed unreachable):
- dispatch.rs:122-164 dispatch_table() lists every dispatchable tool; NO describe_* entry (last live-DB entries are query/patch_package/patch_view/create_or_replace/execute_
**Repro:** Impossibility proof for "live today": to reach run_describe_view with a hostile view body, a JSON-RPC `tools/call` with name="describe_view" must pass handle_tools_call's advertised-tool gate (mcp_protocol.rs:218-221). That gate checks the ToolRegistry built by default_tool_registry(), which registers no describe tool, so the call is rejected with -32601 before any describe code runs. dispatch_tool also has no describe arm. The lockstep test dispatch_table_matches_default_registry (dispatch.rs:360) further forbids a registered-but-undispatched describe path. Therefore no agent input can reach the unsanitized field. The gap becomes a concrete X->Y only after a future bead adds register_descri
**Suggested fix:** When (and ideally just before) wiring run_describe_* into dispatch_table()/default_tool_registry(), route every DB-controlled free-text field through crate::query::sanitize(): view TEXT_VC (describe.rs:153), table/column/view COMMENTS (285,305), constraint SEARCH_CONDITION_VC (349), column DEFAULT_EXPRESSION (280), trigger WHEN_CLAUSE/TRIGGERING_EVENT (199,203). Mirror query.rs/source.rs honesty: add a sanitized_fields counter to the Describe*Response structs and push UnknownReason::ResponseSanitized when any field is rewritten. A small `fn scrub(opt: Option<String>, counter: &mut usize) -> Option<String>` helper keeps call sites tidy. Add a RouterStub regression test feeding a hostile comment/CHECK/view body containing tool-call markup and assert the output is neutralized. Severity is low because there is no reachable caller today.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit edaee1b9a3), re-verified on main. Routed every DB-controlled free-text field in crates/plsql-mcp/src/describe.rs through crate::query::sanitize() (the same K18 scrubber query.rs/source.rs use). Added a `scrub(Option<String>, &mut usize) -> Option<String>` helper and a `sanitized_fields: usize` + `unknown_reasons: Vec<UnknownReason>` to DescribeTableResponse / DescribeViewResponse / DescribeTriggerResponse, pushing UnknownReason::ResponseSanitized when any field was rewritten — mirroring the query/source honesty contract. Sanitiz

title: Fix side-effecting short-circuit in classify_rename so half-matched hints don't silently drop deletes/creates
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-lineage/src/lib.rs:2207 — verdict=keep, severity=low
**Impact:** When classify_rename is eventually wired into the lineage/MCP change pipeline, a malformed or stale rename hint (typo'd/non-existent `after`, or a partial git/persistent-id pair) causes a genuinely-dropped PL/SQL object to silently vanish from the change report — appearing as neither a rename candidate nor an unmatched delete — so a release reviewer wrongly concludes the object still exists. Today the impact is latent: the function has no production caller, so nothing can feed it bad input yet.
**Evidence:** crates/plsql-lineage/src/lib.rs:2206-2215 (explicit_mappings), 2216-2225 (persistent_id_pairs), 2226-2240 (git_renames) all use the side-effecting short-circuit:
    for (before, after) in &hints.explicit_mappings {
        if deletes.remove(before) && creates.remove(after) {  // 2207
unmatched_deletes is built from the residual set at line 2250: `unmatched_deletes: deletes.into_iter().collect(),`.
Runnable proof (throwaway test, now removed) output:
    candidates=[]
    unmatched_deletes=[]                       <-- billing.old_pkg VANISHED
    unmatched_creates=["billing.new_pkg"]
    DATA LOSS: old_pkg vanished from unmatched_deletes! got []  (panic)
Contract violated — doc comment lib.rs:2182 "Unmatched deletes / creates stay in their bucket"; docs/components/lineage.md:154 "Unmatched deletes/creates stay in their bucket — the classifier *never*..."; docs/architecture.md:254 R13 "No
**Repro:** Minimal trigger (proven by running it under cargo test -p plsql-lineage): build a SemanticChangeSet with Dropped "billing.old_pkg" + Created "billing.new_pkg"; pass RenameHints.explicit_mappings = {"billing.old_pkg" -> "billing.TYPO"} where TYPO is not a created object. Result: candidates=[], unmatched_creates=["billing.new_pkg"], unmatched_deletes=[] — old_pkg silently dropped. Root cause: in `deletes.remove(before) && creates.remove(after)`, the left operand executes and mutates the set before the right operand is evaluated; on the false right operand `before` is left consumed-but-unreported. Same pattern at lines 2217 and 2230. Caveat: no in-tree binary path reaches classify_rename today,
**Suggested fix:** In all three loops (lib.rs:2207, 2217, 2230) gate on membership before mutating, or evaluate both removals into bindings and restore on partial match. Cleanest is the contains-check form: `if deletes.contains(before) && creates.contains(after) { deletes.remove(before); creates.remove(after); candidates.push(...); }` so a non-match performs no mutation and the dropped object stays in `deletes` (and thus surfaces in unmatched_deletes). Add a regression test with an asymmetric explicit mapping (e.g. {"billing.old_pkg" -> "billing.typo"}) asserting candidates is empty AND unmatched_deletes contains "billing.old_pkg".

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 8a66991988), re-verified on main. Root-caused and fixed the side-effecting short-circuit in classify_rename (crates/plsql-lineage/src/lib.rs:2206-2240). The condition `deletes.remove(before) && creates.remove(after)` evaluated the left BTreeSet::remove first, mutating the set; on a false right operand (half-matched hint with valid `before` but non-existent `after`) the dropped object was consumed-but-unreported and vanished from unmatched_deletes entirely — neither rename candidate nor unmatched delete. Replaced with non-mutatin

title: Gate run_is_dbms_assert on a DBMS_ASSERT validator allowlist (exclude NOOP) instead of bare contains()
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-symbols/src/dynamic_sql.rs:309 — verdict=keep, severity=high
**Impact:** An EXECUTE IMMEDIATE that concatenates DBMS_ASSERT.NOOP(p_user) (Oracle's documented no-op identity pass-through that validates nothing) — or any unknown/future DBMS_ASSERT entry point — is classified by run_is_dbms_assert as a sanitiser, so the live MCP `dynamic_sql_evidence` tool reports ExprInterpolation.sanitised=true (doc: "the substituted identifier is bounded by the asserted name") and score_dynamic_sql_edge stamps the edge Medium / "object set is bounded by the asserted names" for what is in fact an unbounded SQL-injection surface; this contradicts the IR/SEC001 taint engine, which deliberately keeps the same NOOP-wrapped value tainted.
**Evidence:** crates/plsql-symbols/src/dynamic_sql.rs:309-311 — `fn run_is_dbms_assert(run: &str) -> bool { run.to_ascii_uppercase().contains("DBMS_ASSERT.") }` (no function-name allowlist). Result feeds ExprInterpolation.sanitised at line 213, whose own field doc (line 84-88) claims true ⇒ "the substituted identifier is bounded by the asserted name". medium_or_low (dynamic_sql_confidence.rs:90-96) returns ConfidenceLevel::Medium "...object set is bounded by the asserted names" when sanitised==total.

Throwaway test (since removed, tree clean) over "EXECUTE IMMEDIATE 'SELECT * FROM ' || DBMS_ASSERT.NOOP(p_tab);" printed:
  interpolations: [ExprInterpolation { sanitised: true }]
  level: Medium
  explanation: Some("interpolated dynamic SQL, all 1 interpolation(s) routed through DBMS_ASSERT — object set is bounded by the asserted names")
Identical result for DBMS_ASSERT.SOME_FUTURE_FN(p_tab) (sanitised:
**Repro:** Trigger: recognise_dynamic_sql("EXECUTE IMMEDIATE 'SELECT * FROM ' || DBMS_ASSERT.NOOP(p_tab);", site) → expr_interpolations = [ExprInterpolation { sanitised: true }]; score_dynamic_sql_edge(&ev).level = Medium, explanation contains "bounded". Same for DBMS_ASSERT.<any_unknown_fn>. Wrong output: NOOP performs zero validation, so "sanitised/bounded" is a false safety label. Scope note (one part of the claim NOT yet live): score_dynamic_sql_edge / enrich_dynamic_sql has no consumer outside plsql-symbols' own tests — `rg` of crates/plsql-depgraph, plsql-engine, plsql-lineage finds zero calls — so the finding's "depgraph stamps the edge Medium / lineage doctor()/unsafe_paths()/Trust Block report
**Suggested fix:** Replace the bare contains() in run_is_dbms_assert with a parse of each DBMS_ASSERT.<fn> token plus membership in a VALIDATORS allowlist mirroring plsql-ir/src/flow_intra.rs::is_dbms_assert_sanitizer (SIMPLE_SQL_NAME, QUALIFIED_SQL_NAME, SCHEMA_NAME, ENQUOTE_NAME, SQL_OBJECT_NAME, ENQUOTE_LITERAL), deliberately excluding NOOP so NOOP and any unknown/future entry point fall through to sanitised=false (scorer then returns Low "no DBMS_ASSERT sanitisation"). Add regression tests asserting EXECUTE IMMEDIATE ... || DBMS_ASSERT.NOOP(p_tab) and ... || DBMS_ASSERT.<unknown_fn>(p_tab) score Low with the lone interpolation unsanitised. Note detect_dbms_assert_calls (line 414-421) intentionally keeps NOOP for textual evidence — leave that; only run_is_dbms_assert is the defect. Lower-priority follow-up: when score_dynamic_sql_edge/enrich_dynamic_sql is eventually wired into the depgraph/lineage Trus

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 05cb953f4c), re-verified on main. Root-cause fix in crates/plsql-symbols/src/dynamic_sql.rs:run_is_dbms_assert. The bare `run.to_ascii_uppercase().contains("DBMS_ASSERT.")` classified DBMS_ASSERT.NOOP (Oracle's documented identity pass-through that validates nothing) AND any unknown/future DBMS_ASSERT entry point as a sanitiser, falsely tagging ExprInterpolation.sanitised=true and driving score_dynamic_sql_edge to Medium / 'object set is bounded' for an unbounded injection surface. Replaced with a VALIDATORS allowlist (SIMPLE_SQ

title: Fail-closed admin-keyword scan in classifier parse-failure/catch-all branches so unparseable DCL requires Admin not ReadWrite
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/oraclemcp-guard/src/classifier.rs:618 — verdict=keep, severity=high
**Impact:** A ReadWrite-elevated agent (any non-protected read-write profile) can run privilege-escalation DCL — GRANT DBA TO scott, ALTER USER sys IDENTIFIED BY hacked, ALTER SYSTEM SET ..., SET ROLE dba, CREATE USER/ROLE, AUDIT/NOAUDIT — with no Admin step-up, because the classifier under-levels every Oracle admin statement sqlparser 0.62 cannot parse to ReadWrite instead of Admin.
**Evidence:** classifier.rs:617-624 parse-failure branch: `_ => { return StatementClass { danger: DangerLevel::Guarded, required: Some(OperatingLevel::ReadWrite), objects: Vec::new() }; }`. classifier.rs:626-630 guarded_rw closure also returns `required: Some(OperatingLevel::ReadWrite)`; the catch-all at line 724 `_ => guarded_rw(Vec::new())` uses it. levels.rs:37 defines Admin as "GRANT / REVOKE, ALTER USER/SYSTEM, cross-schema DCL". levels.rs:278-281 evaluate: `if required <= self.effective_level() { Allow } else { RequireStepUp { target: required } }`. exec_grant.rs:124 `if requested_level > entry.granted_level` is the only level rejection. PROBE OUTPUT (cargo test, sqlparser 0.62.0, OracleDialect):
GRANT DBA TO scott -> parse=PARSE_ERR required=Some(ReadWrite) danger=Guarded
ALTER USER sys IDENTIFIED BY hacked -> PARSE_ERR required=Some(ReadWrite)
ALTER SYSTEM SET sga_target = 0 -> PARSE_ERR requi
**Repro:** Trigger: session at ceiling Admin elevated to ReadWrite (escalate_window/set_current_level(ReadWrite)). Input X = "GRANT DBA TO scott". classify() -> Stage A returns PureSql (does not start with DECLARE/BEGIN/CREATE OR REPLACE/PACKAGE/FUNCTION/PROCEDURE/TRIGGER, no side-effect markers) -> classify_statement -> Parser::parse_sql fails -> branch 617-624 -> required=ReadWrite. gate() -> evaluate(Some(ReadWrite)): ReadWrite <= effective_level(ReadWrite) -> Output Y = LevelDecision::Allow. No Admin step-up; exec_grant only checks requested<=granted so it runs at ReadWrite. Verified for 13 distinct admin/DCL statements via throwaway test (added, run, fully reverted; git diff --stat now empty). Dis
**Suggested fix:** In classify_statement: (1) before the parse-failure branch returns (lines 617-624), run a leading/structural admin-keyword scan over the canonicalized statement text (reuse canonical_marker_scan so literals/quoted identifiers are collapsed to a sentinel and cannot smuggle keywords) for statement-leading admin verbs: GRANT, REVOKE, CREATE/ALTER/DROP USER, CREATE/ALTER/DROP ROLE, ALTER SYSTEM, ALTER DATABASE, ALTER PROFILE, AUDIT, NOAUDIT, SET ROLE, and ALTER USER ... IDENTIFIED BY; on a match return danger=Destructive, required=Some(OperatingLevel::Admin); otherwise keep the ReadWrite fail-closed default for genuinely non-admin unparseable SQL. (2) Add explicit arms before the line-724 catch-all for Statement::CreateRole, Statement::AlterRole, Statement::Drop with role/user object type, and Statement::Set when it sets ROLE, mapping to destructive(OperatingLevel::Admin, _). Add regression 

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 0c30cde88c), re-verified on main. Root-cause fix in crates/oraclemcp-guard/src/classifier.rs. The classifier's parse-failure branch (formerly returning Guarded/ReadWrite for everything sqlparser 0.62 cannot parse) now runs starts_with_admin_verb() over canonical_marker_scan output before defaulting: a statement-LEADING, WORD-BOUNDARED admin/DCL verb (GRANT, REVOKE, AUDIT, NOAUDIT, CREATE/ALTER/DROP USER, CREATE/ALTER/DROP ROLE, ALTER SYSTEM/DATABASE/PROFILE, SET ROLE) forces Destructive/Admin; genuinely non-admin unparseable SQL

title: Gate split_script PL/SQL opener heuristic with at_top_level_at_line_start
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-project/src/splitter.rs:159 — verdict=keep, severity=high
**Impact:** A SQL script statement containing a multi-line single-quoted string whose continuation line begins with a PL/SQL opener keyword (BEGIN/DECLARE/CREATE ... PROCEDURE, etc.) causes split_script to silently merge that statement with all following statements into one mislabeled PlSqlBlock; a downstream linter/SAST/lineage consumer iterating the output entirely loses the merged-away statement(s) and analyzes a corrupted concatenation. The triggering string content is user/attacker-controllable.
**Evidence:** crates/plsql-project/src/splitter.rs:159 (current working tree, confirmed via `sed -n '159p'`): `        if looks_like_plsql_opener(line_no_terminator) {` — NOT gated by at_top_level_at_line_start, unlike the lone-`/` path at line 124 (`if trimmed == "/" && at_top_level_at_line_start`) and the terminator at line 168 (`if !in_plsql && ends_top_level_semi`). looks_like_plsql_opener (lines 243-257) does only `line.trim_start().to_ascii_uppercase()` with zero awareness of in_string/in_block_comment. Empirical run of a throwaway integration test against the real public split_script:
  control: count=2 kinds=[Sql, Sql]
  BEGIN-in-INSERT: count=1 kinds=[PlSqlBlock]
    [1..4] PlSqlBlock raw="INSERT INTO t VALUES ('line one\nBEGIN inside the string\nstill data');\nSELECT 2 FROM dual;"
  DECLARE-in-UPDATE: count=1 kinds=[PlSqlBlock]
Both repro assertions failed (got 1, expected 2) against unmodif
**Repro:** split_script("INSERT INTO t VALUES ('line one\nBEGIN inside the string\nstill data');\nSELECT 2 FROM dual;\n") returns 1 statement (PlSqlBlock, lines 1..4) instead of 2 Sql statements. Mechanism: line 1 opens a single-quoted string that consume_line leaves open across the line boundary (in_string=true); on line 2 the continuation text "BEGIN inside the string" is pure string content, but looks_like_plsql_opener matches "BEGIN" and sets in_plsql=true; the genuine top-level `;` on line 3 is gated by `!in_plsql` at line 168 so it never fires, and the following SELECT is absorbed. UPDATE-with-DECLARE variant behaves identically. Fix: gate line 159 with at_top_level_at_line_start (already compute
**Suggested fix:** At crates/plsql-project/src/splitter.rs:159 change `if looks_like_plsql_opener(line_no_terminator) {` to `if at_top_level_at_line_start && looks_like_plsql_opener(line_no_terminator) {`. The at_top_level_at_line_start flag (computed at line 90 as `!in_string && !in_block_comment`) reflects whether THIS line began outside any open string/comment, so the opener keyword is only honored as genuine statement text. Verified: this fixes both the INSERT-with-BEGIN-string and UPDATE-with-DECLARE-string cases (2 statements each) while leaving all 54 existing tests green, including create_package_body_recognised_as_plsql and plsql_block_terminated_by_slash whose opener lines legitimately start at top level. Add the two regression tests asserting len()==2.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 7c2a687561), re-verified on main. Root cause: in split_script the looks_like_plsql_opener heuristic at the old line 159 was applied unconditionally, ignoring cross-line lexical state. A multi-line single-quoted string whose continuation line began with a PL/SQL opener keyword (BEGIN/DECLARE/CREATE...PROCEDURE etc.) matched and set in_plsql=true; that suppressed the genuine top-level ';' terminator (gated by !in_plsql) and merged the statement plus all following statements into one mislabeled PlSqlBlock. Fix: gate the opener chec

title: Fix mid-codepoint panic in classify_loop bare-loop slice (stmt.rs:697)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-ir/src/stmt.rs:697 — verdict=keep, severity=medium
**Impact:** A PL/SQL source file whose body-span slice begins with `FOR` immediately followed by a multibyte UTF-8 char and no ASCII space (e.g. `FORé LOOP NULL; END LOOP;`) makes plsql_ir::lower_statement_body panic; with the shipped release profile (`panic = "abort"`) and no catch_unwind anywhere in the engine/MCP path, this aborts the entire analysis run / crashes the long-running MCP server. Not a security bypass and not reachable from valid PL/SQL, but reachable from malformed/non-Latin/adversarial source, which is in a static analyzer's input domain.
**Evidence:** crates/plsql-ir/src/stmt.rs:695-697 (bare-loop fallthrough):
    let upper = text.to_ascii_uppercase();
    let body = if let Some(end_pos) = upper.rfind("END LOOP") {
        text[4..end_pos].trim().to_string()   // <- line 697, raw byte slice
Empirical reproduction (throwaway test calling the PUBLIC plsql_ir::lower_statement_body):
  thread 'repro_for_multibyte_panic' panicked at crates/plsql-ir/src/stmt.rs:697:13:
  start byte index 4 is not a char boundary; it is inside 'é' (bytes 3..5) of `FORé LOOP NULL; END LOOP;`
Dispatch confirmed by the reproduction reaching line 697: classify (stmt.rs:525-529) routes any text where starts_with_keyword(trimmed,"FOR") is true into classify_loop; starts_with_keyword (465-473) strip_prefix("FOR") then accepts any non-ASCII-alnum next char (é qualifies) → true. In classify_loop the FOR arm guard `upper.starts_with("FOR ")` (660) and WHILE guard (67
**Repro:** Triggering input X → wrong output Y, empirically reproduced: plsql_ir::lower_statement_body("FORé LOOP NULL; END LOOP;") panics at stmt.rs:697:13 ("start byte index 4 is not a char boundary; it is inside 'é' (bytes 3..5)") instead of returning a Statement vector. Verified via a temporary test in crates/plsql-ir/tests/ run with `cargo test -p plsql-ir` (compiled cleanly, test FAILED with the panic). Throwaway test removed afterward; the pre-existing untracked zz_repro_utf8.rs (09:23, prior session) was left as found.
**Suggested fix:** Replace the raw byte-index slice at line 697 with the char-safe idiom already used elsewhere in the same function: change `text[4..end_pos].trim().to_string()` to `text.get(4..end_pos).unwrap_or("").trim().to_string()`. For full consistency also harden the FOR-arm slices at line 665 (`text[4..in_p]` → `text.get(4..in_p).unwrap_or("")`) and 666 (`text[in_p + 2..loop_p]` → `text.get(in_p + 2..loop_p).unwrap_or("")`) and the WHILE-arm slice at line 683 (`text[6..loop_p]` → `text.get(6..loop_p).unwrap_or("")`), which share the identical fixed-offset bug class. Add a regression test asserting lower_statement_body("FORé LOOP NULL; END LOOP;") returns without panicking.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit a634b473a4), re-verified on main. Mid-codepoint panic family in classify_loop (crates/plsql-ir/src/stmt.rs). Primary root cause: bare-loop fallthrough `text[4..end_pos]` panicked on `FORé LOOP NULL; END LOOP;` (byte 4 inside `é`). Fixed to `text.get(4..end_pos).unwrap_or("").trim()`. Also hardened the two sibling fixed-offset slices in the FOR arm (`text[4..in_p]` -> `text.get(4..in_p)`, `text[in_p+2..loop_p]` -> `text.get(in_p+2..loop_p)`) and the WHILE arm (`text[6..loop_p]` -> `text.get(6..loop_p)`), all per the bead's sugges

title: Route invalid-identifier stub pub fn names through disambiguate_overloads' global uniqueness set
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-bindgen/src/emit.rs:335 — verdict=keep, severity=medium
**Impact:** An untrusted or upstream-buggy BindingPlan whose invalid routine name folds+indexes to exactly a legal sibling routine's verbatim Rust name makes plsql-bindgen emit two identical `pub fn` into one module (rustc E0428), so the operator's generated bindings file fails to compile instead of carrying the promised typed BINDING_INVALID_IDENTIFIER error body.
**Evidence:** emit.rs:334-357 fail-closed stub path: `if let Some(problem) = invalid_identifier_reason(package_id, r) { let stub = safe_stub_ident(rust_name, idx); ... writeln!(out, "pub fn {stub}(...)") ...}` — never consults rust_names/taken/claim. safe_stub_ident (emit.rs:293-311) folds non-[A-Za-z0-9_]->`_` and `format!("{base}_{idx}")`. emit_wrappers (emit.rs:53-77) calls `let rust_names = crate::overload::disambiguate_overloads(&plan.routines);` then concatenates per-routine output with NO final dedup pass. overload.rs:87 `let mut taken: BTreeSet<String> = BTreeSet::new();` is function-local and discarded on return; Pass A (overload.rs:95-102) emits legal singletons VERBATIM. rust_ident_problem (emit.rs:156-177) rejects "a b" (space outside [A-Za-z0-9_]) but accepts "a_b_0". Empirical run of a temp test through emit_wrappers: `EMITTED_FN_NAMES = ["a_b_0", "a_b_0"]` then `assertion failed: COLLIS
**Repro:** BindingPlan with routines [{name:"a b", parameters:[p_a:i64 IN]}, {name:"a_b_0", parameters:[p_a:i64 IN]}]. idx 0 "a b" trips rust_ident_problem (space) -> stub path -> safe_stub_ident folds to "a_b" + "_0" = "a_b_0". idx 1 "a_b_0" passes the gate (legal ident, not a keyword), is a singleton, so disambiguate_overloads Pass A emits it verbatim as "a_b_0". emit_wrappers concatenates both => two `pub fn a_b_0(...)` in one module = rustc E0428. Reproduced empirically via a throwaway test appended to the emit.rs test module: emit_wrappers(&plan(vec![mk("a b"), mk("a_b_0")])) yielded fn name set ["a_b_0","a_b_0"]; temp test then removed, `git diff --stat` shows emit.rs unchanged.
**Suggested fix:** Make the stub name consult the same global reserved-name set as the valid wrappers. Cleanest (matches existing overload_ordinal_yields_to_a_real_singleton design): have disambiguate_overloads also own the stub names — for each routine, if invalid_identifier_reason fires, compute the folded base via the safe_stub_ident logic and run it through claim(base, &mut taken) so it is reserved against every other emitted name (legal singletons are already seeded first in Pass A, giving them precedence); return that as the routine's rust_name and have emit_routine emit `pub fn {rust_name}` for the stub body. Alternatively, seed a live `&mut BTreeSet<String>` with all rust_names and pass it into emit_routine so safe_stub_ident probes/escalates (_2,_3,...) via claim() until free. Add a regression test extending two_distinct_invalid_routines_get_unique_stub_names: plan(vec![mk("a b"), mk("a_b_0")]) mu

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit fd107547cb), re-verified on main. Root-cause fix landed. The bug: emit_routine's fail-closed stub path (emit.rs) recomputed safe_stub_ident() LOCALLY and never consulted the global reserved-name set that disambiguate_overloads owns and then discards. So an invalid routine name whose fold+index stub equals a legal sibling singleton's verbatim Rust name produced two identical `pub fn` in one module (rustc E0428) — e.g. routine `"a b"` folds to stub `a_b_0`, colliding with a legal singleton `a_b_0`. Fix (the bead's primary 'cleanes

title: Seed advance_to_decl_end with depth=1 for package/type BODY so nested-routine END; does not truncate the body span
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-parser-antlr/src/lower/mod.rs:1198 — verdict=keep, severity=medium
**Impact:** On the default feature set (antlr-codegen off, the live text-scanner AST producer), a PACKAGE/TYPE BODY containing two or more nested routines gets its declaration span silently truncated at the first nested routine's END;, so every span-based IR consumer (declaration-region ownership, lineage positioning, completeness reporting) attributes the second-and-later routines' entire source range — including their DML/EXECUTE IMMEDIATE statements — to no declaration, contradicting the project's R13 "no uncertainty is silently dropped" invariant.
**Evidence:** advance_to_decl_end at crates/plsql-parser-antlr/src/lower/mod.rs:1198 walks `depth` via block_structure_step; mod.rs:1239-1240 returns `i+1` on the first `;` seen at depth==0. recover.rs:156-170 block_structure_step opens depth ONLY on BEGIN/IF/LOOP/CASE — the package/type BODY's own AS/IS…END envelope is never counted. lower_package (mod.rs:182) and lower_type (mod.rs:282) both call `advance_to_decl_end(bytes, create_start)` with no depth seed. Cargo.toml line 20: `default = []` (antlr-codegen off); lower_source is invoked by crates/plsql-mcp/src/parse_tools.rs:35/70/124. IR copies the span verbatim via make_common for PackageBody (crates/plsql-ir/src/lower.rs:99-110) and TypeSpec|TypeBody (lower.rs:139-143). Throwaway test run (now removed) under `cargo test -p plsql-parser-antlr`: `SRC_LEN=136 / DECL name=pkg start=0 end=70` and `TYP_LEN=185 / TDECL name=tb start=0 end=90`; the asser
**Repro:** lower_source("CREATE OR REPLACE PACKAGE BODY pkg AS PROCEDURE p1 IS BEGIN NULL; END; PROCEDURE p2 IS BEGIN INSERT INTO secret VALUES(1); END; END pkg;", fid) yields a single PackageBody whose span is [0..70] of 136 source bytes — ending at p1's `END;` and excluding the entire p2 routine and the trailing `END pkg;`. A TYPE BODY with two MEMBER FUNCTIONs (185 bytes) is identically truncated to [0..90] at the first member's `END;`. The claim's prose constants (len 142 / [0..72]) are slightly off; the real figures are len 136 / [0..70], but the defect and failure path are exactly as described.
**Suggested fix:** Add a depth-seeded variant `advance_to_decl_end_with_depth(bytes, create_start, 1)` and call it from lower_package/lower_type only when `is_body` is true, so the scan returns on a `;` only after the body's trailing `END[ name];` has decremented depth back to 0. Keep depth 0 for SPEC cases (no BEGIN/END envelope). The SQL*Plus `/` terminator branch (mod.rs:1247-1253) already requires depth==0 and is satisfied only after the body END, so it is unaffected. Add a regression test mirroring inner_end_if_does_not_truncate_decl_span asserting PackageBody/TypeBody span.end.offset == src.len() for a two-routine package body and a two-member type body (the existing lower_package_body test's decls.len()==1/name assertions miss this).

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 4ccce9834b), re-verified on main. Root cause: in crates/plsql-parser-antlr/src/lower/mod.rs, advance_to_decl_end seeds BEGIN..END depth at 0 and returns at the first `;` seen at depth==0. recover::block_structure_step opens depth only on BEGIN/IF/LOOP/CASE, so a PACKAGE/TYPE BODY's own `AS|IS .. END[ name];` envelope is never counted. A body with >=2 nested routines therefore truncated the declaration span at the first nested routine's END; (verified: 70 of 136 bytes for the package repro, exactly matching the bead evidence), or

title: Guard lone-'/' branch in split_script against empty buffer to stop phantom empty Statement on ';'-then-'/' idiom
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-project/src/splitter.rs:124 — verdict=keep, severity=medium
**Impact:** An external consumer of the public split_script API (linter/analyzer) feeding a normal DDL deploy script ending in the SQL*Plus re-execute idiom (`...;` then a bare `/`) gets a spurious extra Statement with raw="" and a line range (e.g. 2..2) pointing only at the terminator line, inflating statement counts and emitting a unit with no real source-span correspondence.
**Evidence:** Code on disk (splitter.rs): the lone-'/' branch pushes UNCONDITIONALLY, no empty-buffer guard:
124  if trimmed == "/" && at_top_level_at_line_start {
135      let raw = buffer.trim_end_matches('\n').to_string();
136      out.push(Statement { kind, line_start: buffer_start_line, line_end: current_line, raw, ... });
By contrast the trailing flush DOES guard:
186  let buffer_trimmed = buffer.trim_end_matches('\n');
187  if !buffer_trimmed.is_empty() { ... out.push(...) }

Empirical run (temp test added then reverted; baseline `cargo test -p plsql-project --lib` = 54 passed):
REPRO_OUT len=2 stmts=[Statement { kind: Sql, line_start: 1, line_end: 1, raw: "CREATE TABLE t (id NUMBER);", has_substitution_variable: false }, Statement { kind: Sql, line_start: 2, line_end: 2, raw: "", has_substitution_variable: false }]

API surface: `pub use splitter::{Statement, StatementKind, split_script};` at 
**Repro:** Triggering input X -> wrong output Y: split_script("CREATE TABLE t (id NUMBER);\n/\n"). Trace: line 1 "CREATE TABLE..." is not a PL/SQL opener (not in looks_like_plsql_opener), ends with top-level ';', so the `!in_plsql && ends_top_level_semi` branch at line 168 flushes statement 0 and buffer.clear()s. Line 2 "/" is not blank/comment (line 94), not a SQL*Plus command (line 103), so line 116-117 sets buffer_start_line=2 with buffer empty; the lone-'/' branch at line 124 fires (at_top_level_at_line_start true), computes raw = "".trim_end_matches('\n') = "", and unconditionally pushes a second Statement {kind:Sql, line_start:2, line_end:2, raw:""}. Result: 2 statements instead of 1 (phantom emp
**Suggested fix:** In the lone-'/' branch (splitter.rs ~124-146), after computing `let raw = buffer.trim_end_matches('\n').to_string();`, add an empty-buffer guard mirroring the trailing-flush guard at line 187: if raw.is_empty() { buffer.clear(); in_plsql = false; continue; } before constructing/pushing the Statement. Add a regression test asserting split_script("CREATE TABLE t (id NUMBER);\n/\n").len() == 1 with the single statement kind=Sql raw="CREATE TABLE t (id NUMBER);", and confirm plsql_block_terminated_by_slash still passes.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 7c2a687561), re-verified on main. Root cause: the lone-'/' branch in split_script pushed a Statement unconditionally, with no empty-buffer guard (unlike the trailing flush at the end which checks !buffer_trimmed.is_empty()). For the SQL*Plus re-execute idiom (';'-terminated statement, then a bare '/' on its own line), the ';' branch already flushed and cleared the buffer, so the '/' line computed raw="" and pushed a phantom zero-width Statement {kind:Sql, raw:"", line N..N}, inflating statement counts. Fix: after computing raw i

title: Make SubprocessPlugin::run I/O concurrent + bounded before wiring the Phase-3 plugin boundary
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/oraclemcp-core/src/plugin.rs:144 — verdict=defense-in-depth, severity=low
**Impact:** None today: SubprocessPlugin::run has no production caller and is exported scaffolding for the explicitly Phase-3-deferred out-of-process/WASM plugin boundary. If/when that boundary is wired to a live request path, a hostile or buggy plugin could wedge the calling host thread forever — either by emitting a >64KB stdout response while a >64KB request is mid-write (synchronous pipe deadlock) or simply by never exiting (no wait deadline) — violating the module's "a plugin crash is an isolated Err, never a host panic" contract.
**Evidence:** plugin.rs:142-150 (verbatim): `if let Some(mut stdin) = child.stdin.take() { let _ = stdin.write_all(line.as_bytes()); let _ = stdin.write_all(b"\n"); }` THEN `let output = child.wait_with_output()...` — the full blocking write precedes the read that drains stdout. EMPIRICAL DEADLOCK PROOF: a throwaway program mirroring this exact order (write_all 200KB to stdin, then wait_with_output; child emits 100KB to stdout before reading stdin) printed "host: starting write_all of 200000 bytes" then HUNG — "write_all COMPLETED" never printed — and `timeout 5` returned exit code 124 (timed out). NO PRODUCTION CALLER: `rg SubprocessPlugin crates/ --type rust` → only plugin.rs:118/123 (def) + plugin.rs:205/223/236/252 (tests) + lib.rs:63 (re-export); all `plugin.run(&req)` sites are tests (plugin.rs:216/231/247/259) using tiny payloads (SELECT 1 FROM dual, Value::Null). The other `.run(` hits (http.r
**Repro:** Trigger (proven mechanically, not yet reachable in product): construct a SubprocessPlugin whose program emits a >64KB stdout response before draining stdin, and call run() with a PluginRequest whose serialized JSON exceeds ~64KB (e.g. a ReadQuery with very large SQL or many binds in args). The host blocks in write_all while the child blocks writing stdout → permanent hang. Simpler trigger for the no-deadline half: any plugin that sleeps/never exits (e.g. command=["/bin/sh","-c","sleep infinity"]) makes wait_with_output() block forever. Both were behaviorally reproduced with a standalone Rust binary (exit code 124 under a 5s timeout). Impossibility-of-current-harm proof: no non-test code path
**Suggested fix:** Two changes, to land when (not before) the plugin boundary gets a real caller in Phase 3: (1) write stdin on a spawned thread (or port to tokio::process — note: tokio in this crate currently lacks the `process` feature, so add it) so the host reads stdout concurrently with writing stdin, eliminating the >64KB pipe deadlock; (2) add a wait deadline (wait-timeout crate's child.wait_timeout, or tokio::time::timeout) that kills the child and returns PluginError::Crashed("plugin timed out") so a never-exiting plugin can't hang the host. Add regression tests: a plugin that reads few stdin bytes then writes a >64KB response must Err not hang, and a never-exiting plugin must hit the deadline. Given there is no live caller yet, this is hardening for the deferred boundary rather than an urgent fix.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 4867a864a2), re-verified on main. Root-caused both halves of the SubprocessPlugin::run hang in crates/oraclemcp-core/src/plugin.rs. Fix 1 (synchronous-pipe deadlock): the request is now written on a dedicated std::thread and stdout/stderr are drained on their own threads, so a >64KB request no longer blocks in write_all against a child that is itself blocked writing a >64KB stdout response. Fix 2 (no wait deadline): added a wall-clock deadline via the wait-timeout crate (new pub field SubprocessPlugin::timeout, DEFAULT_PLUGIN_TI
