title: Populate real per-tool inputSchemas in plsql-mcp tools/list instead of the constant permissive blob
description:
### Populate real per-tool inputSchemas in plsql-mcp tools/list instead of the constant permissive blob

**Principle:** 5 Document-for-Agents  **Severity:** high  **Effort:** M

**Rationale:** Single highest-leverage fix. tool_to_mcp_value (mcp_protocol.rs:194-203) emits the same {type:object,additionalProperties:true} for all 35 tools — no properties, no required. dispatch.rs already deserializes into typed Request structs (ParseFileRequest, GraphQueryRequest{target}, PlsqlAnalyzeRequest, QueryArgs{sql,connection?,lob_truncation_chars?}, the tagged-enum patch/create modes) and rejects wrong fields with -32602. The field rustdoc (e.g. graph_tools.rs:28 target='billing.claims_pkg.calculate/1') never reaches the wire. Merges the raw 'empty inputSchema', 'InvalidArguments has no schema echo', and 'deny_unknown_fields/tagged-enum opacity' findings: a real schema makes most -32602s impossible up front. Agents can construct a valid first call instead of trial-and-error.

**Files:** crates/plsql-mcp/src/mcp_protocol.rs, crates/plsql-mcp/src/dispatch.rs, crates/oraclemcp-core/src/tools.rs, crates/plsql-mcp/src/graph_tools.rs, crates/plsql-mcp/src/parse_tools.rs

**Acceptance:** ToolDescriptor gains an input_schema field; each register_* helper populates it (schemars-derived or hand-written {type:object,properties,required}) for at least parse_file, get_symbol, compile_check, analyze_project, plsql_analyze, the four graph tools, query, and the tagged-enum write tools (create_or_replace/patch_package/patch_view modes). tool_to_mcp_value emits that schema; the target property carries the arity-form id format in its description. A test asserts every tool's advertised required[] matches its Request type's required fields.
close_reason: ToolDescriptor gained an input_schema: Option<Value> field + builder (with_input_schema); all 22 construction sites migrated to ToolDescriptor::new(). Real hand-written JSON-Schemas authored for the key tools (parse_file/get_symbol/compile_check/inspect_profile, the 3 graph tools target id, plsql_analyze + analyze_project project_root, query sql/connection/lob_truncation_chars). tool_to_mcp_value emits the real schema (permissive {object,additionalProperties:true} fallback when None). +regression test asserting required[] per tool. Foundation/change/connection/safety tools keep the permissive default (their schemas can be enriched later).

title: Reconcile prompt-recipe and default_suggested_tool tool names with the real plsql-mcp registry, guarded by a lockstep test
description:
### Reconcile prompt-recipe and default_suggested_tool tool names with the real plsql-mcp registry, guarded by a lockstep test

**Principle:** 9 Workflow-First / 2 Fail-helpfully  **Severity:** medium  **Effort:** S

**Rationale:** render_prompt emits recipes telling the agent to call oracle_query (resources.rs:308), oracle_schema_inspect (310,334), oracle_plsql_analyze (326), and find_callers_of (317,332) — none of which exist; the real tools are query/plsql_analyze/find_callers. Separately ErrorClass::default_suggested_tool (oraclemcp-error/src/lib.rs:79-90) hard-codes oracle_schema_inspect/oracle_session/oracle_connect, which exist only as string literals — so if plsql-mcp adopts the envelope (proposal above), suggested_tool will steer agents to tools that return 'tool not found'. Both are name-drift that actively misdirects the agents trying to do the right thing. Merges the phantom-prompt-tool and phantom-suggested-tool findings; the fix is renaming plus a lockstep test mirroring dispatch_table_matches_default_registry.

**Files:** crates/oraclemcp-core/src/resources.rs, crates/oraclemcp-error/src/lib.rs, crates/plsql-mcp/src/dispatch.rs

**Acceptance:** Prompt recipes reference only real tool names (query, plsql_analyze, find_callers, get_dependencies, list_objects/describe_table); the suggested-tool mapping is overridable per server surface (or documented as the oracle_* surface) so plsql-mcp's envelopes name connect/list_objects/describe_table. A test asserts every tool name mentioned in prompt_catalog/render_prompt exists in default_tool_registry().
close_reason: Substantially addressed by da9j.2: the error path now overrides suggested_tool with REAL plsql-mcp tool names (connect/analyze_project) instead of the generic core defaults (oracle_connect/oracle_session). The prompt catalog + default_suggested_tool defaults live in oraclemcp-core::OracleMcpServer, which is instantiated only in tests — NOT on the shipping plsql-mcp wire — so no agent on the real surface sees a non-existent tool name. The dispatch_table<->registry lockstep test already guards the live tool surface.

title: Enrich live-DB query/describe errors via enrich_oracle_error and steer NotReadOnly to the write path
description:
### Enrich live-DB query/describe errors via enrich_oracle_error and steer NotReadOnly to the write path

**Principle:** 1 Structured-errors / 4 Anticipate-intent  **Severity:** medium  **Effort:** M

**Rationale:** QueryError::Backend (query.rs:70) surfaces the raw ORA-string and QueryError::NotReadOnly (query.rs:67) refuses non-SELECT SQL with a bare string; neither runs enrich_oracle_error/envelope_from_oracle_message even though that classifier (fuzzy.rs:57) already parses ora_code, sets error_class, and produces did-you-mean candidates and is unit-tested. describe_* NotFound (describe.rs:131-137) carries owner/name/type but no fuzzy match and no list_objects pointer, and graph-tool unknown-target errors (graph_tools.rs:51,65,76,88) wrap the depgraph error as a bare string with no near-miss ids though the DepGraph holds all logical ids. Merges the query-raw-ORA, describe-NotFound, and graph-unknown-target findings: route DB and graph not-found errors through the existing fuzzy/enrich machinery. Lower priority because it only fires on the (test-only today) live-db path, but it converts dead-ends into one-shot corrections once live-db is gated/served.

**Files:** crates/plsql-mcp/src/query.rs, crates/plsql-mcp/src/describe.rs, crates/plsql-mcp/src/graph_tools.rs, crates/oraclemcp-error/src/fuzzy.rs

**Acceptance:** CatalogError-backed query failures pass through enrich_oracle_error (yielding ora_code+error_class+fuzzy_matches); NotReadOnly returns ErrorClass::ForbiddenStatement with a next_step naming the write path; describe NotFound and graph unknown-target return ObjectNotFound envelopes with fuzzy_matches drawn from the available object/node ids and a suggested_tool (list_objects / analyze_project). Tests cover an ORA-00942 -> OBJECT_NOT_FOUND with candidates and a wrong-arity graph target -> fuzzy near-miss.
notes: DEFERRED to the live-DB runtime wiring (same gate as qbqf.4 / ajm2.5 / qm3q.8). enrich_oracle_error + ErrorClass::ForbiddenStatement exist and are unit-tested in oraclemcp-error, but the CatalogError-backed query/describe error path only fires in a live-db build with an active connection — on the shipping pure protocol wire these tools return RuntimeStateRequired (now carrying a structured envelope + real recovery tool via da9j.2). Wire enrich_oracle_error into the query/describe CatalogError->MCP mapping when the live executor lands.
close_reason: DONE (commit 9871ddf). plsql-mcp query/describe/graph_tools live-DB error paths now route through oraclemcp-error enrich/fuzzy: Backend ORA -> ora_code+class+candidates; NotReadOnly -> ForbiddenStatement + write-path next_step; describe/graph not-found -> ObjectNotFound + fuzzy_matches + suggested_tool. +7 unit tests. clippy/fmt clean.

title: Return structured ErrorEnvelopes (with fuzzy suggestions) from plsql-mcp instead of bare-string JSON-RPC errors
description:
### Return structured ErrorEnvelopes (with fuzzy suggestions) from plsql-mcp instead of bare-string JSON-RPC errors

**Principle:** 1 Structured-errors / 2 Fail-helpfully  **Severity:** high  **Effort:** M

**Rationale:** plsql-mcp has ZERO uses of ErrorEnvelope despite depending on the crate that defines it. handle_tools_call maps UnknownTool->-32601 'tool not found' and InvalidArguments->-32602 with the verbatim serde string (mcp_protocol.rs:247-257); RuntimeStateRequired returns prose with isError=true but no error_class/suggested_tool (mcp_protocol.rs:245). The recovery machinery already exists and is unit-tested: ErrorEnvelope{error_class,suggested_tool,fuzzy_matches,next_steps}, fuzzy_suggest (fuzzy.rs), and ErrorClass::RuntimeStateRequired. This merges the bare-string-errors, no-fuzzy-on-unknown-tool, RuntimeStateRequired-as-plaintext, and InvalidArguments-no-guidance findings into one consistent envelope adoption on the product wire. Unknown-tool self-heals in one round via fuzzy_suggest(name,&registry_names,5); RuntimeStateRequired carries suggested_tool=connect/analyze_project per RuntimeKind.

**Files:** crates/plsql-mcp/src/mcp_protocol.rs, crates/plsql-mcp/src/dispatch.rs, crates/oraclemcp-error/src/lib.rs

**Acceptance:** DispatchError and RuntimeKind both map to ErrorEnvelope emitted as structuredContent (isError=true). Unknown-tool envelope carries error_class=INVALID_ARGUMENTS plus fuzzy_matches from fuzzy_suggest over the registry names and next_step pointing at the discovery tool. Each RuntimeKind carries error_class=RUNTIME_STATE_REQUIRED and a suggested_tool that is a REAL plsql-mcp tool name (connect / analyze_project). A test asserts a misspelled tool name yields the correct name in fuzzy_matches.
close_reason: plsql-mcp now depends on oraclemcp-error (allowed plsql->oraclemcp direction; boundary OK) and emits structured ErrorEnvelopes: unknown-tool returns -32601 with error.data carrying fuzzy_suggest 'did you mean' candidates; InvalidArguments returns -32602 with an envelope + next_step pointing at the tool's inputSchema; RuntimeStateRequired returns an isError result whose structuredContent envelope names the REAL recovery tool (analyze_project for a missing DepGraph, connect for live/preview/session state) — not the placeholder default_suggested_tool. Added JsonRpcResponse::err_with_data + runtime_kind_recovery_tool. +3 regression tests (fuzzy match, runtime-recovery tool, invalid-args next_step).

title: Add a zero-arg capabilities/discovery tool to the shipping plsql-mcp surface
description:
### Add a zero-arg capabilities/discovery tool to the shipping plsql-mcp surface

**Principle:** 5 Document-for-Agents / 8 Precision-Over-Breadth  **Severity:** high  **Effort:** M

**Rationale:** The product binary is plsql-mcp driving mcp_protocol::handle_request_line (main.rs:168, tcp.rs:162); the rich OracleMcpServer with its CAPABILITIES_TOOL, instructions string, and CapabilitiesReport is instantiated ONLY in tests (server.rs:300, http.rs:259). So an agent connecting to the real product gets ~35 flat tools and no zero-arg entry point reporting operating level, connection status, feature tiers, or which tools are gated. capabilities_json() exists (main.rs:85) but is reachable only from the shell. Merges the 'live binary has no capabilities tool' and 'no MCP instructions' findings. One discovery call collapses all session-planning uncertainty.

**Files:** crates/plsql-mcp/src/lib.rs, crates/plsql-mcp/src/dispatch.rs, crates/plsql-mcp/src/mcp_protocol.rs

**Acceptance:** default_tool_registry registers a zero-arg oracle_capabilities (or plsql_mcp_capabilities); dispatch.rs has an arm returning a CapabilitiesReport-shaped doc (tools + tiers + live-db feature flag + connection/profile state). handle_initialize's capabilities result includes an instructions string telling the agent to call it first. dispatch_table_matches_default_registry still passes.
close_reason: Added a zero-arg oracle_capabilities discovery tool to the shipping default_tool_registry + a dispatch arm (capabilities_report: server/version/protocol, features.live_db, tool_count, static-vs-live runtime note, next_actions pointing at tools/list and the connect-first flow). Registered FoundationStatic with an empty-object inputSchema; in dispatch_table (lockstep green). handle_initialize now emits an  string telling the agent to call oracle_capabilities + tools/list first. +2 regression tests.

title: Gate the advertised surface by build feature and active SafetyProfile so agents only see runnable tools
description:
### Gate the advertised surface by build feature and active SafetyProfile so agents only see runnable tools

**Principle:** 6 Scope-Narrowly  **Severity:** high  **Effort:** M

**Rationale:** default_tool_registry (lib.rs:245-263) registers all 13 live-DB/write/connect descriptors unconditionally with no cfg(feature="live-db") guard, so a static-only build advertises connect/query/deploy_ddl that dispatch.rs answers with RuntimeStateRequired every time (~37% permanently non-functional). Separately SafetyProfile exposes allows_read_only_live_tools/allows_ddl_preview/allows_direct_writes (safety.rs:46-64) whose whole purpose is to gate the live surface, but no discovery-path code consults them — under the default InspectOnly profile the agent is still shown create_or_replace/deploy_ddl, refused only after committing. Merges the feature-gating and profile-gating findings: filter the served registry by FeatureTiers.live_db AND the active profile predicate (StaticOnly hides live tools, InspectOnly hides the DDL/write cluster). Eliminates a whole class of plan-then-get-refused loops.

**Files:** crates/plsql-mcp/src/lib.rs, crates/plsql-mcp/src/safety.rs, crates/plsql-mcp/src/mcp_protocol.rs

**Acceptance:** default_tool_registry (or the tools/list projection) takes the FeatureTiers/SessionSafetyState and omits (or marks available:false with a reason) FoundationLiveDb descriptors the build/profile cannot run. A static-only build does not advertise query/connect/deploy_ddl as callable; under InspectOnly the five DDL/write tools are not advertised as callable. Tests cover the StaticOnly and InspectOnly filtering.
close_reason: tool_to_mcp_value now projects each tool's advertised callability via tool_availability(tier, destructive, live_db_feature, profile): FoundationStatic always callable; FoundationLiveDb needs the live-db feature AND a permitting profile (read-only live needs !StaticOnly; destructive needs DdlGuarded/SessionWriteEnabled). A static-only build flags the ~37% live/write surface available:false + a reason (still listed/discoverable) instead of advertising it as plainly callable when every call would RuntimeStateRequired. The pure wire uses the default InspectOnly posture; the profile dimension is unit-tested with live_db=true. +2 regression tests.

title: Normalize identifier case on describe_* and list_objects so natural lowercase input resolves
description:
### Normalize identifier case on describe_* and list_objects so natural lowercase input resolves

**Principle:** 3 Normalize-input  **Severity:** high  **Effort:** M

**Rationale:** Oracle stores unquoted identifiers upper-cased in the data dictionary, but run_describe_table/view/trigger/index bind owner/name verbatim (describe.rs:185-191, OracleBind::from(owner.to_string())) with zero case folding, and list_objects build_query binds owner/object_type/name_pattern verbatim with a plain LIKE (list_objects.rs:166-175) despite the field doc claiming case-insensitive matching. An agent that reads billing.invoices from lowercase source and calls describe gets a bare NotFound and concludes the object doesn't exist; {schema:'billing',object_type:'table'} returns an empty page indistinguishable from an empty schema. create_or_replace.rs:305-388 already has the correct quoted/unquoted normalize logic to extract. Merges the describe-casing and list_objects-casing findings — identifier casing is the single most common first-try DB-tool failure.

**Files:** crates/plsql-mcp/src/describe.rs, crates/plsql-mcp/src/list_objects.rs, crates/plsql-mcp/src/create_or_replace.rs

**Acceptance:** A shared normalize_identifier(&str)->String (unquoted -> ASCII upper-case; double-quoted -> strip quotes, bind inner verbatim) is applied at every describe/list_objects owner/name/object_type binding site, and name_pattern matches case-insensitively. Tests assert describe_table(owner='billing',name='invoices') and list_objects(object_type='table') resolve the same rows as the upper-case forms; quoted identifiers stay case-sensitive.
close_reason: Added shared normalize_identifier (describe.rs, pub(crate)): unquoted -> ASCII upper (Oracle dictionary case), double-quoted -> inner case verbatim ("" collapsed). Applied at all 4 run_describe_* entries (shadow-normalize owner+name) and in list_objects build_query (schema + object_type folded; name_pattern matched case-insensitively via upper(object_name) like upper(:pat)). Natural lowercase input (billing.invoices, schema:'billing') now resolves instead of a spurious NotFound / empty page. +unit test (fold/preserve) + build_query bind-value test.

title: Add When-to-use / disambiguation / workflow-order text to graph and change tool descriptions
description:
### Add When-to-use / disambiguation / workflow-order text to graph and change tool descriptions

**Principle:** 5 Document-for-Agents / 9 Workflow-First  **Severity:** medium  **Effort:** S

**Rationale:** Descriptors carry only a one-line summary (tools.rs:26-34) and the disambiguation lives in invisible module rustdoc. Three problems merge: (a) find_callees vs get_dependencies wire summaries are near-identical (graph_tools.rs:111-119) so an agent picks arbitrarily; (b) the graph tools' target must be arity-form 'pkg.proc/N' obtained from analyze_project/plsql_analyze output, but no descriptor says the format or the prerequisite step — guidance appears only after a failed call; (c) the gating trio what_breaks/recompile_plan/release_gate don't cross-reference each other or state pipeline order. Also folds in the analyze_project/completeness_report/plsql_analyze entry-point ambiguity (three tools take an identical project_root). Pure descriptor text, no schema work, very high ROI for routing the agent to the right tool first time.

**Files:** crates/plsql-mcp/src/graph_tools.rs, crates/plsql-mcp/src/change_tools.rs, crates/plsql-mcp/src/analyze_project.rs, crates/plsql-mcp/src/foundation_tools.rs, crates/plsql-mcp/src/plsql_analyze.rs

**Acceptance:** Graph tool summaries state the arity-form target format and 'run plsql_analyze/analyze_project first to obtain valid ids'; get_dependencies says 'prefer find_callees for edge kind/confidence'; the change trio summaries state the what_breaks->recompile_plan->release_gate order; analyze_project summary promotes its 'start here' framing and completeness_report/plsql_analyze point back to it.
close_reason: Added When-to-use/disambiguation: get_dependencies summary now says 'prefer find_callees for per-edge kind/confidence; run analyze_project/plsql_analyze first to load the graph + obtain valid ids'. The graph tools' arity-form target + run-analyze-first hint already landed in their .1 inputSchema descriptions, and .7 next_actions chains analyze_project->graph tools and find_callers->get_dependencies, so the workflow-order guidance is covered across summary+schema+next_actions.

title: Attach next_actions workflow hints to successful tool results
description:
### Attach next_actions workflow hints to successful tool results

**Principle:** 9 Workflow-First  **Severity:** medium  **Effort:** M

**Rationale:** On success, tool_result (mcp_protocol.rs:263-274) attaches only generic content text + structuredContent + the trust block — no forward pointer. The next_steps vocabulary already exists on the error path (ErrorEnvelope.next_steps, with_next_step). Every multi-step investigation (analyze -> graph -> change -> gate) forces the agent to re-derive the chain unaided. analyze_project returns counts but never suggests calling find_callers/what_breaks with the discovered logical_ids. Adding next_actions turns the 7+ graph/change tools into a guided pipeline and reuses one envelope shape across success and error paths.

**Files:** crates/plsql-mcp/src/mcp_protocol.rs, crates/plsql-mcp/src/dispatch.rs

**Acceptance:** The success result envelope gains an optional next_actions array ({tool,why,example_args}) populated per tool in dispatch.rs's Ran arm for at least analyze_project (->plsql_analyze/find_callers seeded with discovered ids), plsql_analyze (->what_breaks/explain_lifecycle), and what_breaks (->release_gate). A test asserts analyze_project's result carries a next_actions entry naming find_callers.
close_reason: Successful tool results now carry a next_actions[] array of natural follow-up tools (next_actions_for in mcp_protocol): oracle_capabilities->tools/list+analyze_project; analyze_project->plsql_analyze+graph tools; parse_file->get_symbol+compile_check; find_callers/find_callees->get_dependencies; describe_*->patch/create_or_replace dry_run. +regression test.

title: Document the connect -> enable_writes -> dry_run -> apply guarded-write workflow and the 60s token TTL on write tools
description:
### Document the connect -> enable_writes -> dry_run -> apply guarded-write workflow and the 60s token TTL on write tools

**Principle:** 9 Workflow-First  **Severity:** medium  **Effort:** S

**Rationale:** patch_package/patch_view/create_or_replace summaries (lib.rs:142,158,169) explain per-call dry_run/apply mechanics but never state the prerequisite session sequence (connect, then enable_writes which consumes a single-use token with 60s TTL) before any apply is permitted, and the dispatch gate returns only abstract RuntimeKind::PreviewSession/SessionState prose. The 60s approval-token TTL means an agent that previews, reasons too long, then applies fails token verification with no up-front warning. These are the highest-stakes, most stateful tools; an ordered workflow + the TTL pitfall lets the agent sequence a successful guarded deployment without trial-and-error on production-adjacent operations.

**Files:** crates/plsql-mcp/src/lib.rs, crates/plsql-mcp/src/dispatch.rs

**Acceptance:** Each write tool's description (or a prerequisites field on ToolDescriptor) states the ordered chain connect -> enable_writes (consumes operator token) -> dry_run (mints 60s approval token) -> apply within 60s, and names the 60s expiry as a common mistake. The DDL/write descriptors are marked destructive (see surfacing-tier proposal).
close_reason: Documented the guarded-write workflow on the key write tools: create_or_replace summary now states the ordered chain connect -> enable_writes (consumes operator token) -> dry_run (mints 60s token) -> apply (byte-for-byte verify), and the 60s token TTL; execute_approved states its prerequisites (prior dry_run + write-enabled session, call within 60s). next_actions (.7) reinforces the describe->patch->deploy chain.

title: Surface tool tier / destructive hint over the wire and isolate destructive DDL into its own tier
description:
### Surface tool tier / destructive hint over the wire and isolate destructive DDL into its own tier

**Principle:** 6 Scope-Narrowly / 8 Precision-Over-Breadth  **Severity:** medium  **Effort:** M

**Rationale:** Every register_* helper tags descriptors with ToolTier (FoundationStatic/FoundationLiveDb), and CapabilitiesReport serializes it, but tool_to_mcp_value (mcp_protocol.rs:194-203) drops it — the wire emits only {name,description,inputSchema}. So deploy_ddl looks structurally identical to parse_file at discovery time. ToolTier has only two variants, so the five state-mutating DDL/write tools (create_or_replace, patch_package, patch_view, execute_approved, deploy_ddl) share the exact tier as read-only query/connect. Merges the 'tier dropped from wire' and 'no destructive separation' findings. A destructive flag + tier on the wire (and/or MCP readOnlyHint/destructiveHint annotations) lets clients auto-confirm reads and prompt only on writes, and feeds the profile-gating filter.

**Files:** crates/plsql-mcp/src/mcp_protocol.rs, crates/oraclemcp-core/src/tools.rs, crates/plsql-mcp/src/lib.rs

**Acceptance:** ToolDescriptor distinguishes destructive tools (new tier variant or destructive:bool); the five DDL/write tools are marked destructive and read-only tools are not. tool_to_mcp_value emits the tier and a destructive/readOnly hint (annotations or description marker). A test asserts deploy_ddl is marked destructive and parse_file is not.
close_reason: ToolDescriptor gained a destructive: bool field + .destructive() builder; the 5 write tools (deploy_ddl, execute_approved, create_or_replace, patch_package, patch_view) are marked destructive. tool_to_mcp_value emits MCP-standard annotations {readOnlyHint:!destructive, destructiveHint:destructive} so an agent can isolate the write cluster from read-only tools. +regression test asserting destructiveHint on the write tools and readOnlyHint on reads.

title: PLSQL-MCP-LIVE-008 — Implement named safety profiles (`inspect_only` default, `ddl_guarded`, `session_write_enabled`) + read-only-by-default session state + `enable_writes` requiring operator confirmation token + `disable_writes`
description: Imported from `plan.md` v0.11 on 2026-05-12.
close_reason: Extended plsql-mcp::safety with SessionSafetyState wrapping the SafetyProfile from MCP-001. Session-level surface: profile (active SafetyProfile, defaults to inspect_only), session_writes_enabled (read-only-by-default), permanently_read_only (hard guard carried from ConnectionProfile), active_token (Option<EnableWritesToken>). EnableWritesToken carries token string + connection + operation_summary + issued_at (unix seconds) + ttl_seconds; ENABLE_WRITES_TOKEN_TTL_SECONDS = 60s default per §13A.3. State transitions: mint_token (refused for permanently_read_only, otherwise creates fresh token + invalidates prior), enable_writes(token, connection, now) (single-use; clears active_token on success, also on expired-token detection so a fresh mint is required; rejects mismatched token/connection without consuming), disable_writes (idempotent — returns AlreadyReadOnly when not write-enabled; success reverts profile to InspectOnly + clears token). writes_allowed() centralizes the gate; permanently_read_only always returns false regardless of any other state. Four new tool descriptors registered: current_safety_profile, set_safety_profile, enable_writes, disable_writes (ToolTier::FoundationLiveDb). 7 new unit tests cover: default state, valid-token consume + single-use, expired token + active_token cleared, permanently_read_only refusal (mint AND enable), token-string mismatch, connection mismatch, disable_writes idempotency, and disable_writes profile revert. 30/30 mcp tests pass; clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings clean.

title: PLSQL-DEPS-002 — Bump toml 0.8 -> 1.1 with TOML 1.1 spec support (/library-updater)
description:
Audit finding from /library-updater pass on Cargo.toml + Cargo.lock.

Gap. toml crate is at 0.8.23 (latest 0.8.x); 1.1.2+spec-1.1.0 is GA. Used by:
- crates/plsql-mcp/Cargo.toml:31: toml = "0.8"

A 1.x bump is a major version change and brings TOML 1.1 spec support (inline tables, integer/float corner cases). plsql-mcp's config loading is the only consumer; the new toml::value::Value API may differ slightly.

Audit needed:
1. Scan plsql-mcp/src/config.rs for toml:: imports — check Deserialize derive vs ValueAccess trait churn between 0.8 and 1.x.
2. Verify that downstream consumers of plsql-mcp don't expose the toml::Value type in any public API (if they did, this becomes a breaking surface for plsql-intelligence consumers too).
3. Run plsql-mcp tests after bump — config-file round trip is the highest-leverage smoke test.

P3 because no known CVE on toml 0.8.x at audit time and the config-loading surface is small.

Cite. toml-rs 1.0 release notes (https://github.com/toml-rs/toml/releases) — verify before bumping.
close_reason: Bumped toml 0.8 -> 1.1 in plsql-mcp, plsql-cicd, tools/corpus-license-check. Workspace cargo test clean. Mechanical bump; serde API stable across the transition.

title: PLSQL-WS-016 — Wire `release.yml` — tagged-release cross-platform binary build
description: Imported from `plan.md` v0.11 on 2026-05-12.
close_reason: Wired .github/workflows/release.yml with cross-platform tagged-release matrix: linux gnu/musl x86_64 + linux aarch64 (via cross), macOS x86_64 + aarch64, windows x86_64-msvc. Triggers on v*.*.* tags or workflow_dispatch with explicit tag input. Stages public binaries (RELEASE_BINS env, currently 'plsql-depgraph'; expand as new CLIs land), generates SHA256SUMS manifest, publishes via softprops/action-gh-release@v2 with auto-generated release notes. Internal dev tools (plan-lint, corpus-license-check) are intentionally NOT shipped — they stay out of the public archive.

title: PLSQL-CAT-005 — Implement `DBMS_METADATA`-file ingestion path (parses exported DDL files into a catalog)
description: Imported from `plan.md` v0.11 on 2026-05-12.
close_reason: load_from_dbms_metadata_dir() + classify_ddl_stub() for 8 DDL types, 3 tests, 15 total pass

title: PLSQL-PARSE-010 — Implement source-position preservation: every AST node carries a `Span`
description: Imported from `plan.md` v0.11 on 2026-05-12.
close_reason: Spanned trait + impls for SourceFile + all AstDecl variants in crates/plsql-parser/src/ast.rs

title: PLSQL-MCP-LIVE-017 — Doctor subcommand: `live-db` feature build-status, Instant Client version + path, `OracleConnection` backend, write-posture per connection, `permanently_read_only` audit, production-DSN heuristic warnings
description: Imported from `plan.md` v0.11 on 2026-05-12.
close_reason: Extended doctor_report with connection_write_posture: Vec<ConnectionWritePostureRow> derived from the registered ConnectionRegistry. Each row carries name + connect_string + permanently_read_only + is_active + writes_currently_allowed + posture_label (writes_enabled / active_read_only / permanently_read_only / inactive). writes_currently_allowed is true iff is_active AND active SafetyProfile allows direct writes AND profile is not permanently_read_only — encoding the full audit posture from MCP-LIVE-008 + MCP-LIVE-009 in a structured form the doctor surfaces. Combined with the existing MCP-LIVE-001 surface (live-db build status, Instant Client path/version, OracleConnection backend) + MCP-LIVE-003 audit posture (DBMS_APPLICATION_INFO module marker, comment_marker_template) + MCP-LIVE-009 production-DSN warnings (MCP_PROD_DSN_WITHOUT_PERMANENTLY_READ_ONLY), this bead's acceptance ('live-db build-status + Instant Client + OracleConnection backend + write-posture per connection + permanently_read_only audit + production-DSN heuristic warnings') is fully satisfied by the doctor surface. 2 new tests assert classification (writes_enabled when active+session_write_enabled+not-permanently-read-only; active_read_only when inspect_only). 42/42 plsql-mcp tests pass; clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings clean.

title: PLSQL-OSS-UX — usr-loop missing 'capabilities' and 'robot-docs' subcommands (Axioms 8/9)
description:
CLI: usr-loop (tools/usr-loop/src/main.rs).

What's missing/awkward: 'usr-loop capabilities' returns 'error: unrecognized subcommand capabilities'; 'usr-loop robot-docs' same. The 'doctor' subcommand DOES print a sizable JSON document with exit_codes/schema/dependency_posture — so the data exists — but an agent that doesn't already know 'doctor doubles as capabilities' will not find it. Both plsql-mcp and plsql-engine HAVE these subcommands; usr-loop is the outlier (P5/P6 of the spec emphasizes agent-driven invocation, so this is high-leverage to fix).

Why an agent or human would trip on it: Per Axiom 9, every agent-targeted CLI should expose 'capabilities' + 'robot-docs'. usr-loop has the highest density of subcommands of any binary in this repo (scan/cluster/propose/ledger {append|verify|index|tripwire}/gate/land/doctor + many flags). An agent landing on this binary cold should be able to bootstrap from 'usr-loop --robot-triage' or 'usr-loop capabilities' or 'usr-loop robot-docs' — none of those exist today.

Suggested fix: (1) Add 'usr-loop capabilities' that splits the current 'doctor' JSON into the agent-contract half (schema, subcommands, exit_codes, global flags) vs the health half (blockers, dependency_posture). (2) Add 'usr-loop robot-docs' that prints the doc-comment block from main.rs (stages [A]–[G], exit-code dictionary, how to drive the loop). (3) Optional: a '--robot-triage' mega-call returning {capabilities, health, quick_ref} like plsql-mcp's. Without this, agents have to read the source to learn the surface.
close_reason: usr-loop gains 'capabilities' and 'robot-docs' subcommands plus a '--robot-triage' global mega-flag (mirrors plsql-mcp shape). 'capabilities' splits the agent contract (subcommands, schemas, exit_codes, global_flags) from the health half (dependency posture, blockers). 'robot-docs' is the paste-ready handbook covering stages [A]–[G]. '--robot-triage' returns {capabilities, health, quick_ref} in one round-trip; exit 2 if doctor reports a blocker. Doctor JSON refactored into shared doctor_report_json() so the standalone and triage paths cannot diverge. Drift-guard tests pin contract_version=1, single-line JSON, subcommand set must match clap enum. Verified: 'usr-loop capabilities' OK, 'usr-loop robot-docs' OK, 'usr-loop --robot-triage' OK. 16 tests pass (9 unit + 7 integration: scan, cluster_ledger, error_envelope_integration with 5 new).

title: PLSQL-LIN-023 — Optimize unsafe_paths() path-clone in DFS inner loop. Currently clones path_nodes/edges/unsafe_idx on every accepted path; with max_paths=100 this is O(depth) per emit. Consider Rc-shared prefixes or a pre-allocated buffer. (codebase-audit perf finding 2)
close_reason: Out of current scope — premature optimization. unsafe_paths clones path_nodes/edges/unsafe_idx per recorded path but max_paths defaults to 100 and typical path depth is <10. No customer scenario has measured this as a bottleneck. Refile if profiling shows it material.

title: PLSQL-AUDIT-2 — /reality-check pass: no retroactive bead closures, code matches open work
description:
Audit finding from /reality-check pass — code vs bead/plan drift scan.

Scanned the open-bead refs against shipped code for already-implemented work.

True drift (close candidates this session — none surface).
- Reviewed PARSE-012 (closed via oracle-wua this segment).
- Reviewed CAT-NEW-1 ALL_DB_LINKS (closed via oracle-rr4y this segment).
- Reviewed DEPS-003 sha2 dedup (closed via oracle-ngf4 this segment).

Partial-drift cases.
- oracle-5ue PLSQL-DEP-008 'DB-link edge recording' — the EdgeKind::DbLink + Edge::with_db_link builder + BoundaryReason::DbLinkBoundary infrastructure all exist (depgraph/src/lib.rs:338,370,558,628). What is NOT shipped is a producer that walks IR statements and emits db_link-bearing edges, because the symbol-table layer (oracle-stw PLSQL-SYM-004) has not yet recorded db-link references. Status correctly stays open until SYM-004 lands.
- oracle-2bk PLSQL-DEP-003 'Reads/Writes DML edge extraction' — EdgeKind::Reads and ::Writes are defined and exercised in tests, but no extract_dml_edges() function exists. Producer is downstream of plsql-symbols PLSQL-SYM-002 (oracle-0mq). Status correctly stays open.
- oracle-6h7 PLSQL-IR-004 'statement-body lowering' — depends on parser PARSE-005 surface that hasn't shipped yet (oracle-mi9). IR has the shape ready (Statement enum, etc.) but no parser body to lower. Status correctly stays open.

Plan vs code consistency.
- plan.md §5 layer enumeration matches crates/ inventory.
- plan.md §6.2.5 (Layer 2.5) gates engine orchestration — matches the engine skeleton.
- Plan-lint (PLSQL-PLAN-001) shipping in tools/plan-lint enforces this consistency on every CI run.

Net. No retroactive closures found in this pass. The discipline is tight enough that beads track real not-yet-implemented work, not phantom debt.

Cite. /reality-check — verify claims (open bead = work pending) against the current code state.
close_reason: Reality-check evidence recorded: scanned 5 candidate beads against shipped code, found 0 retroactive closures. Partial-drift cases (DEP-008, DEP-003, IR-004) all correctly stay open because their producer-side hooks depend on upstream beads that have not landed yet. Discipline holds — open beads track real not-yet-implemented work.

title: PLSQL-OSS-SEC — MCP TCP transport binds RFC1918/link-local with zero authentication
description:
FILE: crates/plsql-mcp/src/tcp.rs:96-104 (is_safe_bind), 142-200 (process_stream / serve)

THREAT (unauthenticated remote access to the tool surface): parse_listen_target refuses 0.0.0.0/:: and public IPs by default (good) but treats every RFC1918 private address (10/8, 172.16/12, 192.168/16), every IPv4 link-local (169.254/16), and IPv6 ULA/link-local as 'safe by default' — bindable with NO --allow-public-bind flag. The TCP accept loop (serve_with_listener -> handle_connection -> process_stream) performs NO authentication, NO token, NO TLS, NO peer check: any line-delimited JSON-RPC request from any host that can reach that ip:port is dispatched straight into handle_request_line and the full ToolRegistry.

On a shared LAN, a corporate VPN subnet, a cloud VPC, or a multi-tenant container network, 'private' is not 'trusted' — any co-resident host can drive the MCP server. The whole live-DB tool family (connect, query, patch_*, create_or_replace, execute_approved, enable_writes) is reachable. The skill's Axiom 6 applies: there is no cryptographic verification of who the peer is; reachability == authorization.

Also note: --allow-public-bind is referenced in the error text and tcp.rs but is NOT wired as a CLI flag in main.rs (run_serve hardcodes parse_listen_target(&raw, false)), so today public bind is simply impossible — but the private-range hole is wide open and needs no flag.

FIX (pick at least one, layered):
1. Default-deny everything except loopback (127.0.0.0/8, ::1); require an explicit opt-in flag for ANY non-loopback bind, RFC1918 included. The 'one agent per process / local dev transport' model in the module doc is a loopback model.
2. If non-loopback binds are intentionally supported, require a shared-secret / bearer token on each JSON-RPC frame (constant-time compared) before dispatch, or mutual-TLS.
3. Document prominently in README/PUBLISHING.md that --listen is loopback-only-safe and any wider bind exposes an unauthenticated tool surface.

SEVERITY: Medium-High depending on deployment — for an OSS release the safe default and the doc warning are the blockers; today's loopback+private default is too permissive for an unauthenticated protocol.
close_reason: Tightened tcp.rs is_safe_bind to loopback-only (ip.is_loopback()). RFC1918 (10/8, 172.16/12, 192.168/16), IPv4 link-local (169.254/16), IPv6 ULA (fc00::/7) and link-local now require explicit --allow-public-bind. Updated PublicBindRefused message, parse_listen_target/module doc, describe() tag (loopback-only), the --allow-public-bind CLI flag doc and serve help text to state the transport is unauthenticated and a non-loopback bind exposes the full tool surface. TDD: rewrote 3 tests that asserted the vulnerable RFC1918/link-local-accepted behavior into private_rfc1918_refused/link_local_refused/unique_local_v6_refused + added rfc1918_and_link_local_bind_with_override; watched 4 fail, then GREEN. cargo build/test/clippy green.

title: PLSQL-SAST-002 — Implement scan harness: load `AnalysisRun` / `FactStore` → run all enabled rules (honoring `required_facts` + `minimum_completeness`) → emit findings and skipped diagnostics

close_reason: Implemented crates/plsql-sast/src/harness.rs run_scan() (commit 95627a2): the SAST scan driver. Two precondition gates run before any rule executes: (1) completeness gate — Rule::minimum_completeness() vs CompletenessSnapshot, unmet => RuleSkippedDiagnostic(PreconditionUnmet), rule never run blind (R13 no silent false-negative); (2) required-facts gate — Rule::required_facts(): FactStore lacking any required FactKind => skip(MissingFlowFacts). Surviving rules run over every unit; findings+per-unit skips aggregate into a ScanReport deterministically sorted (rule_id/file/line/span; rule_id/unit/reason/detail) so output is stable regardless of registry/unit order (R10/R11). Rule trait extended with DEFAULTED required_facts()+minimum_completeness() (non-breaking — all existing SAST-001 rules/tests compile + pass unchanged). CompletenessRequirement(requires_catalog/requires_plscope/max_recovered_ratio)+CompletenessSnapshot mirror keep plsql-sast at Layer-2 deps (no plsql-engine dependency — engine CLI maps AnalysisRun->CompletenessSnapshot at call site, same shim convention as CatalogResolutionSource/PlScopeReference). ScanReport serde JSON round-trips. 18 tests pass (8 new + 10 SAST-001 unchanged); cargo clippy --no-deps -D warnings clean. Unblocks SEC001-007/QUAL003-005/STYLE001 rule pack. Rule families + completeness inputs cross-checked vs /oracle SECURITY-OPTIONS-REFERENCE.md + DATABASE-REFERENCE.md.

title: PLSQL-OSS-UX — plan-lint / corpus-bench / corpus-grow / corpus-license-check lack capabilities + robot-docs + --version
description:
CLIs: tools/plan-lint, tools/corpus-bench, tools/corpus-grow, tools/corpus-license-check (all hand-rolled arg parsers).

What's missing/awkward: None of these four tools has a 'capabilities' subcommand or '--capabilities' flag; none has 'robot-docs' or '--robot-docs'; none accepts '--version' (it errors as 'unknown argument'). They all DO have a clean '--help', a documented exit-code dictionary in --help, and a '--robot-json' that emits a stable-schema (schema_id+schema_version) envelope — so they are halfway there. The pattern plsql-bindgen uses ('--capabilities' and '--robot-docs' as flags on a hand-rolled parser) is already proven inside the same codebase and is a 1-file lift per tool.

Why an agent or human would trip on it: An agent calling 'plan-lint --version' to feature-detect or pin a schema version gets an error. There is no in-tool machine-readable contract; the exit-code dictionary lives only in the --help string (no schema_id, no per-tool stable JSON). Agents must parse --help to know the schema_id; this works but is fragile across versions.

Suggested fix: For each of the four tools, copy the plsql-bindgen pattern: add '--capabilities' returning the contract JSON ({binary, version, flags, exit_codes, stdout_contract, schema_id of the robot-json output}) and '--robot-docs' returning a paste-ready handbook. Add '--version' (print '<name> <version>' to stdout, exit 0). One commit per tool, each ~40 lines.
close_reason: Added --capabilities, --robot-docs, --version/-V to plan-lint, corpus-bench, corpus-grow, corpus-license-check (mirrors plsql-bindgen pattern: hand-rolled parser, contract_version pinned by drift-guard tests, single-line JSON valid). Each binary gains a CAPABILITIES_CONTRACT_VERSION=1, capabilities_json() with binary/contract_version/version/flags/exit_codes/stdout_contract/report_schema, robot_docs_text() handbook, and version banner. Drift-guard + parse-side tests pin every contract. Verified: 'plan-lint --version' → 'plan-lint 0.1.0', 'corpus-bench --capabilities' → valid JSON envelope, 'corpus-license-check --robot-docs' → paste-ready handbook. cargo build/test/clippy all green (77 tests across the 5 target crates).

Inline channel: '-- plsql-scan:ignore RULE[,RULE]' => same source line, '-- plsql-scan:ignore-next-line RULE' => following line, '*'/'all' => any rule; marker case-insensitive and MUST be inside a -- comment (literal containing marker text does not suppress, proven by test); comma/space/tab rule lists; audit trail records directive's own comment line + same-line vs next-line kind. title: PLSQL-OSS-UX — usr-loop top-level subcommand help text mixes Markdown bold (**...**) into plain TTY output
description:
CLI: usr-loop (tools/usr-loop/src/main.rs).

What's missing/awkward: The doc-comments for several usr-loop subcommands embed Markdown ('**cluster/dedup**', '**Stage [F] LAND**', '**§4 monotonic tripwire**') which clap copies verbatim into '--help' output. In a plain terminal these render as literal asterisks ('**Stage [F] LAND**'). This affects: cluster, land, ledger tripwire, and the long descriptions of gate / propose.

Why an agent or human would trip on it: An agent reading 'usr-loop --help' sees noisy '**text**' tokens that aren't real syntax. It's a small cosmetic issue, but it makes the help block harder to scan and risks confusing an agent that thinks the asterisks are positional argument shorthand. plsql-mcp/plsql-engine help text avoids this.

Suggested fix: Strip the '**' bold markers in the doc-comments for the affected enum variants (they're only useful in source-code reading). Alternatively, use clap's long_about/about split so the bold-styled text lives only in long_about (Markdown-friendly when rendered, e.g. man pages) and the short about used in --help is plain. Affects ~6 sites in tools/usr-loop/src/main.rs.
close_reason: Stripped literal Markdown bold markers (**...**) from usr-loop /// doc-comments that clap renders into --help. Removed '**cluster/dedup**' (Cluster), '**Stage [F] LAND**' (Land), '**public corpus**' (Index), '**§4 monotonic tripwire**' (Tripwire), and also '**deterministic stub proposer**' / '**public corpus**' in non-clap-rendered doc-comments so source readers and clap output stay consistent. New regression test clap_help_is_markdown_free renders Cli::command().write_long_help() and asserts the output contains zero literal '**' tokens; fails loudly if Markdown bold leaks back in. Verified: 'usr-loop --help' shows clean 'cluster/dedup' / 'Stage [F] LAND' / etc.

title: PLSQL-CAT-NEW-3 — Add ALL_EDITIONS + ALL_EDITIONING_VIEWS for EBR awareness (/oracle audit)
description:
Audit finding from /oracle skill applied to crates/plsql-catalog/src/lib.rs.

Gap. Catalog flattens to the current edition only. Edition-Based Redefinition (EBR) lets a single object exist in multiple editions with different bodies — DEFAULT_EDITION and child editions. ALL_OBJECTS already shows EDITION_NAME, but we don't fetch ALL_EDITIONS (the edition tree) or ALL_EDITIONING_VIEWS (the editioning view shape that masks the underlying table). Lineage in an EBR shop will miss the version-pinned alternatives.

Source. /oracle skill — DATABASE-REFERENCE.md routing for EBR + the existing CatalogSnapshot.edition column on objects. Local file: crates/plsql-catalog/src/lib.rs:1977 (load_catalog_objects already SELECTs edition_name from all_objects).

Proposed shape. Two loaders:
- load_catalog_editions(conn) -> Vec<Edition> over ALL_EDITIONS (EDITION_NAME, PARENT_EDITION_NAME, USABLE).
- load_catalog_editioning_views(conn, scope) -> Vec<EditioningView> over ALL_EDITIONING_VIEWS (OWNER, VIEW_NAME, TABLE_NAME).
Plumbed into CatalogSnapshot::editions / ::editioning_views. CompletenessReport gains an UnknownReason::EditionFlattened when an object has child editions we did not enumerate.

Cite. Oracle Database Reference 26, ALL_EDITIONS and ALL_EDITIONING_VIEWS views + Database Development Guide chapter on EBR.
close_reason: Added Edition {edition_name, parent_edition_name, usable} + EditioningView {owner, view_name, table_name}, CatalogSnapshot::editions (database-wide) and SchemaCatalog::editioning_views (per-schema, both behind #[serde(default)]). Two new loaders: load_catalog_editions (no schema filter — database-wide), load_catalog_editioning_views (per-schema). Wired into build_catalog_snapshot between table_comments and dependencies. 3 orchestration fixtures updated (the fragment-loop variants special-case all_editions to use no params). Test asserts ORA root + PATCH_2026_05 child + INVOICES_E1 editioning view all materialize with correct parent edges. 28 catalog tests pass; clippy --all-targets -D warnings clean. Cite: /oracle skill audit, Oracle Database Reference 26 ALL_EDITIONS + ALL_EDITIONING_VIEWS; Database Development Guide chapter on EBR.

title: PLSQL-OSS-MOCK2 — plsql-lineage::classify_git_diff public docstring self-declares 'This is a stub' even though function is real (limited-scope) work; doc-rot misleads OSS reviewers
description: crates/plsql-lineage/src/lib.rs:442-446. The public `pub fn classify_git_diff` docstring reads: 'This is a stub — full semantic classification requires the parser (Layer 1). Currently classifies by file extension and diff status.' The function actually does real, contract-satisfying work: it runs `git diff --name-status`, walks the lines, and emits typed ChangeRecord::{Created,Dropped,Body} variants by leading status byte (with R/C rename/copy handled). It's not a stub — it's a coarse-grained classifier whose 'semantic' work is limited to file-system status, by design. But because the docstring says 'This is a stub', an OSS reviewer / agent reading rustdoc concludes the function shouldn't be relied on, and the bug-hunting heuristic flags it as a placeholder. classify_dir_diff (called by the MCP classify_change tool via change_tools.rs) has the same shape but no 'stub' framing. Suggested fix: rewrite the docstring to describe what the function actually does ('Classifies changes between two git refs by parsing the file-status output of `git diff --name-status`; emits Created/Dropped/Body records per PL/SQL file. Body records carry git-ref hashes (`git:<ref>`) only, not content-based body diffs') and drop the 'This is a stub' sentence; if richer semantic classification is desired, file a separate bead for that work and add a 'See also <bead-id> for parser-aware classification' line. No code change required, just doc-string accuracy.
close_reason: Rewrote the classify_git_diff docstring to honestly describe what it does today: a structural git-diff classifier that shells to 'git diff --name-status', emits ChangeRecord::{Created,Dropped,Body} per PL/SQL file by leading status byte (A/D/M, with R*/C* rename/copy decomposed into Dropped(old)+Created(new) so renames aren't treated as body changes), stamps old_run_id / new_run_id with the supplied refs, and intentionally does NOT body-diff (parser-level semantic body classification is out of scope). The 'This is a stub' lie is gone. Verified: cargo build -p plsql-lineage OK; cargo test -p plsql-lineage all green; cargo clippy -p plsql-lineage --all-targets -- -D warnings OK (no broken intra-doc links — original draft referenced the private is_plsql_file fn, rewritten to plain prose to keep rustdoc clean).

title: PLSQL-OSS-UX — plsqld treats --help as a cache-dir path (Axiom 0 violation)
description:
CLI: plsqld (crates/plsql-store/src/bin/plsqld.rs).

What's missing/awkward: 'plsqld --help' produces 'plsqld: cache directory --help does not exist (create it explicitly first)' and exits 2. Same for '-h', '--version', '--robot-json'. Bare 'plsqld' (no args) prints 'usage: plsqld <cache-dir>' exit 2 — that's tolerable, but every flag-shaped argument is mis-parsed as a literal cache-dir path. There is no 'capabilities', no 'robot-docs', no 'doctor', no env-var documentation.

Why an agent or human would trip on it: An agent calling 'plsqld --help' to discover the daemon's surface gets a confusing error that suggests creating a literally-named '--help' directory. Worst-case, a less careful agent might 'mkdir --help' then re-run. The error message points the wrong way. Also: once started ('plsqld /tmp/foo'), it blocks indefinitely listening on a Unix socket with zero discovery surface — no banner explaining how to talk to it.

Suggested fix: Treat any first-arg starting with '-' as a flag. Add a real '--help' that documents the positional cache-dir, the socket path it creates, the wire protocol, plus '--version' and an inline pointer to docs. Add 'capabilities --json' even if it just returns {binary, version, listen_socket_template, exit_codes}. Optional: 'plsqld doctor /tmp/foo' to inspect an existing store.
close_reason: plsqld gains --help/-h, --version/-V, --capabilities, --robot-docs (mirrors plsql-bindgen pattern). Any first-arg starting with '-' is treated as a flag before any cache-dir parsing — closes the regression where 'plsqld --help' tried to create a directory literally called '--help'. Dedicated regression test (help_does_not_attempt_to_create_a_help_directory) pins that exact bad-message string against future drift. Capabilities JSON documents the Unix-socket-only network footprint (R17), the wire protocol (serve_envelope), and the listen_socket_template. Unknown-flag handler points at --help instead of misinterpreting. 12 new tests pass (3 lib + 9 integration spawning the binary).

title: PLSQL-OSS-UX — plsql-mcp bare invocation runs an undocumented doctor-equivalent on stdout
description:
CLI: plsql-mcp (crates/plsql-mcp/src/main.rs).

What's missing/awkward: Bare 'plsql-mcp' (no subcommand) prints 3 lines (version banner + WARN/INFO findings) to stdout and exits 0. This is the same content as 'plsql-mcp doctor' (human mode). The behavior is undocumented in --help (which says the COMMAND is optional but never describes what 'no command' means). Capabilities lists 4 commands; bare invocation matches none of them.

Why an agent or human would trip on it: An agent that runs 'plsql-mcp' to 'see if it works' gets diagnostic output on stdout with exit 0 — looks like success, but the content is warnings ('LD_LIBRARY_PATH not set', 'no connections.toml'). An MCP harness that auto-launches the binary expecting JSON-RPC over stdio (because '--help' says 'stdio by default') will instead get human-readable WARN/INFO text on stdout and the process exits — no server. The implicit 'no-command = doctor' shortcut also violates Axiom 4 (stdout should be data; these are diagnostics).

Suggested fix: One of (a) make bare invocation exit 2 with 'usage: ...; for the canonical agent start, run: plsql-mcp --robot-triage' on stderr; (b) make bare invocation start the stdio server (matches --help's wording of 'stdio by default' and matches what an MCP launcher expects); or (c) explicitly document the 'no-command = doctor' shortcut in --help and route the output to stderr / exit 2 if blocker found. Pick one and pin a regression test.
close_reason: Fixed: bare 'plsql-mcp' no longer silently runs doctor on stdout. Now prints clap long help + a 'no subcommand' hint to stderr and exits 2. stdout stays empty (verified: 0 bytes). Regression test main.rs::bare_invocation_does_not_silently_run_doctor_on_stdout pins the contract.

title: PLSQL-OSS-AUDIT — internal bead IDs leak into 142 published rustdoc comments
description:
142 doc comments (///) across the workspace src embed internal issue-tracker IDs -- both 'PLSQL-XXX-NNN' spec/bead codes and 'oracle-xxxx' beads IDs -- directly into rustdoc that 'cargo doc' renders into the public API documentation.

Heaviest offenders (count of /// lines with a bead ref):
  crates/plsql-catalog/src/lib.rs : 30
  crates/plsql-lineage/src/lib.rs : 25
  crates/plsql-engine/src/lib.rs  : 16
  crates/plsql-parser/src/ast.rs  : 11
  ...plus ~15 more files with 2-5 each.

Examples (all are /// doc comments, not // line comments):
  plsql-lineage/src/lib.rs:34  '/// Schema descriptor for classify-change (PLSQL-LIN-000).'
  plsql-lineage/src/lib.rs:41  '/// Schema descriptor for the lineage doctor report (PLSQL-LIN-011).'
  plsql-ir/src/lib.rs:122      '...The concrete Declaration enum lands in PLSQL-IR-002.'
  plsql-ir/src/lib.rs:126      '...the statement enum lands in PLSQL-IR-004; ... PLSQL-SQLSEM-001.'

Why it matters: these IDs are meaningless to anyone outside the project. In the generated docs.rs / cargo doc output an OSS consumer sees 'lands in PLSQL-IR-002' with no way to resolve it -- it reads as leftover internal scaffolding and looks unfinished/unprofessional. The 'lands in X' phrasing also documents internal roadmap state as if it were API contract.

Suggested fix: sweep /// comments and either drop the parenthetical bead IDs or replace them with stable, consumer-meaningful prose. 'The statement enum lands in PLSQL-IR-004' should just describe what the type IS. Internal // (non-doc) comments referencing beads are fine to keep; only the rustdoc /// ones need scrubbing. A regex (rg '///.*\b(PLSQL-[A-Z]+-[0-9]|oracle-[a-z0-9]{4,})') enumerates all 142 sites.
close_reason: Scrubbed 314 PLSQL-* and 38 oracle-bead tracker IDs out of /// and //! doc comments across 170 .rs files (28 in plsql-mcp, 21 in plsql-ir, 18 in plsql-accretion, 15 in plsql-symbols, 11 each in plsql-sast/cicd/bindgen, 9 in plsql-doc, 8 in plsql-support, 5 in plsql-project, 4 each in plsql-store/parser, 3 each in plsql-parser-java/parser-antlr, 2 each in plsql-lineage/engine/depgraph/catalog, 1 each in plsql-privileges/plsql-core, plus 7 in tools/). Kept 5 substantive references: oracle-database (Oracle docs URL in plsql-bindgen/src/structs.rs), oracle-correctness (compound English noun in plsql-accretion/tests/min_fixture.rs), and 3 mentions of the compare-oracle-deps CLI command name (in plsql-depgraph/src/lib.rs and plsql-lineage/src/lib.rs). cargo build --workspace OK; cargo test --workspace 1773 passed, 0 failed, 2 ignored; cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings OK; cargo doc warning count unchanged at 84 (pre-existing). No git commit or push.

title: PLSQL-ENG-AUDIT-1 — plsql-engine::analyze_project is honestly skeleton-only (/mock-code-finder)
description:
Audit finding from /mock-code-finder pass on crates/ and tools/.

State. crates/plsql-engine/src/lib.rs:148 analyze_project(req) -> Result<AnalysisRun, EngineError> currently does:
  let _ = req;
  Err(EngineError::SkeletonOnly)

EngineError::SkeletonOnly carries the message 'plsql-engine skeleton only: orchestration implementation lives in Layer 2.5 beads'. The test analyze_project_reports_skeleton_only_error pins this behavior.

Verdict. This is honest, not a hidden stub:
- The error variant explicitly tells callers the orchestration is not wired.
- A unit test pins the contract so any future caller gets the SkeletonOnly error rather than silently degraded behavior.
- All data types (AnalysisRequest, AnalysisRun, ProjectModel, ParseResult, SemanticModel, FlowSummary, FactStoreSnapshot, AnalysisArtifactDigest/Manifest) are real shapes with serde, just not yet populated.

Action. File this as the audit anchor so future passes verify nothing regresses. The substantive work lands under Layer 2.5 beads (plan.md §6.2.5).

Other skeleton-marked surfaces found and documented elsewhere:
- crates/plsql-mcp/src/{tools,transport,connections,safety}.rs — bead-skeleton style (PLSQL-MCP-001 calls this out)
- crates/plsql-catalog/src/lib.rs:1260 — DDL parsing skeleton (typed)
- crates/plsql-parser/src/lib.rs:237 — parse_file unimplemented!() (typed pointer to PARSE-000A)

Only the plsql-engine case is a hard error path; the others are typed degraded modes. No silent stubs detected.
close_reason: /mock-code-finder audit recorded. plsql-engine::analyze_project at lib.rs:148 returns Err(EngineError::SkeletonOnly) — honest, typed, pinned by analyze_project_reports_skeleton_only_error test. All data shapes (AnalysisRequest/AnalysisRun/ProjectModel/etc.) are real with serde. Implementation lands incrementally under Layer 2.5 beads (plan.md §6.2.5). When those land, the SkeletonOnly variant gets removed. Anchor closed; the Layer 2.5 work itself is tracked by its own beads.

title: PLSQL-CAT-NEW-5 — Add ALL_TAB_COMMENTS + ALL_COL_COMMENTS for documentation lineage (/oracle audit)
description:
Audit finding from /oracle skill applied to crates/plsql-catalog/src/lib.rs.

Gap. Comments on tables, views, and columns (the COMMENT ON TABLE/COLUMN DDL) are stored in ALL_TAB_COMMENTS and ALL_COL_COMMENTS. The current catalog never reads them, so downstream docs generation (plsql-docgen, when it lands) has no description text and can only emit names. The /oracle skill calls these out under Database Reference.

Source. /oracle skill — DATABASE-REFERENCE.md. Local file: crates/plsql-catalog/src/lib.rs (no current loader for either view).

Proposed shape. Two loaders:
- load_catalog_table_comments(conn, scope) -> Vec<TableComment> over ALL_TAB_COMMENTS (OWNER, TABLE_NAME, TABLE_TYPE, COMMENTS).
- load_catalog_column_comments(conn, scope) -> Vec<ColumnComment> over ALL_COL_COMMENTS (OWNER, TABLE_NAME, COLUMN_NAME, COMMENTS).
Plumbed into CatalogSnapshot::table_comments / ::column_comments. plsql-docgen consumes them when generating per-object docs.

Lower priority (P3) because comments don't affect dependency analysis — they affect docs generation only.

Cite. Oracle Database Reference 26, ALL_TAB_COMMENTS and ALL_COL_COMMENTS views.

title: PLSQL-PROP-1 — Add 4 property tests (CST round-trip, cycle invariant, diagnostics monotonic, K18 idempotent) (/testing-property)
description:
Audit finding from /testing-property pass.

State. proptest is a dev-dependency of plsql-parser; one proptest harness lives at crates/plsql-parser/tests/fuzz_corpus_derived.rs (256 cases per run, +64 mutations per corpus input, ~5000 total exercises per CI run).

Properties already enforced.
- exercise_input() never panics on any input (TokenTape::new + reconstruct round-trip).
- 256 proptest cases of random byte strings up to 4 KB never panic.
- 18 corpus-derived seed inputs each mutated 64 times.
Property coverage. The parser-surface panic-freedom property is the only one currently in proptest. Worth adding:

- ConcreteSyntaxTree::reconstruct round-trip: reconstruct(parse(s)) == s byte-for-byte for any s in the corpus. The token-tape supports this; the property test would catch any new lower-pass that drops trivia.
- DepGraph cycle invariants: cycle_detect() output is a subset of the actual SCC decomposition, never reports phantom cycles.
- SemanticModel diagnostics monotonicity: adding objects only ever increases the diagnostic count for completeness-related codes, never decreases.
- K18 sanitize() idempotence: sanitize(sanitize(s).0).0 == sanitize(s).0 for all s (the second pass should never find additional markers if the first redacted everything).

Action. File a follow-up bead to add the 4 property tests above. K18-idempotence is the cheapest add (sits inside plsql-mcp tests, no new dep needed).

Cite. /testing-property — properties (invariants) catch classes of bugs that example-based tests miss. Add them after every PR that touches a parser/lowering/sanitizer surface.

Current property surface: proptest harness on parser TokenTape panic-freedom (256 cases + 64 mutations per corpus seed, ~5000 exercises per CI run). 4 candidate properties documented for follow-up: CST round-trip byte-for-byte, depgraph cycle subset of SCC, diagnostics monotonicity, K18 sanitize idempotence. K18-idempotence is the cheapest to add next.

title: PLSQL-OSS-BUG — split_statements does not depth-track IF/LOOP; every statement after the first inner ';' in an IF/LOOP body is mis-parented (dependency edges silently lost)
description:
FILE: crates/plsql-ir/src/stmt.rs  fn split_statements (lines 156-200), specifically the keyword loop at 172-190.

BUG: split_statements splits a body on ';' at block depth 0. It only depth-tracks BEGIN (depth+=1, line 172-179) and END (depth=(depth-1).max(0), line 180-190). It does NOT track 'IF'/'END IF' or 'LOOP'/'END LOOP'. The function's OWN doc-comment (lines 156-160) explicitly promises the opposite: 'Split source on ; honouring nested BEGIN...END; blocks AND matching IF...END IF; / LOOP...END LOOP; so an inner semicolon does not tear apart a control-flow body.' The code never implements the IF/LOOP half.

WHY IT TRIGGERS: any IF or LOOP body that contains '<stmt>;' separators (i.e. essentially every non-trivial control-flow body). The first ';' inside the body is at depth 0, so split_statements cuts there. The first chunk becomes 'IF cond THEN firstStmt;' (no 'END IF'), classify_if then does rfind("END IF") -> None -> end_pos=len, so it lowers a one-arm IF. Every subsequent inner statement ('ELSIF ... ;', 'ELSE ...;', second/third body statements, and the trailing 'END IF;'/'END LOOP;') is emitted as a SEPARATE top-level Statement::Unrecognized or re-parented to the top level.

CONFIRMED EMPIRICALLY (scratch crate against plsql-ir):
  lower_statement_body("IF a THEN s1(); ELSIF b THEN s2(); ELSIF c THEN s3(); ELSE s4(); END IF;")
   => 5 statements: If{arms:[a/s1();]}, Unrecognized{"ELSIF b THEN s2();"}, Unrecognized{"ELSIF c THEN s3();"}, Unrecognized{"ELSE s4();"}, Unrecognized{"END IF;"}
   extract_call_sites => ["s1"]  -- s2,s3,s4 entirely lost.
  lower_statement_body("IF p_flag=1 THEN INSERT INTO audit_log VALUES(1); UPDATE accounts SET bal=0; END IF;")
   => If{arm: INSERT...}, Sql{Update accounts}, Unrecognized{"END IF;"} -- the UPDATE leaks to top level and is reported UNCONDITIONAL (loses the IF guard); 'END IF;' is a bogus Unrecognized stmt.

DOWNSTREAM IMPACT (HIGH): plsql-engine analyze_project (crates/plsql-engine/src/lib.rs) builds Calls / Reads / Writes dependency edges from body_stmts. On the PRIMARY ANTLR path, tree_lower.rs::lower_statement (lines 542-544) emits AstStatement::If with cond_text = node_text of the WHOLE if_statement node (full 'IF...END IF' source incl. body); ast_stmts_to_ir then does plsql_ir::lower_statement_body(cond_text) (engine lib.rs:667) to recover the body's nested DML. That re-lowering is exactly where this bug fires. On the FALLBACK path (engine lib.rs:929) lower_statement_body(slice) on a decl span hits it too. Net result: call sites and table Read/Write accesses inside IF/LOOP bodies are silently dropped or mis-attributed -> incomplete + incorrect dependency graph with NO honest diagnostic, violating the project's R13 'never hide uncertainty' rule. Tests miss it because every classify_if/extract_call_sites unit test uses a single-statement body or a body with no internal ';'.

SUGGESTED FIX: in split_statements, also depth-track IF (consume_keyword 'IF' -> depth+=1) and LOOP, and on END only decrement for a bare END (not END IF / END LOOP) OR track all three openers consistently so 'END IF'/'END LOOP'/'END' each match their opener. The cleanest is: increment on IF/LOOP/BEGIN/CASE openers and decrement on the matching END form; only split on ';' when depth==0. Note crates/plsql-parser-antlr/src/lower/mod.rs::lower_statement_body (lines 511-556) has the identical BEGIN/END-only bug (currently only reachable from tests) and should be fixed the same way.
close_reason: split_statements (plsql-ir/src/stmt.rs) + lower_statement_body (plsql-parser-antlr/src/lower/mod.rs) now depth-track every block opener — BEGIN/IF/LOOP/CASE — and the matching terminators (bare END plus END IF/END LOOP/END CASE, matched before bare END to avoid double-counting). Inner ';' inside any control-flow body no longer splits the statement. R13: plsql-ir splitter flags an unterminated block and lower_statement_body emits Unrecognized/UnterminatedBlock. Doc-comments rewritten to match. New tests: stmt.rs multi_statement_if/loop/bare_loop_body_is_one_statement, nested_if_inside_loop_stays_one_statement, unterminated_if_block_degrades_with_typed_reason; lower/mod.rs parse005_if/loop_body_not_split_on_inner_semicolons, parse005_nested_if_inside_loop_not_split. All written failing-first, watched fail, then fixed. cargo build --workspace OK; full workspace suite 1713 passed/0 failed; clippy -D warnings clean; forbid(unsafe_code) intact.

title: PLSQL-OSS-UX — corpus-bench on /nonexistent prints 'nothing to benchmark' on stdout (Axiom 14, mixed contract)
description:
CLI: corpus-bench.

What's missing/awkward: 'corpus-bench --corpus-root /nonexistent' prints 'no PL/SQL files found under /nonexistent — nothing to benchmark' to STDOUT and exits 2. The exit code is correct (per --help, exit 2 = invocation error / empty corpus), but stdout is supposed to be the data lane (Axiom 4). An agent piping 'corpus-bench --robot-json | jq .' will see invalid JSON (the human error went to stdout in robot mode too — needs verification but the code path is shared in tools/corpus-bench/src/main.rs).

Additional: the error doesn't tell the agent the default ('did you mean --corpus-root ./corpus?'), even though --help explicitly says default is ./corpus. The agent has to context-switch back to --help.

Why an agent or human would trip on it: A CI that runs 'corpus-bench --robot-json | jq .summary' on a fresh checkout where ./corpus is missing or empty gets a non-JSON line on stdout AND exit 2 — jq blows up with cryptic 'parse error', masking the underlying 'no corpus' problem. Per Axioms 4 + 14, stdout in robot mode should be a (possibly empty) valid JSON envelope OR nothing at all (with the diagnostic on stderr).

Suggested fix: (1) Route the 'no PL/SQL files found' message to stderr in both human and robot modes. (2) In --robot-json mode, still emit a valid envelope ({schema_id, schema_version, file_count: 0, summary: null, per_file: [], error: 'empty_corpus'}) so jq pipelines don't break. (3) Append 'try: corpus-bench --corpus-root ./corpus' to the error if the user passed a non-default path.
close_reason: corpus-bench 'no PL/SQL files found' diagnostic is now strictly on stderr (was already so but is now wrapped with a contextual hint pointing at './corpus' when the user passed a non-default --corpus-root). In --robot-json mode the binary ALSO emits a valid empty-report envelope on stdout — Report.summary is now Option<Summary> (skips serialization when None), per_file: [], file_count: 0, empty_corpus: true (the new field skipped when false). Exit 2 preserved. Integration test empty_corpus_envelope.rs proves: (1) --robot-json | jq . succeeds with structured empty envelope; (2) human mode keeps stdout empty; (3) non-default --corpus-root triggers the default-path hint on stderr; (4) --capabilities & --version & --robotjson-DYM all behave. 7 unit + 6 integration tests pass.
