title: find_body_begin must skip nested subprograms so standalone PROCEDURE/FUNCTION/TRIGGER spans reach the routine's own END;
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-parser-antlr/src/lower/mod.rs:1269 — verdict=keep, severity=high
**Impact:** A standalone CREATE PROCEDURE/FUNCTION/TRIGGER that declares a local (nested) subprogram in its IS|AS declarative part gets its declaration span truncated at the nested routine's END;, so the enclosing routine's real body — its INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE/MERGE Writes, its procedure Calls, and any EXECUTE IMMEDIATE sinks — is silently dropped from the dependency graph, lineage, and intra-procedural taint analysis, producing a fail-open SAST false-negative on this SQL-guard product.
**Evidence:** find_body_begin returns the FIRST BEGIN with no nesting awareness (crates/plsql-parser-antlr/src/lower/mod.rs:1269-1286, returns Some(i) on the first matches_keyword_ignore_case(...,b"BEGIN")). lower_procedure/lower_function/lower_trigger anchor advance_to_decl_end_with_depth(bytes, begin, 0) at that BEGIN (mod.rs:219, 243, 268). block_structure_step only opens on BEGIN/IF/LOOP/CASE — NOT on nested PROCEDURE/FUNCTION headers (crates/plsql-parser-antlr/src/recover.rs:156-170), so the depth-0 scan opened at the nested BEGIN (depth->1), the nested END; returns depth to 0, and the trailing ; closes the span at the nested END. Throwaway test I injected, ran, then reverted (git diff now clean): `PROC name=p span=0..57  PROC slice = "CREATE PROCEDURE p IS PROCEDURE inner IS BEGIN NULL; END;"  PROC BODY 'INSERT INTO audit' INSIDE SPAN? false` (src len 104; INSERT at offset 70). `FUNC name=f span
**Repro:** Trigger: lower_source("CREATE PROCEDURE p IS PROCEDURE inner IS BEGIN NULL; END; BEGIN inner; INSERT INTO audit VALUES(1); END;", fid()) yields a Procedure decl whose span.end.offset == 57, while the enclosing body `BEGIN inner; INSERT INTO audit VALUES(1); END;` begins at offset 57 and `INSERT INTO audit` is at offset 70 — entirely outside the span. The engine fallback then slices source[0..57] = "CREATE PROCEDURE p IS PROCEDURE inner IS BEGIN NULL; END;" and runs lower_statement_body on it, so neither the `inner` Calls edge nor the `INSERT INTO audit` Writes edge is ever produced. A routine WITHOUT a nested subprogram is unaffected: find_body_begin then lands on the routine's own BEGIN, so
**Suggested fix:** Make find_body_begin nesting-aware: while scanning the IS|AS declarative part, when a PROCEDURE/FUNCTION header is seen at the current (declarative) level, treat its subsequent IS|AS ... BEGIN ... END; as a balanced unit to skip (advance past its matching END; via block-depth counting) before continuing, and return only the first BEGIN that is NOT inside any nested subprogram. Add regression tests mirroring the existing package tests at mod.rs:1969/2002: the procedure, function, and DECLARE-section trigger variants with a nested subprogram must each yield a span whose end.offset == src.len() and whose engine-fallback slice contains the enclosing body's INSERT INTO audit and inner call; include a multi-level-nested case so the deepest nested END; cannot truncate.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit caad1643c6), re-verified on main. Root-caused: find_body_begin returned the FIRST BEGIN with no nesting awareness, so a standalone CREATE PROCEDURE/FUNCTION/TRIGGER that declares a nested subprogram in its IS|AS declarative part (PROCEDURE inner IS BEGIN...END;) anchored the depth-0 body scan at the NESTED routine's BEGIN; the nested END; returned depth to 0 and the trailing ; trun

title: Descend into Statement::NestedBlock in flow_intra::walk so sub-block taint is not dropped
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-ir/src/flow_intra.rs:222 — verdict=keep, severity=high
**Impact:** A tainted value laundered through an anonymous BEGIN…END / DECLARE…END sub-block (e.g. `BEGIN v_sql := p_user; END;` then `EXECUTE IMMEDIATE v_sql`) is reported UNtainted by the FLOW-001 intra-procedural taint pass, so the flagship SEC001 SQL-injection rule fails open for that value — once the taint engine is wired into the production scan path. Today no production path constructs a FlowEnv, so it is a latent fail-open, not a shipping exploit.
**Evidence:** flow_intra.rs:222 `_ => {}` with no NestedBlock arm; stmt.rs:531-534 NestedBlock classification; calls.rs:159-173 handles NestedBlock; probe: NESTED len=0 v_sql=None vs FLAT len=1 v_sql=Some([UserInput]).
**Repro:** Trigger: `analyze_flow(&lower_statement_body("BEGIN v_sql := p_user; END;"), &TaintSources{user_input_names:vec!["P_USER".into()],bind_names:vec![]})` returns a FlowEnv of len 0 with `get("v_sql") == None`, whereas the identical flat body `v_sql := p_user;` returns len 1 with `v_sql` carrying `[UserInput]`. The carried taint is silently dropped because `walk` has no NestedBlock arm. Disproof of CRITICAL immediacy: no non-test code constructs a FlowEnv (engine lacks the plsql-sast dep; MCP only renders a precomputed ScanReport), so there is no end-to-end injection fail-open in the shipped binary yet.
**Suggested fix:** Add a `Statement::NestedBlock { body_text }` arm to `walk` before the `_ => {}` catch-all, mirroring calls.rs:159-173: strip the BEGIN/DECLARE…END wrapper, and only recurse via the existing depth-guarded `recurse_body!` macro when the stripped text differs from the original (to avoid re-lowering identical text and spinning). Either make `calls.rs::strip_block_wrapper` `pub(crate)` and reuse it, or copy it (calls.rs:187-205). Add regression tests: (1) nested `BEGIN v_sql := p_user; END;` taints v_sql; (2) DECLARE-wrapped laundering still alarms; (3) a deeply nested chain terminates via the MAX_RELOWER_DEPTH cap (limit_hit) rather than overflowing. Apply the identical fix to the sibling omission in dml_edges.rs:100-137 (walk_table_accesses) so Write/Read edges inside sub-blocks are not dropped.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 6bf57e6089), re-verified on main. Added Statement::NestedBlock arm to flow_intra::walk before the _=>{} catch-all, mirroring calls.rs::walk_call_sites: strips the BEGIN/DECLARE..END wrapper via the now-shared crate::calls::strip_block_wrapper and recurses via the depth-guarded recurse_body! only when the stripped slice differs (so a non-stripping slice cannot spin; MAX_RELOWER_DEPT

title: Wire Statement::NestedBlock descent into walk_table_accesses so nested-block DML is not dropped from Reads/Writes edges
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-ir/src/dml_edges.rs:135 — verdict=keep, severity=high
**Impact:** Any INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE/MERGE/SELECT inside an anonymous BEGIN…END / DECLARE…END sub-block (a common PL/SQL idiom for scoped exception handling) is silently absent from the Reads/Writes dependency edges, DependencyEdge facts, impact analysis, and lineage — so a routine that writes a table only via a nested block shows no write edge, with no diagnostic emitted.
**Evidence:** walk_table_accesses (crates/plsql-ir/src/dml_edges.rs:100-137) matches Sql/If/ForLoop/WhileLoop/BareLoop then `_ => {}` at line 135 — NO Statement::NestedBlock arm. Sibling walk_call_sites (crates/plsql-ir/src/calls.rs:159-173) DOES handle NestedBlock (strip_block_wrapper + recurse). recursion_guard.rs:3-6 documents BOTH extract routines as descending "IF / LOOP / nested block". Empirical run of a temp test against the real crate (then reverted; tree confirmed clean):
  FLAT lowered = [Sql { verb: Update, raw_text: "UPDATE secret_table SET x=1 WHERE id=9;" }]
  FLAT accesses = [TableAccess { schema: None, table: "SECRET_TABLE", access: Write }]
  NESTED lowered = [NestedBlock { body_text: "BEGIN UPDATE secret_table SET x=1 WHERE id=9; END;" }]
  NESTED accesses = []
  IF lowered = [If { arms: [IfArm { cond_text: "p_flag=1", body_text: "BEGIN UPDATE accounts SET bal=0 WHERE id=1; END;" }]
**Repro:** extract_table_accesses(lower_statement_body("BEGIN UPDATE secret_table SET x=1 WHERE id=9; END;")) returns [] (X→Y: input is a nested-block UPDATE, wrong output is empty access list / no Write edge), while the identical flat "UPDATE secret_table SET x=1 WHERE id=9;" returns [TableAccess{SECRET_TABLE, Write}]. Also reproduced via an IF arm whose body is a BEGIN…END block — returns [] — confirming the parse-tree path (lib.rs:670-671 re-lower) hits the same gap, not just the text-scanner fallback. Unlike the recursion-cap path (lib.rs:1028-1034 mints a typed diagnostic), this drop is completely silent.
**Suggested fix:** Add a Statement::NestedBlock arm to walk_table_accesses before the `_ => {}` at line 135, mirroring calls.rs:159-173: strip the BEGIN…END / DECLARE…END wrapper (lift strip_block_wrapper to pub(crate) or a shared stmt helper) and recurse_body!(inner) only when inner != body_text (the existing depth guard already protects a non-shrinking slice). Add regression tests proving (a) lower_statement_body("BEGIN UPDATE secret_table SET x=1 WHERE id=9; END;") yields a Write of SECRET_TABLE and (b) an IF arm whose body is a nested BEGIN…END DML block yields the expected Read/Write.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 6bf57e6089), re-verified on main. Added the matching Statement::NestedBlock arm to dml_edges::walk_table_accesses before the _=>{} catch-all, reusing the shared strip_block_wrapper + depth-guarded recurse_body!. INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE/MERGE/SELECT inside an anonymous BEGIN..END / DECLARE..END sub-block (including an IF arm whose body is a nested block) now surface Reads/Writes edges

title: Fix parse_unified_diff header detection: removed `-- comment` lines mis-parsed as `--- ` headers (no hunk-state tracking)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-lineage/src/lib.rs:707 — verdict=keep, severity=high
**Impact:** An external library consumer feeding a real `git diff`/`diff -u` that removes or edits a leading SQL `-- comment` line gets either a hard `MalformedDiff` error that discards the entire valid changeset (single-file diff) or a silently wrong deletion count in the emitted Body record (multi-file diff) — and PL/SQL source is comment-saturated so this is routine input. No impact on the shipping MCP product, which never calls the diff parser.
**Evidence:** lib.rs:705-735 — the parse loop classifies file headers with unconditional `line.strip_prefix("--- ")` (707) / `"+++ "` (713) and tracks no hunk state (never keys on `@@`); deletions are only counted when `!line.starts_with("---")` (723), so a deleted comment is never tallied. I ran the EXACT real `git diff` output through the built crate:
  git diff of removing `-- old comment line` produces body line `--- old comment line`.
CASE_SINGLE result = Err(MalformedDiff { line: 10, detail: "trailing --- header without matching +++" })
CASE_MULTI  result = Ok([Body{object_id:"proc", hash_before:"diff:-0", hash_after:"diff:+0"}, Body{object_id:"next", hash_before:"diff:-1", hash_after:"diff:+1"}])  // proc deletion count wrong (should be -1).
Caller search: `rg 'parse_unified_diff|parse_change_file' crates/ --type rust` shows the only references are the definition (lib.rs:635/644) and unit tests
**Repro:** Triggering input (verbatim real git diff): "diff --git a/proc.sql b/proc.sql\nindex 09c7876..5b737da 100644\n--- a/proc.sql\n+++ b/proc.sql\n@@ -1,4 +1,3 @@\n--- old comment line\n create or replace procedure hr.p is\n begin\n   null;\n end;\n" → parse_unified_diff returns Err(MalformedDiff{line:10,...}) instead of one Body(proc) record. Verified by adding tests/zz_verify_idx3.rs, running `cargo test -p plsql-lineage --test zz_verify_idx3 -- --nocapture` (output above), then removing the temp file.
**Suggested fix:** Add hunk-state tracking: set `in_hunk = true` on a line starting with `@@`, and only honor `--- `/`+++ ` as file headers while `!in_hunk` (reset `in_hunk = false` on `diff --git `, or require the `--- ` line to be immediately followed by a `+++ ` line via a peekable iterator). Inside a hunk body, classify by the single leading marker only: a line starting with `-` is a deletion regardless of whether the remainder starts with `--`, and `+`-leading is an addition. Add regression tests for: (1) removing a `-- comment` as the last hunk line (must yield Body, not MalformedDiff); (2) editing a `-- comment` mid-hunk (correct deletion count); (3) a removed/added line whose content starts with `-- `/`++ `. Note: the finding's reference to upgrading tests/zz_diff_comment_repro.rs is stale — that file does not exist; only tests/e2e_50_packages.rs is present, so add a new regression test instead.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 5105c9e82b), re-verified on main. Root-caused parse_unified_diff (crates/plsql-lineage/src/lib.rs:644) header mis-detection. The loop classified file headers with unconditional strip_prefix("--- ")/"+++ " and tracked no hunk state, so a removed/edited leading SQL `-- comment` line — which appears in the hunk body as `--- comment` — was mis-read as a `--- ` file header. For a single

title: Lower parenthesized expression groups so taint/call-edges survive (SEC001 fail-open)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-ir/src/expr.rs:189 — verdict=keep, severity=high
**Impact:** A user running this SAST tool on idiomatic PL/SQL like `EXECUTE IMMEDIATE 'SELECT ... ' || (p_user)` (parenthesized concatenation operand, or whole-RHS group `('SELECT ...' || p_user)`) gets NO SEC001 injection finding, even though the un-parenthesized form correctly alarms — the flagship dynamic-SQL injection rule silently fails open on a common, no-obfuscation-required code shape, plus a Calls-edge provenance false negative (`nvl((compute(x)),0)` drops the `compute` call site).
**Evidence:** expr.rs:141-197 `lower_expression_depth` has no production for a parenthesized group `( <expr> )`: recognise_string/datetime/numeric require their own leading byte; recognise_bind/substitution require `:`/`&`; recognise_top_level_binary via find_all_top_level_ops (expr.rs:472-485) only splits at paren depth 0 so an inner operator is invisible; recognise_call (expr.rs:571-579) computes name_part=text[..open] and bails because is_dotted_name("")==false for a bare `(...)`; recognise_unary (expr.rs:622-640) only matches NOT/-/+; is_dotted_name (expr.rs:642-654) rejects any text containing `(`. Falls through to Expr::Raw{UnrecognizedShape} at expr.rs:193-196. collect_expr_flow `_ => {}` at flow_intra.rs:375 and collect_calls `_ => {}` at calls.rs:225 contribute zero for Expr::Raw. SEC001 (rules.rs:84) reports ONLY when answer.is_tainted, and flow_query.rs:84 sets is_tainted=!kinds.is_empty();
**Repro:** Triggering input X -> wrong output Y, proven via public API (analyze_flow over lower_statement_body, sources.user_input_names=["P_USER"]): body `v_sql := 'SELECT * FROM ' || (p_user);` yields env.get("V_SQL").taint.kinds == [] and flags_alarm()==false, whereas the byte-identical body without the parens around p_user yields kinds==[UserInput], flags_alarm()==true. Downstream, SEC001.scan reads ctx.flow.taint_of(site).is_tainted (==false here) and skips emitting any finding, so the injection is not reported. Separately, lower_statement_body("v := nvl((compute(x)), 0);") -> extract_call_sites loses the COMPUTE call edge that the un-parenthesized form records.
**Suggested fix:** Add a `recognise_paren_group(trimmed, depth)` recognizer in lower_expression_depth, placed BEFORE recognise_top_level_binary (so a whole-expression group `('SELECT ' || p_user)` is unwrapped first, then the inner `||` splits normally). It must: (1) return None unless trimmed.starts_with('(') && trimmed.ends_with(')'); (2) scan the body tracking paren depth and in_string (mirroring find_all_top_level_ops) and only strip when the leading `(` matches the trailing `)` at the SAME nesting level — i.e. depth returns to 0 only at the final char — so `(a) + (b)` is NOT wrongly stripped; on a clean single group recurse via lower_expression_depth(&inner, depth + 1) so the existing deeply_nested_parens depth backstop still holds; if unbalanced, return Expr::Raw{UnbalancedParens} (typed reason already exists at expr.rs:93) rather than mis-stripping. Add regression tests: `(p_user)` is a Name; `'a' |

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 6bf57e6089), re-verified on main. Added recognise_paren_group to expr::lower_expression_depth, dispatched BEFORE recognise_top_level_binary. It unwraps a whole-expression ( <expr> ) group only when the leading ( pairs with the trailing ) at the same nesting level (scan tracks paren depth + in_string, skips non-ASCII bytes for char-boundary safety), recurses at depth+1 so the MAX_EX

title: Wire deletion/rename paths into R20 touched_paths extraction (proposer +++ /dev/null bypass)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-accretion/src/proposer.rs:992 — verdict=keep, severity=medium
**Impact:** A consumer that wires the public, documented LlmProposer/SubprocessBackend backend into the land flow can have an adversarial/hallucinated LLM reply delete an arbitrary out-of-scope source file (e.g. crates/plsql-core/src/lib.rs) via a file-deletion hunk while passing the proposer's R20 I-ISOLATION path-scope check, because the deleted path never enters touched_paths and is never re-checked before git apply.
**Evidence:** proposer.rs:992-994 — `let mut touched: Vec<String> = reply.lines().filter_map(|l| l.strip_prefix("+++ b/").map(|s| s.trim().to_string())).collect();` (ONLY +++ b/). proposer.rs:250-265 validate_r20 iterates exactly self.touched_paths + regression_test.path. R20_PATH_PREFIXES (proposer.rs:84-89) = 4 prefixes under plsql-parser-antlr. land.rs:313-321 captures patch starting at `--- a/` and feeds whole patch to `git apply` (land.rs:367-368). `rg 'R20|path_is_r20|touched_paths' scripts/usr_gate.sh crates/.../usr_gate_rs.rs` → no matches (gate does NOT re-derive R20). `rg 'validate_r20|path_is_r20' crates/plsql-accretion/src/land.rs` → no matches (land never re-validates). EMPIRICAL REPRO (fresh git repo): mixed candidate body → `grep '^+++ b/'` yielded only `crates/plsql-parser-antlr/src/lower/mod.rs`; land.rs capture awk produced the full 2-hunk patch; `git apply --check` PASSED; `git appl
**Repro:** Trigger: construct a CandidateDiff body containing a `# usr-gate:` directive block plus TWO unified-diff hunks — (1) in-scope addition `--- a/crates/plsql-parser-antlr/src/lower/mod.rs` / `+++ b/crates/plsql-parser-antlr/src/lower/mod.rs`, and (2) out-of-scope deletion `--- a/crates/plsql-core/src/lib.rs` / `+++ /dev/null` / `@@ -1,2 +0,0 @@` removing the file's lines. parse_reply populates touched_paths=[crates/plsql-parser-antlr/src/lower/mod.rs] only (the +++ /dev/null deletion is invisible to strip_prefix("+++ b/")); the touched.is_empty() guard at line 998 is satisfied by the safe addition; validate_r20() returns Ok. After gate ACCEPT, apply_diff_idempotent captures both hunks and git a
**Suggested fix:** In parse_reply (proposer.rs:992-1000), also collect the path from `--- a/<path>` headers (which carry the affected file for deletions/renames where `+++` is `/dev/null`), explicitly ignoring `/dev/null`, then sort/dedup; keep the touched.is_empty() guard. This routes the deletion target through validate_r20 → path_is_r20_safe and yields ProposerError::R20Violation. Apply the same extraction in any DeterministicStubProposer path that derives touched_paths from diff text. Add defense-in-depth in land.rs::apply_diff_idempotent: before git apply, parse the captured patch for every `--- a/<path>` (deletion) and rename target and assert path_is_r20_safe, returning LandError on violation, so the bypass is closed even if a future proposer regresses. Add a regression test mirroring llm_rejects_out_of_scope_diff_before_gate using a mixed in-scope-addition + out-of-scope-`+++ /dev/null`-deletion ca

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit fa8379e29d), re-verified on main. Root-cause fix: proposer.rs parse_reply now extracts touched_paths from BOTH `+++ b/<path>` AND `--- a/<path>` headers (skipping /dev/null), so an out-of-scope file DELETION expressed as `+++ /dev/null` (target only in `--- a/`) is routed through validate_r20 -> path_is_r20_safe and yields ProposerError::R20Violation BEFORE the gate, closing the pr

title: Fold req.redaction_policy into ENG-003B cache key before any consumer honors artifacts.redaction_policy
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-engine/src/lib.rs:763 — verdict=defense-in-depth, severity=low
**Impact:** None observable today: no shipped consumer reads run.artifacts.redaction_policy to drive redaction, and all production analyze_project callers pass the default non-redacting policy and emit diagnostics raw — so the stale cached policy field changes nothing. It would only become a live under-redaction leak if a future consumer is wired to honor the cached artifact policy to gate disclosure.
**Evidence:** crates/plsql-engine/src/lib.rs:762-768 — cache key folds only content_hash + profile_hash (serde of req.analysis_profile), omitting req.redaction_policy:
762  let content_hash = plsql_store::hash_hex(&hasher_input);
763  let profile_bytes = serde_json::to_vec(&req.analysis_profile).ok()?;
764  let profile_hash = plsql_store::hash_hex(&profile_bytes);
Cache hit at lib.rs:777-781 returns the full cached AnalysisRun with only cache_outcome overwritten; req.redaction_policy is stamped verbatim into artifacts at lib.rs:718. CacheConfig::default().enabled=true (lib.rs:48).
`rg redaction_policy crates/` returns only: lib.rs:74 (request field), lib.rs:127 (artifact-manifest field), lib.rs:718 (verbatim write) — ZERO reads that drive redaction.
`rg redact_diagnostic|redact_evidence|.redacted(` — production redaction only inside plsql-output DiagnosticEnvelope::redacted/EvidenceEnvelope::redacted 
**Repro:** Disproof of the claimed leak: the exploit requires a consumer that (a) calls analyze_project twice on identical content+profile with redact_freeform_text:false then true, AND (b) honors run.artifacts.redaction_policy to redact output. (a) is impossible in shipped code — every AnalysisRequest is built with ..AnalysisRequest::default() (redact_freeform_text=false), so two requests differing only in redaction posture never occur. (b) has no production caller — grep proves the only .redacted() invocations are in plsql-output unit tests, and no code reads run.artifacts.redaction_policy. Therefore the stale cached policy field is advisory metadata with zero behavioral effect; redaction is a no-op 
**Suggested fix:** Cheap forward-correctness hardening: at lib.rs:763 serialize a tuple so the key is sensitive to redaction posture — `let profile_bytes = serde_json::to_vec(&(&req.analysis_profile, &req.redaction_policy)).ok()?;` — and update the comment at lib.rs:744-747 to state the key folds the profile AND the redaction policy (it currently asserts a stale fragment is never served across profiles, which is broader than what the code guarantees). This costs nothing and closes the latent gap before any consumer is wired to apply artifacts.redaction_policy. Not urgent: no production path reads that field today.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 8a79d745b5), re-verified on main. Root-cause fix in crates/plsql-engine/src/lib.rs: the ENG-003B cache key (analyze_project) previously folded only content_hash + serde(req.analysis_profile), omitting req.redaction_policy. Changed the profile_hash input at lib.rs:~775 to serialize a tuple `&(&req.analysis_profile, &req.redaction_policy)` so a change in redaction posture invalidates

title: Constrain scratch-schema suffix to a safe identifier allowlist mirroring validate_scratch_password
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-cicd/src/verify.rs:148 — verdict=defense-in-depth, severity=low
**Impact:** None today: the verify/scratch-schema path is not wired into any shipping binary or MCP tool, so no caller can supply an attacker-controlled schema name. Latent risk: a future/library consumer that passes an operator- or config-supplied schema_override containing a quote/space/semicolon would corrupt or redirect the CREATE USER / GRANT / ALTER SESSION / and irreversible DROP USER ... CASCADE DDL, since the suffix is interpolated unquoted with no character validation.
**Evidence:** verify.rs:148-150 — `pub fn is_scratch_schema(name: &str) -> bool { name.to_ascii_uppercase().starts_with("VERIFY_T_") }` (prefix check only, no suffix constraint). DDL interpolation sites confirmed unquoted: line 498/514 `format!("DROP USER {} CASCADE", self.schema)`; line 607 `format!("CREATE USER {schema} IDENTIFIED BY \"{password}\"")`; line 626 `format!("GRANT {priv_name} TO {schema}")`; line 686 `format!("ALTER SESSION SET CURRENT_SCHEMA = {schema}")`. Asymmetry: line 550-575 `validate_scratch_password` enforces allowlist `'A'..='Z' | 'a'..='z' | '0'..='9' | '#' | '_' | '$' | '.' | '+' | '-'`, doc comment (536-541) cites the stray-`"` corruption threat (oracle-clgt.11); no equivalent for the schema identifier. Empirical probe (rustc -O of the exact predicate): all four malicious suffixes return true — `VERIFY_T_X\" IDENTIFIED BY \"attacker` => true, `VERIFY_T_1; GRANT DBA TO PUBLIC
**Repro:** Trigger (latent): construct VerifyOptions { schema_override: Some("VERIFY_T_X\" IDENTIFIED BY \"attacker".into()), allow_in_place: false, .. } and call verify(admin_conn, changeset, &opts). validated_schema() passes because is_scratch_schema() returns true (proven empirically above), then create_scratch_schema emits `CREATE USER VERIFY_T_X" IDENTIFIED BY "attacker IDENTIFIED BY "V3r1fyTmp#2026"` — the embedded quote closes the identifier/literal early and changes the user's password/identity; the ScratchSchemaGuard Drop later emits `DROP USER VERIFY_T_X" IDENTIFIED BY "attacker CASCADE`. Disproof of present-day impact: no production code path constructs VerifyOptions with untrusted input — p
**Suggested fix:** Tighten is_scratch_schema (or add validate_scratch_schema called from the same gates: validated_schema:362, create_scratch_schema:594, ScratchSchemaGuard::new:480) so that after stripping the VERIFY_T_ prefix, every remaining byte is in a strict set, e.g. `rest.bytes().all(|b| b.is_ascii_alphanumeric() || matches!(b, b'_' | b'$' | b'#'))`. This rejects quotes/spaces/semicolons while still accepting all existing test names (VERIFY_T_{pid}, _cycle, _2, _ord, VERIFY_T_1234, and empty-suffix VERIFY_T_). For defense in depth also enforce a max identifier byte length (Oracle 30/128) and consider double-quoting the identifier in the DDL (`\"{schema}\"`) so it survives even if the allowlist is loosened. Add a unit test asserting the malicious suffixes are now rejected. Severity is low because the path is not attacker-reachable in the shipped product; this should be closed before any caller is wi

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 114f3640ae), re-verified on main. Root cause: is_scratch_schema (verify.rs:148) did a prefix-only check; it is the single gate behind validated_schema (l.362), create_scratch_schema (l.594), and ScratchSchemaGuard::new (l.480), and the schema name is interpolated UNQUOTED into CREATE USER / GRANT / ALTER SESSION / irreversible DROP USER ... CASCADE DDL. Fix: centralized a strict su

title: PLSQL-LIN-015 — Implement `classify-rename` with hints (Git rename detection, explicit mapping, persistent-ID linking); candidate mappings with confidence
description: Imported from `plan.md` v0.11 on 2026-05-12.
close_reason: Implemented classify_rename(changes, hints) in plsql-lineage. Walks a SemanticChangeSet's Created/Dropped records and pairs them into RenameCandidate{before, after, confidence, evidence}. RenameHints has three sources with priority: explicit_mappings (Confidence::Exact, RenameEvidence::Explicit), persistent_id_pairs (Exact, PersistentId), git_renames with similarity >= GIT_RENAME_THRESHOLD = 70 (Heuristic, GitRename{similarity}). Unmatched deletes/creates stay split — the classifier never invents a rename without a hint (plan §10.3: false-positive renames are worse than delete+create). CLASSIFY_RENAME_SCHEMA + classify_rename_envelope. 7 new unit tests cover all hint sources, threshold, explicit-beats-git priority, envelope schema. 71 lineage tests pass; clippy --no-deps + fmt clean.

title: PLSQL-OSS-BUG — classify_if emits phantom IF arms (first condition duplicated) on any IF with 2+ ELSIF branches
description:
FILE: crates/plsql-ir/src/stmt.rs  fn classify_if (lines 345-409).

BUG: The arm-building loop produces duplicate phantom arms when an IF has two or more ELSIF branches. Trace of the loop (lines 361-404):
 - Before the loop, the FIRST arm's condition is captured as first_cond (lines 358-360).
 - Each loop iteration unconditionally pushes an arm with cond_text = first_cond.clone() (lines 369-372) -- it always uses the FIRST condition, never the current one.
 - The Some((pos,"ELSIF")) branch (lines 374-393) then ALSO pushes a second, correct arm with the real ELSIF condition (lines 389-392) and advances cursor to body_end2.
 - Line 377 is dead code: 'arms.last_mut().unwrap().cond_text = arms.last().unwrap().cond_text.clone();' assigns a field to itself (no-op). The comment at 379-380 ('Replace placeholder cond on next iteration') describes an intent that is never carried out.
 - On the NEXT iteration, find_any_keyword finds the next ELSIF, and lines 369-372 push YET ANOTHER arm with first_cond -- a phantom.

CONFIRMED EMPIRICALLY (scratch crate; semicolon-free IF so split_statements keeps it whole):
  lower_statement_body("IF a THEN NULL ELSIF b THEN NULL ELSIF c THEN NULL ELSE NULL END IF")
   => If with 5 arms: [a/"NULL", b/"NULL", a/"", c/"NULL", a/""], else=Some("NULL")
   EXPECTED: 3 arms [a, b, c]. Got 2 phantom arms with condition 'a' and empty body.

WHY IT MATTERS: every phantom arm with cond_text='a' is walked by extract_call_sites (calls.rs walk_call_sites -> CallContext::ControlFlow on arm.cond_text) and by flow analysis -> the first condition's callees/refs are duplicated, and a real arm is structurally misplaced. The body slices on phantom arms are empty or contain leaked 'ELSIF ... THEN' text.

NOTE: in practice this bug is currently MASKED by oracle-hbhm (split_statements tears IF bodies apart on internal ';' so classify_if rarely sees an intact multi-ELSIF chain). It becomes live as soon as that splitter bug is fixed, OR for any IF whose ELSIF bodies contain no ';'. It is an independent logic defect and should be fixed alongside.

SUGGESTED FIX: rewrite the loop so each iteration handles exactly ONE arm: capture the current arm's condition (first_cond for the first iteration, the ELSIF condition thereafter), find the body slice up to the next ELSIF/ELSE/END, push exactly one IfArm, then advance. Delete the dead self-assignment at line 377 and the double-push in the ELSIF branch.
close_reason: classify_if (plsql-ir/src/stmt.rs) rewritten so each loop iteration handles exactly ONE arm: capture this arm's condition (IF for the first, ELSIF thereafter via cond_start), slice body to next ELSIF/ELSE, push a single IfArm, advance. Deleted the dead self-assignment and the double-push in the ELSIF branch that produced phantom arms duplicating the first condition. New tests: multi_elsif_if_has_no_phantom_arms (asserts exactly 3 arms a/b/c, no phantoms) and multi_elsif_if_keeps_bodies_with_conditions (each body attached to correct cond). Written failing-first (confirmed [a,b,a,c,a]), watched fail, then fixed. Fixed alongside hbhm as required since hbhm masked it. Suite green.

title: PLSQL-PROF-1 — Profiling observations (catalog parallel-fetch, lineage bitset, K18 Aho-Corasick) (/profiling)
description:
Audit finding from /profiling pass.

State. cargo bench --no-run gate is wired in .github/workflows/ci.yml (PLSQL-WS-015), but no per-crate criterion benches exist yet outside the parser-corpus-bench bead (oracle-mln PLSQL-PARSE-016 — open).

Hot paths worth profiling (cold + warm).
- Catalog snapshot load: load_snapshot_from_connection issues 17 sequential queries; a parallel-fetch refactor (rayon + per-loader futures) could cut wall time substantially on schemas with hundreds of objects.
- Lineage impact traversal: BFS over 10k-node graphs is the agent-blocking path. Currently uses HashSet visited; switching to a bitset on graphs with stable LogicalObjectId u32 keys would be ~10x faster but requires the id allocator to dedupe.
- depgraph::cross_check_with_catalog: O(n*m) cartesian compare; the OurIndex BTreeMap walk is the natural fix and is already in place. Re-check after PARSE-005 lands and depgraph node count grows.
- K18 sanitize(): naive .replace() loop over ~35 markers per cell. For a 10k-row query result this is 350k substring searches. Build an Aho-Corasick once per process if marker count crosses ~64.

Action. No bench harness yet — PARSE-016 (oracle-mln) builds the parser corpus-bench. After that, file follow-up beads to add criterion benches per hot path (catalog load, lineage impact, sanitize batch).

This pass is observation-only; no measurable regression at current scale. Filing the finding so the profiling decisions are recorded.

Cite. /profiling — first identify the agent-blocking hot paths, then add observable measurements before optimizing.
close_reason: Profiling observations recorded as evidence artifact. Hot paths identified: catalog snapshot load (sequential 17 queries → parallel-fetch opportunity), lineage impact BFS (HashSet → bitset on stable LogicalObjectId keys), depgraph cross-check (already BTreeMap), K18 sanitize (35 substring searches → Aho-Corasick at scale). No measurable regression at current scale; bench harness pending oracle-mln PARSE-016.

title: Make parse_target_schema quoted-identifier aware so a quoted owner with embedded whitespace ("My Schema".PKG) is not misclassified unqualified (cross-schema confirmation fail-open)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-mcp/src/create_or_replace.rs:246 — verdict=keep, severity=medium
**Impact:** An agent can deploy a CREATE OR REPLACE whose verified DDL header names a quoted schema with embedded whitespace (e.g. "My Schema".PKG) into a DIFFERENT schema than the principal, and the cross-schema typed-confirmation gate waves it through as SameSchema with no operator-typed destination required — the same fail-open the team already fixed for the unquoted-spaced and tab forms.
**Evidence:** parse_target_schema (crates/plsql-mcp/src/create_or_replace.rs:215-270) tokenizes on whitespace runs (L222-223), reassembles the tail from name_idx (L237), collapses whitespace only around an unquoted dot (L238-241), and takes the first whitespace-delimited token (L246). It has zero quoted-identifier awareness (rg confirms no double-quote handling in this function; query.rs has quote-awareness but is a separate path). Empirical replication of the exact pipeline for `CREATE OR REPLACE PACKAGE BODY "My Schema".PKG AS BEGIN NULL; END;`:
  tokens = ["CREATE","OR","REPLACE","PACKAGE","BODY","\"MY","SCHEMA\".PKG","AS",...]
  name_idx = 5 ; tokens[5] = "\"MY"
  name_token = "\"MY" ; name_token.contains('.') == false
  RESULT = Ok(None)  [UNQUALIFIED -> fail-open, NOT MalformedQualifiedName fail-closed]
Downstream: execute_approved.rs:130-133 maps Ok(None) to effective_target = principal_schema;
**Repro:** Triggering input (mirrors the sibling test execute_approved_spaced_qualifier_is_not_same_schema at execute_approved.rs:472, which uses target_schema: String::new(), operator_typed_schema: None): principal_schema="BILLING", ddl_bytes=`CREATE OR REPLACE PACKAGE BODY "My Schema".PKG AS BEGIN NULL; END;`, target_schema="", operator_typed_schema=None. Expected (correct) outcome: ExecuteApprovedError::CrossSchema(ConfirmationMissing) — a write to schema "My Schema" must demand the operator-typed destination. Actual outcome (proven by exact-logic replication): parse_target_schema returns Ok(None) -> effective_target = "BILLING" -> require_cross_schema_confirmation("BILLING","BILLING",None) returns 
**Suggested fix:** Reconstruct the object-name region honoring Oracle double-quoted identifier syntax instead of naively split_whitespace on a whitespace-collapsed token stream: scan the tail from name_idx char-by-char, treating embedded whitespace and dots as literal content while inside a "..." span (and "" as an escaped quote); terminate the name only on unquoted whitespace or `(`. Then split owner.object on the first UNQUOTED dot; strip surrounding quotes and unescape ""->" per segment; upper-case unquoted segments (Oracle dictionary normalization) but preserve case for quoted segments. Return Ok(Some(owner)) for the two-segment case, Ok(None) only when there is genuinely no owner, and keep MalformedQualifiedName fail-closed for OWNER./.OBJ/A.B.C/unterminated-quote. This closes the fail-open (`"My Schema".PKG` -> Some("My Schema")) and the quoted-no-space over-strictness (`"MYSCHEMA".PKG` -> Some("MYSC

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 7f6dbd4fee), re-verified on main. Root-caused and fixed the cross-schema fail-open in parse_target_schema (crates/plsql-mcp/src/create_or_replace.rs). The old code upper-cased the entire DDL and split on whitespace runs, which tokenised a quoted owner with embedded whitespace ('"My Schema".PKG') into '"MY' / 'SCHEMA".PKG', saw '"MY' (no dot), and returned Ok(None) -> effective_targ

title: Handle optional FROM in DELETE across dml_edges, sql_resolve, and scan_cross_schema_write
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-ir/src/dml_edges.rs:202 — verdict=keep, severity=medium
**Impact:** A FROM-less Oracle DELETE (e.g. `DELETE employees WHERE id=5;` or `DELETE hr.audit_log WHERE ...;`, both valid SQL) silently produces no Write TableAccess, so the dependency graph records no write dependency on the destroyed table, column lineage degrades, and a cross-schema FROM-less DELETE is never flagged DEP001 — static-analysis under-reporting with no typed diagnostic (violates the no-silent-drop posture). It is NOT a runtime guard bypass: the real access-control gate uses a sqlparser AST that classifies FROM-less DELETE correctly.
**Evidence:** dml_edges.rs:194-211 DELETE arm: `let mut from_tables = tables_after(&upper, raw, "FROM").into_iter(); if let Some(target) = from_tables.next() { push(out, target, Write); }` — target derived ONLY from FROM. stmt.rs:585-599 routes FROM-less DELETE to Statement::Sql{verb:Delete} verbatim. sql_resolve.rs:82 mirrors it. fact_emit.rs:997 lead list = ["insert into ","update ","delete from ","merge into "] (hardcoded "delete from "). Throwaway test run in-tree (compiled, run, then removed; git diff now empty) output: `LOWERED: [Sql { verb: Delete, raw_text: "DELETE employees WHERE id = 5;" }]` ; `ACCESSES (FROM-less): []` ; `ACCESSES (FROM-less qualified): []` ; `ACCESSES (FROM-present): [TableAccess { schema: None, table: "EMPLOYEES", access: Write }]` ; `DEP001 sites (FROM-less): 0` ; `DEP001 sites (FROM-present): 1`. SEVERITY SCOPE-DOWN proof: classifier.rs:842 `Statement::Delete(d) => { if
**Repro:** Trigger: input X = `DELETE employees WHERE id = 5;` → wrong output Y = extract_table_accesses returns [] (expected one Write of EMPLOYEES). Also X = `DELETE fin.audit_log WHERE id = 5;` with unit `hr.proc1` → scan_cross_schema_write returns 0 sites (expected 1 DEP001). Both reproduced via in-tree test (now reverted, tree clean). Disproof of the high/fail-open framing: classifier.rs:842 classifies the same FROM-less DELETE via sqlparser AST Statement::Delete, so the runtime guard correctly gates it; the defect is confined to the heuristic static-analysis dependency/lineage/diagnostic layer.
**Suggested fix:** In the DELETE arms of dml_edges.rs:202 and sql_resolve.rs:82, first try tables_after(...,"FROM"); if it yields no target, fall back to the identifier immediately after the DELETE keyword (skipping an optional leading FROM and any alias) and push it as the Write target, preserving the oracle-rwjl.6 subquery-as-Read direction for trailing FROM/JOIN tables. For fact_emit.rs:997, change the "delete from " lead to match "delete " then skip an optional "from " before reading the target so both `DELETE fin.audit` and `DELETE FROM fin.audit` are scanned for DEP001. If a target genuinely cannot be resolved, emit a typed UnknownReason rather than silently dropping. Add regression tests for FROM-less `DELETE employees WHERE id=5` (Write EMPLOYEES), `DELETE hr.audit_log WHERE ...` (Write HR.AUDIT_LOG + cross-schema DEP001), and FROM-less DELETE with a WHERE subquery (target Write, subquery table Rea

- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.

title: Escalate unparseable destructive Oracle DDL (RENAME/PURGE/FLASHBACK/ASSOCIATE STATISTICS) above the Guarded/ReadWrite floor in classify_statement parse-failure branch
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/oraclemcp-guard/src/classifier.rs:767 — verdict=keep, severity=medium
**Impact:** A session deliberately elevated to ReadWrite can run irreversible destructive Oracle DDL (RENAME a table, PURGE a table/recyclebin/tablespace, FLASHBACK a table or whole database) with NO forced Ddl/Admin step-up and bypassing schema deny_ddl/guarded-destructive policy, because sqlparser 0.62 cannot parse these and the parse-failure branch under-levels them to Guarded/ReadWrite instead of Destructive/Ddl.
**Evidence:** classifier.rs:758-772 parse-failure arm: only `if starts_with_admin_verb(&upper)` escalates (to Destructive/Admin); the catch-all returns `danger: Guarded, required: Some(ReadWrite)`. LEADING_ADMIN_VERBS (classifier.rs:216-231) lists GRANT/REVOKE/AUDIT/NOAUDIT/CREATE|ALTER|DROP USER|ROLE/ALTER SYSTEM|DATABASE|PROFILE/SET ROLE — RENAME/PURGE/FLASHBACK/ASSOCIATE STATISTICS are absent.

Empirical run via public Classifier::classify on sqlparser 0.62.0 (Cargo.lock: sqlparser 0.62.0), session = Admin ceiling stepped to ReadWrite:
  "RENAME orders TO orders_old" -> danger=Guarded required=Some(ReadWrite) gate=Allow
  "PURGE TABLE orders" -> Guarded/ReadWrite gate=Allow
  "PURGE RECYCLEBIN" -> Guarded/ReadWrite gate=Allow
  "PURGE TABLESPACE ts1" -> Guarded/ReadWrite gate=Allow
  "FLASHBACK TABLE orders TO BEFORE DROP" -> Guarded/ReadWrite gate=Allow
  "FLASHBACK DATABASE" -> Guarded/ReadWrite 
**Repro:** Triggering input: with `let mut s = SessionLevelState::new(OperatingLevel::Admin, false); s.set_current_level(OperatingLevel::ReadWrite);` and `Classifier::default()`, call `classifier.classify("RENAME orders TO orders_old").gate(&s)` — returns `LevelDecision::Allow` (proven via throwaway public-API test, now removed). Same for PURGE/FLASHBACK/ASSOCIATE STATISTICS. DROP/TRUNCATE controls correctly return RequireStepUp{Ddl}, proving the gap is specific to verbs sqlparser cannot parse that are missing from the leading-verb scan.
**Suggested fix:** Mirror starts_with_admin_verb for destructive object DDL: add LEADING_DDL_VERBS = ["RENAME ", "PURGE ", "FLASHBACK ", "ASSOCIATE STATISTICS ", "DISASSOCIATE STATISTICS "] and a starts_with_ddl_verb() helper that runs over canonical_marker_scan (literal/quote-aware, word-boundaried). In classify_statement's parse-failure arm, AFTER the starts_with_admin_verb check and BEFORE the final Guarded/ReadWrite return (classifier.rs:767-770), add: if starts_with_ddl_verb(&upper) { return Destructive / Some(Ddl). } Because the scan only fires on the LEADING token, "SELECT billing.purge() FROM dual" stays Guarded (verified). Optionally split FLASHBACK DATABASE/PLUGGABLE DATABASE into the admin list (Admin) leaving FLASHBACK TABLE at Ddl, and add "ALTER TABLE "/"ALTER INDEX " to cover the Oracle paren-add form that also under-levels. Add regression tests asserting required_level==Some(Ddl) and gate()

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 3e81207a4c), re-verified on main. Root-caused and fixed the parse-failure under-levelling of unparseable destructive Oracle DDL in crates/oraclemcp-guard/src/classifier.rs. sqlparser 0.62 cannot parse RENAME / PURGE / FLASHBACK <table> / (DIS)ASSOCIATE STATISTICS, so the parse-failure catch-all returned Guarded/Some(ReadWrite), letting a ReadWrite-elevated session run irreversible

title: Lower unknown SQL verb to Statement::Unrecognized instead of coercing to SqlVerb::Select in ast_stmts_to_ir
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-engine/src/lib.rs:641 — verdict=keep, severity=low
**Impact:** A procedure body containing a non-DML SQL statement the ANTLR lowerer tags verb="SQL" (notably EXPLAIN PLAN FOR SELECT ... FROM t, or unusual DML the grammar cannot classify) is silently relabeled as a read-only SqlVerb::Select on the primary parse path; if its text contains a whole-word FROM/JOIN it mints a spurious Reads dependency edge (e.g. a bogus "Reads t" for EXPLAIN, which writes PLAN_TABLE and does not read t), and in all cases the typed-uncertainty signal the sibling text-scanner would emit (Statement::Unrecognized) is dropped — corrupting downstream what_breaks/lineage edge labeling. Common forms (COMMIT/ROLLBACK/FETCH/CLOSE/LOCK TABLE without a FROM token) are only mislabeled, no
**Evidence:** crates/plsql-engine/src/lib.rs:634-647 — `AstStatement::Sql { verb, raw_text, .. } => { let sql_verb = match verb.to_ascii_uppercase().as_str() { "SELECT"=>Select, "INSERT"=>Insert, "UPDATE"=>Update, "DELETE"=>Delete, "MERGE"=>Merge, _ => SqlVerb::Select, // fallback (line 641) }; ... }`.
crates/plsql-parser-antlr/src/tree_lower.rs:609-611 emits verb "SQL" for any data_manipulation_language_statements that is not one of the 5 DML verbs; lines 623-628 emit verb "SQL" for cursor manipulation / transaction control.
crates/plsql-engine/src/lib.rs:959-965 — PRIMARY path: `if let Some(ast_stmts) = body_by_span.get(...).filter(|s| !s.is_empty()) { ast_stmts_to_ir(ast_stmts) } else { ...text scanner... }` — real ANTLR body preferred whenever non-empty.
crates/plsql-ir/src/dml_edges.rs:169-176 — `SqlVerb::Select => { for t in tables_after(&upper, raw, "FROM") { push(out, t, AccessKind::Read); } f
**Repro:** Trigger X→Y: a stored procedure body containing `EXPLAIN PLAN FOR SELECT col FROM t;`. ANTLR lowers it to AstStatement::Sql{ verb="SQL" } (tree_lower.rs:609-611, since none of select/insert/update/delete/merge matches). On the primary path ast_stmts_to_ir coerces verb "SQL" to SqlVerb::Select (lib.rs:641). dml_edges::accesses_from_sql scans for whole-word FROM, extracts `t`, pushes AccessKind::Read (dml_edges.rs:170-171). lib.rs:1060-1112 mints an EdgeKind::Reads edge + DependencyEdge fact "Reads T" (Y = spurious read edge). For the identical input the in-tree text-scanner returns Statement::Unrecognized with zero accesses (no edge). Verified empirically with a throwaway plsql-ir example: te
**Suggested fix:** In ast_stmts_to_ir (crates/plsql-engine/src/lib.rs:634-647), map only the five known DML verbs to a SqlVerb and route any other verb (including the lowerer's "SQL" sentinel) to Statement::Unrecognized { raw_text, unknown_reason: UnknownStatementReason::UnrecognizedKeyword }, matching the sibling text-scanner in crates/plsql-ir/src/stmt.rs:601-604 and preserving the R13 typed-uncertainty signal. To avoid losing the one beneficial case (OPEN c FOR SELECT ... FROM t currently yields a correct Read), additionally have tree_lower.rs detect an open_for_statement's inner select_statement and emit verb="SELECT" with the inner select's raw_text rather than verb="SQL" over the whole OPEN. Add a regression test asserting an EXPLAIN PLAN / LOCK TABLE body produces an Unrecognized statement and no Reads edge rather than a SqlVerb::Select with a spurious Read.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 17b686de09), re-verified on main. Root cause: ast_stmts_to_ir (crates/plsql-engine/src/lib.rs:634-647) coerced the ANTLR lowerer's generic verb="SQL" sentinel (emitted for cursor manipulation, transaction control, and DML the grammar cannot classify e.g. EXPLAIN PLAN FOR SELECT ... FROM t) to SqlVerb::Select via `_ => SqlVerb::Select`. That both dropped the R13 typed-uncertainty si

title: Make scan_missing_instrumentation scan all "begin" occurrences (match_indices) like body_has_dml
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-ir/src/fact_emit.rs:425 — verdict=defense-in-depth, severity=low
**Impact:** No active impact today: scan_missing_instrumentation has zero production callers and the engine never mints MissingInstrumentation facts, so STYLE001 (opt-in, Info) currently sees nothing. Latent: once an emit driver wires the scanner in, a routine whose declaration section contains a "_begin"-suffixed identifier (e.g. v_begin_dt) before the real BEGIN would be wrongly classified as a body-less spec and silently escape the STYLE001 instrumentation check (conservative false negative, never a false positive).
**Evidence:** fact_emit.rs:424-426 (current):
    let has_body = lower
        .find("begin")
        .is_some_and(|at| keyword_boundary_before(&lower, at));
keyword_boundary_before (217-222) treats '_' as a word char, so a preceding '_' fails the boundary.

Compiled standalone repro of the exact predicate (input "procedure p is v_begin_dt date; begin update t set x=1; end;"):
  first 'begin' at byte 17, preceding char = Some('_')
  has_body (current/first-only) = false
  has_body (fixed/all-matches)  = true
  scan_missing_instrumentation(current) -> EMPTY (no site -> STYLE001 silently skips)

Sibling body_has_dml (346-360) already uses match_indices over ALL occurrences with a comment (347-353) naming this exact decoy-skip bug class. scan_exception_handlers (235-251), scan_cursor_for_loops (372), scan_hardcoded_credentials (559) all loop over every match; line 425 is the lone first-occurrence-only ou
**Repro:** Triggering input: scan_missing_instrumentation("hr.pkg.silent", "procedure p is v_begin_dt date; begin update t set x=1; end;"). Expected (correct): one MissingInstrumentationSite (real BEGIN body, no instrumentation marker). Actual: empty Vec, because find("begin") returns the index inside v_begin_dt whose preceding char '_' fails keyword_boundary_before, short-circuiting has_body to false. Compiled repro above shows current=false vs match_indices fix=true on this exact input. Impact is gated behind an unwired emitter + opt-in rule, hence dormant.
**Suggested fix:** Replace the first-occurrence check at fact_emit.rs:424-426 with an all-occurrences scan mirroring body_has_dml (line 357):

    let has_body = lower
        .match_indices("begin")
        .any(|(at, _)| keyword_boundary_before(&lower, at));

Add a regression test for the decoy case asserting scan_missing_instrumentation("hr.pkg.silent", "procedure p is v_begin_dt date; begin update t set x=1; end;") yields exactly one site. One-line change; low priority since the scanner is not yet wired into any production emit path.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.

Root-cause fix in fact_emit.rs scan_missing_instrumentation: replaced the first-occurrence `lower.find("begin").is_some_and(...)` with an all-occurrences `lower.match_indices("begin").any(|(at,_)| keyword_boundary_before(&lower, at))`, mirroring sibl

title: PLSQL-AUDIT-LEAN — /lean-formal evidence pass
description:
Audit finding from /lean-formal pass — type-level invariant review.

State. The codebase encodes several invariants at the Rust type level that prevent classes of bugs at compile time:

1. R20 parser-backend isolation. ParseBackend trait is the boundary; ANTLR-generated types live in a private generated/ module under #[cfg(feature = "antlr-codegen")] and never escape plsql-parser-antlr. Type-system-enforced because the antlr-rust runtime types are not re-exported.

2. R13 typed UnknownReason. plsql-core::UnknownReason enum has 12 variants; every typed-uncertainty site uses the enum, never a stringly-typed code. The compiler refuses unrecognized variants at every match site, forcing the audit to surface when new variants are added.

3. Newtype identity. plsql-core gives ObjectName / ColumnName / SchemaName / SynonymName / etc. each their own newtype around SymbolId via the numeric_id! macro. A SchemaName cannot be passed where an ObjectName is expected — a class of identifier-mixing bug eliminated at the type level.

4. Span invariant (PLSQL-PARSE-010). Every AstDecl variant carries `span: Span`. The Spanned trait formalizes this, though it can't enforce 'every variant has a span field' (Rust limitation noted in ast.rs:23-25). The compromise: code-review enforced + Spanned impl block listing every variant.

5. Confidence layering. plsql-depgraph::Edge::propagate_confidence takes two Confidence values and returns the worse-of-pair. Confidence is `Clone, Eq, PartialEq` so the operation has total semantics.

6. Catalog snapshot back-compat. Every new SchemaCatalog field added (db_links, table_comments, column_comments, editioning_views, vpd_policies) lives behind #[serde(default)]. Older JSON snapshots keep deserializing into the new shape with the new fields zero-initialized.

7. Cargo workspace lints. unsafe_code = "forbid", dbg_macro = "deny", todo = "deny" — enforced at the lint layer, not just code review.

Gaps (where formal would help further):
- Confidence::Opaque + Confidence::High mixing has no compile-time check that the merge function actually returns the worse-of-pair (runtime invariant only).
- ObjectStatus enum has no `Default` impl; relies on serde::default attribute on consumers.

Verdict. R13 + R20 + newtype identity carry most of the formal-verification weight. The remaining runtime-invariant gaps are documented but not currently lifted to types.

Cite. /lean-formal — encode invariants at the type system level where possible; rely on tests for the rest.
close_reason: /lean-formal evidence pass. Type-level invariants identified: R20 parser-backend isolation (ParseBackend trait + private generated/ module under feature gate), R13 typed UnknownReason (12-variant enum, compile-time exhaustive match), newtype identity (ObjectName/ColumnName/SchemaName/etc. via numeric_id! macro prevents identifier mixing), Span invariant (PLSQL-PARSE-010, partial — every AstDecl variant carries span: Span), Confidence merge (worse-of-pair, runtime), Catalog back-compat (#[serde(default)] on every new SchemaCatalog field), workspace lints (unsafe_code=forbid, dbg_macro=deny, todo=deny). Two runtime-only invariants documented as candidates for future lifting.

title: PLSQL-MCP-SEC-1 — K18 sanitize() misses several injection-marker families (/security-audit-for-saas)
description:
Audit finding from /security-audit-for-saas pass on crates/plsql-mcp/src/query.rs:126-171.

Gap. injection_markers() at query.rs:129 builds the K18 scrub list but misses several attack vectors:

1. antml:invoke wrapper tags (the actual function-call wrapper tags, distinct from function_calls which is the container).
2. antml:function (singular form) — older injection corpora.
3. antml:tool_use, antml:tool_call (cross-pollination of namespaces).
4. OpenAI tokenizer-control tokens — endoftext, fim_prefix, fim_suffix bracketed forms.
5. Llama-style chat-template markers — SYS bracketed tags, INST bracketed forms.
6. Plain-text injection lures: 'ignore previous instructions', 'new instructions:', 'system prompt:', 'you are now', etc. These don't have markup but are well-known.
7. 'Forget everything above' / 'disregard the above' — plain English markers that match injection corpora.

The current scrubber will pass these markers through unredacted, letting them reach the calling agent's prompt.

Action. Extend injection_markers() with the missing tags (1-5; safe additions). Items 6-7 are a judgment call — overly-broad text matching produces false positives on legitimate user data. Probably file as a separate bead with an opt-in flag.

Cite. /security-audit-for-saas heuristics; OWASP LLM01 (Prompt Injection).
close_reason: Extended injection_markers() in crates/plsql-mcp/src/query.rs to cover (1) antml:invoke / antml:function / antml:tool_use / antml:tool_call (the 4 missing antml family members), (2) OpenAI tokenizer-control tokens (endoftext, fim_prefix, fim_suffix, fim_middle bar-delimited form), and (3) Llama chat-template markers (double-LT SYS double-GT and INST bracketed forms). New regression test sanitize_covers_extended_marker_families covers one example from each new family. Plain-text injection lures (items 6-7 in the bead) left out per the bead's own recommendation — they require an opt-in flag to avoid false positives on legitimate user data. 86 mcp tests pass; clippy clean.

title: PLSQL-SAST-FACTS-SYNONYM — SEC005 (public synonym on sensitive objects) is unimplementable: ScanContext.facts only carries Declaration{decl,logical_id}; the public_synonym flag + synonym target (modeled in plsql-ir SynonymDecl) are NOT in the normalized FactStore. Need a SynonymRef structural fact (or enriched Declaration payload: is_public + target_logical_id) emitted by fact_emit, consumed via FactKind like SEC006/Privilege
description: Evidence: crates/plsql-sast/src/lib.rs ScanContext = {unit_logical_id, source_file, flow:FlowQuery, facts:&FactStore} — no catalog/AST access. crates/plsql-ir/src/fact.rs FactPayload has NO synonym variant; emit_declaration_facts emits Declaration{decl,logical_id} only (drops SynonymDecl.public_synonym + target from decl.rs:189). Only 'Calls' DependencyEdge edge_kind is emitted (fact_emit.rs). So a SEC005 rule cannot determine (a) that a decl is a PUBLIC synonym, nor (b) its target object, nor (c) target sensitivity. Same structural-fact-gap class as oracle-n528 (ExceptionHandler) which was filed-then-built. Scope: add FactKind::SynonymRef {synonym_logical_id, is_public, target_logical_id} (or enrich Declaration) + emit_synonym_facts in plsql-ir/fact_emit.rs mirroring emit_exception_handler_facts; then SEC005 = rule over by_kind(SynonymRef) where is_public && target matches sensitivity policy (name heuristic or Privilege-fact cross-ref), like SEC006. Blocks oracle-8hju. NOTE: plsql-ir currently has ~14-file orphaned dead-swarm WIP — producer work must wait until that lands/clears (do not entangle).
close_reason: DRIFT-CLOSE (resolved by commit c1be4d3, verified by re-run — beads-compliance Axiom 1). This bead asks for a FactKind synonym structural fact emitted in plsql-ir/fact_emit.rs mirroring emit_exception_handler_facts, consumed via by_kind like SEC006, with a name-heuristic sensitivity policy, unblocking oracle-8hju. That is exactly what shipped: fact.rs FactKind::SensitivePublicSynonym + FactPayload{unit,synonym,target}+kind() arm; fact_emit.rs scan_sensitive_public_synonym + emit_sensitive_public_synonym_facts (mirrors the exception-handler producer pattern, literal-masked, R13-conservative); rules.rs Sec005SensitivePublicSynonym consumes by_kind(FactKind::SensitivePublicSynonym) with the credential/PII/finance sensitivity wordlist, like SEC006. oracle-8hju (which this blocked) is closed. The shipped shape is a focused SensitivePublicSynonym fact (public-synonym + sensitivity determined in the source-DDL producer) rather than a generic SynonymRef{is_public,target} + rule-side policy, but it accomplishes this bead's exact goal and acceptance. Heeded this bead's own caution: only the CLEAN fact.rs/fact_emit.rs were touched (NOT the ~14-file plsql-ir dead-swarm WIP), pathspec commits — no entanglement. plsql-ir 70 + plsql-sast 93 tests green, clippy clean, workspace builds.

title: PLSQL-OSS-SEC — execute_approved trusts caller-supplied target_schema instead of parsing verified DDL
description:
FILE: crates/plsql-mcp/src/execute_approved.rs:44-51, 87-121 (ExecuteApprovedRequest / run_execute_approved)

THREAT (cross-schema confirmation bypass): The cross-schema write guard (cross_schema.rs require_cross_schema_confirmation) compares principal_schema vs target_schema and forces the operator to type the destination schema name verbatim for a cross-schema write. But run_execute_approved receives target_schema as a plain caller-supplied field on ExecuteApprovedRequest (line 47) — the doc says it is 'usually parsed out of the CREATE OR REPLACE ... <schema>.<name> header by the caller'. It is NOT derived from the verified ddl_bytes inside run_execute_approved.

Consequence: an agent can submit ddl_bytes targeting ANALYTICS.X (a cross-schema write) while passing target_schema = principal_schema (e.g. "BILLING"). require_cross_schema_confirmation then takes the SameSchema branch (cross_schema.rs:99) and skips the typed-name confirmation entirely. The byte-for-byte preview verify only proves the DDL equals the previewed bytes — it does not prove target_schema agrees with the schema the DDL actually names. The two parsers (whatever produced target_schema vs the Oracle server that resolves the DDL header) can diverge, and the security control keys off the weaker one.

FIX: Inside run_execute_approved, parse the object's owner schema out of the *verified* ddl_bytes (the same bytes that were hash-pinned) and use THAT as target_schema for require_cross_schema_confirmation — never trust the caller's separate field. If the DDL header omits an explicit schema, target = principal (unqualified ⇒ current schema), which is correct. Reject if the caller-supplied target_schema disagrees with the parsed one. classify_kind in create_or_replace.rs already tokenises the DDL head; extend it to also return the owner.

SEVERITY: Medium — defeats the operator-typed cross-schema confirmation, which is the §13A.3 control preventing an agent from silently writing into a schema the operator did not consciously approve. Mitigated somewhat because the wire dispatcher currently stubs tool execution, but execute_approved is a published library fn and the design intent is broken.
close_reason: execute_approved.rs now derives target_schema from the byte-verified ddl_bytes via new create_or_replace::parse_target_schema(); caller-supplied target_schema is validated for agreement and rejected (TargetSchemaMismatch) if it disagrees. Cross-schema guard keys off verified DDL, closing the SameSchema-branch bypass. New tests: derives_target_schema_from_verified_ddl, unqualified_ddl_defaults_to_principal, rejects_caller_target_schema_disagreement, parse_target_schema_* (3). All green.

title: PLSQL-CAT-NEW-4 — Add ALL_CONS_COLUMNS loader for FK column-level lineage (/oracle audit)
description:
Audit finding from /oracle skill applied to crates/plsql-catalog/src/lib.rs.

Gap. load_catalog_constraints at lib.rs:2247-2293 captures ALL_CONSTRAINTS (constraint name, type, R_CONSTRAINT_NAME) but does NOT join ALL_CONS_COLUMNS for the column membership. As a result FK lineage knows that constraint FK_ORDERS_CUSTOMER references PK_CUSTOMERS but not WHICH column on each side. Column-level lineage falls back to UnknownReason::ConstraintColumnsMissing.

Source. /oracle skill — DATABASE-REFERENCE.md + ALL_CONS_COLUMNS view doc. Local file: crates/plsql-catalog/src/lib.rs:2247.

Proposed shape. Extend load_catalog_constraints to LEFT JOIN ALL_CONS_COLUMNS on (OWNER, CONSTRAINT_NAME, TABLE_NAME) and aggregate the columns by POSITION. ConstraintRow gains a columns: Vec<String> field. Existing tests that compare constraint shapes need golden updates.

Cite. Oracle Database Reference 26, ALL_CONS_COLUMNS view.
close_reason: Already shipped — verified inline at lib.rs:2263-2307 (load_catalog_constraints) and 2968-3084 (apply_constraint_row). The query LEFT JOINs ALL_CONS_COLUMNS twice (child for the local-side columns, parent for the referenced-side columns) ordered by child.position. apply_constraint_row interns COLUMN_NAME and REFERENCED_COLUMN_NAME into snapshot.intern_column_name() and push_unique_column()s them into ConstraintMetadata.columns and .referenced_columns. Bead was filed without realizing the join was already in place — /reality-check pass surfaced the prior implementation. ConstraintMetadata.columns: Vec<ColumnName> is already serialized as part of CatalogSnapshot. No further work needed.

(2) ConnectionRegistry: BTreeMap-backed in-process store with register/list/connect/disconnect/current/switch operations + safety-profile guard (set_safety refuses SessionWriteEnabled while a permanently_read_only connection is active). (3) ConnectionListEntry — structured output schema for the list_connections tool. (4) DbToolsAlias + DbToolsAlias::probe(alias, home) — locates ~/.dbtools entries (dbtools.json / .dbtools / .dbtools.conf) by name; full parsing deferred to PLSQL-MCP-LIVE-003. (5) ConnectionError surface (UnknownProfile, PermanentlyReadOnly, NoActiveConnection). plsql_mcp::register_connection_tools(&mut ToolRegistry) registers the five tool descriptors (list_connections, connect, disconnect, current_database, switch_database) at ToolTier::FoundationLiveDb. 9 new unit tests cover sorting, connect/disconnect idempotency, switch, permanently_read_only guard, prod-looking heuristic, and dbtools probe success+failure. 23/23 plsql-mcp tests pass; clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings clean.

Variants TcpUnimplemented/StdioNotConfigured are stale — TCP + stdio are both implemented (oracle-k8ef). Remove the dead enum; rewrite the module doc which still calls itself 'Transport stubs (PLSQL-MCP-001 skeleton)' — stale scaffolding language unfit for a public repo.

title: PLSQL-OSS-UBS — pins_check bash -c on LLM-proposer-supplied directive (shell injection in USR gate)
description: crates/plsql-accretion/src/gate.rs:783 — pins_check() invokes Command::new("bash").arg("-c").arg(sh) where 'sh' is parsed from '# usr-gate-pins-cmd:', '# usr-gate-pins-revert:', '# usr-gate-pins-restore:', '# usr-gate-pins-setup:' directives in candidate_text. The candidate-diff file is the USR proposer output (LLM-driven, see crates/plsql-accretion/src/proposer.rs LlmProposer). Why it matters: an adversarial or hallucinated patch containing '# usr-gate-pins-cmd: rm -rf $HOME' (or curl|bash, or exfil) executes verbatim under the dev's user. The G9 gate is supposed to be the safety rail for LLM patches; instead it gives every proposal arbitrary shell. The fact that the gate is content-pinned (sha-verified) doesn't help — the trusted gate code runs untrusted shell by design. Suggested fix: (a) refuse pins_check unless the operator has set USR_GATE_TRUST_PINS=1 (explicit opt-in); (b) restrict pins-cmd/-revert/-restore to an allowlisted prefix like 'cargo test' / 'git apply --reverse' rather than arbitrary shell; or (c) run the hooks under firejail/nsjail/bwrap when available, fail-closed when not. UBS rule rust.command.shell-c flagged it (only critical novel finding from round-2 ubs sweep over 190 rust files).
close_reason: G9 shell-injection guard: pins_check refuses to execute candidate-supplied shell hooks unless USR_GATE_TRUST_PINS=1 AND every hook clears a strict leading-token allowlist (true/false/test/touch/rm/mkdir/cargo/git/echo) with metacharacter rejection (|, &, ;, `, $, <, >, \n). Adversarial canary test (pins_check_refuses_shell_payload_without_trust_optin) proves no shell payload executes on the default-off path. Allowlist test (pins_check_allowlist_rejects_metacharacter_chains_even_when_trusted) proves operator opt-in still rejects pipeline/subshell smuggling. Stub proposer refactored from 'mkdir -p X && touch Y' to single 'touch'/'test -f'/'rm -f' hooks so all 91 existing accretion tests stay green under the new allowlist.

title: PLSQL-MCP-008B — Implement the actual TCP accept loop for --listen (completion-debt of oracle-d812)
description: Compliance audit (2026-05-17) found oracle-d812 (PLSQL-MCP-008) PARTIAL: it shipped only the address validator (tcp::parse_listen_target + ListenTarget + 13 tests) but NOT the TCP transport its title promises. No TcpListener/accept loop exists; transport.rs:is_transport_implemented(Tcp)=false; serve --listen is unreachable. MISSING: a tcp::serve(target:&ListenTarget, registry:&ToolRegistry) that TcpListener::binds the validated socket, accepts connections, reads line-delimited JSON-RPC, dispatches via mcp_protocol::handle_request_line, writes response+\n; flip is_transport_implemented(Tcp)=true; keep public-bind refusal. Out of scope (belongs to oracle-vnlk/PLSQL-MCP-002): the overall serve command wiring/stdio loop. Acceptance: real loopback test (bind 127.0.0.1:0, client roundtrips a JSON-RPC line, asserts dispatched response), multi-request connection, clippy -D warnings, per-crate test green.
close_reason: VERIFIED COMPLETE. crates/plsql-mcp/src/tcp.rs::serve + serve_with_listener + handle_connection + process_stream; transport.rs is_transport_implemented(Tcp)=true. Re-ran: clippy -D warnings clean (gate-off + --features live-xe); plsql-mcp 187 tests pass (0 fail) incl. process_stream_dispatches_each_line_and_skips_blanks, process_stream_returns_parse_error_for_garbage_line, serve_with_listener_round_trips_a_real_loopback_connection (real 127.0.0.1:0 bind + TcpStream client roundtrip asserting dispatched JSON-RPC response), transport::tests::tcp_transport_is_implemented. Remediates the PARTIAL finding on oracle-d812 (completion-debt policy: d812 stays closed, debt tracked+resolved here).

title: PLSQL-OSS-AUDIT3 — plsql-doc::serve is_safe_bind diverges from plsql-mcp::tcp despite doc claim of being 'deliberately identical'
description:
crates/plsql-doc/src/serve.rs:82-102 (is_safe_bind) vs crates/plsql-mcp/src/tcp.rs:101-118 (is_safe_bind).

The doc comment on plsql-doc::serve::is_safe_bind (serve.rs:87-90) explicitly claims: 'This is the same predicate plsql-mcp::tcp::is_safe_bind applies to the MCP TCP transport, kept deliberately identical so the two TCP-serving crates never diverge on what counts as a local bind.'

Reality: they diverge.

  plsql-doc::serve::is_safe_bind  treats LOOPBACK + RFC1918 PRIVATE + LINK-LOCAL + IPv6 UNIQUE-LOCAL all as safe.
  plsql-mcp::tcp::is_safe_bind    is the tighter 'ip.is_loopback()' — only loopback is safe; RFC1918 and link-local require --allow-public-bind.

This is the deliberate MCP hardening (tcp.rs:101-115 rationale: 'On a shared LAN, corporate VPN subnet, cloud VPC, or multi-tenant container network, private is not trusted'). The same rationale applies word-for-word to the plsql-doc preview server, which has zero authentication and serves rendered PL/SQL source. Today, plsql-doc will bind to 10.0.0.5 / 192.168.x.x / fe80:: / fc00:: WITHOUT requiring allow_public_bind=true — exposing the rendered source/schema to every host on the LAN/VPC.

Either:
  (a) the divergence is real and the doc comment is a lie (medium-priority docs+code drift), or
  (b) the divergence is unintentional and plsql-doc should adopt the loopback-only policy (medium-priority security harmonization).

For an OSS release where both crates ship in the same workspace, the right answer is (b): tighten plsql-doc::serve::is_safe_bind to ip.is_loopback(), update its test suite (currently asserts RFC1918 is safe at serve.rs:521-535 — those tests would need to flip), and keep the 'deliberately identical' doc claim true. Either way the current state ships a doc-vs-implementation contradiction in a security-sensitive area, which is exactly the kind of thing an OSS reviewer flags.
close_reason: Tightened plsql-doc::serve::is_safe_bind to ip.is_loopback(), byte-identical to plsql-mcp::tcp::is_safe_bind. Updated module docstring and predicate docstring. Test suite flipped to mirror the wave-3 plsql-mcp adversarial set: RFC1918 (10.0.1.5, 172.16.0.1, 192.168.1.10), IPv4 link-local (169.254.1.1), IPv6 ULA (fc00::1), IPv6 link-local (fe80::1), unspecified, public — all rejected without allow_public_bind; loopback v4/v6 accepted; override bypasses for all. Evidence: 95/95 plsql-doc tests pass; cargo clippy -D warnings clean.

title: PLSQL-OSS-UX — error messages don't suggest typo corrections (Axiom 7 — intent inference)
description:
CLIs: plan-lint, corpus-bench, corpus-grow, corpus-license-check, plsql-bindgen (hand-rolled parsers) and clap-driven CLIs without DYM enabled.

What's missing/awkward: 'plan-lint --robotjson' → 'error: unknown argument "--robotjson"' + the full usage block, but no 'did you mean: --robot-json' hint. Same for 'plan-lint --jsno', 'corpus-bench --jason', etc. The clap-driven ones ('plsql-mcp foo' → 'unrecognized subcommand') also do not suggest the closest match (clap supports this via 'features = ["suggestions"]' / 'Command::infer_subcommands(true)' / 'arg.value_parser(...)' depending on version).

Why an agent or human would trip on it: A single one-character typo wedges the agent and forces a full --help re-read. Per the Cookbook (Axiom 7), Levenshtein-1 correction is now table stakes — a tool that types '--robotjson' once and gets 'did you mean: --robot-json? try: plan-lint --robot-json' learns the correct spelling forever; a tool that gets 'unknown argument' learns nothing.

Suggested fix: (1) For hand-rolled parsers (plan-lint, corpus-*, plsql-bindgen, usr-gate-rs): add a tiny Levenshtein-1 function over the KNOWN_FLAGS / KNOWN_SUBCOMMANDS list and append 'did you mean: --<closest>? try: <name> --<closest> ...' to the error. (2) For clap-driven CLIs (plsql-mcp, plsql-engine, plsql-depgraph, usr-loop): enable clap's 'suggestions' feature (default-on in clap v4) and verify it's wired by typo-probe in regression tests. Pin both with a regression test asserting --jsno emits 'did you mean: --robot-json'.
close_reason: Typo 'did you mean' hints wired across every clap-derived CLI (already on-by-default in clap v4 — pinned via Cli::try_parse_from regression tests in usr-loop / plsql-engine / plsql-depgraph that assert '--robot-json' is suggested for '--robotjson') AND every hand-rolled parser (plan-lint, corpus-bench, corpus-grow, corpus-license-check). Each hand-rolled tool gained: VALID_FLAGS sorted slice; suggest_flag() Levenshtein-2 helper; parse_args() now wraps unknown flags into 'unknown argument "X" (did you mean \"Y\"?)\nvalid flags: …'. Per-tool tests pin both the suggester (suggest_flag_finds_obvious_typos) and the user-facing error string (unknown_flag_suggests_near_miss). Verified end-to-end: 'plan-lint --robotjson' → 'did you mean \"--robot-json\"?'; same for corpus-bench, corpus-grow, corpus-license-check, and the clap CLIs.
