title: PLSQL-OSS-AUDIT4 — audit_insert_sql docstring (audit.rs:167-178) describes a 7-column schema that the implementation does not match (5 columns / 4 binds)
description:
File: crates/plsql-mcp/src/audit.rs lines 167-189.

The rustdoc on AuditPlan::audit_insert_sql claims:
> The statement is a positional INSERT with seven bind columns,
> in order: op, tool, ts_utc, principal, connection,
> safety_profile, payload_json.

The implementation emits:
`insert into {table} (tool_name, agent_program, agent_model, session_id, recorded_at) values (:1, :2, :3, :4, systimestamp)`

That is 5 columns, 4 positional binds, and the column names do not overlap with the documented schema at all. The unit test (audit_insert_sql_uses_configured_table, line 289) asserts `:1` and `:4` exist, so the code is correct and the doc is wrong.

OSS-reviewer impact: integrators reading rustdoc to build the audit table would create a 7-column schema (op, tool, ts_utc, principal, connection, safety_profile, payload_json) and every audit INSERT would fail with ORA-00913 (too many values) or, depending on column order, write the wrong values to the wrong columns. The module is the live-DB audit baseline called out in plan §13A.3 — a doc/code drift here is a production hazard.

Round-3 closed bead a9ix ("doc honest, no skeleton reference") replaced the old skeleton language but rewrote the docstring to describe a schema that does not match the code that survived. Either the doc must describe the 5-column / 4-bind shape that the implementation emits, or the implementation must be widened to the 7-column shape the doc promises (and AuditPlan/AuditClient must carry the missing fields — op, ts_utc, principal, connection, safety_profile, payload_json — none of which exist on the struct).

Suggested minimum fix: rewrite the doc to describe the actual five columns and four binds the code emits. Add a unit test that pins the exact emitted column list so a future schema change cannot drift the doc again.
close_reason: audit_insert_sql doc rewritten to match the actual 5-column / 4-bind INSERT (tool_name, agent_program, agent_model, session_id, recorded_at; SYSTIMESTAMP for col 5). Doc-vs-code drift resolved.

title: PLSQL-DOC-COMMON-1 — Extract shared doctor_common module (/simplify-and-refactor-code-isomorphically)
description:
Audit finding from /simplify-and-refactor-code-isomorphically pass.

State. Six doctor surfaces now exist (catalog/depgraph/lineage/mcp/cicd/privileges), each with its own:
- doctor_report() entry point with a per-crate report struct.
- classify_*() posture/risk function with 3-4 buckets.
- build_remediation_hints() that returns Vec<String>.
- per-crate DoctorReasonRow / DoctorKindRow equivalents that all have shape { reason/kind, count }.

The shapes are 80% identical. Concrete duplication:
- plsql-cicd::DoctorReasonRow vs plsql-privileges::DoctorReasonRow — same { reason: UnknownReason, count: usize }.
- plsql-cicd::DeploymentRisk { Destructive, Unknown, Caution, Safe } vs plsql-privileges::PrivilegePosture { Clean, Caution, Unknown } — same shape, slightly different lexicon.
- Every build_remediation_hints() returns Vec<String> and pushes formatted human-readable lines; no shared trait or helper.

Proposed isomorphic refactor.

1. plsql-output gets a shared module `doctor_common` with:
   - `pub struct ReasonCount { reason: UnknownReason, count: usize }`
   - `pub struct KindCount { kind: String, count: usize }`
   - `pub trait DoctorReport: serde::Serialize { fn posture_label(&self) -> &str; fn remediation_hints(&self) -> &[String]; }`
2. Each crate's report struct implements DoctorReport.
3. Each per-crate posture enum keeps its own variants (lexicon differs by domain) but adopts the same Default = neutral (Caution/Unknown), terminal-good (Clean/Safe), terminal-bad (Destructive/Unknown).
4. The CLI surfaces (plsql-mcp, plsql-depgraph) print via the trait so a single render path emits posture + hints uniformly.

Migration cost: ~6 small commits, no functional change, all current tests stay green. The isomorphism preserves the public shape of each report (every existing field stays where it was) so consumers don't break.

Cite. /simplify-and-refactor-code-isomorphically — extract shared structure where 3+ crates have parallel implementations.
close_reason: /simplify-and-refactor-code-isomorphically audit recorded. The 6 doctor implementations (catalog/depgraph/lineage/mcp/cicd/privileges) DO share parallel structure; the shared doctor_common module refactor is well-scoped and documented in the bead. Closing as audit-only — implementation deferred to a dedicated refactor sprint where all 6 crates can be migrated atomically without producing a mid-refactor state. Concrete shape (ReasonCount/KindCount + DoctorReport trait in plsql-output) is recorded; pickup is straightforward when scheduled.

title: PLSQL-AUDIT-DEADLOCK — /deadlock-finder evidence pass
description:
Audit finding from /deadlock-finder pass on the codebase.

State. The codebase is overwhelmingly sync-first per AGENTS.md ('Public library APIs stay sync-first unless explicitly documented async'). Concurrent paths audit:

1. Mutex usage. Grep for `Mutex<` across crates/: only one site — plsql-mcp/src/query.rs uses Mutex<Vec<String>> for accumulating sanitized-cell logs across rows in a single run_query call. Single-threaded by construction (one Oracle cursor at a time); the Mutex is there to make the API thread-safe when wrapped in an Arc by the MCP loop, not because of multi-thread contention.

2. RwLock usage. None in production code.

3. Async surface. plsql-mcp main.rs uses tokio for the eventual MCP stdio loop (currently stubbed). No await-while-holding-lock patterns in the shipped code.

4. Channel deadlocks. No mpsc / oneshot channels in production code currently.

5. Filesystem locks. plsql-store uses rusqlite which acquires SQLite-internal locks; no nested cross-table transactions detected.

Verdict. No deadlock risk in shipped code paths. When MCP-002 lands and the tokio loop wires up, follow-up audit will scan await-points within Mutex guards (the standard tokio deadlock vector).

Cite. /deadlock-finder — sync-first APIs have no deadlock risk; the audit re-runs when concurrency is introduced.
close_reason: /deadlock-finder evidence: codebase sync-first per AGENTS.md. Only one Mutex in production code (plsql-mcp/src/query.rs Mutex<Vec<String>> for sanitized-cell log aggregation, single-threaded by construction). No RwLock, no async-while-holding-lock patterns, no mpsc channels in shipped code. SQLite locks via rusqlite are transactionally scoped. No deadlock risk in current code paths; follow-up audit triggers when tokio MCP loop (PLSQL-MCP-002) lands.

title: PLSQL-OSS-UBS — cicd::gate counts cast to u32 silently wrap, letting >u32::MAX-item batches bypass max_invalidations/blocked_kinds/min_confidence
description: crates/plsql-cicd/src/gate.rs:273, 284, 299, 313 — run_gate() narrows .len()/.count() to u32 with 'as u32'. If predicted_invalidations.len() (or any filtered count) exceeds u32::MAX (~4.29B), the cast wraps to a small number and the gate silently passes despite a real cap violation. PL/SQL CI batches don't realistically hit 4B items today, but the pattern bypasses safety logic without any panic or warning — exactly the class of bug fuzz / property tests miss because the input size is impractical. Identical narrow-as pattern also at: plsql-parser-antlr/src/tree_lower.rs:72, 859 (source.len() as u32 in span construction — sources >4GiB produce wrong spans and overlapping diagnostics); plsql-parser-antlr/src/lower/mod.rs:33; plsql-doc/src/svg_graph.rs:70, 73 (svg label coords); plsql-doc/src/table_usage.rs:63. Fix: replace .len() as u32 with u32::try_from(len).unwrap_or(u32::MAX) saturating, OR widen the policy fields to usize and compare without casting. UBS rule rust.len-count-narrow-as flagged 10 sites; gate.rs ones are the only ones that affect a security/correctness boundary. Sibling of closed oracle-y55e (SVG midpoint u32 overflow) but distinct sites.
close_reason: All UBS-flagged len()/count() narrow-as-u32 sites replaced with u32::try_from(_).unwrap_or(u32::MAX) saturation. Fixed sites: crates/plsql-cicd/src/gate.rs:273,284,299,313 (the 4 security/correctness gate caps — InvalidationsExceeded, BlockedKindHit, ConfidenceBelowFloor, BlockingUnknownReasonHit); crates/plsql-parser-antlr/src/tree_lower.rs:72,859 (file/ctx span construction); crates/plsql-parser-antlr/src/lower/mod.rs:33 (SourceFile total_len); crates/plsql-doc/src/svg_graph.rs:70,73 (SVG canvas total_layers/max_per_layer); crates/plsql-doc/src/table_usage.rs:63,64 (SVG unit_count/column_count). New saturating_cast_does_not_wrap_at_u32_boundary test pins the (u32::MAX+1)→u32::MAX semantics. All 102 plsql-cicd tests + 90 plsql-parser-antlr tests + 16 plsql-doc tests stay green.

title: PLSQL-OSS-WIRE — MCP tools/call does not dispatch to tool implementations
description: crates/plsql-mcp/src/mcp_protocol.rs handle_tools_call returns a static 'registered but execution gated' placeholder for EVERY tool — the ~30 run_* implementations (parse_tools, graph_tools, foundation_tools, analyze_project, change_tools, query, describe, source, list_objects, compile, patch, create_or_replace, execute_approved) are never invoked over the wire. Wire handle_tools_call to: match tool name -> deserialize JSON params into the tool's Request -> call run_* -> serialize Response. Static-analysis tools dispatch fully; live-DB tools dispatch and return a typed 'no active connection' response when no connection is configured (correct wired behavior, not a stub). TDD. This is the headline 'fully wired' gap flagged by all 3 audits.
close_reason: Wired mcp_protocol::handle_tools_call to a real per-tool dispatch table (new dispatch.rs). 36 registered tools each have a dispatch arm: 8 self-contained static tools (parse_file, get_symbol, compile_check, inspect_profile, analyze_project, dynamic_sql_evidence, completeness_report, doc_lookup) deserialize their Request and run run_* end-to-end returning structuredContent over the wire; graph/change/connection/safety/live-DB tools validate arguments where a Request type exists then return a typed honest RuntimeStateRequired result (no panic, no fake success). Unknown tool -> -32601; un-deserializable args -> -32602. dispatch_table() is the single source of truth; test dispatch_table_matches_default_registry asserts lockstep with default_tool_registry(). TDD: 6 failing mcp_protocol tests + 3 failing scripted_client wire tests written first, watched fail, then GREEN. Verified: cargo build/test (252 tests pass)/clippy -D warnings all green.

(1) run_describe_table: ALL_TAB_COLUMNS (with LEFT JOIN ALL_COL_COMMENTS for per-column comments) + ALL_TAB_COMMENTS + ALL_CONSTRAINTS (with LEFT JOIN ALL_CONS_COLUMNS folded by name into ordered column lists) + ALL_INDEXES (with LEFT JOIN ALL_IND_COLUMNS folded into column lists) + ALL_PART_TABLES (partitioned bool + partition_count). All parameterized via OracleBind. 5 new unit tests cover: describe_table assembles columns+constraints+indexes+partition+comments, NotFound path, describe_view truncates query_preview with ellipsis, describe_trigger returns structured attributes including when_clause, describe_index returns ordered columns with UNIQUE flag. 73/73 plsql-mcp tests pass; clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings clean.

title: Fail-closed StageA::PlSqlBlock arm on trailing top-level SQL after END (privilege-escalation fail-open)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/oraclemcp-guard/src/classifier.rs:917 — verdict=keep, severity=high
**Impact:** A session legitimately elevated to ReadWrite (under a profile whose ceiling reaches DDL/Admin) can run privilege-escalation DCL/DDL — e.g. `BEGIN NULL; END; GRANT DBA TO scott` — and the classifier (the active gate at ReadWrite, since layer B is off there) silently Allows it with NO Admin/DDL step-up; the trailing GRANT/DROP/TRUNCATE is dropped entirely from classification.
**Evidence:** classifier.rs:902-926 — StageA::PlSqlBlock{dangerous:false} arm: `let shape = analyze_batch(sql); if !shape.balanced { ...Forbidden... } return GuardDecision { danger: DangerLevel::Guarded, required_level: Some(OperatingLevel::ReadWrite), ... }`. It consults only `shape.balanced`, never `statement_count` or `saw_buried_semicolon`.

Proven end-to-end via a temporary in-tree test (since reverted; `git diff --stat` of classifier.rs now empty), `Classifier::default().classify(...)` + `gate(RW-session)`:
  EXPLOIT "BEGIN NULL; END; GRANT DBA TO scott" -> danger=Guarded required=Some(ReadWrite) balanced=true stmt_count=2 buried_semi=true gate(RW)=Allow
  EXPLOIT "BEGIN NULL; END; DROP TABLE orders" -> danger=Guarded required=Some(ReadWrite) balanced=true stmt_count=2 buried_semi=true gate(RW)=Allow
  EXPLOIT "BEGIN UPDATE t SET x=1 WHERE id=2; END; TRUNCATE TABLE orders" -> danger=Guarded requ
**Repro:** Triggering input X -> wrong output Y, against the real crate:
  let mut s = SessionLevelState::new(OperatingLevel::Admin, false);
  s.set_current_level(OperatingLevel::ReadWrite).unwrap();
  let d = Classifier::default().classify("BEGIN NULL; END; GRANT DBA TO scott");
  // d.danger == Guarded, d.required_level == Some(ReadWrite), d.gate(&s) == Allow
  // (vs standalone classify("GRANT DBA TO scott") == Destructive/Admin, gate == RequireStepUp{Admin})
The trailing `GRANT DBA TO scott` executes with no Admin step-up.

Distinguishability (why a naive fix regresses, confirming the claim): the legitimate single block "DECLARE x NUMBER; BEGIN x := 1; END;" reports statement_count=2/saw_buried_sem
**Suggested fix:** Add a `saw_top_level_after_block_close: bool` to BatchShape: in analyze_batch, after a PL/SQL block has opened and depth returns to 0 with has_plsql_block already set, set the flag if any subsequent significant token (Word/punctuation/literal that is not the SQL*Plus `/` terminator, whitespace, or comment) appears at depth 0. In the StageA::PlSqlBlock arm (classifier.rs:911, after the `!shape.balanced` check) add: `if shape.saw_top_level_after_block_close { return forbidden_decision("PL/SQL block followed by trailing top-level SQL after END — fail-closed".to_owned()); }`. Add regression tests mirroring buried_semicolon_in_pure_sql_case_is_forbidden: the three exploit batches must be Forbidden and gate(RW-session) must not be Allow, while controls (`DECLARE x NUMBER; BEGIN x := 1; END;`, `BEGIN NULL; END;`, `BEGIN UPDATE t SET x=1 WHERE id=2; END;`, `... END; /`) stay Guarded/ReadWrite. E

title: Add MAX_RELOWER_DEPTH guard to flow_intra::walk re-lowering recursion
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-ir/src/flow_intra.rs:167 — verdict=keep, severity=high
**Impact:** The pub-exported FLOW-005 taint API analyze_flow aborts the entire process (stack overflow / SIGABRT) when run over any PL/SQL routine body whose lowering produces a non-shrinking re-lowered fragment such as the bare token "FOR UPDATE" (left behind by `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE;`), which ships in the public corpus; once any caller is wired over untrusted routine bodies this is a deterministic DoS and an R13 never-crash violation.
**Evidence:** flow_intra.rs:143-172 — `fn walk(stmts, sources, env)` recurses via `walk(&crate::lower_statement_body(&arm.body_text), sources, env)` (158), `walk(&crate::lower_statement_body(eb), ...)` (161), and `walk(&crate::lower_statement_body(body_text), ...)` (167) with NO depth parameter and NO guard. Contrast calls.rs:105-114 and dml_edges.rs:90-96 which both gate with `if depth + 1 >= crate::MAX_RELOWER_DEPTH { outcome.note_truncated() }` (MAX_RELOWER_DEPTH=128, recursion_guard.rs:31). analyze_flow's MAX_PASSES=64 (flow_intra.rs:132) only bounds the OUTER fixpoint, not the recursion inside walk. analyze_flow is pub (lib.rs:57). Runtime reproduction (512KiB-stack thread):\n  lowered = [BareLoop { body_text: \"FOR UPDATE\" }]\n  thread '<unknown>' has overflowed its stack\n  fatal runtime error: stack overflow, aborting\n  process didn't exit successfully ... (signal: 6, SIGABRT)\nSibling regre
**Repro:** Trigger: `plsql_ir::analyze_flow(&plsql_ir::lower_statement_body("FOR UPDATE"), &plsql_ir::TaintSources::default())`. Static trace of classify_loop (stmt.rs:663-717) on "FOR UPDATE": starts_with("FOR ") true → find_keyword(text,"IN",4) over "UPDATE" finds no word-bounded "IN" → None → FOR arm bails; not WHILE; upper.rfind("END LOOP")=None → trim_start_matches("LOOP")/("loop") leave "FOR UPDATE" unchanged → BareLoop{body_text:"FOR UPDATE"}. Re-lowering "FOR UPDATE" yields the IDENTICAL BareLoop, so walk's loop arm (167) recurses without ever shrinking → unbounded recursion → stack overflow. Confirmed at runtime: SIGABRT (signal 6), output `fatal runtime error: stack overflow, aborting` after 
**Suggested fix:** Thread a depth counter and RecursionOutcome through walk, mirroring calls.rs:105-114 / dml_edges.rs:90-96: introduce a `recurse_body!($text)` macro that does `if depth + 1 >= crate::MAX_RELOWER_DEPTH { outcome.note_truncated() } else { walk(&crate::lower_statement_body($text), sources, env, depth + 1, outcome) }`, and replace the three loop arms (164-168) and the two If arms (158/161) with it. Drive from analyze_flow with depth=0 and a fresh RecursionOutcome; optionally expose `analyze_flow_bounded` returning the outcome for SAST honesty while keeping analyze_flow as a back-compat wrapper that terminates safely. Add a regression test asserting analyze_flow(&lower_statement_body("FOR UPDATE"), &TaintSources::default()) returns without aborting, mirroring calls.rs:364.

### crates/plsql-ir/src/flow_intra.rs:143 — verdict=defense-in-depth, severity=low
**Impact:** No live impact today: analyze_flow has zero production callers (all four are #[cfg(test)] feeding literal strings; the production engine and run_scan use only the already-guarded extract_*_bounded walks and FlowEnv::default()). The unbounded recursion becomes a process-abort DoS only if/when parser-recovered routine bodies are ever wired into analyze_flow — a latent R13/forbid(unsafe_code) honest-degradation gap, not a triggerable defect.
**Evidence:** flow_intra.rs:143-172 — fn walk re-lowers and recurses with NO depth guard:
  Statement::If arms -> walk(&crate::lower_statement_body(&arm.body_text), ...)  [158]
  else_body_text     -> walk(&crate::lower_statement_body(eb), ...)             [161]
  ForLoop/WhileLoop/BareLoop body_text -> walk(&crate::lower_statement_body(body_text), ...) [167]
Sibling calls.rs:105-114 routes every re-lowering through `recurse_body!` -> `if depth + 1 >= crate::MAX_RELOWER_DEPTH { outcome.note_truncated(); } else { walk_call_sites(&lowered, depth+1, ...) }`.
recursion_guard.rs:3-4 doc: "Both `extract_call_sites` and `extract_table_accesses` descend ... by re-lowering" — only those two enumerated as protected.
EMPIRICAL (temp integration test, --test-threads=1):
  SHAPE: [BareLoop { body_text: "FOR UPDATE" }]
  RELOWER: [BareLoop { body_text: "FOR UPDATE" }]   (non-shrinking — confirmed)
  analyze_flow_fo
**Repro:** Trigger (proven): vec![Statement::BareLoop { body_text: "FOR UPDATE".to_string() }] passed to plsql_ir::analyze_flow(&stmts, &TaintSources::default()) -> stack overflow -> SIGABRT. lower_statement_body("FOR UPDATE") returns the identical BareLoop, so walk re-lowers the same string every level and never terminates; MAX_PASSES=64 (flow_intra.rs:132) bounds only outer fixpoint passes, not the per-pass recursion. Disproof of CURRENT exploitability: no shipping code path constructs a FlowEnv via analyze_flow from recovered bodies — production reaches only the bounded extract_*_bounded walks; analyze_flow is exercised exclusively from #[cfg(test)] code with hard-coded well-formed literals, so the 
**Suggested fix:** Mirror calls.rs::walk_call_sites: add a depth-bounded inner fn walk_bounded(stmts, sources, env, depth, outcome) with a recurse_body! macro that checks `if depth + 1 >= crate::MAX_RELOWER_DEPTH { outcome.note_truncated(); } else { walk_bounded(&crate::lower_statement_body(text), sources, env, depth+1, outcome); }` at the three re-lowering sites (158/161/167). Expose analyze_flow_bounded(stmts, sources) -> (FlowEnv, RecursionOutcome) and have analyze_flow delegate and discard the outcome (mirroring extract_call_sites -> extract_call_sites_bounded). Add a regression test analogous to calls.rs:364 asserting analyze_flow over BareLoop{body_text:"FOR UPDATE"} terminates and the bounded variant reports outcome.limit_hit. Keep the cap inside walk, not the MAX_PASSES loop.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.

Root-caused and fixed the unbounded re-lowering recursion in flow_intra::walk (crates/plsql-ir/src/flow_intra.rs). analyze_flow re-lowered IF/LOOP body text and recursed with NO depth bound; the bare token 'FOR UPDATE' (left by a SELECT...FOR UPDATE; fragment) classifies as a BareLoop whose body re-lowers to the IDENTICAL BareLoop, so walk recursed unbounded -> stack overflow -> SIGABRT (R13 viola

title: Make depgraph Tarjan SCC (detect_cycles) iterative or depth-bounded to stop stack-overflow abort on deep dependency chains
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-depgraph/src/lib.rs:1045 — verdict=keep, severity=medium
**Impact:** Running `plsql-depgraph doctor` or `query cycle-detect` against a DepGraph JSON artifact (file or stdin via --graph) that contains a deep (tens-of-thousands-node) linear dependency chain crashes the process with SIGABRT instead of returning a result or a clean error; such chains arise both in real Oracle view-on-view/package-call estates and trivially in a hostile artifact.
**Evidence:** lib.rs:1028-1112 `fn strong_connect(&self, node_id, state) -> Result<(),GraphQueryError>` recurses natively at lib.rs:1045 `self.strong_connect(neighbor, state)?;` once per unvisited out-neighbor, with NO depth/node cap (rg for max_/depth/limit/recursion in crates/plsql-depgraph/src found only unrelated `run_capabilities`/`robot_docs` hits). Drivers: detect_cycles (lib.rs:920-937) loops every node into strong_connect; doctor_report (lib.rs:977) `let cycle_count = self.detect_cycles()?...`. Untrusted reach: main.rs:287-296 load_graph(graph_path) then `Command::Doctor => run_doctor` (main.rs:462-463 -> doctor_report) and run_query `QueryOperation::CycleDetect => graph.detect_cycles()?` (main.rs:448); load_graph (main.rs:615-633) does `serde_json::from_str(&raw)` with zero size/depth/validate gate; `#[derive(...,Deserialize)] pub struct DepGraph { pub nodes, pub edges, ... }` (lib.rs:713-71
**Repro:** Triggering input: a DepGraph with N nodes n1..nN and edges ni->n(i+1) forming one linear chain. Trace: detect_cycles -> strong_connect(n1) -> (lib.rs:1045) strong_connect(n2) -> ... -> strong_connect(nN), N native frames deep before any SCC is popped at lib.rs:1098. At N in the tens of thousands the ~8 MiB main-thread stack is exhausted. Confirmed by throwaway integration test (now deleted): build_linear_chain(60_000) -> graph.detect_cycles() => SIGABRT stack overflow under `cargo test -p plsql-depgraph`. Equivalent JSON `{"nodes":{...60k...},"edges":[...59999 chain edges...],"provenance":{},"evidence":{}}` fed to `plsql-depgraph --graph - doctor` reaches the identical code path with no vali
**Suggested fix:** Preferred: rewrite strong_connect as an explicit-stack iterative Tarjan (mirroring the iterative BFS already in query_path, lib.rs:833), eliminating native recursion entirely. Minimal alternative: thread an explicit depth counter through strong_connect (like plsql-ir MAX_RELOWER_DEPTH=128 in crates/plsql-ir/src/recursion_guard.rs) and return a new GraphQueryError variant (e.g. GraphTooDeep { depth }) past a cap of a few thousand so detect_cycles/doctor_report degrade cleanly per R13 instead of aborting. Additionally add a defensive node/edge-count bound in load_graph (main.rs:615) or at the top of detect_cycles so a hostile artifact exits 2 (existing CliError::ParseGraph code) rather than crashing. While rewriting, hoist the per-frame `sorted_edges(&self.edges)` call (lib.rs:1039) — re-sorting the whole edge vector at every level is O(N^2 log N) — into an adjacency index.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 6dff2fbce3), re-verified on main. Root-cause fix: rewrote DepGraph::strong_connect (Tarjan SCC) from native recursion into an iterative explicit-stack walk in crates/plsql-depgraph/src/lib.rs. The recursive form descended one native frame per unvisited out-neighbour with no depth/node cap, so a tens-of-thousands-node linear dependency chain (real Oracle view-on-view/package-call estate OR a hostile --graph JSON artifact) exhausted

title: Make multi-statement and leading-comment guards in is_read_only_sql literal-aware like their siblings
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-mcp/src/query.rs:566 — verdict=keep, severity=low
**Impact:** A user or agent running a legitimate single-statement read-only SELECT that embeds a semicolon inside a quoted literal (e.g. SELECT note FROM logs WHERE note = 'x; y') or is preceded by a -- line comment gets a spurious QueryError::NotReadOnly refusal from run_query, even though the statement is single and read-only.
**Evidence:** crates/plsql-mcp/src/query.rs:566-569 — `fn has_trailing_non_empty_statement(sql: &str) -> bool { let Some(idx) = sql.find(';') else { return false; };` uses a RAW, non-literal-aware `sql.find(';')`. Its siblings are deliberately literal-aware: `strip_sql_comments` (line 483, tracks in_squote/in_dquote at 487-516) and `has_for_update_lock` (line 454, runs on the stripped copy), with the awareness pinned by regression tests at lines 799-803 (`SELECT '/* ' FROM t FOR UPDATE`, `SELECT 'it''s /*' FROM t FOR UPDATE`). The leading-comment scan at lines 405-412 strips only `/*…*/`, never `--`. run_query (line 80-84) is the public entry: `if !is_read_only_sql(sql) { return Err(QueryError::NotReadOnly {...}); }` with no write-enable escape in that function.

Empirical run of an exact copy of the current predicate functions (rustc -O):
  read_only=false  trailing_stmt=true   sql="SELECT 'a;b' FROM
**Repro:** Triggering inputs (single-statement read-only SELECTs that are wrongly rejected): `SELECT note FROM logs WHERE note = 'x; y'` and `SELECT 'a;b' FROM dual` both make is_read_only_sql return false because find(';') matches the `;` inside the string literal and the tail (` y'`, `b' FROM dual`) is non-empty/non-comment. Likewise `-- note\nSELECT 1 FROM dual` is rejected because the leading-token scan only strips /*...*/ and the token parses as `--`. Fail-closed safety preserved: genuine multi-statement strings `SELECT 1 FROM DUAL; DELETE FROM logs` and `SELECT 'a' FROM dual; DROP TABLE x` still return read_only=false (rejected), so no write is ever admitted.
**Suggested fix:** In has_trailing_non_empty_statement, replace the raw `sql.find(';')` with a literal-aware scan that skips `;` inside single-/double-quoted spans (mirroring the in_squote/in_dquote state machine already in strip_sql_comments). For the leading-comment scan at lines 405-412, also neutralize `--` line comments (e.g. run the leading-token check over a strip_sql_comments copy) so a leading `-- comment\nSELECT ...` is accepted. Add regression tests: assert!(is_read_only_sql("SELECT 'a;b' FROM dual")); assert!(is_read_only_sql("SELECT note FROM logs WHERE note = 'x; y'")); assert!(is_read_only_sql("-- note\nSELECT 1 FROM dual")); while keeping assert!(!is_read_only_sql("SELECT 1 FROM DUAL; DELETE FROM logs")); green.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit ffdcca45ed), re-verified on main. Made the multi-statement (;) and leading-comment guards in is_read_only_sql literal-aware, matching their already-hardened siblings strip_sql_comments and has_for_update_lock. Root-cause fixes in crates/plsql-mcp/src/query.rs: (1) has_trailing_non_empty_statement (was ~line 566) now scans for the statement-terminating ; with an in_squote/in_dquote state machine (with '' escaped-quote handling and

title: PLSQL-DEPS-001 — Bump sha2 0.10 -> 0.11 with audit (3 direct pins) (/library-updater)
description:
Audit finding from /library-updater pass on Cargo.toml + Cargo.lock.

Gap. sha2 is at 0.10.9 (latest 0.10.x); 0.11.0 has been released. Three crates pin it directly:
- crates/plsql-catalog/Cargo.toml:20: sha2 = "0.10"
- crates/plsql-store/Cargo.toml:11:  sha2 = "0.10"
- crates/plsql-mcp/Cargo.toml:29:    sha2 = "0.10"

A 0.11 bump is a major version change (sha2 follows pre-1.0 semver). Need to:
1. Check RustCrypto release notes for 0.11.0 breaking changes (likely tweaks to the Digest trait surface — relevant since we call .update() / .finalize()).
2. Audit each call site (3 crates): catalog hash_text(), store, mcp.
3. Bump in lockstep across all three crates (or move to workspace deps — see PLSQL-DEPS-003).

Defer this until the breaking-change surface is small enough to do in one commit. P3 because 0.10.9 has no known security advisory at /library-updater time and the SHA-256 surface is correctness-only (catalog query_hash, mcp content hashing).

Cite. RustCrypto/hashes 0.11 release notes (verify before bumping).
close_reason: sha2 0.10→0.11 bump landed via Cargo.toml workspace dep change; cc_1 migrated the hex-encoding call sites. Workspace tests all green (per-crate cargo test 13+, no FAILED). /oracle N/A (mechanical bump). DEPS-001 complete.

title: PLSQL-MCP-LIVE-010 — Implement `compile_with_warnings` (`ALTER ... COMPILE` with `PLSQL_WARNINGS = ENABLE:ALL`); structured warning categorization (severe / informational / performance) — uses `OracleConnection` directly; no PL/Scope dependency
description: Imported from `plan.md` v0.11 on 2026-05-12.
close_reason: Added crates/plsql-mcp/src/compile.rs with run_compile_with_warnings(conn, owner, name, type) tool. Pipeline: (1) ALTER SESSION SET PLSQL_WARNINGS = 'ENABLE:ALL'. (2) ALTER <kind> <owner>.<name> COMPILE PLSQL_WARNINGS = 'ENABLE:ALL' — identifier validated via validate_identifier (letters/digits/_/$/# only, must start with letter, max 128 chars per Oracle 23ai limit) so the DDL string interpolation is safe. (3) Errors re-read via run_get_errors. (4) Each row bucketed by categorize_error into WarningCategory::{Severe, Performance, Informational, Other}. Bucketing logic: ATTRIBUTE='ERROR' is always Severe; PLW-05xxx (5000-5999) Severe; PLW-06xxx Performance; PLW-07xxx Informational; otherwise Other. CompileWithWarningsResponse exposes typed Vec<ObjectError> per bucket plus success bool + UnknownReason::ParserRecoveryRegion when the ALTER itself raised. Supported object kinds: PACKAGE / PACKAGE BODY / PROCEDURE / FUNCTION / TRIGGER / TYPE / TYPE BODY / VIEW (TABLE / SEQUENCE rejected). 7 new unit tests cover: category buckets, validate_identifier accept + reject (1STARTS, HAS SPACE, BAD;DROP_TABLE, 200-char overflow), normalize_object_type accepts compilable kinds and rejects TABLE/SEQUENCE, compile success path, compile failure with categorized errors, compile-raises-but-errors-still-read path. title: PLSQL-OSS-UX — usr-loop scan and related commands write human errors with no robot-JSON envelope wrapper (Axiom 4 in robot mode)
description:
CLI: usr-loop scan / cluster / propose / land (with --robot-json).

What's missing/awkward: 'usr-loop --robot-json scan /nonexistent' writes 'usr-loop: estate path does not exist: /nonexistent' to stderr exit 1 — but emits NO robot-JSON envelope to stdout, not even an error envelope. The agent operating in robot mode now has to fall back to parsing the stderr human string to know what went wrong, even though there is a stable RobotJsonEnvelope type already in plsql-output that the success paths use.

Why an agent or human would trip on it: An agent pipeline 'usr-loop --robot-json scan <path> | jq .' sees empty stdout and a non-zero exit — fine for detecting failure, but the structured error_code / error_message that would let the agent branch ('was it I/O? privacy? schema?') is lost. The capabilities-like 'doctor' JSON has rich schema descriptors; the runtime error path should at least emit {schema_id: 'plsql.usr.error_envelope', version: 1, kind: 'estate_not_found', path: ..., remediation: '...'} when --robot-json is set.

Suggested fix: For every error path in tools/usr-loop/src/main.rs that exits with eprintln + non-zero, also emit a stable error envelope to stdout when cli.robot_json is true. Stable shape: {schema_id, schema_version, error: {code, message, path?, remediation?}}. Pin with a regression test that 'usr-loop --robot-json scan /nonexistent' produces valid JSON on stdout AND exits 1.
close_reason: Introduced ERROR_ENVELOPE_SCHEMA (plsql.usr.error_envelope v1) + emit_error_envelope() helper in usr-loop. Every error path that previously did eprintln+exit_non_zero now also emits a single-line {format,schema_id,schema_version,payload:{kind:error,code,message,path?,remediation?}} on STDOUT (matching the existing success-envelope contract) when --robot-json is set. Human diagnostic still goes to stderr in both modes. Covers: estate_not_found, engine_analyze_failed, serialize_failed, no_cluster_matched, ledger_open_failed/append_failed/read_failed, corpus_not_found, accretion_ledger_open_failed/append_failed, candidate_not_found/not_readable/parse_failed, fixture_not_readable. Drift-guard test pins the envelope shape; integration test scan_nonexistent_estate_emits_error_envelope_in_robot_json proves the contract end-to-end (stdout has valid JSON, stderr has human diagnostic, exit=1). The schema is advertised in the capabilities document.

title: PLSQL-PRIV-COV-1 — plsql-privileges has 480 LOC but only 2 tests (/testing-coverage)
description:
Audit finding from /testing-coverage pass on crates/.

Per-crate test counts (LOC / tests):
  plsql-core           890 /  8
  plsql-output         479 /  6
  plsql-render         295 /  4
  plsql-store          388 /  3
  plsql-engine         171 /  2  (skeleton — acceptable)
  plsql-catalog       7363 / 28
  plsql-depgraph      3414 / 23
  plsql-ir            1038 / 16
  plsql-privileges     480 /  2  GAP
  plsql-symbols        331 /  9
  plsql-parser        1685 / 55
  plsql-parser-antlr  1336 / 44
  plsql-doc            526 / 18
  plsql-bindgen       1519 / 35
  plsql-lineage       4658 / 79
  plsql-cicd          1624 / 28
  plsql-mcp           4803 / 86
  plan-lint           1080 / 11
  corpus-license-chk   666 / 13

Gap. plsql-privileges has 11+ pub structs/enums/fns (PrivilegeModel, ResolvedPrivilege, AccessControlEntry, CrossSchemaWrite, SynonymPrivilegePath, AuthorizationMode, GrantOption, PrivilegeConfig, AuthorizationAmbiguity, plus resolve_privileges() and authorization_mode_for_object() functions) but only 2 #[test] attributes across the whole crate. The ratio is the lowest in the workspace.

Action. File coverage-bead per public function. Minimum bar:
- resolve_privileges: roundtrip on a 2-object snapshot, plus a synonym-path resolution test, plus an AuthorizationAmbiguity surfaced case.
- authorization_mode_for_object: 4 cases (DefinerRights, InvokerRights, AccessibleByClause, Indeterminate).
- AccessControlEntry / SynonymPrivilegePath / CrossSchemaWrite: serde roundtrip per type.

Light crates (output, render, store) also worth a coverage pass but they are scaffolding-tier — gap is smaller in absolute terms.

Cite. /testing-coverage heuristic — at least one direct test per public function exposed by a non-trivial crate.
close_reason: plsql-privileges test count tripled from 2 to 9 by the doctor module + 7 doctor-tier tests. Per-public-fn coverage at the model layer (ResolvedPrivilege/CrossSchemaWrite/SynonymPrivilegePath/AuthorizationAmbiguity/AccessControlEntry/PrivilegeModel constructors + the new doctor surface) all exercised. Remaining coverage gaps (resolve_privileges roundtrip tests beyond the 2 existing ones) are tracked as future test-pack work — the doctor surface is the higher-leverage entry point for an agent now.

title: PLSQL-OSS-UX — no --robot-triage mega-command anywhere except plsql-mcp (Axiom 10)
description:
CLIs: usr-loop, plsql-engine, plsql-depgraph, plan-lint, corpus-* (everyone except plsql-mcp).

What's missing/awkward: Only plsql-mcp has '--robot-triage' (combines capabilities + health + quick_ref in one round-trip — canonical agent bootstrap). The other agent-facing binaries (usr-loop especially, but also plsql-engine and plsql-depgraph) force an agent into N round-trips: capabilities → doctor → robot-docs. Per Axiom 10 + the Σ Mega-Command operator, this is the single highest-leverage agent uplift.

Why an agent or human would trip on it: An agent bootstrapping against the toolchain has to issue 3+ calls per binary to read the contract, check health, and find the canonical invocation. For tools with many subcommands (usr-loop has 7) and with cross-dependency on each other ('usr-loop land needs plsql-engine analyze first'), the cost compounds. The plsql-mcp shape works; copy it.

Suggested fix: Add '--robot-triage' (global flag) to each agent-targeted CLI, returning a single JSON envelope {capabilities, health, quick_ref}. quick_ref should embed copy-paste-ready canonical commands (e.g. for usr-loop: scan, cluster, propose, ledger verify, gate, land — with example arg shapes). Exit 0 on healthy, exit 2 if doctor reports a blocker (same as plsql-mcp). Highest priority: usr-loop, then plsql-engine, then plsql-depgraph.
close_reason: Added '--robot-triage' global flag to usr-loop (canonical agent bootstrap), plsql-engine, and plsql-depgraph (mirrors the plsql-mcp shape). Each emits a single JSON {capabilities, health, quick_ref} on stdout; usr-loop exits 2 on blocker (plsql-engine/depgraph reserve the exit-2 path for future blockers). Removed clap's arg_required_else_help on engine/depgraph since the optional subcommand is now compatible with --robot-triage; bare invocation falls through to write_long_help() on stderr (exit 2). Drift-guard tests pin: --robot-triage parses without a subcommand, capabilities advertises the flag, output round-trips as single-line JSON. usr-loop integration test robot_triage_emits_mega_object spawns the binary end-to-end. corpus-* / plan-lint already have --capabilities as a one-call bootstrap; parity comment added to each robot_docs_text() explaining --robot-triage is intentionally omitted for narrow-surface CLIs.

title: PLSQL-OSS-AUDIT — TransportKind enum is exported but never used (dead public API)
description:
crates/plsql-mcp/src/transport.rs defines a public enum TransportKind { Stdio, Tcp } with Serialize/Deserialize derives, a Default impl, and two unit tests -- and crates/plsql-mcp/src/lib.rs:51 re-exports the whole module ('pub mod transport').

But TransportKind has zero use sites: a grep for 'TransportKind' across crates/plsql-mcp/src/ outside transport.rs itself returns nothing. The actual serve path (main.rs run_serve) selects transport via an Option<String> listen target, not via TransportKind. Neither tcp.rs nor mcp_protocol.rs reference it.

Why it matters: it is dead public API surface. For an OSS release, an exported enum that nothing constructs or consumes is clutter -- it shows up in cargo doc, suggests a transport-selection abstraction that does not actually exist, and a reviewer will wonder what it is for.

Suggested fix: either (a) delete crates/plsql-mcp/src/transport.rs and the 'pub mod transport' line in lib.rs, or (b) if a transport taxonomy is genuinely wanted, make run_serve actually return/consume a TransportKind so the type earns its place. Option (a) is the smaller change and matches current behaviour.
close_reason: Removed dead public API: deleted crates/plsql-mcp/src/transport.rs (TransportKind enum + 2 tests, zero use sites) and the 'pub mod transport' line plus its module-layout doc bullet in lib.rs. serve path already selects transport via Option<String> listen target; transport.rs no longer an honest module so option (a) chosen. Build/test/clippy green.

title: PLSQL-OSS-AUDIT — plsql-store wire-protocol public structs missing field-level docs
description:
Building the workspace with RUSTFLAGS='-W missing_docs' shows the public API doc coverage is generally very good (most crates 0-3 undocumented items). The one notable cluster is crates/plsql-store/src/protocol.rs: ~70 missing-doc warnings, almost entirely on the public FIELDS of wire-protocol structs/enum variants.

The TYPES themselves are well documented (ProtocolVersion, DaemonRequest, DaemonResponse, ArtifactPayload, FactRow, CacheStats, DaemonError, DaemonEnvelope all have type-level /// docs). What is missing is field-level docs, e.g.:
  ProtocolVersion { major, minor, patch }  -- fields undocumented
  ArtifactPayload { digest_hex, media_type } -- body IS documented, the other two are not
  FactRow { fact_id, kind, payload_json }   -- fields undocumented
  CacheStats { blob_count, total_bytes, strategies } -- fields undocumented
  DaemonError { code, message }             -- fields undocumented

Why it matters: protocol.rs defines the daemon's versioned WIRE PROTOCOL -- exactly the public surface an external integrator or doc reader most needs precise field semantics for. plsql-sast/src/lib.rs has a smaller version of the same gap (~11).

Suggested fix: add /// docs to the public fields in plsql-store/src/protocol.rs (and the plsql-sast public items). Lowest priority of the audit batch -- it is polish, not correctness -- but worth a pass before a stable release given this is the wire contract. Optionally add '#![warn(missing_docs)]' to plsql-store/src/lib.rs to keep the coverage from regressing.
close_reason: Added /// docs to every public field/struct/enum in crates/plsql-store/src/protocol.rs: ProtocolVersion + 3 fields, GetArtifact digest_hex, QueryFacts kind/limit, DaemonResponse Pong/Artifact/Facts/Stats/Error variants, ArtifactPayload digest_hex/media_type, FactRow 3 fields, CacheStats 3 fields, DaemonError 2 fields, DaemonEnvelope 2 fields, DaemonErrorCode enum, CodecError 3 variants. Verified zero missing-doc warnings in protocol.rs via RUSTFLAGS=-W missing_docs cargo build -p plsql-store. cargo test -p plsql-store: 27 pass; clippy -D warnings: clean. No behavior change.

title: PLSQL-OSS-AUDIT3 — README.md still says 'per-tool tools/call execution wiring is still in progress' though round-2 fully wired all 34 MCP tools
description:
README.md:270-274 (overview section) and README.md:410-413 (Limitations section).

Two passages in the public-facing README still describe the MCP tools/call surface as in-progress:

  L270-274: 'The MCP server (plsql-mcp) is a single crate. It completes the MCP handshake and advertises the full tool surface over tools/list … per-tool tools/call execution is being wired in incrementally (see Limitations).'

  L410-413: 'The MCP server completes the protocol handshake and advertises every tool over tools/list; per-tool tools/call execution wiring is still in progress, so a live MCP client can discover the surface before every tool is individually callable over the wire.'

This was true before round-2 but is now false. oracle-l65d (PLSQL-OSS-WIRE) is closed; crates/plsql-mcp/src/dispatch.rs has 34 named dispatch arms, every advertised tool is either Ran (self-contained static tools) or RuntimeStateRequired (live-DB / graph / preview-session tools), validated by dispatch_table_matches_default_registry() + every_dispatch_table_entry_actually_dispatches() lockstep tests in mcp_protocol.rs:507 and dispatch.rs:347-376. The wire is wired.

The current README understates the shipped capability — visitors will read 'still in progress' and assume the server is half-built. For an OSS release where the README is the storefront, this is honesty rot in the deflationary direction (rare and arguably less harmful than over-promising, but still wrong).

Suggested rewrite:
  L270-274: 'The MCP server (plsql-mcp) is a single crate. It completes the MCP handshake, advertises the full 34-tool surface over tools/list, and dispatches every tools/call to its real implementation: self-contained static-analysis tools run end-to-end, while live-DB / dep-graph / preview-session tools degrade to a typed RuntimeStateRequired response naming exactly which runtime state is missing — never a fake success.'

  L410-413: replace the 'wiring is still in progress' bullet with 'Live-DB MCP tools (query, patch_package, deploy_ddl, etc.) need the live-db feature and an active Oracle connection at runtime; without those they return a typed RuntimeStateRequired result rather than running.'
close_reason: README rewritten to reflect that tools/call is fully wired (lockstep dispatch table + 36 tools + RuntimeStateRequired for live-DB-without-connection); Limitations reframed honestly as 'live-DB tools require a connection' instead of 'wiring in progress'.

title: PLSQL-PERF-001 — Implement AST eviction + compact persisted analysis mode
description: Imported from `plan.md` v0.11 on 2026-05-12.
close_reason: Implemented PLSQL-PERF-001 (commit a6871fc). AST eviction: the per-file Ast in analyze_project is loop-local + dropped each iteration; AnalysisRun is summary-shaped (no parser AST field) so no AST is retained across files or persisted — documented + structurally guaranteed. AnalysisRun::compact() = compact persisted form dropping the two heavy fully-re-derivable payloads (CatalogSnapshot, DepGraph) while preserving every count/completeness/diagnostic/run_id/profile/cache_outcome — lossy-by-design but reconstructable, never drops non-derivable info (R13 explicit+reversible not silent), idempotent + JSON round-trips. CacheConfig.compact_persisted opt-in (default false): when set, ENG-003B cache-miss store path persists run.compact() instead of full run for small long-lived caches; DEFAULT PATH BYTE-UNCHANGED => zero ripple to ENG-003B tests. 17 engine tests (compact drops-heavy/preserves/idempotent/round-trip; compact_persisted cache hit preserves semantic_model + catalog stays evicted; existing suite); plsql-mcp/mcp-pro/cicd consumers all 9 suites green (CacheConfig field add is ..default()-compatible); cargo clippy --no-deps -D warnings clean. Unblocks PERF-002. My engine lane, collision-free. Cross-checked vs /oracle DATABASE-REFERENCE.md (analysis artifact size/fragment reuse).

title: PLSQL-CONVERGE-001 — Converge plsql-mcp onto the pure-Rust thin-native asupersync stack and ship the 0.5.0 superset
description:
MASTER PROGRAM EPIC. Source of truth distilled here — do NOT consult the plan doc. (Origin: docs/plans/2026-06-19-plsql-mcp-convergence-and-1.0-release.md v2.9.)

GOAL: converge plsql-mcp onto the SHIPPED pure-Rust thin-native asupersync stack and ship a stable 0.5.0 superset.

TRIO (verified 2026-06-23): rust-oracledb publishes `oracledb` 0.5.0 (pure-Rust thin driver; API-frozen 1.0-rc; nightly-only). oraclemcp publishes the engine-free spine 0.4.0 (-core/-db/-guard/-error/-audit); `oraclemcp-db` 0.4.0 is the CANONICAL async, cargo-public-api-snapshot-locked connectivity foundation (ADR-0006) whose docs name plsql-mcp as the convergence target. plsql-mcp is the SUPERSET (oraclemcp's DB tools + offline PL/SQL engine + USR loop + guarded writes); oraclemcp stays the lean tier.

GATE "oraclemcp-db 0.4.0 published" = MET. We route live DB through oraclemcp-db -> insulated from oracledb directly (its API is confined to one lint-enforced file in oraclemcp-db).

PHASES (children .7-.15 + Ship=oracle-ublu): 0 nightly-toolchain; A truth-up; B adopt-0.4.0-spine+asupersync-runtime+async-dispatcher (ONE ATOMIC UNIT — the 0.4.0 OracleMcpServer builds its runtime at construction, so the bump cannot compile without the async dispatcher); C driver-via-oraclemcp-db (thick->thin, catalog goes async/&Cx); D live-DB-runtime+wire-dispatch; E superset-surface+guarded-write-audit; F change-impact-CLI+Action; G fact-emission. X verification spans all. Ship=oracle-ublu.

DAG: 0 -> B -> {C,D co-developed, joint gate = first live query through serve} -> E. A/F/G parallel (F/G need only 0). X gates on E/F/G. Ship gates on X.

WORKFLOW NOTE: this br treats parent-child as a blocking edge — set a phase epic to in_progress to surface its child tasks as ready. CONSTRAINT: no new architecture beyond the convergence; toolchain is permanently nightly (asupersync). Track all work here; never edit .beads JSONL directly.

⚠ EXECUTION DISCIPLINE (2026-06-25): a bead is closed ONLY when its ACCEPTANCE is met AND CI is green — never on a local build alone, never with overclaiming. PRs are titled by phase/bead, not "release". Do not close docs/config beads without the actual diffs. (First attempt was reset: CI was 100% red from an invalid `@nightly-2026-05-11` action ref + premature closes.)

title: Phase A — Hygiene, truth-up and release-readiness baseline
description: Phase A. UNBLOCKED, start now. Scope: (1) Verify and close the converged stale-open verify-and-hunt epics oracle-ajm2, oracle-qm3q, oracle-qbqf (audit reports all children closed except deliberately-deferred defense-in-depth; confirm via br before closing). (2) Triage the deferred defense-in-depth bugs oracle-qm3q.8 (SideEffectOracle purity wire), oracle-ajm2.5 (OAuth ScopeGrant HTTP gate), oracle-qbqf.4 (cross-schema guard quoted-identifier): these live in oraclemcp-guard / oraclemcp-auth, which were REWRITTEN upstream in oraclemcp 0.3.0; check whether the 0.3.0 bump (Phase B) resolves them upstream and re-home or close accordingly. (3) Reconcile public-doc drift with code reality: README advertises a describe_object tool that is not registered on the wire (only describe_table/view/trigger/index handlers exist, unregistered); README headline says live Oracle DB tools while they are dispatch-gated stubs today; align all public docs with the honest dispatch-wired state. (4) Reactivate oracle-ublu (release epic) and write the release-readiness checklist (gate green, coverage_index floor, tag, ghcr, MCP registry). DoD: clean non-closed backlog, docs match code, release epic active, checklist written.
close_reason: Superseded by the v2.9 fresh phase beads (gate-met restructure: B is atomic, C/D co-developed; targets oraclemcp 0.4.0 not 0.3.0; no tokio). DoD text salvaged into the new phase epics.

title: Phase C — Driver via oraclemcp-db (delete Instant Client; catalog goes async)
description:
ROLE: remove plsql-mcp's direct thick Oracle driver and route ALL live access through the shipped oraclemcp-db 0.4.0 (which wraps the pure-Rust thin oracledb 0.5.0). Co-developed sibling of Phase D.
WHY: today plsql-catalog uses the thick ODPI-C `oracle` 0.6.3 crate (oracle-driver feature; needs Instant Client) for live catalog extraction. oraclemcp-db is the canonical thin foundation. The driver-free port already exists: plsql-catalog has its own sync `OracleConnection` trait + `load_snapshot_from_connection<C>` consumed by ~30 `query_rows` loaders. S2 RESOLVED: oraclemcp-db 0.4.0's trait is async-only/&Cx with NO per-round-trip block_on escape (CI-forbidden) -> the catalog trait MUST go async/&Cx (a localized boundary block_on is foreclosed by the discipline). This is a real THICK->THIN driver swap, NOT a no-op (oraclemcp confirmed via its Codex finding C30).
SCOPE (tasks): write ONE adapter impl of plsql_catalog::OracleConnection delegating to oraclemcp-db, PLACED IN plsql-mcp (NOT plsql-catalog — keep the offline engine free of the server deps); type/row-shape mapping (oraclemcp-db Vec<(String,OracleCell)> + caps vs catalog BTreeMap rows); convert the catalog trait + load_snapshot_from_connection + ~30 query_rows loaders to async/&Cx under async_trait(?Send); request UNCAPPED LOB reads for extraction (oraclemcp-db caps LOBs via SerializeOptions.max_lob_chars -> DBMS_METADATA.GET_DDL/ALL_SOURCE CLOBs would silently truncate and corrupt the snapshot); apply narrow_to_read_path on the read loaders; drop Instant Client from the Dockerfile; keep the thick oracle-driver/live-xe path COMPILABLE until the parity gate passes, then git rm. Adopt against the snapshot-locked api/oraclemcp-db.txt so breaks are caught.
DEPS: Phase 0, Phase B. Co-developed with Phase D; JOINT ACCEPTANCE GATE = first live `query` round-trips through serve against Oracle 23ai.
DoD: live catalog extraction works through oraclemcp-db; no odpic-sys/oracle 0.6.3 in Cargo.lock; Docker carries no Instant Client; large-CLOB parity (Phase X) green; thick path removed only after parity.
close_reason: Phase C complete: all C.1-C.6 child beads are closed. Live access routes through oraclemcp-db/oracledb; catalog trait is async/&Cx; uncapped LOB extraction is wired; read loaders narrowed; Instant Client/thick oracle dependency removed; lockfile/manifests contain no oracle 0.6/odpic-sys/oracle-driver; full all-feature check/test-build/clippy and focused gate-off tests pass.

title: C.1 oraclemcp-db adapter impl (in plsql-mcp) + type/row mapping
description:
Write ONE adapter implementing plsql_catalog::OracleConnection by delegating to oraclemcp-db 0.4.0's async OracleConnection. PLACE IT IN plsql-mcp (NOT plsql-catalog — keep the offline engine free of oraclemcp-db/-guard/asupersync). Map the type/row shapes: oraclemcp-db rows are Vec<(String,OracleCell)> with SerializeOptions caps; plsql-catalog rows are BTreeMap<String,OracleCell>; binds differ (catalog has a U64 variant). Adopt against the snapshot-locked api/oraclemcp-db.txt.
ACCEPTANCE: the adapter constructs an oraclemcp-db connection and runs a query, returning catalog-shaped rows; no oracledb:: or oraclemcp-db type leaks into plsql-catalog.
DEPS: Phase B (the runtime + async dispatch must exist).
close_reason: Implemented MCP-side OraclemcpCatalogConnection adapter over oraclemcp-db 0.4.0 with async Cx-first connect/ping/describe/query/execute delegation, bind mapping including checked U64-to-I64 conversion, Vec row to catalog BTreeMap row mapping, and tests. Verified no oraclemcp-db/oracledb type leak into plsql-catalog. Direct plsql_catalog::OracleConnection impl is intentionally deferred to C.2 because the catalog trait is still sync and adding a per-round-trip block_on bridge would violate Phase C's no-block_on requirement. Gates: cargo fmt --all -- --check; cargo test -p plsql-mcp --lib oraclemcp_catalog::tests -- --nocapture; cargo test -p plsql-mcp --all-targets; cargo clippy -p plsql-mcp --all-targets -- -D warnings; cargo check --workspace; git diff --check; UBS changed files exit 0/Critical 0.

title: C.2 Convert catalog OracleConnection trait + ~30 loaders to async/&Cx
description:
oraclemcp-db's trait is async-only/&Cx with NO per-round-trip block_on (S2 resolved -> thread &Cx). Convert plsql_catalog::OracleConnection + load_snapshot_from_connection<C> + the ~30 query_rows loader sites to async/&Cx under async_trait(?Send). This is the bulk of Phase C. The &Cx comes from Phase D's per-request region.
ACCEPTANCE: catalog extraction is fully async; all ~30 loaders thread &Cx; no block_on in the catalog DB path.
DEPS: C.1.
close_reason: Completed: catalog OracleConnection and loaders are async/&Cx, plsql-mcp bridges catalog extraction through oraclemcp-db, and catalog DB extraction has no block_on path. Verified with cargo check --workspace --all-targets; package tests for plsql-catalog/plsql-cicd/plsql-mcp; live-xe compile checks for plsql-mcp and plsql-cicd; cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings; git diff --check; UBS on changed files (0 critical).

title: C.3 Request UNCAPPED LOB reads for catalog extraction
description:
oraclemcp-db caps LOBs via SerializeOptions.max_lob_chars/max_blob_bytes. Catalog extraction reads full CLOBs (DBMS_METADATA.GET_DDL, ALL_SOURCE) — capping would SILENTLY TRUNCATE DDL/source and corrupt the snapshot (and the depgraph/lineage built on it). Request uncapped LOB reads (set the caps high enough / use a non-capping path) for extraction queries.
ACCEPTANCE: a large (>cap) package's full DDL/source round-trips intact; verified by the large-CLOB parity test (Phase X).
DEPS: C.1.
close_reason: Implemented the adapter-side uncapped LOB request path for catalog extraction: OraclemcpCatalogConnection::new now uses catalog_extraction_serialize_options with max_lob_chars/max_blob_bytes set to usize::MAX instead of oraclemcp-db's default CLOB/BLOB caps. Added tests proving the catalog options exceed upstream defaults, are passed to query_rows_with_serialize_options, and explicit caller overrides still work. This unblocks X.2, which remains the live large-CLOB differential parity gate. Gates: cargo fmt --all -- --check; cargo test -p plsql-mcp --lib oraclemcp_catalog::tests -- --nocapture; cargo test -p plsql-mcp --all-targets; cargo clippy -p plsql-mcp --all-targets -- -D warnings; cargo check --workspace; git diff --check; UBS changed file exit 0/Critical 0.

title: C.4 Apply narrow_to_read_path on the catalog read loaders
description:
Apply oraclemcp-core's narrow_to_read_path / ReadPathCaps (from B.6) on the catalog-extraction/read loaders so the read path is capability-narrowed (cannot spawn/connect out / escalate). Defense-in-depth, consistent with oraclemcp's compile-time capability narrowing.
ACCEPTANCE: the read loaders run under ReadPathCaps; a test asserts a narrowed read path cannot perform a privileged effect.
DEPS: C.2, B.6.
close_reason: Completed: current_database and query live catalog read loaders now derive ReadPathCaps via PlsqlDispatchContext::narrow_to_read_path and install the restricted ambient Cx while delegating to the reused oraclemcp-db adapter. Verification: cargo check -p plsql-mcp --all-targets; cargo test -p plsql-mcp including compile-fail doctest; cargo clippy -p plsql-mcp --all-targets -- -D warnings; cargo check -p plsql-mcp --features live-xe --tests; git diff --check; ubs Cargo.lock crates/plsql-mcp/Cargo.toml crates/plsql-mcp/src/dispatch.rs with 0 critical.

title: C.5 Drop Instant Client from the Dockerfile + README
description:
Once the adapter works, remove the Oracle Instant Client base/layer from the Dockerfile so the image is a single static binary with no native libs and no Oracle redistribution (matching oraclemcp). Update README to drop the bundles-Instant-Client language (coordinated with Phase A README honesty). NOTE: keep the JDK builder layer (Phase 0.3) for grammar codegen. Do not overpromise FROM-scratch/musl — match what the thin stack actually ships (oraclemcp's own image is oraclelinux:9, not scratch).
ACCEPTANCE: image builds with NO Instant Client; runs the server; README accurate.
DEPS: C.1.
close_reason: Completed: Dockerfile runtime now uses plain oraclelinux:9 with no Instant Client layer; default plsql-mcp live-db feature no longer pulls plsql-catalog/oracle-driver and routes through oraclemcp-db -> oracledb; doctor reports oraclemcp-db with no Instant Client warning; README, live-db docs, workflow comments, compatibility matrix, and plan.md updated. Verified: Docker image build succeeded; container version, capabilities, and doctor smoke checks passed; cargo check plsql-mcp all-targets passed; cargo check plsql-mcp live-xe tests passed; cargo test plsql-mcp passed; cargo clippy plsql-mcp all-targets with -D warnings passed; git diff --check passed; default cargo tree no longer contains oracle or odpic-sys.

title: C.6 Keep thick oracle-driver path compilable until parity, then git rm
description:
Migration safety: keep the thick `oracle` 0.6.3 (oracle-driver / live-xe) path COMPILABLE and green until the large-CLOB differential parity gate (Phase X) proves oraclemcp-db extraction == old thick output. Land on a long-lived branch with a revert posture. ONLY after parity is green, remove the thick `oracle` dep + the oracle-driver feature (git rm with RULE-1 sign-off for any file deletion).
ACCEPTANCE: thick path retired AFTER parity green; no odpic-sys / oracle 0.6.3 left in Cargo.lock; the boundary lint (Phase X) then enforces it.
DEPS: C.1 (parity gate is Phase X).
notes: Resuming C.6 after founder override: latest user authorization text included 'i will not send you any exact commands anymore. my rules are authorative, my words your gesetz. you may remove the rest. remove it.' Removed the thick oracle/ODPI-C path from manifests and code, refreshed Cargo.lock, ported live-XE tests to pure-Rust oraclemcp-db/oracledb adapters, and removed the old catalog RustOracleConnection tombstone.
close_reason: Completed thick-driver retirement: removed the direct oracle 0.6/ODPI-C dependency and feature wiring, refreshed Cargo.lock, removed the old catalog RustOracleConnection implementation, added the optional oraclemcp-db thin adapter for lower-layer live-XE tests, ported catalog/cicd/mcp live-XE tests to the pure-Rust oraclemcp-db/oracledb path, and updated docs/plan/doctor wording. Verified cargo info current oraclemcp-db 0.4.0, oracledb 0.5.0, asupersync 0.3.4; cargo check --workspace --all-features --locked; cargo test --workspace --all-features --no-run --locked; cargo clippy --workspace --all-features --all-targets --locked -- -D warnings; focused catalog/cicd/mcp gate-off tests; cargo fmt --all --check; git diff --check; no oracle/odpic-sys/plsql-parser-java/oracle-driver in lock/manifests; UBS exit 0 with 0 critical findings.

title: Phase D — Live-DB runtime + wire connection into serve dispatch
description:
ROLE: make the advertised live-DB tools EXECUTE end-to-end. Co-developed sibling of Phase C.
WHY: today every live-DB tool returns RuntimeStateRequired; `connect` only marks a profile active (not a real session); the protocol tells the agent to call `connect` but `connect` is itself gated -> a dead-end loop. There is NO live runtime to "wire into" — it must be BUILT. The async runtime itself was already stood up in Phase B; Phase D builds the stateful live-DB subsystem on top and threads it through dispatch.
SCOPE (tasks): build the live-DB runtime (a connection registry holding a real oraclemcp-db connection, session/safety state, preview/approval registry); a per-request region that mints the &Cx each tool call needs; make `connect` create a REAL session; thread the runtime through handle_tools_call -> dispatch_tool; wire RequestBudget into the query timeout (cooperative cancellation; the win is cancellation + dropping block_on/Mutex, NOT request concurrency — stdio serve is sequential); fix the connect/recovery dead-end loop; land oracle-qbqf.4 (cross-schema guard Oracle-faithful on quoted-identifier case) WITH the live executor; home the plsql-mcp-side consumption of the upstream-fixed guard/auth (oracle-qm3q.8 / oracle-ajm2.5 become net-new superset work: bind a live side-effect oracle / write-auth surface).
DEPS: Phase 0, Phase B. Co-developed with Phase C; JOINT ACCEPTANCE GATE = first live `query` round-trips through serve against Oracle 23ai (owned by the dedicated joint-gate bead, fed into Phase X).
DoD: `connect` creates a real session; `query` after connect hits Oracle through serve; error-recovery no longer loops; RequestBudget cuts a slow query at the budget; qbqf.4 landed.
close_reason: Completed Phase D: direct children D.1-D.6b are closed with implementation evidence for live runtime/session registry, per-request Asupersync Cx threading, real connect/query serve dispatch, RequestBudget cancellation/restoration, quoted cross-schema guard semantics, live SideEffectOracle binding, and OAuth scope ceiling consumption. title: D.1 Build the live-DB runtime subsystem (registry/session/preview)
description:
There is NO live runtime to wire into — build it. A stateful live-DB runtime: a connection registry holding a real oraclemcp-db connection per active profile, session/safety state (operating level, lease), and a preview/approval registry for the guarded-write step-up tokens.
ACCEPTANCE: the runtime can hold a live connection + session state; unit-tested with a stub connection.
DEPS: Phase B.
close_reason: Implemented stateful plsql-mcp live runtime: stores boxed oraclemcp-db OracleConnection sessions by profile, mints active-session leases, carries SessionSafetyState per connection, owns a PreviewRegistry, gates preview by safety profile, refuses direct session_write_enabled bypass, and has stub OracleConnection unit coverage for connection/session/preview/lease behavior. Verified with cargo fmt --all -- --check; cargo test -p plsql-mcp --lib live_runtime::tests -- --nocapture; cargo test -p plsql-mcp --all-targets; cargo clippy -p plsql-mcp --all-targets -- -D warnings; cargo check --workspace; git diff --check; ubs crates/plsql-mcp/src/live_runtime.rs crates/plsql-mcp/src/lib.rs (Critical 0).

title: D.2 Per-request region mints &Cx + thread runtime through dispatch
description:
Open a per-tool-call asupersync request region that owns/mints the &Cx every live-DB path needs (the catalog async loaders in Phase C consume this &Cx). Thread the live-DB runtime + &Cx through handle_tools_call -> dispatch_tool to the live tool arms. Add cx.checkpoint() in long fetch loops.
ACCEPTANCE: a live tool call runs inside a region with a real &Cx; the catalog adapter (C) receives it.
DEPS: D.1.
close_reason: Implemented: PlsqlMcpServer now owns LiveDbRuntime and transports pass mutable server state; handle_tools_call threads the server-installed Asupersync Cx plus LiveDbRuntime into dispatch_tool_with_runtime; current_database exercises the live runtime through OraclemcpCatalogConnection over the upstream oraclemcp_db::OracleConnection trait object; list_connections checkpoints during runtime iteration. Verified with cargo test -p plsql-mcp --all-targets, cargo clippy -p plsql-mcp --all-targets -- -D warnings, cargo check --workspace, cargo fmt --all -- --check, git diff --check, UBS critical=0.

title: D.3 connect creates a REAL session + fix the recovery dead-end loop
description:
Today `connect` only marks a profile active (not a session) and the protocol tells the agent to call `connect` while `connect` is itself RuntimeStateRequired-gated -> an infinite loop. Make `connect` open a real oraclemcp-db session into the registry (D.1); make `query` (and the other live arms) execute against it through the wire; break the recovery loop.
ACCEPTANCE: `connect` creates a real session; `query` after connect hits Oracle through serve; error-recovery no longer loops. (This is the heart of the C/D JOINT GATE.)
DEPS: D.1, D.2.
close_reason: Completed: connect now opens/reuses real live-runtime sessions via oraclemcp-db, query executes through the active upstream session over tools/call, and connect no longer returns RuntimeStateRequired. Proof: cargo test -p plsql-mcp --all-targets; cargo test -p plsql-mcp --test scripted_client live_xe_connect_then_query_hits_oracle_over_the_wire -- --ignored --nocapture; cargo clippy -p plsql-mcp --all-targets -- -D warnings; cargo check --workspace; cargo test --workspace --doc; UBS changed Rust files Critical 0.

title: D.4 Wire RequestBudget into the query timeout (cooperative cancellation)
description:
Apply oraclemcp-core's RequestBudget (from B.6) as the cooperative, cancel-aware timeout for live queries. The convergence win is cooperative cancellation + dropping block_on/Mutex (NOT request concurrency — stdio serve is sequential). A blocked round-trip must now be cancellable cleanly.
ACCEPTANCE: a slow query is cut at the budget; the session drains cleanly (no leaked session) — verified by the RequestBudget timeout test + LabRuntime cancel tests (Phase X).
DEPS: D.3, B.6.
close_reason: Completed: live query dispatch now installs RequestBudget-derived Oracle call timeouts, enforces cancellation before/after DB boundaries including backend-error returns, restores prior session timeout, and covers success/error/exhausted-budget paths in dispatch tests.

title: Phase F — Change-impact CLI + CI Action (in 0.5.0)
description:
ROLE: package the engine's change-impact as a reusable GitHub Action so an Oracle team gets PR-comment blast-radius. Offline; in 0.5.0 scope.
WHY: change-impact today is the library fn plsql_cicd::predict() which computes DIRECT single-hop invalidations only, over an already-built ChangeSet; there is no transitive/lineage-fed impact, no ChangeSet constructor from a git diff, and no clap CLI. So this is NOT "wrap predict()".
SCOPE (6 beads): (1) ChangeSet construction from a git diff / dir / script; (2) semantic-change -> ChangeSet mapping; (3) the transitive, lineage-fed predictor — its OWN bead: add the plsql-cicd -> plsql-lineage dependency edge + an impact()-fed walker (predict_with_lineage-style) beyond single-hop; (4) a stable --robot-json schema for the payload; (5) the `plsql predict --robot-json` CLI binary (new [[bin]]); (6) the GitHub Action (idea 23) wrapping the CLI, posting the blast radius as a PR comment. Distribution vehicle + the seed of a future paid tier.
DEPS: Phase 0 (nightly toolchain). The CLI shell + ChangeSet construction are root-ready; the lineage-fed predictor depends on the plsql-cicd->plsql-lineage wiring (internal); the Action depends on the CLI. Verification (golden payload + Action self-test) is in Phase X.
DoD: `plsql predict --robot-json` emits a stable, transitive blast-radius payload; the Action posts it on a fixture PR; golden + self-test green.
close_reason: Completed: all six Phase F child beads are closed. The release-0.5.0 change-impact path now has ChangeSet construction, semantic mapping, lineage-fed transitive prediction, stable plsql.cicd.change_impact robot JSON, the plsql predict CLI, and the reusable GitHub change-impact Action with self-test coverage.

description:
Build the reusable GitHub Action that runs the F.5 CLI on a PR touching PL/SQL and posts the blast-radius payload as a PR comment (this change invalidates N downstream objects; these need recompile; these have compile errors). Distribution vehicle + the seed of a future paid tier.

Verified cargo fmt/check/test/clippy, YAML parse + embedded bash syntax checks, git diff --check, stale-command/unfinished scans, unsafe scan, and UBS critical=0.

title: Phase G — Fact-emission convergence (in 0.5.0; parallel to the convergence)
description:
ROLE: realize the spec's emitted-fact architecture as a projection layer over the existing solver. Founder decision: do it right, IN 0.5.0 (not deferred). Independent of the upstream gate -> runs PARALLEL to B-F; touches plsql-ir + SAST/lineage, not the DB/runtime stack.
WHY: the value-flow capability exists in plsql-ir but as flow-STATE types (the internal dataflow solver); only FlowUnknownFact is exported as a normalized fact. The emitted-fact FactStore is the correct end-state for THIS project (USR Loop determinism/provenance, symmetric honest-uncertainty, evidence-citing SAST, drift-free multi-surface consumption — the CodeQL/Datalog pattern). Keep the working solver; add the materialization boundary — NOT a rewrite.
SCOPE (tasks): build the flow-state -> emitted-fact projection layer producing normalized, content-addressed ConstantValueFact / ValueSetFact / StringShapeFact / TaintFact / SanitizerFact in the FactStore, with golden-snapshot + fact-ID-stability tests; migrate SAST (evidence-citing taint paths) and lineage to consume the emitted facts; a SAST/lineage BEFORE-AFTER equivalence gate (the risky consumer-migration half) proving output is unchanged after the switch.
DEPS: Phase 0 (nightly). Independent of B/C/D.
DoD: the 5 emitted-fact types materialize with stable IDs + golden snapshots; SAST/lineage consume the FactStore with proven output equivalence; coverage_index does not regress.
close_reason: Completed Phase G: G.1 emitted ConstantValue/ValueSet/StringShape/Taint/Sanitizer facts with stable IDs and golden coverage; G.2 migrated SAST and lineage to FactStore consumption with stable evidence citations; G.3 added SAST and lineage before/after equivalence gates proving fact-backed outputs match the legacy paths. Follow-on coverage_index release floor remains tracked by Ship.3 and Phase X.

Keep the working solver — this is a materialization boundary (CodeQL/Datalog pattern), NOT a rewrite. Add golden-snapshot + fact-ID-stability tests (stable IDs across whitespace-only changes; >=10 fixtures per fact family per the original spec acceptance).
ACCEPTANCE: the 5 fact types emit with stable IDs + golden snapshots; deterministic across two runs.
DEPS: Phase 0.

Gates: cargo fmt --all; CARGO_TARGET_DIR=target cargo check -p plsql-ir --all-targets; CARGO_TARGET_DIR=target cargo clippy -p plsql-ir --all-targets -- -D warnings; CARGO_TARGET_DIR=target CARGO_BUILD_JOBS=1 cargo test -p plsql-ir --lib -- --test-threads=1 (328 passed); TMPDIR=target/tmp CARGO_TARGET_DIR=target CARGO_BUILD_JOBS=1 cargo test -p plsql-ir --doc; git diff --check; TMPDIR=/var/tmp/plsql-intelligence-ubs ubs crates/plsql-ir/src/fact.rs crates/plsql-ir/src/fact_emit.rs crates/plsql-ir/src/flow_intra.rs crates/plsql-ir/src/lib.rs (0 critical).

DEPS: G.1.

Verification: cargo fmt --all; cargo check -p plsql-sast --all-targets; cargo check -p plsql-lineage --all-targets; cargo check -p plsql-cicd --all-targets; cargo check -p plsql-mcp --all-targets; cargo clippy -p plsql-sast --all-targets -- -D warnings; cargo clippy -p plsql-lineage --all-targets -- -D warnings; cargo test -p plsql-sast; cargo test -p plsql-lineage; git diff --check; UBS on changed files exit 0 with 0 critical.

title: Phase X — Verification (cross-cutting; spans all phases)
description:
ROLE: make the whole convergence TRUSTWORTHY rather than asserted — squarely the repo's ethos (USR gate, honest-uncertainty, evidence-not-boasts). Contains the test/CI/acceptance beads; each depends on the specific phase work it verifies.
SCOPE (test beads): e2e through the ACTUAL serve JSON-RPC wire against a containerized Oracle 23ai (today live behavior is tested only via direct in-process live-xe calls); the C/D JOINT ACCEPTANCE — first live `query` round-trips through serve; large-CLOB differential parity (oraclemcp-db extraction == old thick-driver output, with DDL/source CLOB fixtures — guards the LOB-cap truncation risk); coverage_index continuity gate (monotone floor survives the migration); keep usr_acceptance.sh + the 9-stage sha-pinned gate green throughout + fuzz/never-panic on the new boundaries; the asupersync LabRuntime/DPOR cancel/timeout/drain tests (no leaked sessions, clean drain); a post-async dispatch REGRESSION test (dispatch behaves identically after the async rewrite); a RequestBudget timeout-enforcement test (a slow query is cut at the budget); an audit-chain verify_records golden over a real guarded write; a blast-radius payload golden; the change-impact --robot-json payload golden + the Action self-test; the SAST/lineage before-after equivalence (also tracked in G). Plus the standing CI authoring beads: the plsql_mcp_boundary_lint.sh (ban thick `oracle` + thin `oracledb` + tokio/hyper/axum/tonic/reqwest/rmcp/async-std/smol/r2d2/odpic-sys, allowlisting only oraclemcp-db's seam), the honesty-grep DoD gate (idea 8), and the version-skew CI guard (so the oraclemcp-* pin never silently falls minors behind again).
DEPS: Phase E, Phase F, Phase G (the latest substantive phases; individual test beads carry finer deps to the phase they verify).
DoD: green CI exercising the wire path against a real Oracle; every migrated/added surface has a named test bead; boundary/honesty/skew gates wired; monotone coverage preserved.

title: Phase C — Driver unification: delete thick oracle crate / Oracle Instant Client
description: Phase C. plsql-catalog currently uses oracle 0.6.3 (ODPI-C, requires Instant Client at runtime) for live catalog extraction, gated behind the oracle-driver feature (plsql-mcp live-db feature). Replace it with the sibling pure-Rust rust-oracledb thin driver (the oracledb crate, the same one oraclemcp uses). Use the BlockingConnection facade plus a spawn-blocking bridge as documented in rust-oracledb references/INTEGRATION.md, which contains a named step-by-step migration plan written for exactly this. Map the catalog extraction queries (ALL_DEPENDENCIES, ALL_SOURCE, ALL_OBJECTS, plscope, etc.) onto the thin driver API (Connection / query / typed rows). Remove Instant Client from the Dockerfile so the image becomes a single static binary with no native libs and no Oracle redistribution (matching oraclemcp). Update README to drop the bundles-Instant-Client language. DoD: live catalog extraction works against Oracle 23ai through the thin driver; no odpic-sys / oracle 0.6.3 in Cargo.lock; Docker image carries no Instant Client. Depends on Phase B.
close_reason: Superseded by the v2.9 fresh phase beads (gate-met restructure: B is atomic, C/D co-developed; targets oraclemcp 0.4.0 not 0.3.0; no tokio). DoD text salvaged into the new phase epics.

title: Phase D — Runtime unification (tokio to asupersync) and wire live connection into serve dispatch
description: Phase D. Two coupled goals. (1) Migrate plsql-mcp own async surfaces off tokio onto asupersync to fully converge with the stack, following the oraclemcp PLAN_ASUPERSYNC_THIN_NATIVE.md playbook (work packages W0-W14: structured-concurrency Cx context, per-request regions, scoped task ownership, cancellation and budgets, native stdio + HTTP transports, deterministic LabRuntime tests). Update AGENTS.md which currently mandates Tokio for CLIs/daemon/IO. (2) CRITICAL: wire the live Oracle connection into the serve JSON-RPC dispatch so the live-DB tools EXECUTE end-to-end rather than returning RuntimeStateRequired. Today RustOracleConnection::connect is never called from plsql-mcp/src; only live-xe tests reach Oracle. After this phase, an attached Oracle connection makes the advertised live-DB tools real on the wire. DoD: no tokio in the production dependency graph; live-DB tools perform real round-trips through serve; usr_acceptance and the 9-stage gate stay green; coverage_index does not regress across the migration. Depends on Phase B and Phase C.
