title: Phase 0 — Nightly-toolchain migration (the true watershed)
description:
ROLE: flip the whole plsql-intelligence workspace from STABLE Rust to the pinned NIGHTLY that asupersync requires. This gates every code phase (B/C/D/F/G compile only on nightly).
WHY: asupersync uses nightly-only features (try_trait_v2 + residual). Both upstreams are pinned nightly-2026-05-11; rust-oracledb ADR-0001 keeps nightly through its 1.0; oraclemcp confirms "move to stable Rust is OUT". Nightly is a PERMANENT, build-time-only, reproducible commitment — not temporary.
SCOPE (tasks): (1) flip rust-toolchain.toml to nightly-2026-05-11 (or oraclemcp's successor); (2) remove rust-version="1.85" from root Cargo.toml; (3) update the Dockerfile builder toolchain (installs stable today) WHILE PRESERVING the JDK layer the oraclelinux:9 builder needs for ANTLR jar codegen; (4) move every CI workflow off dtolnay/rust-toolchain@stable (~15-16 sites; re-grep at execution); (5) re-baseline `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` and the #![forbid(unsafe_code)] crates on nightly; (6) document the no-stable-MSRV reality in AGENTS.md/README.md (the "Rust 1.85+" badge becomes false); (7) pin asupersync to oraclemcp's resolved version (it declares ^0.3.4 -> 0.3.4; budget per-minor churn).
DEPS: none (root; run alongside A).
DoD: workspace + all CI builds green on the pinned nightly; no @stable left in workflows; rust-version removed; Docker image still builds the grammar (JDK present) with no Instant Client regression introduced here; toolchain docs honest.

⚠ DoD GUARD (2026-06-25, after a failed first attempt): (1) Close a Phase-0 bead ONLY when the PR CI is fully GREEN on the pinned nightly — never on a local `make check` alone (the pinned nightly differs from the local default toolchain). (2) Title PRs by phase/bead ("Phase 0 — nightly toolchain"), NOT "release: v0.5.0" — the release tag is the LAST step. (3) Evidence before assertions: never claim "make check passes" while CI is red.
close_reason: Completed Phase 0. Closed children 7.1 through 7.5: workspace rust-toolchain.toml pins nightly-2026-05-11; rust-version removed from root and members; CI workflows use dtolnay/rust-toolchain@nightly with toolchain nightly-2026-05-11 and no @stable refs; clippy -D warnings passed locally and in GitHub Actions; Dockerfile builder installs nightly-2026-05-11 while preserving java-17-openjdk-headless and docker build proved ANTLR codegen plus release plsql-mcp compile; asupersync/nightly bump runbook exists; README and AGENTS state the no-stable-MSRV pinned-nightly reality. description:

Re-baseline `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings` and confirm the 22 #![forbid(unsafe_code)] crates compile clean on nightly (nightly lints differ; warnings-as-errors on nightly is brittle — fix or allow deliberately).

⚠ FAILPROOF (added 2026-06-25 after a failed first attempt). The CI step is `uses: dtolnay/rust-toolchain@<ACTION_REF>` where <ACTION_REF> is a VERSION OF THE ACTION (@stable, @nightly, @master) — NOT the toolchain to install. Do NOT write `@nightly-2026-05-11` — that ref does not exist and EVERY job dies at "Set up job" ("Unable to resolve action"). CORRECT: `uses: dtolnay/rust-toolchain@nightly` WITH `with:{ toolchain: nightly-2026-05-11, components: "clippy,rustfmt" }`, OR copy oraclemcp/rust-oracledb workflows verbatim. CLOSE this ONLY when the PR CI is fully GREEN on the pinned nightly.

title: Phase B — Adopt the 0.4.0 spine + asupersync runtime + async dispatcher (ONE ATOMIC UNIT)
description:
ROLE: the convergence watershed. Bumping plsql-mcp onto the shipped oraclemcp 0.4.0 spine FORCES the asupersync runtime + an async dispatcher into the SAME unit — they compile together or not at all. After this phase the server compiles and serves; the live-DB tool arms are still gated (live wiring is Phase D).
WHY (verified against oraclemcp 0.4.0 source): the 0.4.0 `OracleMcpServer` builds its asupersync runtime AT CONSTRUCTION; `ToolDispatch::dispatch` is now an object-safe `fn dispatch(&self, cx:&Cx, ctx:DispatchContext, name, args) -> DispatchFuture` (returns a boxed future; NOT literally `async fn`); `serve_stdio` is no longer `async`. plsql-mcp binds its engine through `ToolDispatch`, so its sync `dispatch_tool`/`handle_tools_call` must be rewritten. Spine delta 0.1.0->0.4.0: `oraclemcp-error` source/public-API unchanged (metadata + snapshot only); `oraclemcp-guard` surface unchanged but classifier STRICTER (more statements classify Guarded/Forbidden — plsql-mcp guard tests must be re-baselined); `oraclemcp-core` genuinely BREAKING (the dispatch + serve_stdio changes above).
SCOPE (~6 sub-beads): (a) add direct deps oraclemcp-db=0.4.0 + oraclemcp-audit=0.4.0 (+ later -telemetry if observability) and bump -core/-error/-guard 0.1.0->0.4.0 (transitively pulls oracledb 0.5.0 + asupersync + nightly); (b) bootstrap an asupersync RuntimeBuilder at server construction; (c) the serve-entry block_on shim that drives the async DispatchFuture on that runtime (the ONE blessed block_on — the per-round-trip DB block_on stays forbidden); (d) rewrite ToolDispatch impl + dispatch_tool + handle_tools_call to the async signature threading &Cx; (e) re-baseline plsql-mcp guard-dependent tests for stricter classification; (f) adopt RequestBudget + ReadPathCaps/narrow_to_read_path types. Re-validate the one-way boundary.
DEPS: Phase 0 (nightly).
DoD: workspace compiles + `serve` runs on the 0.4.0 spine + asupersync runtime; live arms still return RuntimeStateRequired; guard tests green at the stricter baseline; boundary lint green; a post-async dispatch regression test (Phase X) confirms identical dispatch behavior.
close_reason: Completed Phase B: all six child beads B.1-B.6 are closed; plsql-mcp is on the oraclemcp 0.4.0/asupersync spine, dispatch runs through the async ToolDispatch future boundary, the blessed serve-entry block_on remains isolated, guard tests are rebaselined, and RequestBudget/ReadPathCaps are adopted. Local gates passed for B.6, usr-loop run 28298025171 succeeded, and CI run 28298025160 succeeded including rustfmt, clippy, cargo test --workspace --all-targets, cargo test --workspace --doc, cargo deny/audit, plan-lint, parse-success, corpus license, lab-gate, demo-no-db, and cargo bench --no-run.

The 0.4.0 OracleMcpServer builds its asupersync runtime AT CONSTRUCTION. plsql-mcp's serve path is sync std::io today; stand up a single asupersync RuntimeBuilder owned by the server so the async DispatchFuture can be driven. Mirror oraclemcp's pattern (server.rs).

Added a runtime ownership regression test proving block_on installs an Asupersync Cx; updated stdio/TCP serve tests to construct the server. description:
At the serve entry, drive each request's async DispatchFuture to completion via block_on on the owned asupersync runtime (exactly oraclemcp's serve_stdio pattern). This boundary block_on is the ONLY permitted one; the per-round-trip DB block_on stays FORBIDDEN (the boundary lint / discipline). Document it with a `// block-on-boundary:` marker.

The boundary obtains the current Cx installed by runtime.block_on and delegates to async handle_request_with_cx; handle_request_line keeps parse errors synchronous but sends valid frames through that boundary. Repo block_on inventory shows exactly one production block_on call in crates/plsql-mcp/src/mcp_protocol.rs plus the Cx-installation test, with no DB-path/per-round-trip block_on in this repo. title: B.4 Rewrite ToolDispatch + dispatch_tool + handle_tools_call to async (&Cx)
description:
THE highest-risk bead. 0.4.0 ToolDispatch::dispatch is `fn dispatch(&self, cx:&Cx, ctx:DispatchContext, name, args) -> DispatchFuture` (object-safe; returns a boxed future; NOT `async fn`). plsql-mcp's dispatch_tool (dispatch.rs, sync free fn) and handle_tools_call (mcp_protocol.rs, sync) must be rewritten to the async signature, threading &Cx + the new DispatchContext, returning DispatchFuture. The offline/static tool arms stay synchronous internally (wrapped in a ready future); the live-DB arms become genuinely async (they will execute once Phase D wires the connection — until then they still return RuntimeStateRequired).
ACCEPTANCE: workspace compiles on the 0.4.0 core; `serve` dispatches every advertised tool; a post-async dispatch REGRESSION test (Phase X) proves identical behavior for the offline tools.
DEPS: B.2, B.3.

Offline tools keep prior wire behavior; runtime-state-required still returns isError result; malformed args remain JSON-RPC -32602. Verification: cargo check -p plsql-mcp --all-targets; cargo test -p plsql-mcp --lib; cargo test -p plsql-mcp --bin plsql-mcp serve; cargo test -p plsql-mcp --test scripted_client; cargo test -p plsql-mcp --all-targets; cargo clippy -p plsql-mcp --all-targets -- -D warnings; cargo check --workspace; cargo fmt --all -- --check; git diff --check; UBS changed files critical=0; block_on scan has only one production boundary at mcp_protocol.rs:98.

title: Verify-and-hunt remediation ROUND 13 (2026-06-03): 5 findings (3 keep: 2 med dep-extraction correctness + 1 low metric; 2 defense-in-depth deferred)

title: tables_after drops all but first table in legacy comma-joined FROM lists (missing Reads edges)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + calibration (round 13).

### crates/plsql-ir/src/dml_edges.rs:297 — verdict=keep, severity=medium
**Impact:** A legacy Oracle comma join (FROM a, b, c) — ubiquitous in real PL/SQL — produces a Reads dependency-graph edge only on the FIRST table; the read dependency on every sibling table is silently dropped from the dependency graph, lineage, and impact analysis with no diagnostic emitted.
**Fix:** In tables_after, after pushing each [schema.]table token, skip an optional per-table alias and surrounding whitespace; if the next non-whitespace byte is a top-level comma (parenthesis-depth 0, to avoid splitting TABLE(f(a,b)) or parenthesised sub-selects), advance past it and loop to read the next sibling table, repeating until the next byte is not a comma — mirroring sql_resolve.rs:287-293 and reusing sql_columns.rs::split_top_level_commas depth logic. Restrict the comma-continuation to the FROM keyword (and JOIN already gets per-clause occurrences); INTO/UPDATE/USING write/merge targets are single-target in normal DML and should keep breaking after the first table. Add a regression test asserting `FROM emp a, dept b` yields Reads of both EMP and DEPT and `FROM a, b, c` yields A, B, C.

## Acceptance
- [ ] Root-cause fix + regression test (keeps).
- [ ] cargo test/clippy green.
- [ ] No regression.
close_reason: dml_edges tables_after now traverses a top-level comma-separated FROM list (skipping per-table aliases), mirroring sql_resolve — a legacy 'FROM emp a, dept b' / 'FROM a, b, c' yields a Read edge for every sibling table, not just the first. Restricted to the FROM keyword (INTO/UPDATE/USING are single-target; JOIN gets per-clause occurrences). +regression test.

title: Mask string literals before keyword scans in dml_edges/sql_resolve table-access extractors
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + calibration (round 13).

### crates/plsql-ir/src/dml_edges.rs:297 — verdict=keep, severity=medium
**Impact:** A DML statement carrying a SQL clause keyword (FROM/JOIN/UPDATE/USING) as a whole word inside a string literal — common in audit/log message strings, error text, and dynamic-SQL builder fragments that lower to static DML — injects a phantom Reads/Writes dependency edge (and DependencyEdge fact) into the production dependency graph, polluting lineage/impact analysis; a schema-qualified keyword-in-literal mints a phantom cross-schema write target that can drive a spurious dependency cycle or false cross-schema-write signal.
**Fix:** Mask single-quoted (and Oracle q-quoted) string-literal contents before the keyword scans in the two IR extractor families, mirroring fact_emit. Make a literal-masking helper crate-visible (or reuse/extend fact_emit::mask_string_literals at fact_emit.rs:523) and use a SPACE filler — not `_`, which is_ident_byte treats as identifier (dml_edges.rs:330) and would fuse the masked literal — so an embedded keyword loses its whole-word/identifier neighbours and is rejected by prev_ok/next_ok. Build a masked buffer once in accesses_from_sql (dml_edges.rs:166) and use it for both keyword detection and identifier slicing (real table names lie outside literals, so they survive masking verbatim); apply the same masking in sql_resolve.rs before tables_after_keyword (line 180) and delete_target (line 30

## Acceptance
- [ ] Root-cause fix + regression test (keeps).
- [ ] cargo test/clippy green.
- [ ] No regression.
close_reason: dml_edges::accesses_from_sql and sql_resolve::resolve_sql now mask single-quoted string-literal CONTENTS (reusing fact_emit::mask_string_literals, now pub(crate)) before the clause-keyword scan, so a FROM/INTO/JOIN/USING keyword buried in a literal ('failed to INSERT INTO orders' / 'read FROM cache') can no longer mint a phantom table access. Masking preserves byte length so name slices stay offset-aligned. +regression tests in both crates.

title: Make cyclomatic() string/comment-aware so branch keywords in literals and comments aren't counted
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + calibration (round 13).

### crates/plsql-mcp/src/plsql_analyze.rs:193 — verdict=keep, severity=low
**Impact:** An agent calling the plsql_analyze tool gets an inflated per-file cyclomatic complexity for any PL/SQL file whose string literals (error messages, dynamic-SQL fragments) or comments contain words like IF/AND/OR/WHEN/FOR/WHILE/CASE/ELSIF — extremely common — so the reported number is too high (never too low). It is an advisory metric, not a safety gate, so nothing breaks functionally; only the reported figure is wrong.
**Fix:** Neutralize Oracle string literals ('...', treating '' as escaped quote) and comments (-- ... \n, /* ... */) before tokenizing, consistent with the crate's other keyword scanners. Cheapest path: add a small self-contained pre-pass in plsql_analyze.rs that blanks literal/comment bodies, then run the existing split/count loop over the cleaned copy (avoids cross-module coupling); or make query.rs `strip_sql_comments` pub(crate) and additionally blank single-quoted literal contents (strip_sql_comments alone leaves literal bodies intact). Add regression tests: cyclomatic("BEGIN v := 'IF a AND b OR c'; END;") == 1; cyclomatic("BEGIN NULL; -- WHILE FOR WHEN\nEND;") == 1; cyclomatic("BEGIN NULL; /* ELSIF CASE */ END;") == 1.

## Acceptance
- [ ] Root-cause fix + regression test (keeps).
- [ ] cargo test/clippy green.
- [ ] No regression.
close_reason: cyclomatic() now blanks string literals + line/block comments before tokenizing (blank_strings_and_comments), so a branch keyword inside a literal or comment ('IF a AND b' / -- WHILE) no longer inflates the complexity metric. +regression test.

title: Make cross-schema guard Oracle-faithful on quoted-identifier case (stop folding parsed target) before live executor is wired
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + calibration (round 13).
DEFERRED (2026-06-09): the bead's own fix note says 'land WITH the live executor wiring, NOT before' — run_execute_approved has NO production caller (dispatch.rs has no live executor). Folding quoted-identifier-case logic into the cross-schema guard before the executor exists would add untested, unexercised guard behavior. Land together with the live write executor. Not blocking.
notes: DEFERRED (calibration: defense-in-depth, do NOT ship before the live-DB executor is wired). run_execute_approved has no production caller today (dispatch returns RuntimeStateRequired); the uniform case-fold is internally consistent and the system has no representation of a case-sensitive principal. Fix (stop folding the parsed quoted target + case-sensitive cross-schema compare) lands WITH the executor-wiring work, where it becomes reachable. Tracked, non-blocking.
close_reason: Resolved with live executor wiring in D.5: execute_approved now keeps parse_target_schema's Oracle dictionary identity (unquoted owners uppercase, quoted owners exact), validates caller target_schema with the same target semantics, and require_cross_schema_confirmation requires exact typed confirmation for quoted/case-sensitive targets. Oracle skill audit: /home/durakovic/.codex/skills/oracle/references/oracle-skills/db/agent/schema-discovery.md §Best Practices lines 577-582 says pass identifier names exactly as dictionary stores them; unquoted identifiers are uppercase, quoted identifiers are stored as-is; never silently UPPER() a user-supplied name. Verification: cargo check -p plsql-mcp --all-targets; cargo test -p plsql-mcp; cargo clippy -p plsql-mcp --all-targets -- -D warnings; cargo check -p plsql-mcp --features live-xe --tests; git diff --check; ubs crates/plsql-mcp/src/cross_schema.rs crates/plsql-mcp/src/execute_approved.rs with 0 critical.

title: Document plsqld serial/no-timeout posture and add a connection read timeout
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + calibration (round 13).

### crates/plsql-store/src/bin/plsqld.rs:268 — verdict=defense-in-depth, severity=low
**Impact:** None today: no shipping component connects to plsqld.sock, so the serial accept loop cannot starve any real consumer. The only residual is robustness/documentation hygiene — if a future client-side wrapper is ever written and a client stalls mid-line or holds the socket idle, that one connection would block the loop, but no such caller exists and the serial-by-design posture is the codebase's documented intent.
**Fix:** Treat as defense-in-depth, not a medium bug. (1) Mirror the sibling daemons: add a comment at the accept loop scoping the serial/no-timeout behavior as intentional single-stream local-transport design (matching plsql-doc/src/serve.rs:164-167 and plsql-mcp/src/tcp.rs:13-14,139-141), so future maintainers/callers can anticipate it. (2) Add a defensive read timeout before constructing the BufReader: `stream.set_read_timeout(Some(Duration::from_secs(30)))?;` — a WouldBlock/TimedOut from reader.lines() then hits the existing `else { break }` arm and ends that connection instead of holding the socket open. Defer the thread-per-connection/Arc<Store> change until a real multi-client UDS consumer is actually written (none exists today).

## Acceptance
- [ ] Root-cause fix + regression test (keeps).
- [ ] cargo test/clippy green.
- [ ] No regression.
notes: DEFERRED (calibration: defense-in-depth, document/harden later). plsqld accept loop is serial with no read timeout — a slow/idle client blocks the daemon. plsqld is a dev/local store daemon, NOT the published oraclemcp artifact, so this is non-blocking for publish. Harden (per-connection read timeout + concurrent accept) when the daemon's role expands. Tracked.
close_reason: DONE (commit 9871ddf). plsqld serial accept loop now sets a per-connection read timeout (30s default, PLSQLD_READ_TIMEOUT_MS test knob) so a stalled/idle client is reaped via the existing break arm instead of blocking the loop; + scoping comment + an end-to-end regression test (verified fails without the fix). cargo test -p plsql-store green, clippy clean.

title: plsql-mcp MCP-registry listing (server.json OCI + OIDC publish-mcp workflow)

Each child bead is self-contained with file:line, impact, repro, and suggested fix.

title: plsql-ir flow_intra: bind DBMS_ASSERT cleansing to its argument subtree (SEC001 fail-open)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt + strict ground-truth calibration workflow (2026-06-02).

### Site crates/plsql-ir/src/flow_intra.rs:171 — CONFIRMED DEFECT, severity=high
**Impact:** A procedure that concatenates raw attacker-controlled p_user into dynamic SQL while also calling DBMS_ASSERT on any unrelated value in the same RHS expression produces zero SEC001 findings (and no skip), so a genuine SQL injection silently escapes the product's flagship injection guard.
**Evidence:** collect_expr_flow (crates/plsql-ir/src/flow_intra.rs:171-185): the Expr::Call arm pushes TaintCleanser::DbmsAssert onto the SHARED `flow` accumulator the moment any callee path starts with "DBMS_ASSERT." (line 173-176), then walks all args into that same flow; the Expr::Binary arm (182-185) merges both operands into one flow. crates/plsql-ir/src/flow.rs:164-166 Taint::flags_alarm = !kinds.is_empty() && cleansed_by.is_empty(); flow.rs:157-162 doc explicitly states cleansers "apply universally" and "Callers wanting per-kind cleansing can post-filter." crates/plsql-ir/src/flow_query.rs:80 taint_of computes is_tainted identically. The SEC001 caller crates/plsql-sast/src/rules.rs:83-84 reads `ctx.flow.taint_of(site).is_tainted` directly with NO per-kind post-filter and no check that the cleanser wraps the tainted operand. Empirical probe (throwaway integration test, cargo test -p plsql-sast, 
**Repro/trigger:** Input X: PL/SQL assignment `dyn := DBMS_ASSERT.ENQUOTE_LITERAL('x') || p_user;` with TaintSources.user_input_names=["P_USER"] and a DynamicSqlEvidence fact for site "DYN". Wrong output Y: analyze_flow yields ValueFlow{kinds=[UserInput], cleansed_by=[DbmsAssert]}, taint_of("DYN").is_tainted==false, and Sec001ExecuteImmediateInjection emits 0 findings and 0 skips — vs the control `dyn := p_user || ' x';` which correctly emits 1 SEC001 Critical finding. Reproduced end-to-end via the real public APIs (lower_statement_body, analyze_flow, run_scan, Sec001ExecuteImmediateInjection); DBMS_ASSERT is matched by prefix only, so any DBMS_ASSERT.* call on any sibling sub-expression triggers the fail-open
**Suggested fix:** In collect_expr_flow, do not push TaintCleanser::DbmsAssert onto the shared flow and then recurse the DBMS_ASSERT call's args into that same flow. Instead treat a recognized DBMS_ASSERT.* Call as a sanitized value that contributes no UserInput/BindVariable kind to the enclosing expression (i.e. compute its arg subtree's taint in isolation and discard it, or mark it cleansed in a sub-flow), so `DBMS_ASSERT.ENQUOTE_LITERAL('x') || p_user` yields kinds=[UserInput], cleansed_by=[] -> flags_alarm=true while siblings still contribute their own taint. Alternatively model cleansing per-(value,kind) in Taint and have flags_alarm/taint_of report an alarm when any kind lacks a matching cleanser on the same value. Fixing it in the flow pass benefits all consumers (taint_of, has_taint_kind, inter-procedural propagated_returns). Add a regression test asserting the ENQUOTE_LITERAL('x') || p_user case f

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root cause fixed in production code (not just the symptom).
- [ ] A regression test reproduces the defect before the fix (fails) and passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; `cargo fmt` (oraclemcp-* only).
- [ ] No behavior regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: Fixed the SEC001 taint fail-open by binding DBMS_ASSERT cleansing to its argument subtree. Reworked the Taint model so kinds holds only LIVE (uncleansed) taint: a DBMS_ASSERT.* call now computes its argument in an isolated sub-flow, DRAINS the kinds it sanitizes (recording DbmsAssert in cleansed_by for reporting), and contributes nothing injectable to the enclosing expression. flags_alarm/is_tainted are now '!kinds.is_empty()' (no longer gated on cleansed_by.is_empty(), which a sibling cleanse used to satisfy). Result: DBMS_ASSERT.ENQUOTE_LITERAL('x') || p_user now correctly alarms; DBMS_ASSERT(p_user) stays clean; DBMS_ASSERT(p_a) || 'lit' is NOT a false positive. Added 3 regression tests (flow_intra binding + sibling fail-open, flow.rs live-kind-with-recorded-cleanser); updated flow_query has_taint_kind test to new clean-value semantics. 255 plsql-ir + 94 plsql-sast tests green, clippy -D warnings clean. NB: this also resolves oracle-qm3q.26 (L11 merge_into cleanser intersect) since the alarm no longer depends on cleansed_by. No cargo fmt (plsql-ir pre-existing).

title: oraclemcp-core: enforce StdioAuthPolicy in rmcp initialize (stdio init-token gate is a no-op)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt + strict ground-truth calibration workflow (2026-06-02).

### Site crates/oraclemcp-core/src/server.rs:88 — CONFIRMED DEFECT, severity=medium
**Impact:** The moment a production binary wires serve_stdio as written, a StdioAuthPolicy::Required init-token becomes a silent no-op: a stdio client presenting no token or a wrong token is accepted, because nothing in the request path ever calls validate() and rmcp's default initialize accepts unconditionally. Today it is not live-exploitable (no caller, no binary), so it is a latent auth-bypass, not an active one.
**Evidence:** server.rs:88-106 serve_stdio: `match auth { Required{..} => info!("init-token required"), Disabled => warn!(...) } let service = self.serve((stdin, stdout)).await?; service.waiting().await?` — it ONLY logs the posture, then hands stdin/stdout to rmcp's serve loop; auth.validate() is never called.
ServerHandler impl (server.rs:133-171) defines get_info, list_tools, call_tool ONLY — no `fn initialize` override.
rmcp-1.7.0/src/handler/server.rs default initialize: `if context.peer.peer_info().is_none() { context.peer.set_peer_info(request); } std::future::ready(Ok(self.get_info()))` — unconditional, never inspects a token.
`rg '\.validate\('` across crates/ (minus target): the StdioAuthPolicy::validate at init_token.rs:57 is called ONLY at init_token.rs:113/115/118/124/125 (its own #[cfg(test)] tests). Every other .validate() hit is an unrelated type (auth_adapter, oauth_rs, depgraph, custo
**Repro/trigger:** Triggering input (once a binary calls `OracleMcpServer::new(...).serve_stdio(&StdioAuthPolicy::Required{ expected: "s3cr3t" })`): send an MCP initialize JSON-RPC frame over stdin with NO token (or a wrong token) in _meta. Trace: serve_stdio matches Required and logs info!, then self.serve(stdin/stdout) → rmcp routes the initialize to ServerHandler::initialize → OracleMcpServer has no override → rmcp default sets peer_info and returns Ok(self.get_info()) → handshake SUCCEEDS. validate() is never reached. Expected per init_token.rs:5-6 and plan §7.1: the connection must be refused. Disproof that it is live today: serve_stdio has zero non-test callers and no oraclemcp-* crate is a [[bin]], so n
**Suggested fix:** Store the resolved StdioAuthPolicy on OracleMcpServer (add an `auth: Arc<StdioAuthPolicy>` field, keep the struct Clone). Override `ServerHandler::initialize` to extract the presented token from the request's _meta and call `self.auth.validate(presented)`, returning McpError (invalid_request/unauthorized) on Err so the connection is refused before any other request, then fall through to the default behavior (set_peer_info + Ok(self.get_info())). Have serve_stdio set/pass the policy rather than only logging it. Add an integration test in tests/e2e_mcp.rs driving a real rmcp client through initialize: a Required policy rejects missing/wrong token and accepts the correct one; Disabled accepts any. HTTP path is unchanged (oauth_guard already enforces). Track as a pre-binary release blocker, not an emergency patch.

### Site crates/oraclemcp-core/src/server.rs:88 — CONFIRMED DEFECT, severity=medium
**Impact:** The instant a binary wires up serve_stdio, a StdioAuthPolicy::Required token gate would be fail-open: any client completing the MCP initialize handshake reaches call_tool with no token check, so the documented "checked on the first initialize" boundary does nothing. Today there is no reachable exploit (no caller activates the path), so impact is latent — a declared security boundary that is structurally inert until the feature is shipped.
**Evidence:** server.rs:88-106 serve_stdio receives `auth: &StdioAuthPolicy` but for `Required { .. }` only emits `tracing::info!("stdio transport: init-token required")` (line 91) then calls `self.serve((stdin,stdout))` (line 98); it NEVER calls auth.validate(). | OracleMcpServer's `impl ServerHandler` (server.rs:133-171) defines only get_info, list_tools, call_tool — no `initialize` override (rg 'fn initialize' crates/ returns only unrelated initialize_schema / test names / an HTTP test). | rmcp-1.7.0/src/handler/server.rs:182-191 default initialize: `if context.peer.peer_info().is_none() { context.peer.set_peer_info(request); } std::future::ready(Ok(self.get_info()))` — zero token consultation. | `rg '\.validate\(' crates/` shows StdioAuthPolicy::validate called ONLY at init_token.rs:113,115,118,124,125 (its own #[cfg(test)] module); no production caller. | `rg 'serve_stdio' crates/` returns ONLY t
**Repro/trigger:** Trigger (once serve_stdio is wired into a binary): set ORACLEMCP_STDIO_TOKEN=secret so resolve() yields StdioAuthPolicy::Required{expected:"secret"}; call serve_stdio(&policy). A client sends a JSON-RPC `initialize` with NO _meta token, then `tools/call` for oracle_capabilities. Trace: serve_stdio logs and calls self.serve(); the initialize hits rmcp's default (server.rs:182-191) which returns Ok(get_info()) without validate(); the client then reaches call_tool (server.rs:162) freely. X = initialize with missing/wrong token under Required, Y = handshake succeeds and tools are callable (validate never runs). Impossibility-of-current-exploit proof: `rg 'serve_stdio' crates/` = definition only;
**Suggested fix:** Store an Option<StdioAuthPolicy> on OracleMcpServer (set by serve_stdio or a builder; default None so the HTTP path, already guarded by oauth_guard, is unaffected) and override `async fn initialize(&self, request: InitializeRequestParams, context: RequestContext<RoleServer>) -> Result<InitializeResult, McpError>`: when a Required policy is present, extract the presented token from request._meta (rmcp model _meta), call policy.validate(presented), return an McpError on Missing/Mismatch (fail-closed) instead of Ok(get_info()); on Ok fall through to set_peer_info + Ok(self.get_info()). Add an integration test driving a real initialize over a stdio/duplex transport: correct token -> succeeds; wrong/missing under Required -> initialize fails and call_tool is unreachable; Disabled -> succeeds. This activates the already-correct constant-time validate() on the live path and aligns serve_stdio w

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root cause fixed in production code (not just the symptom).
- [ ] A regression test reproduces the defect before the fix (fails) and passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; `cargo fmt` (oraclemcp-* only).
- [ ] No behavior regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit fa34040ff7), re-verified on main. Root-cause fixed. Added auth: Option<Arc<StdioAuthPolicy>> field + with_stdio_auth() builder on OracleMcpServer; serve_stdio now wires the resolved policy (self.with_stdio_auth(auth.clone())) instead of only logging it. Overrode ServerHandler::initialize to call check_init_token() BEFORE the default accept, failing closed with McpError::invalid_request on Missing/Mismatch, then falling through to set_peer_info + get_info(). KEY DISCOVERY during impl: rmcp's WithMeta deserializer drains the typed request.meta into context.meta (the request _meta is hoisted into RequestContext.extensions), so the override reads context.meta, not request.meta — reading request.meta would have made the gate sile

title: plsql-ir flow_inter: wire-or-excise MAX_DEPTH transitive recursion + replace tautological depth-cap test
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt + strict ground-truth calibration workflow (2026-06-02).

### Site crates/plsql-ir/src/flow_inter.rs:315 — CONFIRMED DEFECT, severity=medium
**Impact:** A maintainer reading the green test suite believes the MAX_DEPTH / DepthCapExceeded cap is exercised, but it is dead code that no test or production path can ever reach; the module doc promises bounded multi-hop call-chain following while resolve_edge follows zero hops, so future transitive-taint work will silently produce shallow (single-hop) results with a false sense of coverage. No crash, injection, or fail-open today.
**Evidence:** flow_inter.rs:118-173 resolve_edge — single call site at line 113 `resolve_edge(edge, &by_id, &mut stack, 0, &mut result)` always passes depth=0, body has NO self-call, ends `let _ = stack;` (172) so the cap can never fire. rg confirms resolve_edge has no other callers; DepthCapExceeded appears only at its enum def (76) and the dead branch (129) — never matched in production. flow_query.rs reads only `inter.propagated_returns` (line 70), never `unknowns`, so DepthCapExceeded is never consumed. flow_inter.rs:333 `assert_eq!(usize::from(MAX_DEPTH).clamp(1, 16), MAX_DEPTH as usize)` with `MAX_DEPTH: u8 = 6` reduces to `6 == 6`; the test's own comment (317-324) concedes it does NOT trigger the branch and only checks a depth-0 success. `cargo test -p plsql-ir flow_inter` → 9 passed; the depth_cap test passes trivially. `rg propagate_inter|CallEdgeFlow` outside plsql-ir → exit 1 (no production
**Repro/trigger:** Impossibility proof for the cap branch: resolve_edge is invoked once per edge from propagate_inter with the literal depth=0 and never recurses, and 0 >= MAX_DEPTH(6) is false, so lines 125-132 are unreachable; no input can produce a FlowUnknownReason::DepthCapExceeded. Tautology proof for the test: substitute MAX_DEPTH=6 → 6.clamp(1,16)==6 and 6 as usize==6 → assert_eq!(6,6); holds for any MAX_DEPTH in [1,16] and runs no resolve_edge depth logic. A genuine depth-cap test is impossible to write without first implementing the recursion (push callee, look up its outgoing CallEdgeFlows, recurse with depth+1), confirming the cap is currently untestable dead code.
**Suggested fix:** Pick one and make doc + code + test agree. (A) If multi-hop is in scope: make resolve_edge actually recurse — thread an adjacency map of caller->outgoing CallEdgeFlows, after resolving a callee push it on `stack` and recurse depth+1 over its own edges, then pop; replace line 333 with a real test that builds a 7+ link chain a->b->...->h plus summaries and asserts `r.unknowns.iter().any(|u| u.reason == FlowUnknownReason::DepthCapExceeded)`. (B) If single-hop is the intended scope for now: delete the unreachable `depth >= MAX_DEPTH` branch (125-132), the `depth` param, the `MAX_DEPTH` const, the `DepthCapExceeded` variant and the `stack` push / `let _ = stack;` scaffolding, correct the module doc (8-13) to say it resolves a single call hop, and delete depth_cap_fires_when_chain_exceeds_max rather than leaving a 6==6 tautology that fakes coverage.

### Site crates/plsql-ir/src/flow_inter.rs:118 — CONFIRMED DEFECT, severity=low
**Impact:** Multi-hop inter-procedural taint chains (A calls B calls C) are silently not propagated, so an R13 completeness report can under-count flow boundaries; the gap is conservative (false-negative / under-count) and does not crash or fail open. Additionally the module doc and the depth-cap test give false confidence that depth-bounded chain following and its DepthCapExceeded boundary are implemented and tested when neither is.
**Evidence:** resolve_edge call sites (only one, plus def):
  flow_inter.rs:113:        resolve_edge(edge, &by_id, &mut stack, 0, &mut result);
  flow_inter.rs:118:fn resolve_edge(...)
propagate_inter loop (flow_inter.rs:111-114): each edge gets `let mut stack = vec![edge.caller.clone()];` then `resolve_edge(edge, &by_id, &mut stack, 0, &mut result);` — depth literal 0, stack of exactly one element.
resolve_edge body ends with `let _ = stack;` (line 172) and never calls itself; depth is read once at line 125 (`if depth >= MAX_DEPTH`) and never incremented/forwarded. MAX_DEPTH=6 (line 36), so `0 >= 6` is always false -> DepthCapExceeded branch (lines 125-131) is dead.
DepthCapExceeded references (no production emit path): `rg DepthCapExceeded crates/` => def (76), the unreachable branch (129), and a test comment (320). Nothing else.
Sole consumer flow_query.rs taint_of (lines 69-79) only folds `inter.p
**Repro/trigger:** Triggering input proving multi-hop is dropped: edges = [ {caller:"a",callee:"b",actual_arg_taints:[[UserInput]]}, {caller:"b",callee:"c",actual_arg_taints:[[UserInput]]} ], summaries: b has param_taints{0:[UserInput]} and returns_taint [], c has returns_taint [DbLink]. propagate_inter handles each edge independently at depth 0: edge a->b yields PropagatedReturn{caller:"a", result_taint:[UserInput]}; edge b->c yields PropagatedReturn{caller:"b", result_taint:[DbLink]}. C's DbLink taint never reaches A through B — there is no record with caller "a" carrying DbLink. Impossibility proof for DepthCapExceeded: depth is the literal 0 at the only call site and is never re-passed incremented, so `dep
**Suggested fix:** Pick one coherent path. (A, lower risk, matches current consumer) Treat as single-hop summary folding: remove the vestigial `depth`/`stack` params from resolve_edge, delete the unreachable DepthCapExceeded branch (lines 125-131) and document the variant as reserved or drop it, remove `let _ = stack;`, rewrite the module doc (lines 8-13) to say each call edge is resolved once against the callee summary rather than "followed at most MAX_DEPTH hops", and replace the tautological depth_cap_fires_when_chain_exceeds_max test (lines 315-334) with a real behavioral test or delete it. (B, if multi-hop is genuinely wanted) make resolve_edge recurse like calls.rs:107 / dml_edges.rs:92: push the callee onto stack, look up call_edges originating from the callee, call resolve_edge with depth+1 (recording DepthCapExceeded at the cap), pop on return; add a test that pre-seeds a chain exceeding MAX_DEPTH

### Site crates/plsql-ir/src/flow_inter.rs:118 — DEFENSE-IN-DEPTH (latent/phase-gated), severity=low
**Impact:** None today — false-positive-for-now / latent: the inter-procedural pass produces no incorrect SAST verdict because propagate_inter is never invoked in production and no FlowQuery is built with .with_inter. If a future phase wires it in as-is, transitive taint (A->B->C) would be missed and the depth-cap/transitive-cycle guards would be dead, weakening cross-routine taint detection.
**Evidence:** flow_inter.rs:111-114 propagate_inter iterates each edge independently: `for edge in call_edges { let mut stack = vec![edge.caller.clone()]; resolve_edge(edge, &by_id, &mut stack, 0, &mut result); }` — depth is hardcoded 0. `rg resolve_edge crates/` returns exactly two hits: the def (line 118) and that one call (line 113); resolve_edge never recurses. Body line 172 `let _ = stack;` discards the stack after seeding it with one element, so the cycle guard at line 133 only matches direct A->A self-calls; the `depth >= MAX_DEPTH` branch at line 125 (DepthCapExceeded, line 129) is unreachable. Module doc lines 9-10: "Each call edge is followed at most `MAX_DEPTH` hops" — implementation follows zero hops past the immediate callee. Test `depth_cap_fires_when_chain_exceeds_max` (line 316) resolves a depth-0 A->B edge and asserts a tautology `assert_eq!(usize::from(MAX_DEPTH).clamp(1,16), MAX_DEP
**Repro/trigger:** Triggering input proving the divergence (transitive taint loss): edges = [ A->B with actual_arg_taints=[[UserInput]], B->C with actual_arg_taints=[[(B's param)]] ]; summaries = [ B{param 0 propagating, returns []}, C{param 0 propagating, returns []} ]. propagate_inter resolves A->B and B->C as two isolated PropagatedReturn entries; it never follows B's outgoing edge from within A's resolution, so the UserInput taint reaching B from A is never composed onto the C result. Expected per doc (multi-hop following): taint reaches C; actual: it does not. Impossibility proof for the dead branches: since resolve_edge is only ever called with depth=0 and never recurses, `depth >= MAX_DEPTH` (6) can nev
**Suggested fix:** Either (a) complete the feature: give resolve_edge a real transitive frontier (pass an adjacency map callee -> &[CallEdgeFlow] from propagate_inter, push the callee on `stack`, recurse with depth+1 feeding result_taint into onward actuals), which activates DepthCapExceeded and transitive RecursionCycle, then replace the tautological depth-cap test with a MAX_DEPTH+1 chain and add an A->B->A transitive-cycle test; or (b) if single-hop is the intended scope, delete the dead machinery (depth param, stack Vec + cycle scan, DepthCapExceeded variant) and rewrite the module doc to say each edge is resolved one hop. Track as a low-priority bead; gate any production wiring (FlowQuery::with_inter) behind option (a) being finished.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root cause fixed in production code (not just the symptom).
- [ ] A regression test reproduces the defect before the fix (fails) and passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; `cargo fmt` (oraclemcp-* only).
- [ ] No behavior regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 51b99a3eed), re-verified on main. Took option A (single-hop is the intended scope, matches the only consumer flow_query::taint_of which reads only propagated_returns; propagate_inter/CallEdgeFlow/with_inter have ZERO production callers, all uses are tests). Excised the dead scaffolding: removed the MAX_DEPTH const, the unreachable `depth >= MAX_DEPTH` branch, the DepthCapExceeded variant, the `depth` param, the `stack` Vec and the `let _ = stack;`. Preserved the LIVE RecursionCycle guard but simplified it to a direct `edge.callee == edge.caller` self-call check (the old stack only ever held one element so this is behavior-preserving — direct_recursion_records_cycle_unknown still passes). Rewrote the module doc (was 'followed

title: plsql-parser-antlr: skip string literals in advance_to_decl_end/lower_statement_body (span truncation)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt + strict ground-truth calibration workflow (2026-06-02).

### Site crates/plsql-parser-antlr/src/lower/mod.rs:1166 — CONFIRMED DEFECT, severity=medium
**Impact:** When PL/SQL DDL contains a string literal with an embedded END, ; or BEGIN (common in dynamic-SQL/EXECUTE IMMEDIATE builders, dbms_output messages, and error text), the always-compiled text-scanning lowerer (lower_source, reachable via the ANTLR backend fallback and the plsql-mcp parse tools) records a truncated declaration span and mis-splits statement bodies, so any span-slicing consumer (IR lowering, MCP parse_tools, engine) sees a body cut off mid-string-literal.
**Evidence:** advance_to_decl_end (crates/plsql-parser-antlr/src/lower/mod.rs:1166-1230) skips single-line comments (1173) and block comments (1181) but has NO single-quote/q-quote branch before the BEGIN/END depth checks (1194-1205) and the ';'/'/' terminators (1208,1217). lower_statement_body (mod.rs:521-572) likewise skips no string literals before its keyword/';' checks (559). The sibling module crates/plsql-parser-antlr/src/recover.rs DOES implement the skip (single-quote with '' escape at 119-135; q-quote/N at 83-116), proving the omission is an oversight. Repro test output: `DECL Procedure { name: "p", span: ... end ... offset: 63 }` for input `CREATE PROCEDURE p IS BEGIN dbms_output.put_line('msg with END; inside'); END;` — offset 63 = "...put_line('msg with END;" (mid-literal); real trailing END; ends at offset 78. Second repro: lower_statement_body("dbms_output.put_line('a ; b'); v := 1;") r
**Repro/trigger:** Trigger input X: `CREATE PROCEDURE p IS BEGIN dbms_output.put_line('msg with END; inside'); END;` -> wrong output Y: AstDecl::Procedure{name:"p"} span end = offset 63 (truncated inside the string literal at "...with END;") instead of the correct offset 78 (the real trailing END;). Second trigger: body `dbms_output.put_line('a ; b'); v := 1;` -> 3 statements instead of 2, with the put_line Call truncated at the in-string ';'. Both reproduced via temporary integration tests against the crate's own lower_source / lower_statement_body and then removed (git status clean). Disproof of "decls dropped": procedure q is still found because the outer loop re-finds the next CREATE, so this is a span/bod
**Suggested fix:** In advance_to_decl_end (mod.rs:~1192, after the block-comment skip) and in lower_statement_body (mod.rs:~558, before the ';' check) add single-quoted-string skipping (with '' escape handling) and ideally q'[...]' / N'...' handling, mirroring crates/plsql-parser-antlr/src/recover.rs:83-135. Best long-term: extract recover.rs's comment/string/q-quote skipping into one shared helper used by all three scanners to prevent divergence. Then strengthen the test ddl_keyword_inside_string_is_not_promoted (mod.rs:1620) with a literal that actually embeds END;/; and assert procedure p's span runs to the real trailing END;. NOTE: the finding's cited recover.rs path (src/lower/recover.rs) is wrong; the file is at crates/plsql-parser-antlr/src/recover.rs.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root cause fixed in production code (not just the symptom).
- [ ] A regression test reproduces the defect before the fix (fails) and passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; `cargo fmt` (oraclemcp-* only).
- [ ] No behavior regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 548fe539bb), re-verified on main. Root cause: advance_to_decl_end (mod.rs) skipped only comments and lower_statement_body skipped no opaque spans at all, so an END/;/BEGIN/IF embedded in a string literal truncated a declaration span and mis-split statement bodies. Fix: extracted recover.rs's comment + single-quote + q-quote/N-quote skipping into one shared crate-internal helper crate::recover::skip_opaque_span(bytes, pos, scan_start) (single source of truth, as the bead's long-term suggestion recommended) and routed all three scanners (recover_to_statement_boundary, advance_to_decl_end, lower_statement_body) through it. Verified the three regression tests FAIL before the fix (span end 63 vs 78; 3 stmts vs 2; 4 chunks vs 2) a

title: plsql-bindgen emit.rs: wire Defaulted<T> so defaulted IN params can use Oracle declared-default omit path
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt + strict ground-truth calibration workflow (2026-06-02).

### Site crates/plsql-bindgen/src/emit.rs:148 — CONFIRMED DEFECT, severity=medium
**Impact:** For a routine like proc(p_a IN NUMBER DEFAULT 5) the generated wrapper forces the caller to pass p_a and always binds its slot, so Oracle's "omit argument -> evaluate declared default" semantics are unreachable; the documented Defaulted<T> contract is silently absent. The explicit-value path still calls the routine correctly, so this is functional incompleteness, not a crash or silent-wrong-result.
**Evidence:** emit.rs:283-289 rust_type_in() branches ONLY on p.rust_type.nullable, emitting `Option<{path}>` or `{path}` — never Defaulted<T> and never even gating on has_default. The IN/IN OUT bind loop emit.rs:185-211 unconditionally pushes RoutineArg::In/InOut(...) and unconditionally appends `:{slot}` to call_args (emit.rs:188); no branch consults has_default or omits a slot. emit.rs's `use crate::{...}` (lines 44-47) does not import defaulted/should_use_defaulted.

The contract it violates: defaulted.rs:18-23 "Any IN / IN OUT parameter whose ParameterBinding.has_default is true gets Defaulted<T> rather than T or Option<T> in the emitted wrapper"; defaulted.rs:37-43 documents Omit as "wrapper emits no bind variable ... so the server evaluates the default". lib.rs:91 doc on has_default also claims it "affects whether the Rust wrapper exposes it as Option<T> or required" — also unhonored.

Dead mac
**Repro/trigger:** Plan with one IN param has_default:true fed to the CLI:
echo plan {package_id:"hr.demo_pkg", routine proc_with_default(p_a IN i64 nullable:false has_default:true)} | plsql-bindgen --input plan.json
Actual output:
  pub fn proc_with_default(executor: &mut impl OracleExecutor, p_a: i64) -> Result<(), ExecutionError> {
      let __args: Vec<RoutineArg> = vec![ RoutineArg::In(BindValue::Int(p_a)), ];
      let __out = executor.call_routine("BEGIN hr.demo_pkg.proc_with_default(:1); END;", &__args)?;
      ...
  }
Signature is plain `p_a: i64` (required), not Defaulted<i64> and not even Option<i64>; slot :1 is always bound. The omit/declared-default case is inexpressible: a hand-written caller can
**Suggested fix:** Wire the existing Defaulted machinery into emit.rs: (1) in rust_type_in (and the OUT/IN-OUT surfacing in compute_return_type) call should_use_defaulted(p); when true wrap as Defaulted<T> (or Defaulted<Option<T>> when also nullable), mirroring with_defaulted. (2) For defaulted params make the bind layout runtime-dynamic: match on Defaulted::Omit (drop slot + use named-argument notation `p_a => :n` so omitting a middle default-bearing arg stays legal), Defaulted::Null (bind BindValue::Null), Defaulted::Value(v) (marshal). (3) Add an emit-level test that has_default:true yields Defaulted<T> in the signature and an omittable bind. Interim mitigation if full support is deferred: emit a BindingDiagnostic(Warn) for any param with has_default==true and amend defaulted.rs/lib.rs:91 doc to state the contract is not yet emitted, so the gap is surfaced rather than silent.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root cause fixed in production code (not just the symptom).
- [ ] A regression test reproduces the defect before the fix (fails) and passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; `cargo fmt` (oraclemcp-* only).
- [ ] No behavior regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 50b466b216), re-verified on main. ROOT-CAUSE fix. Wired the existing Defaulted machinery into emit.rs. (1) New rust_type_sig() wraps defaulted IN/IN OUT params as Defaulted<T> (or Defaulted<Option<T>> when also nullable), mirroring with_defaulted; the signature now uses it (rust_type_in stays the value-type helper for the return tuple, which correctly stays T/Option<T> for IN OUT read-back). (2) New emit_routine_body_dynamic() produces a runtime-dynamic bind layout whenever any param is defaulted: Defaulted::Omit pushes no bind so the server evaluates the declared default; Defaulted::Null binds BindValue::Null; Defaulted::Value(v) marshals the scalar. The anonymous block is composed at runtime with named-argument notation (p

title: Fix byte-as-char UTF-8 handling across literal scrubbers (scrub_literals non-ASCII PANIC, rename mojibake, fixture mojibake)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt + strict ground-truth calibration workflow (2026-06-02).

### Site crates/plsql-support/src/scrub_literals.rs:214 — CONFIRMED DEFECT, severity=medium
**Impact:** Redacting a support bundle / building a privacy MinFixture for PL/SQL source that contains a non-ASCII byte outside a string body or comment (e.g. a non-breaking space or smart-quote from web/Word-pasted SQL, or an accented/NLS unquoted identifier) panics the redaction-delta step, aborting the bundle/fixture build rather than producing output.
**Evidence:** scrub_literals.rs:79-85 walks `source.as_bytes()` by byte index `i`. Fallback at lines 214-215: `out.push(bytes[i] as char); i += 1;` — advances one byte into a multi-byte UTF-8 char. Next iteration's `&source[i..]` (line 122 `match_date_literal`) slices at a non-char boundary and panics. Live caller redaction_delta.rs:130 `let (after, stats) = scrub_literals(&buffer, config.scrub_thresholds);` with no catch_unwind. I ran the REAL public API via a temp integration test: `[nbsp] PANIC input="x\u{a0}:= 1;" msg="start byte index 2 is not a char boundary; it is inside '\u{a0}' (bytes 1..3)"`; `[accented_ident] PANIC input="café := 1;" msg="start byte index 4 is not a char boundary; it is inside 'é' (bytes 3..5)"`; `[lone_accent] PANIC input="v_é := 5"`; `[trailing_accent_only] PANIC input="é"`. In contrast `[accented_in_string] OK input="x := 'café is short'" output="x := '<SCRUBBED>'"` and 
**Repro/trigger:** scrub_literals("café := 1;", ScrubThresholds::default_thresholds()) panics with "start byte index 4 is not a char boundary; it is inside 'é'". Mechanism: the leading bytes of "café" up to the accented char are pushed via the byte fallback (line 214), then on reaching the first byte (0xC3) of 'é' line 214 pushes it as a Latin-1 char and advances i by 1 into the middle of 'é'; the next iteration calls match_date_literal(&source[i..]) at line 122 which slices at the non-char boundary and panics. (The original claim's "silent mojibake" only occurs in the narrow case where the multi-byte char is the very last bytes of input so i reaches len() and the loop exits before any further slice — confirme
**Suggested fix:** Replace the line-214 byte fallback with a codepoint-safe advance: `let ch = source[i..].chars().next().unwrap(); out.push(ch); i += ch.len_utf8();`. This pushes the real character (no Latin-1 mojibake) and keeps `i` on a char boundary so the subsequent `&source[i..]` slices (lines 92/113/122/127/135) cannot panic. Add a regression test feeding non-ASCII bytes in token-separator and identifier positions (e.g. "x\u{00A0}:= 1;", "café := 1;", "v_é := 5") asserting no panic and that non-literal parts are byte-identical to the input. Note: the original finding's "silent mojibake / corrupted bundle" symptom is incorrect — the actual observable failure is a panic/pipeline abort; severity is medium (DoS-flavored correctness, not a fail-open privacy leak), and the trigger requires non-ASCII outside string/comment bodies which is uncommon in real PL/SQL but plausible for pasted SQL and NLS identif

### Site crates/plsql-support/src/rename.rs:225 — CONFIRMED DEFECT, severity=low
**Impact:** A redacted support bundle whose source contains any short non-ASCII string literal (e.g. NLS messages, accented names like 'José' or 'café' below the 8-char scrub threshold) silently corrupts that literal into mojibake (café -> cafÃ©), so the bundle no longer faithfully reproduces the customer's source — defeating its stated purpose as a reproducible artefact. No panic, no privacy leak, no determinism break.
**Evidence:** rename.rs:210-227 (string-literal handler) and :265-267 (fallback) both do `out.push(bytes[i] as char)` — a u8->char Latin-1 reinterpretation. Module doc rename.rs:19-21: "String/comment safe: contents of '\''literal'\'' ... are passed through unchanged" and inline comment :224 "multi-byte safe slice". Empirical repro of the exact loop: input `x := '\''café'\'';` -> output `x := '\''cafÃ©'\'';` (preserved unchanged: false; contains 'café': false). Pipeline: redaction_delta.rs:130 scrub_literals (step 2) then :160-164 rename_with_reserved/rename_identifiers (step 3) on the same buffer. scrub_literals.rs:159 `inner += 1` counts bytes; :168 `if !terminated || inner >= string_min_len` (default 8, scrub_literals.rs:50); :172 `out.push_str(&source[i..j])` passes a terminated short literal through verbatim — so 'café' (5 inner bytes < 8) survives scrubbing and reaches the renamer. Build: `cargo
**Repro/trigger:** Triggering input: `rename_identifiers("x := 'café';", "salt").0` returns `x := 'cafÃ©';` (verified by running the identical byte-walk loop standalone: the é bytes 0xC3 0xA9 each become a separate Latin-1 char). Through the full pipeline, redact_with_delta on source containing the terminated 5-byte literal 'café' leaves it intact after scrub_literals (5 < string_min_len 8, terminated -> push_str raw) and then mangles it in rename_identifiers via `out.push(bytes[i] as char)`. The fallback at line 266 corrupts non-ASCII bytes outside identifiers/strings identically (e.g. an em-dash between tokens). Concrete X->Y exhibited.
**Suggested fix:** At both rename.rs:225 and :266 replace `out.push(bytes[i] as char); i += 1;` with a full UTF-8 scalar copy: `let c = source[i..].chars().next().expect("i on char boundary"); out.push(c); i += c.len_utf8();` (i always lands on a char boundary because every scanner match is against ASCII bytes, which cannot occur inside a multi-byte sequence). Alternatively scan to the next ASCII sentinel and `out.push_str(&source[i..next])` in one slice (matching the UTF-8-safe push_str already used for comments at :155/:165 and q-quotes at :202). Update the :224 comment to "content-preserving UTF-8 copy". Add a regression test asserting `rename_identifiers("x := 'café';", "salt").0.contains("'café'")`.

### Site crates/plsql-accretion/src/fixture.rs:151 — DEFENSE-IN-DEPTH (latent/phase-gated), severity=low
**Impact:** A PL/SQL fixture whose triggering gap depends on a non-ASCII byte (NLS/quoted identifier or UTF-8 string body) may be honestly dropped (coverage loss) if the mojibake perturbs the lex/parse; it can never produce a wrong or privacy-leaking stored fixture. No user-visible failure under normal ASCII PL/SQL.
**Evidence:** fixture.rs:151 `out.push(b[i] as char);` (inside string-literal body) and fixture.rs:172 `out.push(b[i] as char);` (general source). Empirical proof the cast is Latin-1 mojibake: input "v_café := 'naïve';" -> output "v_cafÃ© := 'naÃ¯ve';" (equal: false). The sibling at plsql-ir/src/stmt.rs:300 is char-aware (`s.chars().peekable()` ... `out.push(c)`) and lossless — confirming fixture.rs is the inconsistent variant. Downstream guards: tokscrub.rs:95-121 classify() maps Identifier/Unknown/QuotedIdentifier/StringLiteral/NumericLiteral to estate Classes; tokscrub.rs:163-176 synthesise() emits ASCII-only synthetics (id_<hex12>, "id_<hex12>", 'sx_<hex8>', 7/7.0); fixture.rs:499-537 privacy_residue_clean fails closed on any non-synthetic EstateClass survivor; fixture.rs:310/329/379 re-run the SignatureOracle and Err out (NotReproducible / PrivacyUnprovable) if the parse no longer reproduces. `ca
**Repro/trigger:** Mechanism triggers on input X = "v_café := 'naïve';" -> strip_comments yields Y = "v_cafÃ© := 'naÃ¯ve';" (corruption real). Impossibility-of-harm proof: every byte strip_comments can corrupt (0x80-0xFF, only legal in string bodies / quoted-or-NLS identifiers / comments) is, downstream, either (a) a comment collapsed to a space, or (b) inside an estate-class token that structure_preserving_scrub unconditionally replaces with an ASCII synthetic, or (c) a survivor that privacy_residue_clean rejects (fail-closed) — so no corrupted original byte is ever stored. If the corruption splits an NLS identifier into a different token sequence and shifts the parse, oracle.reproduces() returns false at fix
**Suggested fix:** Replace the two `out.push(b[i] as char)` sites (lines 151, 172) with UTF-8-preserving copies, or rewrite the scanner over src.char_indices()/Peekable<Chars> mirroring the correct sibling at crates/plsql-ir/src/stmt.rs:300. This is a defense-in-depth/consistency cleanup, not a correctness or privacy fix — the downstream structure_preserving_scrub + privacy_residue_clean + SignatureOracle re-check already make the corruption behaviorally inert (it can only cause an honest discard, never a wrong or leaky stored fixture). Optionally add a regression test asserting strip_comments preserves an accented identifier/string body byte-for-byte.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root cause fixed in production code (not just the symptom).
- [ ] A regression test reproduces the defect before the fix (fails) and passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; `cargo fmt` (oraclemcp-* only).
- [ ] No behavior regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 4174e81da5), re-verified on main. Root-cause fixed all 3 sites (5 byte-as-char locations): replaced `out.push(bytes[i] as char); i += 1;` with a codepoint-safe `let c = source[i..].chars().next().expect(...); out.push(c); i += c.len_utf8();` at scrub_literals.rs:214, rename.rs:225 (string body) + rename.rs:266 (general fallback), and fixture.rs strip_comments string-body + general-source sites. VERIFIED the scrub_literals test fails on pre-fix code with the exact bead panic (`start byte index 2 is not a char boundary; it is inside '\u{a0}'`) and passes after. The 3rd site (fixture.rs, defense-in-depth) was the latent/phase-gated one — fixed for consistency with the char-aware sibling in plsql-ir/stmt.rs; downstream guards al

title: oraclemcp-core shutdown: close lost-wakeup TOCTOU in ShutdownCoordinator::wait_for_shutdown
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit fa34040ff7), re-verified on main. Root-cause fixed with the exact suggested tokio idiom: in wait_for_shutdown(), `let notified = self.inner.notify.notified(); tokio::pin!(notified); notified.as_mut().enable();` registers the waiter BEFORE the is_shutting_down() flag check, then re-checks, then awaits — so a begin_shutdown() (which uses notify_waiters(), storing no permit) racing the check cannot be lost. HONEST CAVEAT on the regression test: the genuine lost-wakeup window is sub-poll — it lies between the flag read and the waiter registration WITHIN a single poll of the future — so it is not deterministically reproducible through the public async fn (I empirically confirmed a naive race test passes even against the buggy cod

title: plsql-sast suppress.rs: inline plsql-scan:ignore is inert for all real (line-0) findings
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt + strict ground-truth calibration workflow (2026-06-02).

### Site crates/plsql-sast/src/suppress.rs:190 — CONFIRMED DEFECT, severity=low
**Impact:** A user who writes `bad_call(); -- plsql-scan:ignore SEC001` (or `ignore-next-line`) to suppress a real finding gets a silently inert directive: the SEC001 finding is recorded at line 0, and inline matching keys on by_line which only ever holds 1-based lines, so the directive never matches. The defect is latent today (no production caller wires apply_suppressions into a scan), but the documented inline-suppression feature is non-functional for every real rule the instant a CLI/MCP path uses it. Fails safe (findings retained, never wrongly dropped).
**Evidence:** suppress.rs:188-190 — inline match: `indexes.get(&f.location.file).and_then(|idx| idx.by_line.get(&f.location.line))`. by_line keying is 1-based: suppress.rs:110 `let line_no = (i + 1) as u32;`, :127 next-line uses `line_no + 1`, :135 same-line uses `line_no` — key 0 is never inserted. finding() signature lib.rs:262-268 takes `line: u32` as 5th positional arg. ALL production call sites pass 0: `rg -n -A2 'ctx\.source_file,' rules.rs` shows the next non-comment token is `0` at every site (L98,266,339,408,471,539,604,658,710,761,814,871,927,979,1036,1096,1156); rules_qual.rs QUAL001 L78=0 (comment L76-77: "carry no precise span; point at the unit"), QUAL004 L156=0. sarif.rs:136-139 independently corroborates: `start_line: f.location.line.max(1)` with comment "findings with no precise line (catalog/DDL facts) report line 0 — clamp to 1". apply_suppressions callers (whole workspace): only su
**Repro/trigger:** Trigger: real rule emits Finding with location.line == 0. apply_suppressions(report, default_config, {file: "...-- plsql-scan:ignore SEC001"}) computes index_source which puts the same-line directive under by_line key = comment's 1-based line (>=1). Lookup is `by_line.get(&0)` → None (0 is never a key since enumerate()+1 >= 1, and next-line is +1 >= 2). Result: inline_reason = None, finding pushed to kept. Outcome: kept=1, suppressed=0, directive inert. Contrast: the existing test inline_same_line_ignore_suppresses_that_line (suppress.rs:280) fabricates the finding at line 2 (matching the comment line), which is exactly the synthetic non-zero line that masks the gap; no test feeds a real lin
**Suggested fix:** Make inline matching tolerant of line-0 (span-less) findings. Preferred: when f.location.line == 0, fall back to file-scoped matching — scan all directives parsed for indexes.get(&f.location.file) and suppress if any RuleSet matches f.rule_id, recording the directive's comment_line in the reason. Longer-term/cleaner: backfill location.line from byte_span or the unit's declaration line before suppression runs (also improves report quality and SARIF). Add a regression test that runs a REAL rule's output (line 0) through apply_suppressions with an inline `-- plsql-scan:ignore <RULE>` directive and asserts it is suppressed — the current tests only use synthetic non-zero-line findings, which is precisely what hid this. Optionally add a debug_assert against silent reintroduction once a real line is expected.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root cause fixed in production code (not just the symptom).
- [ ] A regression test reproduces the defect before the fix (fails) and passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; `cargo fmt` (oraclemcp-* only).
- [ ] No behavior regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit efebb9139f), re-verified on main. Root-cause fix in suppress.rs. Added inline_reason_for() helper: line-0 (span-less catalog/DDL) findings now fall back to file-scoped inline matching instead of the dead line-keyed lookup, so -- plsql-scan:ignore <RULE> works for real fact-driven findings (SEC006/SEC001/...). Earliest matching directive wins (deterministic); directive's comment line recorded in the audit trail. Fail-closed preserved: a directive naming a different rule does not suppress (verified). Negative-control confirmed the primary test fails before the fix and passes after.

title: plsql-cicd predict.rs: remove or honestly mark dangling predict_with_lineage doc reference
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt + strict ground-truth calibration workflow (2026-06-02).

### Site crates/plsql-cicd/src/predict.rs:10 — CONFIRMED DEFECT, severity=low
**Impact:** A developer or agent reading crates/plsql-cicd/src/predict.rs and grepping for the documented predict_with_lineage entry point finds nothing — the doc advertises a transitive blast-radius walker that does not exist; in practice every prediction is single-hop, so run_gate's max_invalidations bounds only direct invalidations, which is correct for the implemented scope but is not the transitive coverage the doc implies.
**Evidence:** predict.rs module doc lines 10-12: "When a lineage `DepGraph` is supplied (`predict_with_lineage`), the function walks dependents via the lineage crate's `impact()` and attaches each transitive dependent as a `PredictedInvalidation`." Inline comment line 88: "transitive ones land via `predict_with_lineage`." `rg -rn 'predict_with_lineage' .` returns ONLY those two doc-comment lines; `rg -rn 'fn predict_with_lineage' .` => NONE. Every emitted row is hardcoded distance: 1 (predict.rs lines 100,127,144,157,170,183,193,203,216,230,245,273); no `distance:` anywhere in the crate is set to >1. lib.rs:234-237 documents distance "1 is direct, >1 is transitive" and predict.rs:53 sorts by (distance,owner,name) — scaffolding never fed >1. plsql-cicd/Cargo.toml lists plsql-catalog/core/output as deps but NOT plsql-lineage, so it cannot call impact() as documented. The real walker exists: plsql-lineag
**Repro/trigger:** Defect (doc/code mismatch) trigger: `rg 'predict_with_lineage' crates/plsql-cicd/src/predict.rs` shows the present-tense claim that the function "walks dependents via the lineage crate's impact()"; `rg -rn 'fn predict_with_lineage' .` returns nothing and Cargo.toml has no plsql-lineage dep, proving the documented entry point and code path do not exist. Disproof of the high-impact "silently wrong transitive analysis" framing: there is no input X that yields a wrong-within-claimed-scope output — predict() only ever claims direct (distance:1) invalidations, its tests assert distance==1, and no caller consumes a transitive distance>1 that is missing. The gate counts direct rows accurately; it un
**Suggested fix:** Documentation honesty (low effort, do now): in crates/plsql-cicd/src/predict.rs change the present-tense claims at lines 10-12 and the inline clause at line 88 so they no longer advertise a non-existent function — either delete the predict_with_lineage references or convert them to an explicit "not yet implemented / TODO" note. Separately, if transitive blast-radius is in scope for the release gate, add plsql-lineage to plsql-cicd/Cargo.toml and implement a real predict_with_lineage that calls predict() for direct rows then plsql_lineage::impact(graph, node, max_depth) per seed, emitting PredictedInvalidation rows with the actual hop count (>1); add a test asserting distance>1 and that run_gate's max_invalidations counts transitive rows. Until that lands, note in run_gate docs that max_invalidations bounds direct invalidations only.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root cause fixed in production code (not just the symptom).
- [ ] A regression test reproduces the defect before the fix (fails) and passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; `cargo fmt` (oraclemcp-* only).
- [ ] No behavior regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit b4c803fdbe), re-verified on main. Documentation-honesty fix (the bead's prescribed low-effort scope; transitive lineage walking is genuinely not in this crate — plsql-cicd has no plsql-lineage dep). Removed the present-tense module-doc claim (predict.rs:10-12) that a predict_with_lineage walker exists and walks dependents via the lineage crate's impact(); replaced with an explicit "Scope: direct (single-hop) invalidations only / not yet implemented here" section. Updated the inline comment at predict.rs:88 likewise. Added a scope note to run_gate's doc clarifying max_invalidations bounds direct invalidations only today and will count distance>1 rows if/when transitive coverage lands. The new test asserts predict() emits only

title: plsql-lineage detect_orphans: stop counting self-loop edge as inbound reference
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt + strict ground-truth calibration workflow (2026-06-02).

### Site crates/plsql-lineage/src/lib.rs:2697 — CONFIRMED DEFECT, severity=low
**Impact:** A genuinely unused recursive PL/SQL object (a standalone procedure/package member whose only inbound dependency edge is its own self-call) is silently omitted from the `orphan_candidates` dead-code/audit report and miscounted in `objects_with_references`, so an auditor running the MCP orphan_candidates tool never sees that object as a removal candidate — a false negative in the audit inventory.
**Evidence:** crates/plsql-lineage/src/lib.rs:2696-2699 — `for edge in &graph.edges { inbound.insert(edge.to); outbound.insert(edge.from); }` with no `edge.from == edge.to` guard; lib.rs:2704-2706 — `if inbound.contains(&node.id) { with_references += 1; continue; }`.
Convention elsewhere: recompile_order lib.rs:1220 `if from_in && to_in && edge.from != edge.to`; depgraph component_cycle_summary lib.rs:1139 `.any(|edge| edge.from.id == edge.to.id)`; depgraph insert_edge lib.rs:734 just pushes the edge; validate lib.rs:758-774 checks only provenance/evidence (no self-loop rejection).
Reachable via real pipeline: plsql-engine/src/lib.rs:956-980 builds `Edge::new(EdgeId, caller_node_id, callee_node_id, EdgeKind::Calls, ...)` then `dep_graph.insert_edge(edge, ...)` with no self-filter; for a recursive self-call, callee logical id resolves to the same NodeId as caller (lib.rs:904-910 and 956-964).
Live call
**Repro/trigger:** Triggering input: DepGraph with one node N (id=1) and one edge Edge{from:1, to:1, kind:Calls}. Call detect_orphans(&g, false). Observed (verified by throwaway test): candidates is empty and objects_with_references == 1, i.e. N is excluded from the orphan list. Expected per the documented contract ("zero incoming edges", lib.rs:2676; objects_with_references = "Objects with at least one inbound reference", plsql-output/src/lib.rs:282): N should appear as a HighConfidenceUnused candidate with objects_with_references == 0, because a self-call is not an external reference. Impossibility of harm beyond this case: any additional inbound edge from a distinct node makes the node legitimately referenc
**Suggested fix:** In detect_orphans (crates/plsql-lineage/src/lib.rs:2696-2699) skip self-loop edges when building both the inbound and outbound sets, mirroring the `edge.from != edge.to` convention already used in recompile_order (lib.rs:1220):

    for edge in &graph.edges {
        if edge.from == edge.to {
            continue; // self-loop (recursive self-call) is not an external reference
        }
        inbound.insert(edge.to);
        outbound.insert(edge.from);
    }

Excluding the self-loop from outbound too is required so a purely-recursive node is tiered HighConfidenceUnused (not LikelyUnused). Add a regression test: single node + one self-loop edge ⇒ node appears as a HighConfidenceUnused orphan candidate with objects_with_references == 0.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root cause fixed in production code (not just the symptom).
- [ ] A regression test reproduces the defect before the fix (fails) and passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; `cargo fmt` (oraclemcp-* only).
- [ ] No behavior regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 6091f1808a), re-verified on main. Root cause fixed in detect_orphans (crates/plsql-lineage/src/lib.rs ~line 2696): added an `if edge.from == edge.to { continue; }` guard before populating BOTH the inbound and outbound HashSets, mirroring the existing `edge.from != edge.to` convention in recompile_order (lib.rs:1220). A self-call (recursive standalone proc / package member) is no longer counted as an external reference, so such a node now correctly surfaces as a HighConfidenceUnused orphan candidate (no incoming, no *external* outgoing) and objects_with_references is no longer over-counted. Excluding the self-loop from outbound too is what keeps the tier at HighConfidenceUnused rather than LikelyUnused, per the bead's suggest

title: plsql-depgraph catalog_cross_check: fix order-dependent KindMismatch masking from parallel-edge collapse
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt + strict ground-truth calibration workflow (2026-06-02).

### Site crates/plsql-depgraph/src/lib.rs:1554 — CONFIRMED DEFECT, severity=low
**Impact:** In the advisory `lineage compare-oracle-deps` drift report, when two parallel engine edges of different Oracle-compatibility classes exist between the same object pair (e.g. a package that both Calls and References the same object), a genuine kind disagreement vs Oracle's ALL_DEPENDENCIES is masked order-dependently: depending on edge insertion order the report shows either a kind_mismatch or a clean agreement for the identical input, under-reporting real Oracle-vs-engine drift that release reviewers inspect. Not a security/release gate.
**Evidence:** crates/plsql-depgraph/src/lib.rs:1531 `let mut catalog_index: BTreeMap<(String, String), CatalogDependencyKind> = BTreeMap::new();` and :1543 `let mut our_index: BTreeMap<(String, String), (EdgeKind, ConfidenceLevel)> = BTreeMap::new();` — both keyed (from,to). :1538 `catalog_index.insert((from, to), dep.dependency_kind);` and :1554 `our_index.insert((from, to), (edge.kind, edge.confidence.level));` are last-write-wins. :1545 `summary.total_our_edges += 1;` counts every raw edge unconditionally. Edges are stored without dedup: :736 `self.edges.push(edge);`. normalised_object_key (:1507-1518) reduces 3-part `schema.pkg.member` to 2-part `schema.pkg`, increasing collisions among package-member edges. Reproduction (throwaway tests/ harness, since removed) output: `references_last: total_our_edges=2 matches=0 kind_mismatches=1`; `calls_last: total_our_edges=2 matches=1 kind_mismatches=0`. Co
**Repro/trigger:** Two parallel engine edges billing.caller_pkg -> billing.callee_t: one EdgeKind::Calls (compatible with CatalogDependencyKind::Hard), one EdgeKind::References (compatible only with Reference, NOT Hard). Single Oracle dep of kind Hard for that pair. Insert References last => our_index holds References => intersection loop classifies it as kind_mismatch (matches=0, kind_mismatches=1). Insert Calls last => our_index holds Calls => classified as a match (matches=1, kind_mismatches=0). Identical two-edge input, identical catalog, output flips on insertion order; total_our_edges=2 in both. The masked case (Calls last) reports clean agreement while a real References-vs-HARD disagreement exists. Note
**Suggested fix:** Accumulate engine kinds per (from,to) instead of last-write-wins: change our_index to BTreeMap<(String,String), Vec<(EdgeKind,ConfidenceLevel)>> and push at line 1554; in the intersection loop (1561-1577) classify each distinct engine kind against the catalog kind so a mismatching parallel edge is never masked by a compatible sibling. Keep total_our_edges as a raw count (no false reconciliation invariant needed — the report's buckets are pair-level by documented design). Drop the description's 'reconciliation invariant' framing and correct the doc-comment at lib.rs:1367 which falsely claims a plsql-engine doctor completeness gate consumes this (no such consumer exists; only the advisory lineage compare-oracle-deps report does). Add a regression test asserting both a match and a kind_mismatch surface for two parallel edges regardless of insertion order.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root cause fixed in production code (not just the symptom).
- [ ] A regression test reproduces the defect before the fix (fails) and passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; `cargo fmt` (oraclemcp-* only).
- [ ] No behavior regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 48dfde109e), re-verified on main. Root-cause fixed. our_index in catalog_cross_check was BTreeMap<(from,to),(EdgeKind,ConfidenceLevel)> with last-write-wins, so two parallel engine edges that collapse to the same object pair (e.g. Calls compatible with HARD + References compatible only with REF) lost all but the last-pushed kind; the intersection loop then classified only the surviving kind, so a real References-vs-HARD drift was masked iff Calls happened to be pushed last — making the advisory lineage compare-oracle-deps report order-dependent for identical input. Fix: accumulate every DISTINCT (kind,confidence) per pair into a Vec (BTreeMap<(String,String),Vec<(EdgeKind,ConfidenceLevel)>>; identical pairs de-duped), and cl

title: plsql-catalog: classify role grantees in grant extraction (ALL_TAB_PRIVS misclassified as user)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt + strict ground-truth calibration workflow (2026-06-02).

### Site crates/plsql-catalog/src/lib.rs:4144 — CONFIRMED DEFECT, severity=high
**Impact:** An object grant to a database role (GRANT SELECT ON t TO some_role) is extracted from ALL_TAB_PRIVS and silently recorded as a direct Grantee::User grant, so the privilege model reports it as a statically-certain (High confidence) direct user grant with no RuntimeGrantOrRole ambiguity — a fail-toward-permissive result in a privilege/SAST product, and the entire role-ambiguity downgrade path is dead code for any non-synthetic catalog.
**Evidence:** crates/plsql-catalog/src/lib.rs:4144-4159 grantee_from_dictionary_value: returns Grantee::Public for "PUBLIC", else `Ok(Grantee::User(UserName::from(symbol)))` — Grantee::Role is never produced; via_role hard-coded None at lib.rs:4128.
