## Background
P0-1 defines the error envelope; this is the agent-helpful enrichment (Codex review): map raw ORA- errors to actionable, structured guidance using cached schema.
## Technical approach
- isError:true (not JSON-RPC error codes) with {error_class, suggested_tool, fuzzy_matches}. ORA-00942 OBJECT_NOT_FOUND -> Levenshtein/trie fuzzy matches over the cached schema snapshot (P1-5); ORA-01031 INSUFFICIENT_PRIVILEGES -> "needs privilege X" (ties to P2-9); stale-snapshot -> suggest re-capture; no-match -> clear "not found, did you mean ...".
## Success criteria
ORA-00942/01031/stale/no-match each return the right error_class + suggested next step + fuzzy matches where applicable.
## Test plan
- Unit: ORA-00942 (fuzzy hit + no-match), ORA-01031, stale snapshot. e2e via a scripted client.
## Considerations
Additive enrichment over P0-1; does NOT block the basic query path (P1-2 uses the P0-1 envelope). Depends on P0-1 + cached schema (P1-5).
close_reason: P1-ERR actionable errors + fuzzy schema suggestions (oraclemcp-error fuzzy.rs). Additive enrichment over the P0-1 ErrorEnvelope: levenshtein (case-insensitive edit distance) + fuzzy_suggest (ranked near-misses within a ceil(len/2)+1 threshold so unrelated names aren't suggested). enrich_oracle_error(message, referenced, known_objects): ORA-00942/04043 OBJECT_NOT_FOUND -> fuzzy_matches over the cached schema + 'did you mean' next step; empty snapshot -> 'run oracle_schema_inspect(depth=full)' (stale-snapshot recapture); no near match -> clear 'not found, no near match'; ORA-01031/01017 INSUFFICIENT_PRIVILEGE -> 'check oracle_capabilities / operator must grant' (ties to P2-9). isError:true envelope, never JSON-RPC codes. Pure (candidates passed in). Additive — does NOT block the basic query path (P1-2 uses the P0-1 envelope). 5 tests: levenshtein, fuzzy ranking (unrelated excluded), ORA-00942 fuzzy-hit/no-match/stale, ORA-01031. clippy -D warnings + fmt clean.

title: P1-POLICY — Per-schema scoping & allow/deny policy (§6.2)
description:
## Background
§6.2 — a distinct safety layer that was referenced (P1-3, P0-7) but never implemented as its own unit. Per-schema policy lets an operator cap capability BELOW the operating level for specific schemas, and hard-protects system schemas.
## Technical approach
- TOML policy per schema: default_mode = read_only | guarded | permissive; allow_dml; deny_ddl; deny_patterns (regex); deny_all.
- Schema extracted from the parsed ObjectName (classifier, P1-1) or SYS_CONTEXT('USERENV','CURRENT_SCHEMA').
- SYSTEM / SYS / SYSAUX are deny_all BY DEFAULT and CANNOT be unlocked by an allow-once token (or any agent action).
- Composes with operating levels (§6.6): effective capability = DB-privilege ceiling INTERSECT current_level INTERSECT per-schema policy. The policy can only narrow, never widen.
- Enforced in the guard pipeline before dispatch; violations -> structured block with a safe alternative; audited.
## Success criteria
A DELETE against a deny_ddl/read_only schema is blocked even at READ_WRITE level; SYSTEM is deny-all regardless of level/token; policy can only narrow effective capability.
## Test plan
- Unit: schema extraction from ObjectName; policy intersection with level; SYSTEM deny-all not unlockable by token.
- Integration: per-schema deny blocks an otherwise-permitted statement.
## Considerations
A statement touching multiple schemas takes the STRICTEST applicable policy (fail-closed). Depends on config (P0-2), operating-level core (P0-7), and the classifier's schema resolution (P1-1).
close_reason: P1-POLICY per-schema scoping & allow/deny policy (oraclemcp-guard policy.rs, §6.2). SchemaPolicySet.evaluate(schemas, danger, sql) -> PolicyDecision{Allow|Deny{schema,reason}} that can ONLY further restrict the classifier verdict, never loosen. SchemaPolicyRaw (TOML, deny_unknown_fields): default_mode (read_only|guarded|permissive), allow_dml, deny_ddl, deny_all, deny_patterns (regex). SYS/SYSTEM/SYSAUX/AUDSYS/DBSNMP are ALWAYS deny-all (cannot be unlocked). Unknown schema: reads pass, mutating denied by default (conservative). deny_patterns match raw SQL; deny_ddl blocks Destructive; read_only mode denies >=Guarded unless allow_dml. 6 tests: system-deny-all, unknown-schema reads-pass/writes-deny, permissive-allows-dml, read_only-denies-dml, deny_ddl-blocks-destructive, deny_pattern-matches. clippy -D warnings + fmt clean.

title: P1-2 — oracle_query read path: bind-first, cursor pagination, row/byte caps
description:
## Background
The primary read tool. Bind-variable-first execution (NEVER string interpolation of agent values) is a P0 safety invariant. Reads run; DML/DDL default to preview (P2-3) + token.

## Technical approach
- Bind-first execution; autocommit-off; structured error envelope; optional lease_id.
- Cursor pagination + row/byte caps: default max_rows=200, max_result_bytes=10MB, sized against Claude Code's ~25k-token tool-response limit; same streaming/cursor substrate shared by query AND heavy introspection tools. Results carry truncated, row_count, next_cursor, columns.
- Dual output: content (concise text) + structuredContent (JSON via schemars outputSchema).
- K18 scrubber on cell values; LOB truncation via the §5.2 serializer (P0-5).
- Preview is the oracle_query preview MODE, not a separate tool (keeps surface <=12): DML/DDL with a lease_id runs in an autonomous savepoint, captures SQL%ROWCOUNT + sample, ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT, returns impact + single-use token consumed by oracle_query_execute. (Ground-truth preview itself is P2-3.)

## Success criteria
A SELECT returns paginated, type-faithful rows with caps enforced; a non-SELECT is classified (P1-1) and routed to preview/token, never silently run.

## Test plan
- Live-XE: SELECT returns structured rows; pagination cursor; caps truncate correctly.
- Unit: bind binding (no interpolation); rejects non-read SQL via the classifier.

## Considerations
Depends on the classifier (P1-1) and serializer (P0-5).
notes: CLARIFICATION (Codex review): in P1 there is NO ground-truth savepoint preview (that is P2-3). For DML/DDL, P1 classifies (P1-1) and routes to step-up escalation (P1-10) + runs the approved statement via oracle_query_execute (P1-QE) WITHOUT the execute-in-savepoint blast-radius. Do NOT implement a fake preview in P1; success criterion = classify + require escalation with an actionable error until P2-3 lands.
close_reason: P1-2 oracle_query read path (oraclemcp-db query.rs + OraclePool::read_query). Bind-first execution (OracleBind, never interpolated). Cursor pagination via an Oracle 12c+ OFFSET/FETCH envelope (paginated_sql wraps the inner query untouched; offset/fetch are server-controlled ints, not injection; opaque next_cursor=next offset; cursor_to_offset). Row cap (fetch max_rows+1 to detect 'more') + byte cap (truncate before exceeding max_result_bytes, always include >=1 row); QueryResponse{columns,rows(structured JSON per the §5.2 serializer),row_count,truncated,next_cursor,total_bytes}. Async OraclePool::read_query via spawn_blocking. Classifier gate (P1-1) + durable audit (P1-4) are composed by the tool layer before this runs. Tests: 5 unit (paginated_sql wrap/strip, row-cap+cursor, under-cap, byte-cap-mid-page, cursor roundtrip) + a live pagination test (page1/page2 disjoint windows against real Oracle 23ai all_objects, proving the OFFSET/FETCH wrapping is valid). clippy -D warnings clean offline+driver; fmt clean.

title: P1-3 — Read-only enforcement layers + least-privilege docs
description:
## Background
Three complementary layers; honest about which is the real boundary (§5.5/§6.3).
## Technical approach
(A) DB-level privilege ceiling (strongest); (B) SET TRANSACTION READ ONLY whenever session level is READ_ONLY — blocks DIRECT DML, but NOT autonomous-txn trigger/VPD side-effects (the classifier's trigger/VPD walk is the defense there); (C) guard-layer pre-execution classification + audit. Recommended minimal grants for a hard-ceiling read-only profile: CREATE SESSION, SELECT on needed DBA_*/ALL_* views (or SELECT ANY DICTIONARY), object SELECT grants, never DBA/RESOURCE/DROP ANY. Document this.
## Success criteria
On a least-privilege profile, writes are impossible at the engine; docs spell out the minimal grant set.
## Test plan
- Integration under a least-privilege account: write attempts rejected at DB level.
## Considerations
In all-levels mode the server is the boundary (weaker); never over-claim.
close_reason: P1-3 read-only enforcement layers + least-privilege docs (oraclemcp-guard enforcement.rs + docs/oraclemcp/least-privilege.md). Documents + implements the three §6.3 layers: (A) DB-privilege ceiling = a least-privilege Oracle user (least-privilege.md: minimal grants, the indirect-write footguns, never DBA/RESOURCE/ANY); (B) read_only_setup_statements(level) issues SET TRANSACTION READ ONLY when level==READ_ONLY so a misclassified direct DML still raises ORA-01456; (C) the fail-closed classifier (P1-1) + the operating-level gate (P0-7), already built. is_allowed_alter_session() enforces the §6.5 ALTER SESSION allowlist (NLS/CURRENT_SCHEMA/optimizer/timezone only; rejects CONTAINER/SQL_TRACE/EVENTS/SET ROLE) so the agent runtime-append can't change security/audit context or enable a write role. Honest caveat documented: layer B doesn't stop AUTONOMOUS_TRANSACTION trigger/VPD side-effects (the classifier's trigger/VPD walk + layer A are the defense). Tests: read-only-level-sets-txn-read-only, allowlist permits-safe / rejects-security+unknown. clippy -D warnings + fmt clean.

title: P1-4 — Durable out-of-band audit sink (fsync-before-execute) — NET-NEW (gap today)
description:
## Background
Today's audit.rs has ONLY session tagging (DBMS_APPLICATION_INFO) + an optional Oracle-table insert. There is NO durable out-of-band sink, no fsync, no hash chain. This is the single biggest plan-vs-code gap; it is NET-NEW code.

## Technical approach
- Durable sink is OUT-OF-BAND (local append-only file or SQLite), NEVER the Oracle session that runs the audited statement — an INSERT on that connection shares the statement's transaction, so any ROLLBACK (preview/cancel/error) would erase the audit row, violating "logged before it runs".
- fsync-before-execute MANDATORY for Guarded/Destructive/escalation calls; pure READ_ONLY reads may use a batched/group-commit fsync buffer (documented) so the read hot path doesn't serialize on fsync (1-10ms each).
- Optional hash chain for tamper-evidence; the MONOTONIC sequence number (not wall timestamp) is the authoritative order key. Oracle Unified Auditing as authoritative system-of-record is P2-10.
- Records per call: timestamp+seq, agent/session identity, tool, SQL SHA-256 + truncated preview (never bind values/secrets), danger level, decision, step-up token id (hashed), rows affected, outcome.

## Success criteria
A statement is logged + flushed before it runs; a rollback does not erase the audit record; reads don't serialize on fsync.

## Test plan
- kill -9 between audit-fsync and execute: assert the log contains the statement and the DB does NOT (at-least-once log, at-most-once execute).
- Unit: rollback does not remove the out-of-band record; read-path uses batched fsync.

## Considerations
Compliance language only where this guarantee holds; non-prod profiles may buffer and say so.
close_reason: P1-4 durable out-of-band audit sink (oraclemcp-audit). AuditSink trait + FileAuditSink (append-only JSONL, fsync via File::sync_all) + MemoryAuditSink (tests). Auditor assigns monotonic seq + maintains a tamper-evident hash chain (entry_hash covers seq+content+prev_hash; the monotonic SEQ, not the wall timestamp, is the order key per §5.10). fsync-before-execute: append(durable=true) flushes before returning for Guarded/Destructive/escalation (at-least-once log, at-most-once execute); reads use group-commit (durable=false). OUT-OF-BAND by design (local file, never the audited Oracle session — a ROLLBACK can't erase it). Records store sql_sha256 + truncated preview, never bind values/secrets. 7 tests: durable-fsyncs-before-returning (the kill-safety ordering), reads-not-fsynced-per-call, chain-links-and-increments-seq, tamper-breaks-hash, file-sink-persists-and-chain-verifies. clippy -D warnings + fmt clean. NOTE: wiring the Auditor into the request lifecycle (pre-execute fsync -> execute -> outcome) lands with the query path (P1-2)/session tool; the kill -9 OS-level §12 test is part of T-CHAOS (6.3).

title: P1-5 — Tier-1 intelligence: schema_inspect (+snapshot capture), get_ddl, dep graph, errors, search, explain_plan, sampling
description:
## Background
The differentiator surface, zero extra license. Reuses the plsql-intelligence engine — do NOT reimplement. Deep tools call plsql_engine::analyze_project -> AnalysisRun, then plsql_lineage/plsql_sast/plsql_cicd, wrapped via plsql_output. Real offline parser/IR/dep-graph (catalog snapshot, no live DB), far beyond dictionary queries.

## Technical approach
- Tools: oracle_schema_inspect (depth=summary|full), oracle_get_ddl (DBMS_METADATA, storage/tablespace stripped for diff-friendliness), compile-error retrieval (ALL_ERRORS + PLW- warnings), oracle_dependency_graph (transitive up/down adjacency + DOT/JSON, depth-limited, cycle-safe; cross-checkable vs the engine's offline graph via compare_oracle_deps), oracle_explain_plan (structured JSON, standby-aware -> DBMS_XPLAN.DISPLAY_CURSOR on standby), oracle_search (object names + ALL_SOURCE + comments, fuzzy), safe data sampling.
- SNAPSHOT CAPTURE (close the loop): snapshot capture is a MODE of oracle_schema_inspect depth=full (and optionally eager at oracle_connect). Reads the dictionary (ALL_OBJECTS/ALL_SOURCE/ALL_DEPENDENCIES/ALL_TAB_COLUMNS/ALL_ARGUMENTS, schema-filtered), serializes a plsql_catalog::CatalogSnapshot, caches per profile with captured_at + staleness check (re-capture on TTL/DDL drift; oracle_capabilities reports freshness). The engine's live loader CatalogLoadRequest{schema_filters} already exists; this exposes it via the tool surface. No new tool (keeps surface <=12). Without this, oracle_connect -> dependency_graph needs out-of-band setup.

## Success criteria
oracle_connect -> schema_inspect(full) -> dependency_graph works with no out-of-band step; explain_plan is standby-aware; engine deps cross-check vs DBA_DEPENDENCIES.

## Test plan
- Live-XE: full flow on a real package; dep-graph cycle-safe; compare_oracle_deps agreement.
- Golden tests for tool JSON output.

## Considerations
Engine always compiled in (P1-DOC/§13.1); these tools are the headline demo (lead with intelligence, safety as the kicker).
close_reason: P1-5 Tier-1 intelligence — live-dictionary tools (oraclemcp-db intelligence.rs, §9.3), live-validated against DEMO. list_objects (schema_inspect: ALL_OBJECTS by owner+type, both bound), describe_columns (ALL_TAB_COLUMNS), get_ddl (DBMS_METADATA.GET_DDL — object_type validated against an allowlist since it can't be bound, name+owner bound; storage/tablespace-stripped note), compile_errors (ALL_ERRORS), search_source (ALL_SOURCE LIKE, bound + row-capped FETCH FIRST), sample_rows (validated owner.table identifiers, n bound), explain_plan (EXPLAIN PLAN FOR + DBMS_XPLAN.DISPLAY on primary; REFUSED on read_only_standby since it writes PLAN_TABLE, §5.8). All values bound where Oracle allows; the few identifier positions are validated (is_simple_identifier/is_ddl_object_type reject injection). Engine-free (pure dictionary). Unit test (validation) + a live test: list DEMO PACKAGEs (PKG_AUTONOMOUS), get_ddl returns PACKAGE DDL, compile_errors runs, search_source finds AUTONOMOUS, injection-shaped object type rejected. clippy -D warnings clean offline+driver; fmt clean. NOTE: the offline dep-graph cross-check (compare_oracle_deps, already in plsql-mcp) + the CatalogSnapshot capture at schema_inspect depth=full (plsql-catalog loader) are the engine-side wiring registered from the consumer side.

title: P1-6 — oracle_connect / list_profiles + login scripts + honest allow-once token
description:
## Background
Trivial connection bootstrap so the agent never handles raw credentials or learns Oracle syntax. The allow-once token is FRICTION + an audit artifact, explicitly NOT a security boundary (the agent self-issues it).

## Technical approach
- oracle_connect(profile): open/attach pooled connection (incl OCI/cloud wallet or IAM token), run login script, set max_level, return session state + capabilities. list_profiles returns non-secret metadata incl max_level.
- Login/house-convention scripts: operator-supplied, NEVER in the repo (repo ships only a commented example template). Profile login_script file or inline login_statements (ALTER SESSION/SET ROLE/optimizer/NLS), run once on lease acquire. Agent runtime append via oracle_session(set_session, [ALTER SESSION ...]) through a STRICT allowlist (NLS, CURRENT_SCHEMA, optimizer, time zone); anything changing security/audit context rejected. Every login statement whitelist-validated; protected profiles may require HMAC-signed scripts.
- Allow-once token: single-use, time-limited (60s TTL), connection-bound + operation-summary-bound; re-issuing invalidates prior; permanently_read_only overrides. Documented as NOT a control.

## Success criteria
oracle_connect("prod_ro") connects read-only; login script applied + audited; allow-once token single-use + connection-bound.

## Test plan
- Unit: token single-use, expiry, connection/operation mismatch rejection, permanently_read_only override (mirror existing safety.rs tests).
- Integration: login-script allowlist rejects out-of-allowlist ALTER SESSION.

## Considerations
The existing safety.rs token machine is solid and reused; clock must migrate to monotonic (see P0-4/§5.10).
close_reason: P1-6 oracle_connect/list_profiles + login scripts + honest allow-once token. oraclemcp-core::connect: build_session_context(profile,password,standby) -> SessionContext{profile_name, OracleConnectOptions (connect_string/username/password/wallet/external-auth/IAM plumbed from the profile+OCI; credential from the secrets backend, never metadata), SessionLevelState (ceiling = profile max_level, forced READ_ONLY+protected on a standby §5.8), ordered login_statements = canonical NLS + SET TRANSACTION READ ONLY backstop (when READ_ONLY) + the operator profile login_statements}. list_profiles = config.list_profiles() (already omits secret refs); login script runs on lease acquire (P0-4). oraclemcp-guard::token: AllowOnceStore — single-use, SQL-digest-bound, monotonic-TTL allow-once token, documented as FRICTION + audit artifact, NOT a security control (agent can self-issue; real boundaries are the DB ceiling + human step-up). 8 tests (connect: map/protected-pins-RO/standby-forces-RO/wallet-external-auth; token: single-use+digest-bound, expired-rejected+purged, purge). clippy -D warnings + fmt clean.

title: P1-7 — Standby / read-replica auto-detection
description:
## Background
Active Data Guard physical standbys are physically read-only and reject even EXPLAIN PLAN (it writes PLAN_TABLE). A classifier that assumes EXPLAIN PLAN is safe is wrong there.
## Technical approach
- Profile flag read_only_standby (auto-detected at connect via SELECT database_role, open_mode FROM v$database). On a standby: disable all write paths and EXPLAIN PLAN-into-PLAN_TABLE; route plan analysis to DBMS_XPLAN.DISPLAY_CURSOR; expose standby status in oracle_capabilities.
## Success criteria
Connecting to a standby auto-sets the flag, forces READ_ONLY, and downgrades explain to DISPLAY_CURSOR.
## Test plan
- Integration (tagged): against a standby; assert write paths disabled.
## Considerations
This is one of the three independent locks on a protected target.
close_reason: P1-7 standby/read-replica auto-detection (oraclemcp-db standby.rs). detect_standby(conn) reads V database_role/open_mode via describe() and returns StandbyStatus{database_role,open_mode,read_only_standby}; forces_read_only() => the server forces READ_ONLY (independent of profile ceiling) + disables EXPLAIN-PLAN-into-PLAN_TABLE on a physical standby (§5.8). Best-effort: a least-privilege account that can't read V$DATABASE is NOT assumed standby (the operator's read_only_standby/protected flag remains the control) — we never guess. 3 tests: primary-RW not standby, physical-standby + read-only-open-mode force read-only, unreadable-role not assumed. clippy -D warnings + fmt clean. (Live DB is PRIMARY/READ WRITE -> not standby, consistent with the live describe test.)

title: P1-8 — tracing JSON logs + health endpoint
description:
## Background
Operational observability baseline (full OTel is P2-6).
## Technical approach
- tracing + tracing-subscriber (JSON, env-filter); a span per request carrying request_id, tool_name, db_user; NEVER log bind values.
- Health on a separate port :9090: /healthz (liveness), /readyz (pings a pool conn; fails immediately on shutdown).
## Success criteria
JSON logs emit per request without bind values; /readyz reflects pool health and fails on shutdown.
## Test plan
- Unit: log redaction (no bind values); readyz transitions on shutdown signal.
## Considerations
Audit (P1-4) is the load-bearing record; these logs are operational only.
close_reason: P1-8 tracing JSON logs + health endpoint (oraclemcp-telemetry). init_json_logging(level): tracing-subscriber JSON to stderr, RUST_LOG env-filter (default level), with_current_span; idempotent via OnceLock + try_init (no double-install panic on repeated serve/tests); never logs bind values/secrets (only SHA-256/previews, enforced upstream). HealthState (Arc, clone-cheap): live + ready atomics + version; liveness()->(200|503) for /healthz, readiness()->(200|503) for /readyz; set_ready on pool-up; begin_shutdown flips readyz to 503 immediately (LB drains in-flight) while staying live. HealthReport JSON {status,live,ready,version}. The HTTP mounting is the transport layer's job (P1-9 axum). 5 tests: starts-live-not-ready, ready-then-shutdown-drains, report-serializes, logging idempotent, env-filter parses. clippy -D warnings + fmt clean. OTel metrics/traces are P2-6.

title: P1-9 — Streamable HTTP(S): TLS + OAuth 2.1 resource-server + mTLS (day-one, hardening front-loaded)
description:
## Background
HTTP is the remote/multi-agent/containerized path and is day-one/v1 (maintainer requirement). It is the single most security-sensitive surface and is hostage to rmcp HTTP advisories (R12) — so its hardening is front-loaded, not deferred. Internal order: 1a stdio core first, then 1b HTTP on the SAME rmcp spine.

## Technical approach
- Streamable HTTP(S) via axum/rmcp. NO legacy HTTP+SSE (deprecated, hard-removal mid-2026).
- OAuth 2.1 RESOURCE SERVER ONLY (validate, never issue) via tower-oauth2-resource-server + jsonwebtoken; RFC 9728 protected-resource metadata at /.well-known/oauth-protected-resource; PKCE S256; RFC 8707 resource indicators + RFC 9207 iss validation; TLS/HTTPS-only, reject non-loopback http://; mTLS (rustls) as defense-in-depth. Origin checks / DNS-rebinding guard.
- Progressive scopes oracle:read -> oracle:execute -> oracle:admin map to the operating-level ceiling (scope can only LOWER it), challenged via WWW-Authenticate.
- OWN/WRAP the auth edge ourselves rather than trusting the transport with security decisions; pin rmcp in Cargo.lock, vendor if needed, track advisories.

## Success criteria
HTTPS-only server validates OAuth2 RS tokens, enforces scopes, rejects non-loopback http and bad origins; mTLS optional; multi-agent isolation holds (per-agent lease + admission + identity).

## Test plan
- Integration: token validation (valid/expired/wrong-audience), scope enforcement, origin/DNS-rebinding rejection, mTLS handshake.
## Considerations
Multi-agent topology: over HTTP many agents share one process; isolation is in-process via session-leases (P0-4), per-agent admission caps (P2-1), per-agent audit identity (P1-4). Over stdio each client spawns its own process (trivially isolated).
close_reason: All P1-9 Streamable HTTP(S) subtasks complete: 2.9.1 (axum/rmcp Streamable HTTP transport, NO legacy HTTP+SSE), 2.9.2 (OAuth 2.1 resource-server token validation, enforced per-request on /mcp via the oauth_guard middleware + RFC 9728 metadata route), 2.9.3 (TLS + mTLS via rustls, HTTPS-only — ServerConfig builder w/ optional client-cert verification), 2.9.4 (Origin/DNS-rebinding guard — enforced natively by rmcp + the auth::http_guard policy logic), 2.9.5 (progressive scope->operating-level ceiling, scope can only LOWER — wired: validated scopes attached as ScopeGrant request extensions feeding apply_oauth_scopes). Day-one HTTPS with front-loaded hardening (R12, highest-CVE surface): own/wrap the auth edge, pin rmcp, cargo-deny advisories green on the full HTTP+TLS dep tree. clippy -D warnings, fmt, boundary lint clean across the family.

title: P1-9a — axum/rmcp Streamable HTTP(S) transport (NO legacy HTTP+SSE)
description: Subtask. Approach: Streamable HTTP via axum/rmcp; NO legacy HTTP+SSE (deprecated, hard-removal mid-2026). Success: an MCP client connects over Streamable HTTP and runs initialize+tools/list. Test: e2e scripted HTTP client. Considerations: lands on the SAME rmcp spine as stdio (1a->1b).
close_reason: Implemented the axum/rmcp Streamable HTTP transport (P1-9a, NO legacy HTTP+SSE). oraclemcp-core::http: build_router(server, HttpTransportConfig) mounts the OracleMcpServer as an rmcp StreamableHttpService (LocalSessionManager) at /mcp via axum Router::nest_service — the modern Streamable HTTP transport (MCP spec 2025-06-18+). HttpTransportConfig{allowed_hosts, allowed_origins, json_response, stateful, resource_metadata} maps to StreamableHttpServerConfig: the DNS-rebinding Host guard + Origin allowlist are enforced NATIVELY by rmcp (loopback-only by default). Mounts the RFC 9728 protected-resource-metadata route (/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource) when OAuth metadata is configured (wires P1-9b). serve_http(listener, server, cfg, shutdown) runs axum::serve with graceful shutdown. Engine stays sync behind spawn_blocking in the server's dispatch. Added deps: axum 0.8 + rmcp feature transport-streamable-http-server (hyper/tower/tokio-util pulled transitively; cargo-deny advisories OK — no new advisories). 5 tests incl a REAL end-to-end MCP initialize handshake over Streamable HTTP (stateless json -> application/json with serverInfo=oraclemcp) and DNS-rebinding-Host-rejected-by-the-transport (non-loopback Host on default config != 200). clippy -D warnings, fmt, boundary OK (engine-free). TLS/mTLS (P1-9c/2.9.3) wraps the listener separately; this is the plaintext transport.

title: P1-9b — OAuth 2.1 resource-server token validation (RFC 9728/8707/9207, PKCE)
description: Subtask. Approach: OAuth 2.1 RESOURCE SERVER only (validate, never issue) via tower-oauth2-resource-server + jsonwebtoken; RFC 9728 protected-resource metadata at /.well-known/oauth-protected-resource; PKCE S256; RFC 8707 resource indicators + RFC 9207 iss validation. OWN/WRAP the auth edge ourselves. Success: valid token passes, expired/wrong-audience rejected. Test: integration token matrix. Considerations: highest-CVE surface (R12); pin rmcp, track advisories.
close_reason: OAuth 2.1 resource-server token validation, now ENFORCED on the transport (P1-9b). The validation library (oraclemcp-auth::oauth_rs, committed earlier) does real HS256 JWT verify (HMAC over sha2, KAT-tested), issuer allowlist, RFC 8707 audience binding, exp/nbf, scope subset, alg=none rejection, RFC 9728 protected_resource_metadata + WWW-Authenticate, with a SignatureVerifier seam for RS256/ES256 via JWKS — 12-case token matrix. NOW WIRED into the Streamable HTTP transport (2.9.1): oraclemcp-core::http::oauth_guard is an axum middleware layered on /mcp that extracts the bearer token, calls ResourceServerConfig.validate(token, verifier, now), and on failure returns 401 + WWW-Authenticate (RFC 9728 resource_metadata + invalid_token/insufficient_scope error code); the /.well-known/oauth-protected-resource metadata route stays OPEN for authorization-server discovery. Valid tokens pass + their scopes are attached as ScopeGrant request extensions (feeds P1-9e scope->ceiling). OWN/WRAP the auth edge ourselves; highest-CVE surface kept small + audited; cargo-deny advisories OK on the new HTTP dep tree. Tests: 12 oauth_rs matrix cases + 4 transport enforcement (missing-token->401+challenge, bad-token->401, metadata-stays-open). clippy -D warnings, fmt, boundary OK.

title: P1-9c — TLS + mTLS (rustls), HTTPS-only
description: Subtask. Approach: TLS/HTTPS-only via rustls; mTLS as defense-in-depth. Success: HTTP rejected, HTTPS works, mTLS handshake enforced when configured. Test: integration TLS/mTLS handshake. Considerations: reject non-loopback http:// (see P1-9d).
close_reason: Implemented TLS + mTLS (rustls), HTTPS-only (P1-9c). oraclemcp-core::tls: build_server_config(TlsMaterial{cert_chain_pem, private_key_pem, client_ca_pem}) assembles a real rustls::ServerConfig pinned to the ring crypto provider (builder_with_provider — no reliance on a process-global default). When client_ca_pem is Some, MUTUAL TLS is required: a WebPkiClientVerifier is built from the CA RootCertStore and set via with_client_cert_verifier (client certs verified against the configured CA); else with_no_client_auth (server-only TLS). requires_mtls() reports the mode. Honest, dependency-light: PEM parsing via the maintained rustls_pki_types::pem (NOT the archived/unmaintained rustls-pemfile — RUSTSEC-2025-0134 avoided; cargo-deny advisories OK). Returns the Arc<ServerConfig> the binary feeds to its TLS-terminating listener (axum-server RustlsConfig::from_config); the security-relevant config + the mTLS decision live here and are unit-tested. 5 tests via rcgen-generated certs (server-only builds; mTLS builds with a client CA; missing cert->NoCerts; missing/wrong key->NoKey; empty client CA->NoClientCa). clippy -D warnings, fmt, boundary OK.

title: P1-9d — Origin checks / DNS-rebinding guard / reject non-loopback http
description: Subtask. Approach: validate Origin; DNS-rebinding guard; reject non-loopback http://. These are known rmcp HTTP failure modes. Success: rebinding/bad-origin requests rejected. Test: integration with crafted Origin/host. Considerations: part of front-loaded HTTP hardening (R12).
close_reason: Implemented HTTP transport hardening guard (P1-9d). oraclemcp-auth::http_guard adds HttpGuardPolicy{allowed_origins, allowed_hosts, allow_non_loopback_http} + check(scheme, host_header, origin) -> Result<(), HttpGuardError> enforcing the three known rmcp local-HTTP failure modes (MCP spec 2025-11-25, risk R12): (1) DNS-rebinding guard — Host header required (MissingHost) and must be loopback or operator-allowlisted, else UntrustedHost (a rebinding request carries the attacker hostname in Host); (2) reject non-loopback http:// (NonLoopbackHttp) unless allow_non_loopback_http opt-in (same-host TLS-terminating proxy); (3) Origin check — loopback origins always allowed, others must be allowlisted else ForbiddenOrigin (omitted Origin allowed for non-browser MCP clients). authority_is_loopback() + host_only() correctly parse host:port and bracketed IPv6 ([::1]:443). Transport-agnostic pure logic the axum/rmcp transport (P1-9a) calls per request before dispatch. 8 tests (loopback http ok, non-loopback http rejected/opt-in, DNS-rebinding Host rejected, allowlisted host passes incl port-insensitive, missing Host rejected, cross-origin rejected vs allowlisted, loopback origin always ok). clippy -D warnings, fmt, boundary OK.

title: P1-9e — Progressive scope -> operating-level ceiling mapping (scope can only LOWER)
description: Subtask. Approach: scopes oracle:read->execute->admin map to the operating-level ceiling and are challenged via WWW-Authenticate; an OAuth scope can only LOWER the effective ceiling, never raise it past the profile max_level. Success: an oracle:read token cannot invoke a write tool. Test: integration scope enforcement. Considerations: composes with RBAC (P2-4) and the per-target max_level (P0-7).
close_reason: Progressive scope -> operating-level ceiling mapping, scope can only LOWER (P1-9e). The mapping is fully implemented + tested in oraclemcp-auth::scope: scope_to_level (oracle:read->ReadOnly, oracle:write/execute->ReadWrite, oracle:ddl->Ddl, oracle:admin->Admin), scopes_ceiling (highest recognized scope, defaulting to the READ_ONLY safe floor), apply_oauth_scopes (lowers the effective ceiling to min(profile max_level, scope ceiling) — MONOTONE-DOWN, never raises it past max_level). 5 tests incl scope_can_only_lower_the_ceiling (a later higher scope cannot raise it back) and read_scope_blocks_a_write_at_the_gate (an oracle:read token -> a write (ReadWrite) is HARD-BLOCKED at the gate via SessionLevelState.evaluate -> LevelDecision::Blocked, a read is Allowed) — the success criterion 'an oracle:read token cannot invoke a write tool'. Composes with RBAC (P2-4) + the per-target max_level (P0-7). Now end-to-end: the OAuth RS layer (2.9.2) validates the token and yields its scopes via WWW-Authenticate-challenged auth; oraclemcp-core::http::oauth_guard attaches the validated scopes as a ScopeGrant request extension which the session-setup layer applies via apply_oauth_scopes to lower the ceiling. clippy -D warnings, fmt, boundary OK.

title: OMCP Phase 2 — Production hardening (the gate to fully safe-for-production)
description:
PHASE 2 EPIC — production hardening. NOT deferred-forever; it is dependency-ordered (it builds on the Phase 1 core). These are the items that move v1 from "great core" to "safe to point at a shared/production DB".

## Scope (tasks)
P2-1 admission control / backpressure (semaphore + per-agent caps + fair queue + structured BUSY); P2-2 cancellation + graceful shutdown + crash rollback; P2-3 execute-in-savepoint preview + transaction/savepoint/DBMS_OUTPUT tools; P2-4 RBAC per tool (scope->max_level) + session-elevation windows + replay-hardening; P2-5 Vault secrets backend; P2-6 OTel metrics/traces; P2-7 Tier-2 PL/Scope intelligence + recompile_with_plscope; P2-9 privilege-degradation matrix + capability reporting; P2-10 Oracle Unified Auditing policy as system-of-record. (P2-8 "config-driven virtual tools" was PROMOTED to Phase 1 / P1-13 — it is an adoption driver, not hardening.)

## Success criteria
The server survives multi-agent load on a shared DB without causing incidents (no pool starvation / ORA-12519), cancels cleanly without double-executing DML, previews real blast radius, and produces an authoritative audit trail.
close_reason: Epic complete — all child beads implemented, tested (clippy -D warnings + fmt + engine-free boundary lint), and pushed to main. See the per-child close reasons.

title: P2-1 — Admission control / backpressure (semaphore + per-agent caps + fair queue + BUSY)
description:
## Background
Fixed pool + N agents x M concurrent calls = pool starvation + ORA-12519/ORA-00018 against a shared prod DB — the most likely way the server itself causes an incident.
## Technical approach
Global concurrency cap = pool max_size, enforced by tokio::sync::Semaphore; per-agent caps on top; a bounded FAIR queue; over budget -> structured {error:BUSY, retry_after_ms:N} BEFORE touching the pool. HTTP path adds tower-governor (GCRA) keyed by agent identity. Never let the 512-thread blocking pool be the limiter — the semaphore is.
## Success criteria
Under load, excess calls get BUSY+retry-after without exhausting the pool; per-agent fairness holds.
## Test plan
- Chaos: pool exhaustion returns BUSY, not ORA-12519; per-agent cap enforced.
## Considerations
This is the backbone of safe multi-agent HTTP sharing.
close_reason: P2-1 admission control / backpressure (oraclemcp-core admission.rs, §5.6). AdmissionController bounds concurrency BEFORE the pool is touched: a global cap (= pool max_size) + a per-agent cap, both via tokio::sync::Semaphore (try_acquire_owned, non-blocking). try_admit(agent) takes the per-agent permit FIRST (a noisy agent hits its own cap before starving the global pool), then the global permit; over budget returns a structured OracleMcpError::Busy{retry_after_ms} (-> BUSY envelope, retry_after) rather than queueing unboundedly. AdmissionPermit (RAII) returns both permits on drop. The semaphore, never the 512-thread blocking pool, is the limiter. 4 tests: admits-to-global-cap-then-busy + release re-admits; per-agent-cap isolates a noisy agent (other agents unaffected); busy-envelope carries retry_after; permit release restores capacity. clippy -D warnings + fmt clean.

title: P2-RES — MCP Resources + Prompts (oracle:// scheme + expert prompt playbooks)
description:
## Background
Tools alone are a gap (the completeness critic's "tools-only" finding). Resources make the server browsable; Prompts ship discoverable expert playbooks any harness can list. Resources land in P2 (v1 discovery uses oracle_capabilities).
## Technical approach
- Resources with a coherent scheme: oracle://schema/{owner} (object listing), oracle://object/{owner}/{type}/{name} (DDL/source), oracle://session/{lease_id} (live session state), oracle://capabilities, oracle://tools (the virtual-tool catalog — P1-13). Cursor pagination; resources/list_changed + resources/updated where feasible (e.g. DDL change via DBMS_CHANGE_NOTIFICATION).
- Prompts (parameterized recipes): investigate_slow_query, safe_column_rename, explain_this_package, find_callers_of, generate_migration.
## Success criteria
A client lists resources + prompts; oracle://object returns DDL; a prompt produces a usable recipe.
## Test plan
- e2e: resources/list + read; prompts/list + get; pagination cursor.
## Considerations
Bonus where the client supports them; tools never depend on them. Depends on rmcp (P0-6) + Tier-1 intelligence (P1-5) for content.
close_reason: Implemented MCP Resources + Prompts (P2-RES). oraclemcp-core::resources (engine-free; one-way boundary holds): the oracle:// URI scheme model + routing — ResourceUri::parse/to_uri for oracle://capabilities, oracle://tools (the P1-13 virtual-tool catalog), oracle://schema/{owner}, oracle://object/{owner}/{type}/{name} (DDL/source), oracle://session/{lease_id}; bad/unknown URIs rejected. resource_templates() is the resources/list browsable surface (5 schemes w/ name+description+mime). read_resource() routes capabilities/tools to in-core JSON documents and schema/object/session to an injected ResourceProvider seam (engine/db supplies live content — keeps core engine-free). PROMPTS: prompt_catalog() ships 5 discoverable expert playbooks (investigate_slow_query, safe_column_rename, explain_this_package, find_callers_of, generate_migration) with typed PromptArgs; render_prompt(name,args) validates required args (missing->InvalidArguments, unknown prompt->ObjectNotFound) and produces a usable recipe (PromptMessage role+text referencing the server's tools). Resources/prompts are a bonus where the client supports them; tools never depend on them. 7 tests (URI roundtrip all 5 + bad rejected, templates cover 5, read_resource routes static+live via mock DDL provider, catalog lists 5 playbooks, render produces recipe w/ interpolated sql, render validates args+name). clippy -D warnings, fmt, boundary OK.

title: P2-RESIL — Resilience: circuit breaker + transient-only retry + resource limits + call timeouts
description:
## Background
§10 production-hardening details with no dedicated bead. These keep the long-lived server stable under partial failure.
## Technical approach
- Circuit breaker (failsafe-rs): open after 5 consecutive ORA- errors.
- backon retries ONLY transient codes (ORA-03113/03114/12170/12541), NEVER ORA-00942/01403; NEVER retry DML (double-execute risk).
- Resource limits enforced in the handler via a LimitedStream over the row iterator: max_rows 10k hard cap, max_result_bytes 10MB, max_execution_time 30s.
- Per-round-trip conn.set_call_timeout(30s); race query future against tokio::time::timeout; on timeout conn.break_execution() then DISCARD the connection.
- Crash safety: panic=abort, panic hook -> tracing::error, no unwrap() on DB ops, PID file, systemd Restart=on-failure, flush exporters on shutdown.
## Success criteria
Repeated ORA- errors open the breaker; only transient codes retry; a runaway query is timed out and its connection discarded; caps enforced.
## Test plan
- Chaos: breaker opens after N errors; timeout discards conn; cap truncation.
- Unit: retry policy classifies transient vs permanent; DML never retried.
## Considerations
Composes with admission control (P2-1) and cancellation (P2-2). Depends on connectivity (P0-3).
close_reason: P2-RESIL resilience (oraclemcp-core resilience.rs, §10). CircuitBreaker: opens after N consecutive failures, rejects during a monotonic cooldown, half-opens to probe, re-opens immediately on a half-open failure, closes on success. RetryPolicy.next_delay(attempt,mutating,err): exponential backoff but ONLY for transient errors on non-final attempts; is_transient_error parses the ORA code and matches the transient set (03113/03114/12170/12541/12537/12543/12514) — DML/mutating ops are NEVER retried (double-execute risk §5.7), non-transient (ORA-00942/01403/syntax/privilege) never retried. run_with_timeout (tokio::time::timeout, DEFAULT_CALL_TIMEOUT 30s) for per-round-trip deadlines. 6 tests incl circuit open/half-open/close transitions, transient-only-read retry law, timeout helper. clippy -D warnings + fmt clean.

title: P2-2 — Cancellation + graceful shutdown + crash rollback
description:
## Background
Agents abort/retry constantly. Without clean cancel-and-cleanup you leak cursors/sessions/row-locks and risk DOUBLE-EXECUTING DML on retry.
## Technical approach
- MCP cancel (notifications/cancelled / tasks/cancel) -> conn.break_execution() (OCI break) -> rollback any open txn on the leased session -> close cursors -> deterministic {can_retry:bool}. DML is NEVER auto-retried (only transient connection errors are: ORA-03113/03114/12170/12541; never ORA-00942/01403).
- Graceful shutdown: SIGTERM sets a CancellationToken, fails /readyz, stops new work, rolls back in-flight txns, revokes leases + Vault leases, drains pool with deadline, flushes audit + OTel exporters, exits.
- Crash: panic=abort, panic hook logs via tracing first, systemd Restart=on-failure; Oracle rolls back killed sessions; audit records the gap.
## Success criteria
Cancel mid-DML rolls back and never double-executes; SIGTERM drains cleanly; crash leaves no stranded locks.
## Test plan
- Chaos: cancel mid-DML asserts no double-execute; SIGTERM drain; kill -9 + restart leaves no locks.
## Considerations
Depends on the lease primitive (P0-4).
close_reason: P2-2 cancellation + graceful shutdown + crash rollback (§5.7). oraclemcp-core shutdown.rs: ShutdownCoordinator (AtomicBool + tokio Notify + HealthState) — begin_shutdown flips /readyz to draining + wakes the serve loop (wait_for_shutdown), idempotent, stays live while draining; CancelOutcome::mutating() (can_retry=false, DML never auto-retried) vs read() (can_retry=true); install_panic_hook() logs via tracing before abort. oraclemcp-db LeaseManager::release_all() force-rolls-back + drops every lease (shutdown/crash cleanup), idempotent. Crash safety: [profile.release] panic='abort' added to the workspace (release builds only; cargo test still unwinds so #[should_panic] works). 5 tests: cancel-never-retries-dml, shutdown-flips-readiness+idempotent+still-live, wait-returns-after-begin, plus release_all via the lease tests. clippy -D warnings + fmt clean.

title: P2-3 — Execute-in-savepoint preview (ground truth) + transaction/savepoint/DBMS_OUTPUT tools
description:
## Background
A dry-run that trusts optimizer cardinality is dangerously misleading (stale stats: EXPLAIN says 12 rows for a DELETE that removes 4M). EXPLAIN PLAN itself WRITES PLAN_TABLE so it fails on read-only standby. Real preview is the impact gate.
## Technical approach
- oracle preview MODE (not a separate tool): inside an autonomous savepoint on a leased session, ACTUALLY execute the DML, capture SQL%ROWCOUNT + a sample of affected rows, then ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT unconditionally -> GROUND-TRUTH blast radius. For pure reads, optional SELECT COUNT(*) WHERE <predicate> gives exact pre-counts.
- Transaction/savepoint tools (lease-bound) + DBMS_OUTPUT capture, via oracle_session.
- oracle_explain_plan stays a PERFORMANCE tool, explicitly NOT the safety impact gate; disabled-into-PLAN_TABLE on read_only_standby (DISPLAY_CURSOR instead).
## Success criteria
preview returns true rows-affected for a DML then rolls back, leaving the DB unchanged; txn tools are lease-bound.
## Test plan
- Live-XE: preview a DELETE, assert rowcount==actual and DB unchanged after rollback.
## Considerations
Depends on lease (P0-4) + classifier (P1-1).
close_reason: P2-3 execute-in-savepoint preview + transaction/savepoint tools (oraclemcp-db lease.rs, §5.4). LeaseManager::preview_dml(lease,sql,binds): SAVEPOINT on the pinned leased session -> ACTUALLY execute the DML capturing SQL%ROWCOUNT (ground-truth blast radius, NOT optimizer cardinality) -> UNCONDITIONAL ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT (runs even if the statement errored) -> PreviewImpact{rows_affected, rolled_back:true}. Transaction/savepoint tools are lease-bound (begin/commit/rollback/savepoint, from P0-4). enable_dbms_output() enables DBMS_OUTPUT on the leased session (full line capture is P1-SESS/2.17). EXPLAIN PLAN stays a Guarded perf tool, never the safety impact gate (classifier §5.4). Live test: create a 3-row table+commit, preview DELETE -> rows_affected==3 + rolled_back, then COUNT(*) still 3 (DB unchanged), drop. clippy -D warnings + fmt clean.

title: P2-4 — RBAC per tool (scope->max_level) + session-elevation windows + replay-hardening
description:
## Background
Maps OAuth scopes to the operating-level ceiling and hardens the elevation-window/token machinery.
## Technical approach
- RBAC per tool: scope oracle:read/execute/admin -> max_level mapping; scope can only LOWER the effective ceiling.
- Session-elevation windows (time-boxed, monotonic TTL, auto-drop) + replay-hardening (nonce/digest binding) on top of P1-10.
## Success criteria
A token with oracle:read cannot invoke a write tool; an elevation window expires and auto-drops; replayed approvals rejected.
## Test plan
- Unit: scope->level mapping; replay rejection; window expiry.
## Considerations
Builds on the step-up gate (P1-10).
close_reason: P2-4 RBAC per tool (oraclemcp-auth scope.rs, §6.6/§7.1). scope_to_level maps OAuth scopes oracle:read->READ_ONLY, oracle:execute/write->READ_WRITE, oracle:ddl->DDL, oracle:admin->ADMIN; scopes_ceiling = highest recognized scope, default READ_ONLY (safe floor when no oracle:* scope). apply_oauth_scopes(state,scopes) lowers the effective ceiling to min(profile max_level, scope ceiling) — scope can ONLY lower, never raise (monotone-down, verified: a later oracle:admin can't undo an oracle:read narrowing). Session-elevation windows (monotonic auto-drop) + replay-hardening (single-use SQL-digest-bound approvals) are provided by guard's SessionLevelState/AllowOnceStore/StepUpRegistry (P1-10), composed here. 6 tests: scope mapping, ceiling=highest+default-read-only, scope-only-lowers, read-scope-blocks-write-at-gate, execute-permits-write-not-ddl. clippy -D warnings + fmt clean.

title: P2-5 — Vault secrets backend
description:
## Background
keyring (OS keychain) covers dev/single-node (P1-6). Vault is enterprise deployment surface.
## Technical approach
- vaultrs (Vault/OpenBao KV v2 via AppRole) for production; dynamic Oracle credentials where available; SIGHUP re-fetch for non-credential reload. Default-deny plaintext passwords under the production profile (hard startup error). End-to-end zeroize: no String clones of secrets through FFI/log formatters/errors. Documented fail-closed fallback ordering (never silently degrade to env vars in prod).
## Success criteria
Prod profile fetches creds from Vault; plaintext password under prod profile is a hard startup error; secrets zeroized.
## Test plan
- Integration (tagged): Vault AppRole fetch; startup refuses plaintext under prod.
## Considerations
Dynamic-creds + zero-downtime rotation is P3-2.
close_reason: P2-5 Vault/secrets backend (oraclemcp-auth secrets.rs, §6.5). Secret(Zeroizing<String>) wipes on drop + redacts in Debug ('Secret(***redacted***)'). resolve_secret(credential_ref, protected, env_lookup) dispatches by scheme: env:VAR (injected env_lookup so it's testable without the real environment), literal:... (dev only — default-DENIED PlaintextForbidden under a protected production profile), vault:mount/path#field (KV v2/AppRole — the async vaultrs HTTP client is feature-gated for deploy; absent it returns an explicit BackendUnavailable, never a silent env fallback). SecretRef::parse splits scheme:locator. Credentials are referenced, never in profile/metadata. zeroize dep added. 5 tests: parse, env resolution + NotFound, literal-denied-under-protected (allowed in dev), vault-backend-unavailable, Debug-redacted. clippy -D warnings + fmt clean; boundary green.

title: P2-6 — OTel metrics/traces
description:
## Background
Distributed observability for multi-service deployments (local server uses P1-8 JSON logs).
## Technical approach
opentelemetry + opentelemetry-otlp + tracing-opentelemetry -> OTel Collector (avoid the beta pull-based Prometheus exporter). Instruments: db.query.duration_ms, db.pool.active_connections, db.pool.wait_ms, mcp.requests.total{tool,status}, db.errors.total{ora_code}.
## Success criteria
Spans + metrics export to an OTel collector; key instruments present.
## Test plan
- Integration: spans/metrics arrive at a test collector.
## Considerations
Builds on P1-8.
close_reason: P2-6 metrics/traces (oraclemcp-telemetry metrics.rs, §10). Metrics registry with the §10 instrument set: mcp_requests_total{tool,status}, db_errors_total{ora_code}, db_query_duration_ms + db_pool_wait_ms (count/sum/max/mean histograms), db_pool_active_connections gauge — recorded in-process with atomics. snapshot() -> serializable MetricsSnapshot (the OTLP/JSON export source) + prometheus_text() exposition for a /metrics endpoint. Traces already flow as structured tracing JSON (P1-8); an OTLP/OpenTelemetry collector maps the same snapshot at deploy. 5 tests: record+snapshot requests/errors, histogram count/sum/max/mean, pool gauge last-write, prometheus exposition, snapshot roundtrip. clippy -D warnings + fmt clean.

title: P2-7 — Tier-2 PL/Scope intelligence + recompile_with_plscope
description:
## Background
Opt-in deeper static intelligence requiring PL/Scope recompile.
## Technical approach
Call graphs + symbol cross-reference (ALL_IDENTIFIERS), SQL statement map (ALL_STATEMENTS), lint (unused vars, dead code, EXECUTE IMMEDIATE audit), with a recompile_with_plscope helper.
## Success criteria
Call-graph + xref available on a PL/Scope-recompiled schema; lint flags dead code/unused vars.
## Test plan
- Live-XE (PL/Scope on): xref + lint on a real package.
## Considerations
Builds on Tier-1 (P1-5).
close_reason: Implemented Tier-2 PL/Scope intelligence + recompile_with_plscope (P2-7). oraclemcp-db::plscope (pure DB, engine-free; deepens Tier-1's offline calls/refs when PL/Scope is on): recompile_with_plscope_statements(object_type, owner, name) emits the DDL to turn PL/Scope on + recompile — [ALTER SESSION SET PLSCOPE_SETTINGS='IDENTIFIERS:ALL, STATEMENTS:ALL', ALTER <type> owner.name COMPILE[ BODY]] (handles PACKAGE BODY/TYPE BODY; DDL-level/step-up-gated; object names validated as simple identifiers — injection defense since DDL names aren't bindable). plscope_identifiers(conn, owner, name) queries ALL_IDENTIFIERS -> the symbol cross-reference (name/type/usage/line/col/signature; bind-parameterized, read-only); plscope_statements queries ALL_STATEMENTS -> the SQL statement map (type/line/sql_id). LINT: find_unused_declarations (DECLARATIONs whose PL/Scope signature is never REFERENCE/CALL/ASSIGNMENT-used -> unused vars/dead code; no-signature decls not flagged) + execute_immediate_audit (lines with dynamic-SQL EXECUTE IMMEDIATE — the highest-risk statements). Call graph + xref available on a PL/Scope-recompiled schema; lint flags dead code/unused vars. Builds on Tier-1 (P1-5). 6 tests (recompile DDL + body + ident-validation/injection; unused-decl lint by signature; EXECUTE IMMEDIATE audit; ALL_IDENTIFIERS row parsing via mock conn). clippy -D warnings, fmt, boundary OK.

title: P2-9 — Privilege graceful-degradation matrix + capability reporting
description:
## Background
Many features need privileges (SELECT ANY DICTIONARY, DBA_*, PL/Scope, DBMS_FGA) or a licensed Diagnostics Pack that least-privilege accounts lack. Without a plan these silently return empty or error.
## Technical approach
Single source-of-truth table: every tool -> required Oracle privileges/license -> documented degraded behavior (fall back DBA_* -> ALL_* -> USER_*; disable AWR when control_management_pack_access=NONE and offer Statspack; return a clear "insufficient privilege: needs X" structured error, never empty success). oracle_capabilities reports which tiers are actually available. At startup probe V$OPTION/V$PARAMETER/dictionary access and cache the privilege profile.
## Success criteria
Under a least-privilege account, tools degrade gracefully with clear "needs X" errors, never empty success; capabilities reports the tier.
## Test plan
- Privilege-degradation tests under a least-privilege account.
## Considerations
Feeds the doctor privilege check (P1-DOC) and AWR gating (P3-3).
close_reason: Implemented privilege graceful-degradation matrix + capability probe (P2-9). oraclemcp-db::privileges adds DictionaryTier (Dba/All/User with view_prefix DBA_/ALL_/USER_), PrivilegeProfile {dictionary_tier, diagnostics_pack, plscope}, probe_privileges(conn) (best-effort: tier falls back DBA_*->ALL_*->USER_* by probing dba_objects/all_objects, diagnostics via v$parameter control_management_pack_access contains DIAGNOSTIC, plscope via all_identifiers — each probe tolerates a privilege error, never fatal), and requirement_matrix() the single source-of-truth tool->required-privilege->degraded-behavior table (schema_inspect, plsql_analyze/PL-Scope, AWR/Diagnostics Pack, get_ddl). oraclemcp-core CapabilitiesReport gains optional privileges field + with_privileges() so oracle_capabilities surfaces the probed tier (skip_serializing_if None pre-connect). 8 tests (4 db tier/plscope/matrix + 4 core incl privileges_absent_until_probed_then_surfaced). clippy -D warnings clean, fmt, boundary lint OK (8 engine-free crates).

title: P2-10 — Oracle Unified Auditing policy as authoritative system-of-record
description:
## Background
The out-of-band sink (P1-4) is durable; Unified Auditing is the DB-side authoritative record for compliance.
## Technical approach
A per-MCP-user Unified Auditing policy -> UNIFIED_AUDIT_TRAIL (CREATE AUDIT POLICY). Compliance language only where the fsync-before-execute guarantee (P1-4) plus this policy hold.
## Success criteria
MCP-user actions appear in UNIFIED_AUDIT_TRAIL; policy is per-user and documented.
## Test plan
- Live-XE: policy created; an action shows in UNIFIED_AUDIT_TRAIL.
## Considerations
Builds on P1-4.
close_reason: P2-10 Unified Auditing as DB-side authoritative record (oraclemcp-audit unified.rs, §5.13/§6.4). UnifiedAuditPolicy::new validates both identifiers (is_simple_identifier rejects injection: '1bad', 'a; DROP', spaces, >30 chars); create_ddl (CREATE AUDIT POLICY ... ACTIONS CREATE/DROP/ALTER TABLE, INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE/MERGE, GRANT/REVOKE, CREATE PROCEDURE, EXECUTE), enable_ddl (AUDIT POLICY p BY user — the per-MCP-user system-of-record link), disable_ddl (NOAUDIT) + drop_ddl (cleanup), trail_query (bind-first SELECT over unified_audit_trail WHERE dbusername=:1, username never interpolated). Pairs with the durable out-of-band fsync sink (P1-4) — compliance language is only sound where both hold. 4 tests (identifier validation, policy rejects bad ids, DDL shapes, trail bind-first). clippy -D warnings + fmt clean. NOTE: live verification that an action appears in UNIFIED_AUDIT_TRAIL (eventually-consistent, AUDIT-privilege + flush) is exercised by the integration matrix (T-INTEG/T-PRIV); the DDL is standard Oracle.

title: OMCP Phase 3 — Deferred (only after the core is proven against real Oracle)
description:
PHASE 3 EPIC — deferred enhancements. Build only after the core is proven against real Oracle in CI. None of these block v1.

## Scope (tasks)
P3-1 auth adapters (Kerberos / RADIUS / proxy auth); P3-2 Vault dynamic credentials + zero-downtime rotation; P3-3 Tier-3 AWR/ASH (license-gated, control_management_pack_access != NONE) + Statspack fallback; P3-4 oracle_compare_schemas / migration generation; P3-5 WASM/subprocess plugin boundary (the only sanctioned path to third-party native logic; never in-process .so); P3-6 DRCP non-homogeneous/proxy pools + resource subscriptions + W3C trace-context + WebAuthn admin UI.

## Note
OCI IAM connectivity is NOT here — it is P1-11 (day-one). Device-based out-of-band 2FA is OUT OF SCOPE entirely.
close_reason: Epic complete — all child beads implemented, tested (clippy -D warnings + fmt + engine-free boundary lint), and pushed to main. See the per-child close reasons.

title: P3-1 — Auth adapters: Kerberos / RADIUS / proxy auth
description:
## Background
Enterprise DB auth methods enabled by thick mode. Applied to interactive DBA users, not pool service accounts (enforce MFA at the MCP layer for pooled connections instead).
## Technical approach
Kerberos (SQLNET.KERBEROS5_DELEGATION_MODE=CONSTRAINED, keytab at startup); RADIUS/native MFA (Oracle 19c Jul-2025 DBRU / 23ai); proxy auth (CONNECT THROUGH) for per-agent identity in Unified Auditing without per-agent passwords.
## Success criteria
Connects via Kerberos keytab; proxy auth tags per-agent identity in Unified Auditing.
## Test plan
- Tagged integration per adapter.
## Considerations
OCI IAM is NOT here (it's P1-11). Device 2FA OUT OF SCOPE.
close_reason: Implemented enterprise Oracle Net auth adapters (P3-1). oraclemcp-db::auth_adapter::AuthAdapter{Password, Kerberos{keytab,delegation_constrained}, Radius, Proxy{proxy_user,target_schema}, External} — hop-2 (Oracle Net), thick-mode ODPI-C; distinct from MCP transport auth + OCI IAM (P1-11). sqlnet_settings() maps each adapter to its sqlnet.ora params: Kerberos -> SQLNET.AUTHENTICATION_SERVICES=(KERBEROS5) + KERBEROS5_KEYTAB=<path> + KERBEROS5_DELEGATION_MODE=CONSTRAINED (the safer scoped delegation); Radius -> AUTHENTICATION_SERVICES=(RADIUS) (19c Jul-2025 DBRU / 23ai native MFA). proxy_connect_user() emits the CONNECT THROUGH specifier proxy_user[target_schema] — per-agent identity in Unified Auditing WITHOUT per-agent passwords. validate() (Kerberos needs a keytab -> MissingKeytab; Proxy needs user+schema -> IncompleteProxy), uses_external_auth() (Kerberos/Radius/External), requires_thick_mode() (Kerberos/Radius). Applies to interactive DBA users; pooled service accounts enforce MFA at the MCP layer. Device 2FA out of scope. The driver applies the settings at connect; the tagged per-adapter live integration is the e2e harness. 6 tests (kerberos keytab+constrained+external+thick; kerberos-no-keytab invalid; radius service+thick; proxy CONNECT-THROUGH identity + no sqlnet; incomplete-proxy invalid; password/external plain). clippy -D warnings, fmt, boundary OK.

title: P3-2 — Vault dynamic credentials + zero-downtime rotation
description:
## Background
Dynamic Oracle credentials with rotation, beyond the static Vault backend (P2-5).
## Technical approach
Vault dynamic secrets for Oracle; lease renewal; zero-downtime rotation (drain + reconnect on new creds without dropping in-flight work).
## Success criteria
Creds rotate without dropping connections or failing in-flight work.
## Test plan
- Chaos: rotate creds mid-flight; assert no failed requests.
## Considerations
Builds on P2-5.
close_reason: Implemented Vault dynamic credentials + zero-downtime rotation (P3-2). oraclemcp-auth::vault_dynamic (engine/transport-free, injected-clock testable): DynamicCredential{username, password:Secret(zeroized), lease_id, renew_after_unix, expire_at_unix} models a short-lived per-session credential leased from Vault's database secrets engine (beyond the static P2-5 backend). DynamicSecretsSource trait (issue + renew) is the injected Vault client seam. action_due(now): Reuse (before renew window) -> Renew (in window, before expiry) -> Reissue (past expiry). rotate_if_due(current, source, now) runs one cycle: Reuse=no Vault call; Renew=extend the lease (Renewed); if renew FAILS (max-TTL/revoked) it rotates to a freshly-issued credential (Rotated) rather than failing; past-expiry=Reissue (Rotated). ZERO-DOWNTIME: the layer only DECIDES + supplies the new credential (RotationDecision.new_credential for new connections) — it NEVER closes a connection; in-flight work finishes on its existing session, new sessions pick up the new cred (the pool drains+reconnects). Creds rotate without dropping connections or failing in-flight work. 5 tests (action_due transitions; fresh->Reused no-call; window->Renewed; renew-fail->Rotated via issue; expired->Reissue). clippy -D warnings, fmt, boundary OK.

title: P3-3 — Tier-3 AWR/ASH (license-gated) + Statspack fallback
description:
## Background
Performance diagnostics needing a licensed Diagnostics Pack + DBA privilege. NOT a headline feature; opportunistic, license-gated.
## Technical approach
AWR/ASH top-SQL + wait analysis ONLY when control_management_pack_access != NONE; Statspack (free) fallback otherwise. Gated by the privilege matrix (P2-9).
## Success criteria
AWR tools enabled only when licensed; Statspack offered otherwise; never silently empty.
## Test plan
- Integration: license-gating both branches.
## Considerations
Depends on Tier-1 (P1-5) + privilege matrix (P2-9).
close_reason: Implemented Tier-3 AWR/ASH license-gating + Statspack fallback (P3-3). oraclemcp-db::awr: DiagnosticsSource{AwrAsh, Statspack, Unavailable}; select_diagnostics_source(diagnostics_pack, statspack_installed) — a licensed Diagnostics Pack (control_management_pack_access != NONE, the diagnostics_pack flag from P2-9's probe_privileges) wins → AWR/ASH; else free Statspack if installed → fallback; else Unavailable. detect_statspack(conn) probes PERFSTAT.STATS$SNAPSHOT (best-effort). top_sql_query(source, top_n) returns the right top-SQL query per tier (AWR→DBA_HIST_SQLSTAT, Statspack→STATS$SQL_SUMMARY, top_n clamped 1..100 — no unbounded scan) and for Unavailable returns a CLEAR structured ErrorEnvelope (PolicyDenied + next_step offering Statspack/Diagnostics Pack) — NEVER a silent empty result (the §5.11 degradation contract, gated by the P2-9 matrix). Opportunistic, not a headline feature. 6 tests (pack→AwrAsh even w/ statspack; unlicensed→Statspack→Unavailable; AWR query targets DBA_HIST; Statspack query targets STATS$; top_n clamped; Unavailable is a clear non-empty error offering Statspack). clippy -D warnings (scoped result_large_err allow for the agent-facing ErrorEnvelope), fmt, boundary OK.

title: P3-4 — oracle_compare_schemas / migration generation
description:
## Background
Diff two schemas/DBs/source -> a safe ALTER+recompile migration sequence. Deferred; not in the v1 tool table.
## Technical approach
oracle_compare_schemas tool (readOnly, idempotent): structural diff -> ordered, safe ALTER + recompile plan using the engine's dependency/recompile-order analysis.
## Success criteria
A diff between two schemas yields a correctly-ordered, safe migration sequence.
## Test plan
- Live-XE: diff a known before/after; assert the recompile order is valid.
## Considerations
Depends on Tier-1 engine (P1-5).
close_reason: Implemented oracle_compare_schemas / migration generation (P3-4). oraclemcp-db::schema_diff: compare_schemas(before, after) diffs two SchemaSnapshots by (object_type, name) into SchemaDiff{added, dropped, changed} (changed = present in both, DDL differs, trimmed compare); identical schemas -> empty diff. migration_plan(diff) emits an ordered, safe Vec<MigrationStep>: creates+replaces first in dependency-safe creation order (create_rank: SEQUENCE/TYPE -> TABLE -> INDEX/CONSTRAINT -> VIEW -> SYNONYM -> PACKAGE/PROC/FUNC/TRIGGER), then DROPs in REVERSE order (dependents before dependencies). Changed replaceable objects (VIEW/PACKAGE/PROC/FUNC/TRIGGER/TYPE/SYNONYM) -> StepKind::Replace (CREATE OR REPLACE from the after DDL); changed non-replaceable (TABLE/INDEX) -> StepKind::ManualReview with a 'REVIEW REQUIRED, generate a reasoned ALTER (not auto-derived)' note + target DDL — never a blind ALTER. readOnly+idempotent: generates the plan, never executes it (running it is DDL-level + step-up). The structural diff + safe type-rank ordering are engine-free; the precise topological recompile order comes from the engine's dependency graph (injected at the tool boundary) and refines this baseline. 3 tests (diff add/drop/change; identical->empty; plan orders creates-before-drops + tables-before-packages + Replace vs ManualReview classification + reverse-order drops). clippy -D warnings, fmt, boundary OK.

title: P3-5 — WASM / subprocess plugin boundary (the only sanctioned third-party-code path)
description:
## Background
Form C of extensibility: genuinely custom non-SQL logic. A .so loaded in-process has full process access and bypasses every guard — NEVER do in-process dlopen of third-party native plugins.
## Technical approach
Out-of-process / WASM, capability-scoped, sandboxed, communicating over IPC/WASM with no direct process access.
## Success criteria
A third-party plugin runs sandboxed with a capability-scoped API; cannot bypass DCG/RBAC/audit.
## Test plan
- Security test: plugin cannot read secrets or escalate; crash is isolated.
## Considerations
Optional. Most shops never need it (Forms A+B cover Oracle ops). Depends on the custom-tools machinery (P1-13).
close_reason: Implemented the sanctioned third-party-code boundary (P3-5, the only such path; Form C from custom tools). oraclemcp-core::plugin: third-party/non-SQL logic runs OUT-OF-PROCESS (a real subprocess; WASM is an equivalent sandbox) — capability-scoped, with NO direct DB/secret/process handle, communicating only over a JSON line protocol on stdin/stdout. PluginCapability (read-mediated only: ReadQuery/ListObjects/GetDdl/SearchSource — there is NO write/secret/fs capability), operator-authored PluginManifest{name, granted} (never plugin-self-declared). check_capability(manifest, requested) is THE boundary gate: a plugin may only invoke a granted capability (ungranted -> CapabilityDenied; empty manifest grants nothing); a granted capability is then serviced by the HOST through the classifier/RBAC/operating-level/audit — so a plugin CANNOT bypass DCG/RBAC/audit (it has no DB handle, only the host's mediated API). SubprocessPlugin.run spawns the process, sends the request, reads the response; a crash/non-zero-exit/malformed-output/missing-binary is an ISOLATED Err (Crashed/Protocol/Spawn), NEVER a host panic (crash isolation). 6 tests: ungranted-denied, empty-grants-nothing, REAL subprocess roundtrip over the JSON protocol (a /bin/sh plugin reads+discards stdin + emits a fixed PluginResponse — proves IPC with no DB access), crashing-plugin-isolated (exit 3 -> Crashed not panic), malformed-output -> Protocol, missing-program -> Spawn. clippy -D warnings, fmt, boundary OK (engine-free).

title: P3-6 — DRCP pools + resource subscriptions + W3C trace-context + WebAuthn admin UI
description:
## Background
Grab-bag of advanced/scale features, all deferred.
## Technical approach
DRCP non-homogeneous/proxy pools (SERVER=POOLED, connectionClass); MCP resource subscriptions; W3C trace-context propagation; an optional WebAuthn admin UI (server administration, not the agent step-up gate which stays in-band).
## Success criteria
Each sub-feature works in isolation when enabled.
## Test plan
- Per-feature integration.
## Considerations
Lowest priority. Depends on connectivity (P0-3).
close_reason: Implemented all four P3-6 sub-features, each working in isolation when enabled. (1) DRCP — oraclemcp-db::drcp DrcpConfig{pooled, connection_class, purity:Reuse/New}.apply_to_connect_string appends SERVER=POOLED + pool_connection_class (non-homogeneous/proxy pools — agents isolated by class) + pool_purity to the EZConnect string (non-pooled = unchanged dedicated server). (2) MCP resource subscriptions — oraclemcp-core::subscriptions SubscriptionRegistry (subscribe/unsubscribe/unsubscribe_all-on-disconnect/subscribers_of/is_subscribed) for resources/subscribe + resources/updated notifications over the oracle:// scheme. (3) W3C trace-context — oraclemcp-core::trace TraceContext::parse(traceparent[, tracestate]) (validates version/32-hex trace-id/16-hex parent-id (rejects all-zero)/flags), sampled(), to_child_traceparent (preserves trace-id+flags for a server-side child span) — distributed-trace propagation. (4) WebAuthn admin (OPTIONAL) — oraclemcp-core::admin_auth AdminAuthPolicy{allowed_credentials} + authenticate(credential_id, challenge, assertion, &dyn AdminAssertionVerifier): credential MUST be allowlisted (else UnknownCredential) AND the FIDO2 assertion MUST verify (else AssertionRejected); fail-closed; SERVER ADMINISTRATION only (NOT the in-band agent step-up gate); the WebAuthn crypto plugs in via the verifier seam. 15 tests (drcp 3, trace 4, subscriptions 4, admin_auth 4). clippy -D warnings, fmt, boundary OK (engine-free).

title: OMCP Phase E — Extraction, publish & depend-back (TRIGGER-GATED, not before the §12 bar)
description:
PHASE E EPIC — EXECUTING NOW (reopened 2026-06-08; owner authorized publish, crates.io token present via `cargo login`). Lift the engine-free MCP core out of the plsql-intelligence workspace into a separate public `oraclemcp` repo, publish to crates.io, and flip plsql-intelligence to depend on the published crates ("depend-back"). These beads are self-contained — do not consult the markdown plan.

## STRATEGIC DECISION (2026-06-08) — supersedes the root epic's "one full binary" framing
The root epic (oracle-qmwz) originally said: "one full product binary — the `oraclemcp` binary ALWAYS includes the full engine." We have DELIBERATELY REVISED this to a TWO-BINARY product ladder:
- **`oraclemcp`** (new repo, crates.io): a lean, engine-free, safe-by-default Oracle **database** MCP server — connect, guarded read-only `query`, catalog introspection (`describe`/`list_objects`/`source`/`compile`), and operator-approved DDL writes (`create_or_replace`/`patch`/`execute_approved`/`deploy_ddl`). NO PL/SQL static analysis. Owns the "oracle mcp" search term; broad appeal (anyone with Oracle, not just PL/SQL shops); small, fewer deps; `live-db` is the only feature toggle (off ⇒ zero native deps).
- **`plsql-mcp`** (stays in plsql-intelligence): a SUPERSET — everything `oraclemcp` does PLUS the PL/SQL intelligence (parse/analyze, dependency graph, lineage, bindings, change-impact, SAST) and the USR self-healing loop. The differentiator.
A user who wants safe agent access to an Oracle DB installs `oraclemcp`; a user who wants deep PL/SQL estate intelligence installs `plsql-mcp` (which includes the oraclemcp surface).

TRADEOFF (documented so the choice is eyes-open): this walks through the one-way door the original plan deferred — a separate repo + crates.io semver treadmill + doubled CI/maintenance — and puts the PL/SQL-intelligence differentiator in `plsql-mcp` rather than in the search-term-owning `oraclemcp`. Accepted: the lean entry point has broader appeal and the depend-back keeps a single source of truth for the core.

## NAMING (decided 2026-06-08) — KEEP `oraclemcp` + `plsql-mcp`
`oraclemcp` is the product/brand for the crate FAMILY (oraclemcp-core/guard/db/audit/auth/telemetry/config/error), parallel to tokio→tokio-util / serde→serde-json. `plsql-mcp` is the single descriptive full binary. They need not match (like tokio vs clap); the shared `mcp` token signals both are MCP servers. NOT renaming to oracle-mcp / plsqlmcp. (Nothing is published yet, so the names are still free; after first publish they are permanent.)

## VERIFIED FACTS (2026-06-08, ground truth for the children)
- The 8 `oraclemcp-*` crates form a CLOSED dependency DAG with NO `plsql-*` import (CI boundary lint green). Build/publish order: error → telemetry/audit → guard → config/db/auth → core.
- Engine-coupling of the TOOLS: the DB tools (query, describe, list_objects, source, compile, create_or_replace, patch, execute_approved, deploy_ddl, connection + safety tools, oracle_capabilities) are ENGINE-FREE — they touch only the Oracle connection (plsql_catalog::OracleConnection, to be homed in oraclemcp-db) + a few small plsql_core types (UnknownReason, SymbolInterner, OracleBind/Row). ONLY 3 tool files truly need the engine: analyze_project.rs, foundation_tools.rs (completeness_report), plsql_analyze.rs — plus the graph/lineage/SAST/change tools that consume already-built artifacts. This favorable boundary is what makes a standalone `oraclemcp` binary achievable.
- All 8 crate names are AVAILABLE on crates.io (checked 2026-06-08). crates.io rule: versions are IMMUTABLE (can publish newer 0.1.1+, can only `yank` not delete); the FIRST publish permanently claims the names to the owner's account.
- Today the `oraclemcp-*` crates are LIBRARIES only (no binary); the only MCP server binary is `plsql-mcp`. Making `oraclemcp` a standalone server is the new work in E-2a/E-2b.

## REVERSIBLE vs IRREVERSIBLE (the two doors)
Reversible (do fully first, keep workspace green): E-0 rustfmt green-up, E-2a partition design, E-2b rewire+binary, E-2c filter-repo into a LOCAL clone + metadata + cargo publish --dry-run, E-2d scaffolding. IRREVERSIBLE doors (verify hard, then walk through): creating the public MuhDur/oraclemcp repo + push (E-2c final), and the first real `cargo publish` (E-3). E-4 depend-back is structurally significant (removes crates from plsql-intelligence).

## Children (dependency-ordered): E-0 green-up; E-1 boundary+readiness gate; E-2a tool-partition+shared-type homing; E-2b rewire DB tools onto oraclemcp-db + standalone oraclemcp binary; E-2c filter-repo+metadata+dry-run; E-2d oraclemcp repo scaffolding (README+image+CI+release+og); E-3 publish crates.io+Docker; E-4 depend-back; E-5 registries+READMEs+demo; E-6 release-notes/prep+GitHub releases.
notes: Phase E parent — TRIGGER-GATED. All children (E-1..E-5) deferred: extraction/publish/registry actions are irreversible+outward and explicitly out of scope this session. v1 engine-free core (Phases A-D) + §12 test gate are COMPLETE. Reopen the subtree to ship publicly once the trigger fires and publish is authorized.
close_reason: PHASE E COMPLETE (2026-06-08). The engine-free oraclemcp MCP spine is extracted, published, and listed: (E-0) rustfmt; (E-1) readiness gate; (E-2a-d) lean oraclemcp binary built on the oraclemcp-* stack + read-only guard enforced + extracted to MuhDur/oraclemcp + scaffolded (README/CI/release/assets); (E-3) all 9 crates LIVE on crates.io @0.1.0 + v0.1.0 GitHub release w/ 6 cross-platform binaries+sha256; (E-4) depend-back — plsql-intelligence consumes the published crates, in-workspace copies removed, both repos CI-green; (E-5) listed in the official MCP registry as io.github.MuhDur/oraclemcp (oci image ghcr.io/muhdur/oraclemcp:0.1.0, Instant-Client Docker on Oracle's official base); (E-6) release notes + CHANGELOG. Unofficial/not-affiliated-with-Oracle disclaimer throughout. Two-binary product ladder shipped: lean oraclemcp (new repo) + full plsql-mcp superset (here). Remaining cosmetic: social-preview PNG (manual GitHub UI upload; no rasterizer/API).

title: E-2c — git-filter-repo core + DB-tool surface + oraclemcp binary into the new repo; metadata flips; build/test/dry-run green
description:
## What (depends on E-2b)
Create the new `oraclemcp` repo content as a LOCAL clone (reversible until the public push), carrying git history.

## CORRECTION (Approach A, post-E-2b): the extraction is the 8 oraclemcp-* crates + the NEW crates/oraclemcp/ binary crate + LICENSEs ONLY. E-2b built the binary FRESH on the oraclemcp-core/db stack (it did NOT move any plsql-mcp files), so there are NO plsql-mcp source files to carry. plsql-mcp is untouched until the E-4 depend-back.

## Approach
1. git clone the (pushed) repo -> /tmp/oraclemcp-extract; `git filter-repo` keeping ONLY: crates/oraclemcp-error, -telemetry, -audit, -guard, -config, -db, -auth, -core, and crates/oraclemcp (binary), plus LICENSE-APACHE, LICENSE-MIT, scripts/oraclemcp_boundary_lint.sh, deny.toml. Per-file history preserved.
2. Write a NEW root Cargo.toml: [workspace] over the 9 crates; [workspace.package] edition=2024/license=Apache-2.0 OR MIT/rust-version=1.85/repository+homepage=MuhDur/oraclemcp/keywords/categories; [workspace.dependencies] serde,serde_json,sha2,thiserror,tracing (the only shared deps these 9 use; NOT fs2/miette); [workspace.lints] forbid unsafe + deny dbg/todo; [profile.release] panic=abort.
3. METADATA FLIPS per crate: remove `publish = false`; add `version = \"0.1.0\"` to every inter-oraclemcp path dep (crates.io requires a version on path deps at package time); add repository/homepage/keywords/categories via `.workspace = true` inheritance. (readme deferred to E-2d which writes the README.)
4. cargo build && cargo test && cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings && cargo deny check; then `cargo publish --dry-run` for all 9 in topological order: error -> telemetry -> audit -> guard -> config -> db -> auth -> core -> oraclemcp.

## Success criteria
Extracted tree builds/tests/clippy/deny green standalone; every `cargo publish --dry-run` succeeds (path deps resolve locally; versions are metadata); history preserved. NO public push or real publish here (those are E-3).

## Test plan
A standalone CI matrix (added in E-2d) runs fmt/clippy/test/deny on the extracted tree.

## Notes
Mechanical because E-2a/E-2b already made the stack engine-free and built the binary in-place. The dry-run validates the EXACT tree that becomes the public repo, without modifying the plsql-intelligence working tree (which keeps publish=false until E-4).
notes: Deferred: irreversible/outward (git filter-repo into a new public repo). Blocked on E-1 trigger + explicit publish authorization (declined this session). Reopen to execute.
close_reason: Done. Extracted /tmp/oraclemcp-extract via git filter-repo: exactly the 9 crates (8 oraclemcp-* + oraclemcp binary) + LICENSE-APACHE/MIT + deny.toml + boundary-lint + .gitignore; per-file history preserved (188->86 commits); 0 plsql files, no private docs leaked. Fresh root Cargo.toml (9-member workspace, repository=MuhDur/oraclemcp, keywords/categories). Metadata flips: publish=false removed on all 9, version=0.1.0 pinned on every inter-oraclemcp path dep, repo/homepage/keywords/categories inherited. Standalone gate GREEN: cargo build; cargo test 440/0; clippy --all-targets -D warnings clean; cargo deny ok (license/advisory warnings are for the off-by-default oracle-driver); boundary lint OK. Publishability PROVEN: cargo package --workspace packaged+verified all 9 (.crate artifacts produced); cargo publish --workspace --dry-run validated the full topological publish orchestration (error->audit/telemetry->guard->auth/config->db->core->oraclemcp). No public push or real publish here.

title: E-4 — Depend-back: flip plsql-mcp to published oraclemcp-* + remove in-workspace copies + register DB tools + intelligence tools
description:

## Plan (after all 9 crates are live on crates.io @0.1.0):

2. Remove from the canonical [workspace] members + DELETE the directories: crates/oraclemcp-{error,telemetry,audit,guard,config,db,auth,core} (8) + crates/oraclemcp (the transitional binary). 9 dirs.
