title: Canonicalize Stage A marker scan so whitespace/comments cannot split EXECUTE IMMEDIATE / PRAGMA AUTONOMOUS_TRANSACTION
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/oraclemcp-guard/src/classifier.rs:167 — verdict=keep, severity=high
**Impact:** An untrusted agent can downgrade an opaque, unanalyzable dynamic-SQL or autonomous-transaction PL/SQL block from Forbidden (never dispatchable) to Guarded (dispatchable after a ReadWrite step-up, or outright Allow in a ReadWrite session) by inserting a comment, extra space, tab, or newline between the two keywords of a multi-word side-effect marker — letting an EXECUTE IMMEDIATE whose embedded statement could be DROP/GRANT/arbitrary DML slip past the guard's hard fail-closed boundary.
**Evidence:** classifier.rs:158 `let upper = sql.trim_start().to_ascii_uppercase();` (uppercase ONLY — not whitespace-collapsed, not comment-stripped). classifier.rs:167 `let dangerous = PLSQL_SIDE_EFFECT_MARKERS.iter().any(|m| upper.contains(m));` — literal substring scan. Markers at classifier.rs:120 `"EXECUTE IMMEDIATE"` and :128 `"PRAGMA AUTONOMOUS_TRANSACTION"` are single-space literals. classify() at :737-740 returns forbidden_decision (required_level=None) when dangerous, else falls through to :748-757 returning Guarded/required_level=ReadWrite for a balanced block. levels.rs:266-271 maps required=None -> Blocked{Forbidden} (never dispatchable); :278-281 maps a satisfiable required level -> Allow or RequireStepUp.\n\nEmpirical run (CARGO_TARGET_DIR=$PWD/target cargo test -p oraclemcp-guard against Classifier::default(), tmp integration test, --nocapture):\nCASE   control_single_space: danger=Fo
**Repro:** Trigger: `Classifier::default().classify("BEGIN EXECUTE/**/IMMEDIATE 'DELETE FROM orders'; END;")` returns danger=Guarded, required_level=Some(ReadWrite). The control `BEGIN EXECUTE IMMEDIATE 'DELETE FROM orders'; END;` returns danger=Forbidden, required_level=None. Same divergence for double-space, tab (\t), newline (\n) between EXECUTE and IMMEDIATE, and for `DECLARE PRAGMA/**/AUTONOMOUS_TRANSACTION; BEGIN COMMIT; END;`. X (comment/whitespace-perturbed dangerous block) -> Y (Guarded instead of Forbidden) directly observed. Single-token markers (DBMS_SQL/UTL_FILE/...) are unaffected because they contain no internal space.
**Suggested fix:** Before the `dangerous` computation in stage_a, derive a canonicalized scan string: strip PL/SQL comments (`--` to end-of-line and `/* ... */`) and collapse all whitespace runs to a single ASCII space, then uppercase — `let scan = strip_plsql_comments(&upper).split_whitespace().collect::<Vec<_>>().join(" ");` — and run the marker `.contains` against `scan` instead of `upper`. More robustly, reuse the OracleDialect tokenizer already used by analyze_batch: drop whitespace/comment tokens, uppercase bare Word tokens, and scan the token stream for adjacent `EXECUTE`+`IMMEDIATE` and `PRAGMA`+`AUTONOMOUS_TRANSACTION` sequences plus the single-token markers. Lock it in by adding the comment/double-space/tab/newline evasion variants (for both multi-word markers) to tests/adversarial_corpus.rs.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 0369191894), re-verified on main. Root cause: Stage A marker scan ran a literal substring `.contains` over the merely-uppercased source (classifier.rs:167 `upper.contains(m)`), so a comment/space/tab/newline wedged between the two keywords of a multi-word marker (EXECUTE IMMEDIATE / PRAGMA AUTONOMOUS_TRANSACTION) split it and downgraded a Forbidden dynamic-SQL/autonomous-transaction block to Guarded. Fix: added `canonical_marker_scan()` which tokenizes with OracleDialect (string/q'[]'/quoted-identifier literals stay single token

title: Use constant-time comparison for CiToken secret in CiToken::authorizes
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/oraclemcp-guard/src/stepup.rs:130 — verdict=keep, severity=low
**Impact:** A real CI escalation credential is compared with non-constant-time `&str ==`, leaking per-byte match position via timing; in principle an attacker who can repeatedly call the escalate tool could recover the secret and non-interactively elevate the session level (ReadWrite/DDL/Admin) without human confirmation. In practice the timing delta is swamped by MCP transport/scheduler jitter, so the concrete exploit is unrealizable — the real value is consistency/defense-in-depth hardening to match the codebase's own constant-time standard.
**Evidence:** stepup.rs:127-131 — `pub fn authorizes(&self, presented_secret: &str, target: OperatingLevel) -> bool { !self.deadline.is_expired() && presented_secret == self.secret && target <= self.scope }`. CiToken doc (stepup.rs:106-108): "A real credential to protect — unlike the self-issued allow-once token, this auto-approves up to its `scope` ceiling." Data flow: session_tool.rs:282-310 forwards MCP `SessionAction::Escalate { ci_secret }` unmodified into `resolve_with_ci_token(&cid, token, secret)`; stepup.rs:227-239 passes `presented_secret` straight into `token.authorizes(...)`. Convention sites: init_token.rs:57-82 (`StdioAuthPolicy::validate` uses `constant_time_eq` for a caller-presented shared init token over the MCP transport, with a doc comment on the length-leak tradeoff); oauth_rs.rs:253-263 (`constant_time_eq` for HMAC). `rg subtle|constant_time crates/oraclemcp-guard/` returned noth
**Repro:** Triggering path: agent invokes oracle_session(Escalate, target_level=ReadWrite, challenge_id=<cid>, ci_secret=X) over MCP. X reaches `presented_secret == self.secret` at stepup.rs:130 unmodified. Because `&str` PartialEq compares bytes left-to-right and short-circuits on the first mismatch, the return time correlates with the length of the matching prefix — the side channel exists. Impossibility-of-practical-exploit proof (severity downgrade): the per-byte short-circuit difference is on the order of nanoseconds and is dominated by MCP/stdio transport latency, JSON (de)serialization, the stepup `Mutex` lock, and OS scheduler jitter (milliseconds); additionally each attempt must resolve agains
**Suggested fix:** Replace `presented_secret == self.secret` at stepup.rs:130 with a constant-time byte comparison matching the codebase convention. Add a private `fn constant_time_eq(a: &[u8], b: &[u8]) -> bool` to the guard crate identical to init_token.rs:73 / oauth_rs.rs:254 (length check, XOR-accumulate over zipped bytes, return `diff == 0`) and call `constant_time_eq(presented_secret.as_bytes(), self.secret.as_bytes())`, keeping the `&& !self.deadline.is_expired() && target <= self.scope` clauses. Better: factor the duplicated `constant_time_eq` (now in three crates) into one shared helper, or adopt the vetted `subtle` crate's `ConstantTimeEq`. Add a regression note in tests/token_security.rs mirroring the init-token constant-time assertion.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 0369191894), re-verified on main. Root cause: CiToken::authorizes compared the real CI escalation secret with non-constant-time `presented_secret == self.secret` (stepup.rs:130), leaking matching-prefix length via per-byte short-circuit timing — inconsistent with the codebase's own constant-time standard (oraclemcp-core::init_token, oraclemcp-auth::oauth_rs). Fix: added a private `constant_time_eq(a,b)` to stepup.rs (length check + XOR-accumulate over zipped bytes, identical to the other two crates) and call `constant_time_eq(pr

title: Reuse escaped `job` binding in build_deploy_plan poll_sql (symmetric ''-escaping) + assert even quote-count on poll_sql in test
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-mcp/src/execute_approved.rs:227 — verdict=keep, severity=low
**Impact:** If a caller passes a job_name containing a single quote, build_deploy_plan emits a malformed poll_sql (literal closes early: WHERE job_name = 'x'y') while submit_block stays balanced — a self-inflicted broken run-status poll, not a security/injection or cross-schema vector. In practice mostly moot: Oracle scheduler job-name identifiers cannot contain a bare quote so CREATE_JOB itself would reject the name, and no runtime currently executes poll_sql.
**Evidence:** execute_approved.rs:220 `let job = job_name.replace('\'', "''");` — escaped binding. Line 222 submit_block interpolates `'{job}'` (escaped). Lines 226-228 poll_sql interpolates RAW `WHERE job_name = '{job_name}'`. Test gap confirmed at line 525: `assert_eq!(plan.submit_block.matches('\'').count() % 2, 0);` — only submit_block, never poll_sql. dispatch.rs:347-352 `struct DeployDdlArgs { job_name: String, ddl_bytes: String }` with only `#[serde(deny_unknown_fields)]` — no identifier/regex validation. dispatch.rs:306-311 deploy_ddl arm: `let _args: DeployDdlArgs = parse_args(...)?; Ok(DispatchOutcome::RuntimeStateRequired(RuntimeKind::LiveConnection))` — does NOT call build_deploy_plan. `rg poll_sql|DeployDdlPlan crates/` outside execute_approved.rs returns only the lib.rs re-export (lib.rs:94-95) — no production consumer of poll_sql. Crate builds and 3 build_deploy_plan tests pass (test re
**Repro:** Triggering input: build_deploy_plan("x'y", "CREATE TABLE t (a NUMBER)"). Output Y: poll_sql contains the unbalanced literal `WHERE job_name = 'x'y'` (3 single quotes, odd) while submit_block is correctly ''-doubled (12 quotes, even). Compiled and ran the exact format! logic standalone; printed: submit_block even=true, poll_sql even=false. Disproof of higher severity: same caller controls both DDL and job_name, poll_sql is a fixed SELECT against a read-only system view (USER_SCHEDULER_JOB_RUN_DETAILS), so no privilege/trust boundary crossed; and a single quote is not a legal Oracle scheduler job-name identifier char, so CREATE_JOB would reject the name regardless — the operation fails uniform
**Suggested fix:** In build_deploy_plan, interpolate the already-escaped `job` instead of raw `job_name` in poll_sql: change `WHERE job_name = '{job_name}'` (lines 226-228) to use `'{job}'`. This makes the escaping symmetric with submit_block. Extend build_deploy_plan_escapes_single_quotes (line 518) to also assert `plan.poll_sql.matches('\'').count() % 2 == 0` so the regression is covered. Optionally add identifier validation for job_name at the dispatch boundary (DeployDdlArgs, dispatch.rs:349) to fail fast on non-identifier names rather than emitting SQL the runtime would reject.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 0f4928de20), re-verified on main. Root cause: build_deploy_plan (crates/plsql-mcp/src/execute_approved.rs:~226) interpolated the RAW job_name into poll_sql (WHERE job_name = '{job_name}') while submit_block used the already-''-escaped `job`. A job_name with a single quote produced an unbalanced poll literal (WHERE job_name = 'x'y'). Fix: interpolate the escaped `job` into poll_sql, symmetric with submit_block. Low severity as the bead notes (Oracle scheduler job-name identifiers cannot contain a bare quote and no runtime current

title: Split <expr> runs on || (not just string literals) so adjacent-|| concatenations don't mis-tag an unsanitised interpolation as DBMS_ASSERT-bounded
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-symbols/src/dynamic_sql.rs:260 — verdict=keep, severity=low
**Impact:** An agent calling the MCP `dynamic_sql_evidence` foundation tool on a dynamic statement that adjacently concatenates an asserted name with a raw identifier (no quoted literal between them) receives `expr_interpolations:[{sanitised:true}]`, falsely reporting the unsanitised `p_raw` run as DBMS_ASSERT-bounded. The downstream confidence score is Medium/"object set is bounded" — but that scorer feeds nothing in production, so there is NO fail-open in the actual injection detector (SEC001) or any wired audit; the harm is a wrong per-interpolation sanitisation flag in the foundation-tool output an agent may read.
**Evidence:** Walker (dynamic_sql.rs:204-248): `current_expr` flushes a `<expr>` run only inside the `c == '\''` branch (line 222 `current_expr_open`), never on `||`. `run_is_dbms_assert` (dynamic_sql.rs:260-262) = `run.to_ascii_uppercase().contains("DBMS_ASSERT.")`. Empirical repro via a throwaway integration test (since removed; tree clean of my artifact) using the exact claimed input `EXECUTE IMMEDIATE 'SELECT * FROM ' || DBMS_ASSERT.SIMPLE_SQL_NAME(p_tab) || p_raw || ' WHERE 1=1';` printed:
  fragments = ["SELECT * FROM ", "<expr>", " WHERE 1=1"]
  expr_interpolations = [ExprInterpolation { sanitised: true }]
  confidence = Medium
  explanation = "interpolated dynamic SQL, all 1 interpolation(s) routed through DBMS_ASSERT — object set is bounded by the asserted names"
CONTROL (literal separator, the existing qm3q.21 test shape) = [{sanitised:true},{sanitised:false}] => Low. cargo built/ran cleanly
**Repro:** TRIGGERS: recognise_dynamic_sql("EXECUTE IMMEDIATE 'SELECT * FROM ' || DBMS_ASSERT.SIMPLE_SQL_NAME(p_tab) || p_raw || ' WHERE 1=1';", "p:1") yields expr_interpolations=[{sanitised:true}] (should be 2 entries, the p_raw run sanitised:false) and score_dynamic_sql_edge => Medium "bounded" (should be Low "partial/unsanitised"). DISPROOF of the high-severity fail-open: the scoring functions have no production consumer (grep above), OpaqueDynamic edges exist only in tests, unsafe_paths/Trust Block never receive this confidence, and SEC001 detects the injection via an independent taint path — so no live injection-audit path drops or downgrades the finding.
**Suggested fix:** Two-part fix in dynamic_sql.rs: (1) in extract_execute_immediate, also break the non-literal `current_expr` run on the `||` operator (emit one ExprInterpolation per ||-delimited sub-expression), not only at `'` boundaries, so per-interpolation coverage reflects each substituted identifier; (2) harden run_is_dbms_assert so it returns true only when every non-trivial (non-empty, non-bare-literal) ||-piece itself contains a `DBMS_ASSERT.` call, rather than the any-mention contains() check. Add a regression test mirroring partial_dbms_assert_coverage_is_low_not_medium but WITHOUT a quoted literal between the asserted name and the raw identifier (the adjacent-|| case). Note for the bead: this is a recogniser-correctness / honest-evidence fix surfaced through the MCP dynamic_sql_evidence tool; the claimed downstream "unsafe_paths drop / Trust Block bounded" fail-open does NOT exist today becau

### crates/plsql-symbols/src/dynamic_sql.rs:225 — verdict=keep, severity=low
**Impact:** A reviewer querying the MCP `dynamic_sql_evidence` tool on `EXECUTE IMMEDIATE 'GRANT ' || DBMS_ASSERT.SIMPLE_SQL_NAME(p_priv) || p_target` sees expr_interpolations=[{sanitised:true}] — one placeholder marked clean — which hides that p_target flows in unsanitised, falsely reassuring them the dynamic statement is bounded. (Does NOT miss the SEC001 critical injection finding, which uses an independent taint path.)
**Evidence:** extract_execute_immediate only flushes current_expr into an ExprInterpolation on a string-literal opening quote (dynamic_sql.rs:222-228) or at end-of-body (243-248); it never splits on `||`. run_is_dbms_assert (260-262) is a bare substring check: `run.to_ascii_uppercase().contains("DBMS_ASSERT.")`. PoC run (added to crate, then reverted; `rg ZZZ_poc` now CLEAN) of input `EXECUTE IMMEDIATE 'GRANT ' || DBMS_ASSERT.SIMPLE_SQL_NAME(p_priv) || p_target;` printed: `POC fragments = ["GRANT ", "<expr>"]`; `POC interps = [ExprInterpolation { sanitised: true }]`; `POC level = Medium`; `POC explain = Some("...all 1 interpolation(s) routed through DBMS_ASSERT — object set is bounded by the asserted names")`. medium_or_low (dynamic_sql_confidence.rs:90) returns Medium because total==1 && sanitised==1. The documented invariant (dynamic_sql.rs:45-56, 83-89: "one entry per <expr> placeholder ... true me
**Repro:** Trigger: recognise_dynamic_sql("EXECUTE IMMEDIATE 'GRANT ' || DBMS_ASSERT.SIMPLE_SQL_NAME(p_priv) || p_target;", "p:1") yields expr_interpolations==[{sanitised:true}] (verified). Existing regression test partial_dbms_assert_coverage_is_low_not_medium (dynamic_sql_confidence.rs:194-233) does NOT cover this: its sample `... || DBMS_ASSERT.SIMPLE_SQL_NAME(p_tab) || ' WHERE c=''' || p_raw || '''';` inserts a literal between the asserted and raw runs, forcing two interpolations. Scope correction: score_dynamic_sql_edge / dynamic_sql_confidence_level / enrich_dynamic_sql / EnrichedDynamicSql have NO production caller (rg across crates/ shows only intra-module + tests/zzz_repro_finding5.rs); the on
**Suggested fix:** Preferred (option 1 in the finding): in extract_execute_immediate, when flushing current_expr at lines 222-228 and 243-248, split the run on top-level `||` (respecting nested parens/quotes so DBMS_ASSERT(...) args are not split) and emit one ExprInterpolation per operand, tagging each with run_is_dbms_assert(operand). Then the PoC yields [sanitised:true, sanitised:false] and medium_or_low drops to Low. Cheaper alternative (option 2): make run_is_dbms_assert conservative — sanitised:true only if EVERY top-level `||` operand in the run is itself a DBMS_ASSERT.<fn>(...) call. Add a regression test mirroring the no-separator PoC asserting Low (not Medium) and an explanation without "bounded". (Option 2 alone leaves the per-interpolation COUNT inaccurate for downstream consumers, so option 1 is better.)

### crates/plsql-symbols/src/dynamic_sql.rs:226 — verdict=keep, severity=low
**Impact:** The MCP `dynamic_sql_evidence` tool reports an injectable `||`-adjacent interpolation as `sanitised: true` (and the dormant confidence scorer would stamp it Medium "object set is bounded"), so an analyst/consumer reading the advisory evidence sees an unsanitised value rated safer than it is; the primary SEC001 injection verdict is unaffected (it uses live taint), so no real injection goes unreported.
**Evidence:** extract_execute_immediate (crates/plsql-symbols/src/dynamic_sql.rs:222-248) only closes an <expr> run at a string-literal quote; line 237 pushes every non-literal char (incl `||`) into one buffer. run_is_dbms_assert (line 260-262): `run.to_ascii_uppercase().contains("DBMS_ASSERT.")` — whole-run substring test. medium_or_low (dynamic_sql_confidence.rs:90-95) stamps Medium when `total>0 && sanitised==total`.
Ran the real crate via a throwaway integration test (plsql_symbols::recognise_dynamic_sql + score_dynamic_sql_edge):
  ADJACENT body `'SELECT * FROM ' || foo || DBMS_ASSERT.SIMPLE_SQL_NAME(p) || ' WHERE 1=1'`:
    fragments=["SELECT * FROM ","<expr>"," WHERE 1=1"]
    expr_interps=[ExprInterpolation{sanitised:true}]
    level=Medium  explanation="interpolated dynamic SQL, all 1 interpolation(s) routed through DBMS_ASSERT — object set is bounded by the asserted names"
  CONTROL (literal
**Repro:** Triggering input: recognise_dynamic_sql("EXECUTE IMMEDIATE 'SELECT * FROM ' || foo || DBMS_ASSERT.SIMPLE_SQL_NAME(p) || ' WHERE 1=1';", site) -> ev.expr_interpolations == [ExprInterpolation{sanitised:true}] (length 1), and score_dynamic_sql_edge(&ev).level == Medium with "object set is bounded by the asserted names". `foo` is unsanitised yet the single collapsed run is tagged sanitised=true because run_is_dbms_assert finds "DBMS_ASSERT." anywhere in the run. Control with a string literal between the terms yields [false,true] -> Low(partial), proving the run-splitting (not the scorer) is the defect. Existing regression test partial_dbms_assert_coverage_is_low_not_medium uses a literal separat
**Suggested fix:** At the two run-close sites (dynamic_sql.rs:224-226 and 245-247) split `current_expr` on "||" into trimmed non-empty chunks and push one ExprInterpolation per chunk, tagging each via run_is_dbms_assert on that chunk alone (the single "<expr>" fragment placeholder may stay since shape comparison is count-independent). Then the claimed body yields interps [foo=false, DBMS_ASSERT=true] and medium_or_low takes the `sanitised<total` branch -> Low(partial). Skip empty/whitespace-only chunks from leading/trailing `||`. Add a regression test using the `||`-adjacent (no intervening literal) shape asserting expr_interpolations.iter().any(|e| !e.sanitised) and Low/"partial" verdict, to lock the gap the existing literal-separated test misses. Also update run_is_dbms_assert's doc comment which currently asserts an invariant ("the run is one `||`-delimited expression chunk") the code does not uphold.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 60e7546cf5), re-verified on main. Root-cause fix in crates/plsql-symbols/src/dynamic_sql.rs: EXECUTE IMMEDIATE non-literal runs are now split on top-level || (paren-depth aware) so each substituted operand gets its own <expr> placeholder + ExprInterpolation, tagged via run_is_dbms_assert(operand) individually. Previously a run that adjacently concatenated an asserted identifier with a raw one (no intervening string literal, e.g. `DBMS_ASSERT.SIMPLE_SQL_NAME(p_tab) || p_raw`) collapsed into one <expr> tagged sanitised:true via a

title: Fail-closed guard: reject non-identifier routine/param names before emit (BINDING_INVALID_IDENTIFIER), covering both static pub-fn path and dynamic format! path
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-bindgen/src/emit.rs:148 — verdict=keep, severity=low
**Impact:** A hostile or upstream-buggy BindingPlan whose routine/parameter name is not a legal Rust identifier makes plsql-bindgen exit 0 with no diagnostic while emitting uncompilable Rust (`pub fn f", DROP TABLE users; --(...)`), silently breaking the consumer's build and defeating the crate's documented "valid Rust + typed BINDING_* failure on bad input" contract; the --robot-json audit only records generated_bytes, never compilability, so CI does not catch it. No runtime SQL injection at line 148 (the SQL block is Debug-escaped); harm is a fail-loud compile break, not data corruption.
**Evidence:** emit.rs:148 `let _ = write!(out, "pub fn {}(executor: &mut impl OracleExecutor", r.name);` and emit.rs:151 `let _ = write!(out, ", {}: {}", p.name, rust_type_sig(p));` — both write unvalidated Strings into Rust identifier positions. main.rs:212 `let mut plan: BindingPlan = match serde_json::from_str(&json)` then main.rs:228 `let generated = emit_wrappers(&plan);` — untrusted input, no validation. `rg is_ident|valid_ident|sanitize|validate|BINDING_INVALID crates/plsql-bindgen/` → no validation helper. emit_wrappers callers: only main.rs:228 (rest are #[test]). Live repro against built binary, EXIT=0, empty stderr:
  pub fn f", DROP TABLE users; --(executor: &mut impl OracleExecutor, p_a => :99 -- broken: i64) -> Result<(), ExecutionError> {
      RoutineArg::In(BindValue::Int(p_a => :99 -- broken)),
      let __out = executor.call_routine("BEGIN hr.pkg.f\", DROP TABLE users; --(:1); END;"
**Repro:** Trigger X: pipe a BindingPlan with routine name `f", DROP TABLE users; --` and IN param name `p_a => :99 -- broken` into `plsql-bindgen --input`. Output Y: exit 0, no stderr, generated `pub fn f", DROP TABLE users; --(...)` which rustc rejects ("prefix `f` is unknown"). The cited line 148 is the function-name break; line 151 is the param break. The verifier's downgrade is confirmed: line 148's SQL block is emitted with `{block:?}` (Debug), so the quote is escaped to `\"` and cannot break out — no injection there. The genuine token-injection vector is the DYNAMIC path at emit.rs:392 (and the unescaped `{name}` at 484/504/512/524): feeding name `inj"); evil("x` with a defaulted param emits `le
**Suggested fix:** Add a `fn rust_ident_problem(s: &str) -> Option<&'static str>` (Some when empty, leading digit, any char outside [A-Za-z0-9_], or a Rust keyword) and, at the top of emit_routine before line 148, check r.name and every p.name; on failure emit a well-formed typed-error body — derive a deterministic safe stub identifier for the `pub fn` signature, return `Err(ExecutionError { code: "BINDING_INVALID_IDENTIFIER".to_string(), message: ... })` reporting the original name, and return early — exactly mirroring the BINDING_DEGENERATE_FUNCTION path. Crucially route the SAME guard so it also protects the dynamic path's `{name}`/`r.name` interpolations at emit.rs:392/484/504/512/524, which is the higher-impact (true token-injection) site. Add a test parallel to degenerate_function_emits_typed_error_not_panic asserting names with spaces/quotes/`=>` yield a BINDING_INVALID_IDENTIFIER body (compilable R

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 3d2d055350), re-verified on main. Same root-cause fix as oracle-rwjl.7 (the two beads overlap): the rust_ident_problem helper rejects empty / leading-digit / non-[A-Za-z0-9_] / Rust-keyword names; safe_stub_ident derives a unique stub per routine index so two distinct invalid routines never collide. Covers the higher-impact dynamic-path token-injection site as well as the static pub-fn/param positions.

title: Add single-probe gate (or soften docs) to CircuitBreaker HalfOpen before wiring into dispatch
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/oraclemcp-core/src/resilience.rs:108 — verdict=defense-in-depth, severity=low
**Impact:** None today — the circuit breaker has no production caller (only unit tests + tests/chaos.rs), so the claimed "probe stampede against a fragile Oracle" cannot occur; the issue is a latent doc/behavior divergence to fix before the breaker is wired into live dispatch.
**Evidence:** resilience.rs:110-124 — `match inner.state { CircuitState::Closed | CircuitState::HalfOpen => true, CircuitState::Open => { ... if elapsed >= self.cooldown { inner.state = CircuitState::HalfOpen; true } else { false } } }`. The `Inner` struct (lines 76-80) has only `consecutive_failures`, `state`, `opened_at` — no probe-in-flight counter, so every caller observing HalfOpen returns true. Doc at line 72: "One trial request is allowed to test recovery". `on_failure` (line 140) re-trips on any HalfOpen failure, bounding over-admission.
Workspace grep (rg 'CircuitBreaker|allow_request' --type rust .): only crates/oraclemcp-core/src/resilience.rs (def+unit tests), src/lib.rs:39 (re-export), tests/chaos.rs (test caller). `rg 'resilience|breaker|circuit' crates/oraclemcp-core/src/server.rs crates/oraclemcp-db/src/*.rs` → ZERO hits, confirming not wired into dispatch.
Build: `cargo test -p oracle
**Repro:** Behavior is real: with cb = CircuitBreaker::new(1, Duration::ZERO), trip it to Open via on_failure(), then call allow_request() once (flips Open->HalfOpen, returns true) — a second and third allow_request() also return true because the arm `Closed | HalfOpen => true` admits every caller while state stays HalfOpen. But the harmful end-to-end failure (N agent calls stampede Oracle) is UNREPRODUCIBLE: no production path gates requests through allow_request() — server.rs and oraclemcp-db contain zero references. The breaker is Phase-2 scaffolding (file header: "plan §10; bead P2-RESIL"). Multiple HalfOpen probes is also a recognized design (resilience4j permittedNumberOfCallsInHalfOpenState), so
**Suggested fix:** Before wiring the breaker into the live dispatch path, reconcile prose and behavior. Cheapest correct fix: soften the HalfOpen doc (line 72) and CircuitBreaker doc (line 83) to state that recovery probing may admit concurrent requests (matches the current first-result-decides impl and resilience4j-style designs). If single-probe semantics are actually wanted, add `probe_in_flight: bool` to `Inner`, set it true on the Open->HalfOpen flip (line 118) and reject (return false) concurrent HalfOpen callers when it is already set, then clear it in both on_success and on_failure; add a concurrency test asserting a second allow_request returns false while a probe is outstanding. No urgency: there is no production caller today.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 619012a5cb), re-verified on main. Implemented the single-probe gate (the stronger of the two suggested fixes) so the CircuitBreaker HalfOpen behavior matches its documented 'one trial request' contract instead of softening the docs. Root cause: in crates/oraclemcp-core/src/resilience.rs the old allow_request() arm `CircuitState::Closed | CircuitState::HalfOpen => true` admitted EVERY caller while in HalfOpen — a latent probe stampede before the breaker is wired into live dispatch. Fix: added `probe_in_flight: bool` to Inner; set

title: Harden verb-prefix matching in dead text-scanner classify_statement (real twin lives in plsql-ir stmt.rs)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-parser-antlr/src/lower/mod.rs:680 — verdict=defense-in-depth, severity=low
**Impact:** None at the cited location — classify_statement in lower/mod.rs is dead with respect to the call/flow/DML pipeline, so no Calls edge is dropped and no phantom DML is emitted from it. The same verb-prefix bug DOES bite in a different file (plsql-ir/src/stmt.rs:501), where an IF/LOOP body line like `update_stats(p_id);` is misclassified as Statement::Sql{Update}, dropping its Calls edge and minting a phantom Reads/Writes table edge.
**Evidence:** cargo build -p plsql-ir -p plsql-engine: "Finished `dev`" (code compiles).

CITED FILE — crates/plsql-parser-antlr/src/lower/mod.rs:679-687 (the claim is locally accurate):
  for verb in ["SELECT","INSERT","UPDATE","DELETE","MERGE"] { if u.starts_with(verb) { return AstStatement::Sql{...} } }
with the `:=` branch at 703 and the statement-level Call branch at 711-722 AFTER it. The whole-word helper keyword_at (line 625) exists but is not used here.

BUT this classifier is dead for the pipeline:
1. lower_source returns `body_statements: Vec::new()` (mod.rs:50), so its classify_statement output never reaches the engine.
2. The live backend Antlr4RustBackend::parse (backend.rs:309) builds the AST via lower_parse_tree (tree_lower.rs:60), which populates body_statements through lower_statement (tree_lower.rs:510) using REAL grammar nodes: stmt.assignment_statement() (524), sql_s.data_manipulat
**Repro:** Disproof of the cited-location impact: classify_statement (lower/mod.rs:645) is only invoked by lower::lower_statement_body (mod.rs:521), whose sole callers are tests (mod.rs:1817, 1976). lower_source hardwires body_statements=Vec::new() (mod.rs:50); the engine's body extraction (lib.rs:947-962) pairs ast.body_statements by span and otherwise falls back to plsql_ir::lower_statement_body — neither route ever reads classify_statement's AstStatement output. The live backend classifies via grammar nodes (tree_lower.rs:510-639), where `update_stats(p_id);` matches stmt.call_statement() -> AstStatement::Call and `update_count := p_user;` matches stmt.assignment_statement() -> AstStatement::Assignm
**Suggested fix:** Cited file (defense-in-depth only, classifier is dead): in crates/plsql-parser-antlr/src/lower/mod.rs gate the verb loop (680) and the NULL/RAISE/RETURN/EXECUTE-IMMEDIATE/IF/LOOP prefixes (652-701) on keyword_at(u, 0, kw) instead of bare u.starts_with(kw), reusing the existing whole-word helper at mod.rs:625. The actually-impactful fix is the twin in crates/plsql-ir/src/stmt.rs:453-538: replace the bare trimmed.starts_with(verb) verb loop (501) and the NULL/COMMIT/RAISE/RETURN/EXIT prefixes (456-491) with a whole-word boundary check so `update_stats(p_id)` reaches the statement-level Call fallthrough (Unrecognized) and `update_count := p_user` reaches the `:=` Assignment branch (528). Add regression tests asserting plsql_ir::lower_statement_body("update_stats(p_id);") yields a call site and ("update_count := p_user;") yields Statement::Assignment. File the bead against stmt.rs as the pri

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 65d2916244), re-verified on main. Hardened the leading-keyword classifier in plsql-parser-antlr/src/lower/mod.rs:classify_statement (the cited, defense-in-depth location). Replaced every bare `u.starts_with(kw)` prefix check with the existing whole-word helper `keyword_at(u, 0, kw)` for NULL, RAISE, RETURN, EXECUTE IMMEDIATE, the DML verb loop (SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE/MERGE), IF, and LOOP/FOR/WHILE. Without word-boundaring, an assignment or call whose target/callee merely begins with a verb word (e.g. `DELETED_FLAG := 'Y';`,

title: coverage_report keys per-routine state by name, miscounting overloaded PL/SQL packages
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-bindgen/src/doctor.rs:79 — verdict=defense-in-depth, severity=low
**Impact:** If a consumer calls the public coverage_report API on a BindingPlan for an overloaded PL/SQL package (legal duplicate routine names), the doctor report misreports skipped/clean/emit_percent and lists duplicate names in skipped_routines_sample; purely a report-accuracy cosmetic issue, no codegen or safety impact, and no in-repo caller currently exercises the overloaded path.
**Evidence:** doctor.rs:79-82 keys state by name: `let mut by_routine: BTreeMap<&str, RoutineState> = BTreeMap::new(); for routine in &plan.routines { by_routine.insert(routine.name.as_str(), RoutineState::default()); }`. Read-back loop doctor.rs:100-113 iterates `&plan.routines` once per binding and re-reads the shared name-keyed state. lib.rs:62-63 documents `name` as "Subprogram name as it appears in the package". overload.rs:1-8 confirms PL/SQL overloading produces duplicate names; `fn disambiguate_overloads(routines: &[RoutineBinding]) -> Vec<String>` (overload.rs:67) returns a PARALLEL Vec and takes &[..] (cannot mutate name). `rg disambiguate_overloads crates/` shows callers only in overload.rs tests + the lib.rs:34 re-export — NO production caller. emit.rs:148 writes `r.name` directly. `rg coverage_report crates/` shows the only non-doctor callers are tests/xe_roundtrip.rs:209 (CI gate) and th
**Repro:** Trigger: build a BindingPlan with routines = [hire, hire, list] and diagnostics = [Skip targeting "hire"], then call coverage_report. The two "hire" bindings collapse to one name-keyed RoutineState (has_skip=true), and the read-back loop counts BOTH as skipped. Reproduced standalone (mirror of doctor.rs logic): reports skipped=2, emitted_clean=1, emit_percent=33, skipped_routines_sample=["hire","hire"], versus the per-binding truth skipped=1, emitted_clean=2, emit_percent=66. NOTE the finding's claim that "emitted+skipped==total breaks" is FALSE: the read-back loop tallies once per binding, so the invariant always holds (repro prints `true`); the genuine defects are the skewed clean/skip spl
**Suggested fix:** Key per-routine state per binding index instead of per name: build a Vec<RoutineState> of length plan.routines.len() plus a name->indices map, fan each name-targeted diagnostic out to all bindings sharing that name, and tally each index exactly once so skipped/clean/emit_percent and the sample reflect per-binding reality (dedup the sample if duplicate names are undesirable). The finding's full sketch is reasonable, but drop the incorrect "invariant breaks" justification — the sum already equals routines_total. Add a regression test with two same-named overloads where only one carries a Skip. Lower priority since no active caller exercises the overloaded path.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 3d2d055350), re-verified on main. Rewrote coverage_report (crates/plsql-bindgen/src/doctor.rs) to key per-routine state by binding INDEX (Vec<RoutineState>) plus a name->indices map; each name-targeted diagnostic fans out to every binding sharing that name and each index is tallied exactly once (emitted+skipped==total invariant preserved). skipped_routines_sample is now deduped. Empirically the clean/skip counts match the old name-keyed logic; the genuine observable defect fixed is the duplicate name in the sample (old: ["hire",

title: Fix UTF-8 char-boundary panic in replace_case_insensitive fast path (content-driven DoS in sanitize)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-mcp/src/query.rs:337 — verdict=keep, severity=high
**Impact:** A single crafted VARCHAR2/CLOB cell from a hostile/compromised database (e.g. value "İuser: Ω" with U+0130 + U+2126 OHM, or "aaİassistant: ẞ") makes sanitize() panic mid-codepoint; with release panic="abort" this aborts the entire plsql-mcp server process — a remote, content-driven denial of service that defeats the very scrubber meant to defend against malicious DB content.
**Evidence:** query.rs:337 guard: `if hay_lower.len() == haystack.len() && needle_lower.len() == needle.len()` then slices ORIGINAL haystack at hay_lower offsets (line 340 `&haystack[cursor..start]`, line 344 `&haystack[cursor..]`). Empirical byte deltas: İ(U+0130) 2→3 (+1); Ω(U+2126 OHM) 3→2 (−1); ẞ(U+1E9E) 3→2 (−1). For "İuser: Ω"(U+2126): haystack.len()=11, lower_len=11 → guard FIRES; find() returns offset 3 in hay_lower coords, cursor=9, but byte 9 is inside 'Ω'(bytes 8..11). Ran REAL public sanitize() via temporary probe test (now removed, git status clean): `cargo test -p plsql-mcp probe_sanitize_charboundary` → `thread 'query::tests::probe_sanitize_charboundary_ohm' panicked at crates/plsql-mcp/src/query.rs:344:31: start byte index 9 is not a char boundary; it is inside 'Ω' (bytes 8..11) of \`İuser: Ω\`` and `..._eszett ... panicked at crates/plsql-mcp/src/query.rs:344:31: start byte index 16 i
**Repro:** Trigger X→Y: input "İuser: Ω" where Ω = U+2126 OHM SIGN (NOT U+03A9), or "aaİassistant: ẞ" (ẞ = U+1E9E). Passed to sanitize() → fast-path guard fires (total lengths equal via canceling lowercase byte deltas) → slice of original haystack at hay_lower offset lands mid-codepoint → panic "byte index N is not a char boundary" → process abort. Correction to claim: the literal Ω must be U+2126 OHM (3→2 bytes); U+03A9 GREEK CAPITAL OMEGA is 2→2 bytes and would make hay_lower.len()(11) != haystack.len()(10), routing to the safe else branch (verified: produces "İ[redacted]Ω" with no panic). The ẞ (U+1E9E) variant in the claim is correct as written.
**Suggested fix:** Adopt the claim's fix: eliminate the hay_lower/haystack offset cross-use. Since reachable needles are ASCII, replace the length-guard fast path with an eq_ignore_ascii_case windowed byte scan over the original string (gate on needle.is_ascii(), else fall back to a char_indices()-based per-codepoint case-fold scan that only indexes the original at char_indices() positions). Add regression tests asserting sanitize("İuser: \u{2126}") and sanitize("aaİassistant: ẞ") return without panicking and redact the marker.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 0f4928de20), re-verified on main. Root cause: replace_case_insensitive (crates/plsql-mcp/src/query.rs) used a fast path gated only on EQUAL TOTAL byte length between haystack and its lowercased copy, then sliced the ORIGINAL string at offsets derived from the lowercased copy. to_lowercase can preserve total length while shifting internal codepoint boundaries (İ U+0130: 2->3 bytes +1, Ω U+2126 OHM: 3->2 bytes -1, ẞ U+1E9E: 3->2 -1), so canceling deltas fired the guard yet the derived offset landed mid-codepoint -> panic 'byte ind

title: Word-boundary the keyword-prefix tests in IR classify() so verb-prefixed assignments are not swallowed (taint fail-open)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-ir/src/stmt.rs:501 — verdict=keep, severity=high
**Impact:** A PL/SQL assignment whose LHS local starts with a keyword/verb (return_*, null_*, update_*, delete_*, commit_*, select_*, insert_*, exit_*, raise_*, merge_*) inside any IF/LOOP/FOR/WHILE body (or the parse-recovery fallback) is misclassified as Return/Null/Sql/TransactionControl/etc., so the assignment is dropped by flow_intra::walk; user-tainted data laundered through such a local (e.g. return_val := p_user; v_sql := return_val;) is never recorded as tainted, producing a SQL-injection fail-open in the taint pass plus phantom/lost dml_edges and call extraction.
**Evidence:** crates/plsql-ir/src/stmt.rs:453-538 classify(): `if trimmed.starts_with("NULL")` (456), COMMIT/ROLLBACK/SAVEPOINT (459-461), RAISE (466), RETURN (475), EXIT (484), the SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE/MERGE loop (501-516), all bare starts_with with no word boundary; the `:=` arm `if let Some((lhs, rhs)) = text.split_once(":=")` is LAST at line 528. flow_intra.rs:143-171 walk() matches only Assignment/If/ForLoop/WhileLoop/BareLoop, with `_ => {}` no-op at line 169. Reproduced via temporary integration test (now removed; git status clean of my file): INPUT "return_val := p_user;" -> [Return { value_text: Some("_val := p_user") }]; "update_x := p_user;" -> [Sql { verb: Update }]; "null_count := 5;" -> [Null]; "commit_count := 1;" -> [TransactionControl { verb: "COMMIT_COUNT" }]; "exit_code := 0;" -> [Exit]; "raise_amount := 100;" -> [Raise { exception: Some("_amount := 100") }]; "delete_flag"/"s
**Repro:** Trigger X: body "return_val := p_user; v_sql := return_val;" with TaintSources { user_input_names: ["p_user"] }. Wrong output Y: lower_statement_body yields [Return { value_text: Some("_val := p_user") }, Assignment { target: "v_sql", rhs_text: "return_val" }]; analyze_flow returns env.get("v_sql").taint.kinds == [] (flags_alarm()==false). Correct behavior (control through a non-keyword local v_tmp) returns kinds == [UserInput]. The divergence proves the assignment is swallowed before the `:=` arm. Inside an IF/LOOP body the same input is reached even on the preferred AST path (lib.rs:670-671 re-lower via classify).
**Suggested fix:** Either add a trailing word-boundary helper (keyword matches only when the next char is not [A-Za-z0-9_$#]) to every starts_with test in classify(), or — more robustly — check for a top-level `:=` (outside string literals) and emit Statement::Assignment BEFORE the keyword classifiers, since a top-level `:=` makes the statement unambiguously an assignment. Add regression tests: lower_statement_body("return_val := p_user;") yields Statement::Assignment { target: "return_val", .. } (and for null_*/update_*/commit_* prefixes), plus a flow_intra test asserting v_sql is tainted for "return_val := p_user; v_sql := return_val;".

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 85ac48919a), re-verified on main. Root-cause fix in crates/plsql-ir/src/stmt.rs classify(). Added two helpers: starts_with_keyword() (whole-word prefix test — keyword matches only when the next char is not [A-Za-z0-9_$#]) and top_level_assign_pos() (finds the first := outside any string literal via char-boundary-safe iteration over chars + byte-offset tracking). Restructured classify() ordering: body-introducing constructs (EXECUTE IMMEDIATE, IF, LOOP/FOR/WHILE, BEGIN/DECLARE) are matched FIRST so a := inside their body is not m

title: Carve out DBMS_ASSERT.NOOP (and SYS-prefixed asserts) in flow_intra taint sanitizer branch
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-ir/src/flow_intra.rs:235 — verdict=keep, severity=high
**Impact:** A PL/SQL unit that wraps raw user input in DBMS_ASSERT.NOOP (Oracle's documented identity pass-through that performs no validation) before EXECUTE IMMEDIATE is silently reported clean by SEC001, so a genuine SQL-injection path goes undetected by the product's flagship Critical injection rule.
**Evidence:** flow_intra.rs:233-235 — `Expr::Call { callee, args } => { let path = callee.parts.join(".").to_ascii_uppercase(); if path.starts_with("DBMS_ASSERT.") {` matches ALL DBMS_ASSERT functions uniformly. Lines 250-263: args are computed into an isolated `sanitized` flow, `TaintCleanser::DbmsAssert` is recorded, and the live `kinds` are dropped (never merged into parent `flow`). flow.rs:169 `pub fn flags_alarm(&self) -> bool { !self.kinds.is_empty() }`. flow_query.rs:84 `let is_tainted = !kinds.is_empty();` feeds rules.rs:83-84 `let answer = ctx.flow.taint_of(site); if answer.is_tainted {` — the SEC001 ExecuteImmediate sink. Cross-crate contrast confirmed: plsql-symbols/src/dynamic_sql.rs:365-391 enumerates NOOP alongside the real sanitizers AND stores the specific `function: fname` in each DbmsAssertCall, proving per-function discrimination is feasible and that the project already distinguishe
**Repro:** Added a temporary unit test in flow_intra.rs and ran `cargo test -p plsql-ir ... -- --nocapture` (then reverted; `git status --porcelain` shows the file clean). Output:
  NOOP kinds=[] cleansed_by=[DbmsAssert] flags_alarm=false        (for v_sql := DBMS_ASSERT.NOOP(p_user); with p_user a UserInput source)
  concat-NOOP kinds=[] cleansed_by=[DbmsAssert] flags_alarm=false  (for v_sql := 'SELECT * FROM ' || DBMS_ASSERT.NOOP(p_user);)
  SYS-prefixed SIMPLE_SQL_NAME kinds=[UserInput] cleansed_by=[] flags_alarm=true  (secondary gap: a real sanitizer with a schema prefix is treated as transparent — fail-closed/over-reporting)
The first two are the fail-open: raw user input reaches a dynamic-SQL sin
**Suggested fix:** In flow_intra.rs replace the `path.starts_with("DBMS_ASSERT.")` guard with logic that (a) tolerates an optional schema prefix (e.g. detect via `let seg: Vec<&str> = path.split('.').collect(); matches!(seg.as_slice(), [.., "DBMS_ASSERT", _])`), and (b) only takes the sanitizer branch when the trailing segment is a real validating function (allowlist: SIMPLE_SQL_NAME / SCHEMA_NAME / ENQUOTE_NAME / SQL_OBJECT_NAME / ENQUOTE_LITERAL, mirroring plsql-symbols/src/dynamic_sql.rs). For NOOP or any unrecognized DBMS_ASSERT entry point, fall through to the transparent branch (lines 268-274) so the argument's taint reaches the parent and still alarms. Add regression tests: `... || DBMS_ASSERT.NOOP(p_user)` must yield flags_alarm()==true; `SYS.DBMS_ASSERT.SIMPLE_SQL_NAME(p_tab)` must yield flags_alarm()==false. Severity high (not critical): a real fail-open in a Critical injection rule, but it requi

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.

Replaced the uniform `path.starts_with("DBMS_ASSERT.")` sanitizer guard with new helper is_dbms_assert_sanitizer(path): matches `[.., "DBMS_ASSERT", func]` (tolerating an optional schema prefix like SYS.) and only treats it as a cleanser when func is in the validating allowlist (SIMPLE_SQL_NAME/QUALIFIED_SQL_NAME/SCHEMA_NAME/ENQUOTE_NAME/SQL_OBJECT_NAME/ENQUOTE_LITERAL — mirroring plsql-symbols/src/dynamic_sql.rs). DBMS_ASSERT.NOOP (Oracle ide

title: Wire disambiguate_overloads into emit_wrappers so overloaded packages produce compilable Rust
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-bindgen/src/emit.rs:61 — verdict=keep, severity=high
**Impact:** Feeding any overloaded PL/SQL package (extremely common — two+ subprograms sharing a name, e.g. hire(id)/hire(name)) to the bindgen CLI silently emits a .rs file with duplicate `pub fn` items, so the operator's subsequent `cargo build` fails with rustc E0428 and the generator's core promise (emit compilable wrappers) is broken for that package.
**Evidence:** emit.rs:53-69 emit_wrappers iterates plan.routines directly; emit.rs:148 `let _ = write!(out, "pub fn {}(executor: &mut impl OracleExecutor", r.name);` writes the PL/SQL name verbatim with no dedup. overload.rs:67 disambiguate_overloads exists with doc "Without this the emitter could write two identical `pub fn`s into one module (rustc E0428)." Workspace grep proves it is never called by production code:
  $ rg -n 'disambiguate_overloads' --type rust crates/
  overload.rs:67 (def), overload.rs:{271,278,289,...,468} (all inside #[cfg(test)]), lib.rs:34 (pub use re-export only)
main.rs:212 deserializes BindingPlan via serde_json::from_str; main.rs:228 `let generated = emit_wrappers(&plan);` with no name normalization. lib.rs:62-63 documents RoutineBinding.name as "Subprogram name as it appears in the package". No production BindingPlan/RoutineBinding builder exists — every struct literal i
**Repro:** Minimal triggering input: a BindingPlan with two routines both named "hire" differing only by parameter (one `p_emp_id IN i64`, one `p_emp_name IN String`). Piped through `plsql-bindgen --input plan.json` it exits 0 and writes a module containing two `pub fn hire(...)` items. Compiling that module fails: `error[E0428]: the name `hire` is defined multiple times`. Input X (2-overload plan) → wrong output Y (uncompilable Rust, no skip diagnostic). The already-written, already-tested disambiguate_overloads would assign distinct names (test two_overloads_get_param_name_suffixes asserts ["hire_p_emp_id","hire_p_emp_name"]) but is never invoked on the emit path.
**Suggested fix:** In emit_wrappers, compute names once: `let names = crate::overload::disambiguate_overloads(&plan.routines);` then `for (i, routine) in plan.routines.iter().enumerate() { emit_routine(&mut out, &plan.package_id, routine, &names[i]); }`. Change emit_routine to take `rust_name: &str` and use it for the `pub fn {rust_name}(...)` identifier (emit.rs:148) and all emitted error-message interpolations, but keep `r.name` for the composed `BEGIN {package_id}.{r.name}(...)` block so the server-side call still targets the real overloaded PL/SQL name. Add a regression test feeding a 2-overload plan and asserting two distinct pub fn names with no duplicate. Optionally emit a doc-comment on suffixed wrappers recording the original overload name.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 3d2d055350), re-verified on main. Wired crate::overload::disambiguate_overloads into emit_wrappers (crates/plsql-bindgen/src/emit.rs): compute rust_names once, pass per-routine rust_name + index into emit_routine; the `pub fn` identifier now uses the disambiguated name while the composed `BEGIN {package_id}.{r.name}(...)` block keeps the real overloaded PL/SQL name. Two `hire` overloads now emit hire_p_emp_id / hire_p_emp_name instead of duplicate `pub fn hire` (E0428).

title: DELETE-with-subquery mis-tags WHERE sub-SELECT tables as Write in dml_edges and sql_resolve
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-ir/src/dml_edges.rs:178 — verdict=keep, severity=medium
**Impact:** A DELETE whose WHERE clause reads a staging/lookup table via a subquery (e.g. DELETE FROM t WHERE id IN (SELECT id FROM staging)) emits a spurious `Writes staging` dependency/lineage edge with reversed data-flow direction, corrupting the dependency graph and potentially feeding wrong over-privilege/impact findings in this SQL-guard product. This is a very common Oracle pattern, so the false write is frequently triggered. No crash, data loss, or fail-open security bypass.
**Evidence:** crates/plsql-ir/src/dml_edges.rs:178-182 DELETE arm pushes EVERY FROM table as Write:
  SqlVerb::Delete => {
      for t in tables_after(&upper, raw, "FROM") {
          push(out, t, AccessKind::Write);
      }
  }
tables_after (lines 198-228) loops over ALL whole-word "FROM" occurrences and never stops at clause keywords. Twin defect crates/plsql-ir/src/sql_resolve.rs:75-79 DELETE arm tags every tables_after_keyword(...,"FROM") triple TableUsageKind::Write (the STOP list bounds a single FROM run but the outer while-loop re-finds the subquery FROM).

End-to-end run through the real public API (cargo test -p plsql-ir, --nocapture):
  ACCESSES = [TableAccess { table: "T", access: Write }, TableAccess { table: "STAGING", access: Write }]
  dml_edges STAGING write=true read=false
  MODEL tables = [TableUse { table: "T", usage: Write }, TableUse { table: "STAGING", usage: Write }]
  sql_resol
**Repro:** Triggering input X = `DELETE FROM t WHERE id IN (SELECT id FROM staging)`. Wrong output Y: STAGING classified AccessKind::Write (dml_edges) / TableUsageKind::Write (sql_resolve), when STAGING is read-only. Verified twice: (1) standalone rustc replica of tables_after returns ["t","staging"] and pushes both Write; ["employees","closed_depts"] for a second realistic case. (2) Integration test through plsql_ir::lower_statement_body -> extract_table_accesses and plsql_ir::sql_resolve::resolve_sql produced [T:Write, STAGING:Write] with STAGING write=true read=false. Throwaway test removed after confirming.
**Suggested fix:** In dml_edges.rs SqlVerb::Delete arm, treat only the FIRST tables_after(&upper, raw, "FROM") entry as AccessKind::Write (the DELETE target) and the remaining FROM entries as AccessKind::Read (subquery reads); also add the JOIN tables as Read. Apply the symmetric fix in sql_resolve.rs:75-79: first tables_after_keyword(...,"FROM") triple is TableUsageKind::Write, the rest Read. A more robust long-term fix bounds the DELETE target run at the WHERE/clause boundary (as tables_after_keyword's STOP list already does within a single occurrence) and recurses the WHERE sub-SELECT through the SELECT read path. Add regression tests asserting DELETE FROM t WHERE id IN (SELECT id FROM staging) yields T=Write and STAGING=Read (never STAGING=Write) in both modules.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 85ac48919a), re-verified on main. Root-cause fix in two modules. crates/plsql-ir/src/dml_edges.rs SqlVerb::Delete arm: only the FIRST tables_after(FROM) entry is AccessKind::Write (the DELETE target); remaining FROM entries (WHERE sub-SELECT) are AccessKind::Read; JOIN tables are Read. crates/plsql-ir/src/sql_resolve.rs SqlSemanticVerb::Delete arm: symmetric fix — first FROM triple is TableUsageKind::Write, the rest Read, plus JOIN as Read. So `DELETE FROM t WHERE id IN (SELECT id FROM staging)` now yields T=Write, STAGING=Read

title: Validate/escape identifiers in plsql-bindgen dynamic path so untrusted BindingPlans fail closed with a typed BINDING_* body instead of emitting uncompilable Rust
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-bindgen/src/emit.rs:392 — verdict=keep, severity=medium
**Impact:** An untrusted/malformed BindingPlan whose package_id or routine name contains a double-quote or brace (routed through the defaulted-parameter dynamic path) makes plsql-bindgen emit exit-0 but UNCOMPILABLE Rust instead of the fail-closed typed BINDING_* diagnostic the module contractually guarantees for every other attacker-shaped plan — a fail-open codegen-contract violation on the documented CLI input surface. The "second-order PL/SQL injection into the live DB" escalation does NOT hold: the same identifier is emitted raw into the `pub fn` signature and `match &name {` scrutinee, so any name carrying PL/SQL metacharacters fails `cargo build` and never reaches Oracle.
**Evidence:** emit.rs:390-394 (dynamic path) hand-writes the block with raw interpolation:
`    let __block = format!(\"BEGIN {prefix}{package_id}.{}({{}}); END;\", __call.join(\", \"));` , r.name`
contrast emit.rs:267-269 (static path) which is safe: `executor.call_routine({block:?}, &__args)?` (Debug-escaped).
Fields are plain serde String with NO validation: lib.rs:48 `pub package_id: String`, :63 `pub name: String` (RoutineBinding), :86 `pub name: String` (ParameterBinding). main.rs:212 `serde_json::from_str(&json)` then main.rs:228 `emit_wrappers(&plan)` with no guard between. rg for valid|sanitiz|is_ident|escape|validate in the crate returns only flag-name/comment hits.

Run (built ./target/debug/plsql-bindgen):
- repro_quote.json (name `f\", DROP TABLE t; --`, defaulted param) → EXIT 0, emitted:
  `pub fn f\", DROP TABLE t; --(executor: ...`
  `    let __block = format!(\"BEGIN hr.emp_pkg.f\", 
**Repro:** TRIGGER (codegen-contract violation, confirmed): JSON BindingPlan {package_id:"hr.emp_pkg", routines:[{name:"f\", DROP TABLE t; --", kind:"procedure", parameters:[{name:"p_id", mode:"IN", rust_type:{path:"i64",nullable:false}, has_default:true}], return_type:null}]} → plsql-bindgen exits 0 and emits Rust that rustc rejects (prefix `f` unknown / unexpected closing delimiter), instead of the contractual exit-0 + valid-Rust + typed BINDING_* fail-closed body (emit.rs:161-165). DISPROOF of the live-DB injection escalation: for the runtime __block to be poisoned the output must first compile, but the param/routine name is emitted RAW into `pub fn {name}(` (emit.rs:151) and `match &{name} {` (emit
**Suggested fix:** Add an identifier-validation gate (the module's existing fail-closed pattern) before interpolating r.name / package_id / p.name into generated Rust. Validate package_id as dot-qualified segments matching ^[A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9_$#]*$ and routine/parameter names as a single such segment; on failure, emit a typed BINDING_INVALID_IDENTIFIER body (mirroring BINDING_DEGENERATE_FUNCTION at emit.rs:166-178 / BINDING_UNSUPPORTED_MARSHALLING at 183-194) and return, keeping exit 0 + compilable Rust. Cover BOTH the static and dynamic paths and every raw-identifier position: the `pub fn {name}` signature (emit.rs:148), the `{name}: {sig}` parameter (emit.rs:151), the `match &{name}` scrutinee and `format!(\"{name} => :{{__n}}\")` literals (emit.rs:484/498/504/512/524), and the dynamic `__block` literal (emit.rs:392). Add tests feeding names containing `\"`, `\\`, `{`, `}`, `)`, `;`, `=>` and assert the 

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 3d2d055350), re-verified on main. Added a fail-closed identifier gate at the top of emit_routine that validates package_id (dot-qualified Oracle-safe segments, $/# allowed since only ever in PL/SQL string literals) and every routine/parameter name (legal Rust identifier). On failure it emits a deterministic safe-stub `pub fn` with a typed BINDING_INVALID_IDENTIFIER body (message Debug-escaped via {msg:?} so hostile text cannot break the generated literal), exit-0 + compilable Rust. Guard runs BEFORE static/dynamic path selection

title: Fix parse_target_schema to normalise whitespace around the schema-qualifier dot (cross-schema confirmation fail-open)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/plsql-mcp/src/create_or_replace.rs:226 — verdict=keep, severity=medium
**Impact:** An agent can deploy a CREATE OR REPLACE into a third schema (e.g. principal BILLING, DDL header `... ANALYTICS . PKG ...`) while the operator-typed-destination cross-schema confirmation is silently skipped — the write is misclassified SameSchema and waved through; Oracle still routes the object to ANALYTICS. The human byte-exact preview step remains as a backstop, so it is not fully unmitigated.
**Evidence:** create_or_replace.rs:210-237 parse_target_schema(): `let tokens = upper.split_whitespace().collect(); let name_idx = 3 + kind.split_whitespace().count(); let name_token = name_raw.split('(').next()...; match name_token.split_once('.') { Some((owner,object)) if !owner.is_empty() && !object.is_empty() => Ok(Some(owner)), _ => Ok(None) }`. Standalone reproduction of this exact logic (rustc -O): `ANALYTICS.PKG` => parsed=Some("ANALYTICS"); `ANALYTICS . PKG` => parsed=None; `ANALYTICS .PKG` => parsed=None; `ANALYTICS. PKG` => parsed=None (classify_kind returns PACKAGE BODY for all four, so all pass the kind gate and get previewed). Downstream execute_approved.rs:130-133: `let parsed_schema = parse_target_schema(&verified.ddl_bytes)?; let effective_target = parsed_schema.map(..).unwrap_or_else(|| req.principal_schema...)` — None => effective=principal. cross_schema.rs:98-105: principal_upper==
**Repro:** Trigger: agent submits DDL `CREATE OR REPLACE PACKAGE BODY ANALYTICS . PKG AS BEGIN NULL; END;` to create_or_replace dry-run (accepted, classified PACKAGE BODY, token minted), then execute_approved with principal_schema="BILLING", target_schema="" (or "BILLING"), operator_typed_schema=None. parse_target_schema returns None -> effective_target="BILLING" -> caller_target empty so agreement check at execute_approved.rs:139 is skipped -> require_cross_schema_confirmation("BILLING","BILLING",None) takes principal==target branch -> confirmed:true SameSchema, no typed confirmation demanded. Oracle (proven live) routes the package body to schema ANALYTICS. The no-space `ANALYTICS.PKG` form correctly
**Suggested fix:** In parse_target_schema (create_or_replace.rs:217-237), reassemble the object-name region from name_idx onward and collapse whitespace around the qualifier dot before split_once('.'), e.g. `let tail = tokens[name_idx..].join(" "); let normalised = tail.replace(" . ", ".").replace(" .", ".").replace(". ", "."); let name_token = normalised.split_whitespace().next().unwrap_or("").split('(').next().unwrap_or("");` so `OWNER . OBJECT`/`OWNER .OBJECT`/`OWNER. OBJECT` all yield Some(OWNER). Prefer failing CLOSED on a malformed/ambiguous dotted form (owner or object empty) rather than Ok(None). Add regression tests for all three spaced forms plus a PROCEDURE param-list form `OPS . DO_IT(p IN NUMBER)` (normalise before the '(' cut), and an execute_approved test asserting a BILLING principal with header `... ANALYTICS . PKG ...` is NOT SameSchema and requires the operator-typed destination. A real 

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit 0f4928de20), re-verified on main. Root cause: parse_target_schema (crates/plsql-mcp/src/create_or_replace.rs) read only tokens[name_idx] (a single whitespace-split token). A whitespace-spaced qualifier (ANALYTICS . PKG / ANALYTICS .PKG / ANALYTICS. PKG) split into separate tokens, so the name token was a bare ANALYTICS with no dot -> Ok(None) -> execute_approved defaulted effective_target to the principal -> SameSchema, operator-typed cross-schema confirmation silently skipped while Oracle still routed the object to ANALYTICS. F

title: Include sql_preview in the audit tamper-evidence hash (documented "any edit breaks verification" invariant is false for the only operator-legible field)
description:
Source: verify-and-hunt re-hunt + ground-truth calibration (2026-06-02, round 2).

### crates/oraclemcp-audit/src/record.rs:177 — verdict=keep, severity=medium
**Impact:** An actor with write access to the append-only audit log (the exact threat the hash chain defends against) can rewrite the only human-legible record of a statement — sql_preview, e.g. change "DELETE FROM payroll WHERE ..." to "SELECT 1" — on a persisted Guarded/Destructive line without touching sql_sha256, and chain verification still reports the log as untampered, silently misleading a forensic reviewer. The documented invariant "any edit or reorder breaks verification" is false for this field.
**Evidence:** crates/oraclemcp-audit/src/record.rs:177 — `for field in [timestamp, agent_identity, tool, sql_sha256, danger_level] {` — sql_preview is absent from the hashed field loop, and never appears in any other `hasher.update(...)` call (lines 176-184). compute_entry_hash (163-192) receives 10 params; sql_preview is not one of them. Both callers feed the identical set: AuditRecord::chained (112-123) and hash_is_valid (144-155). sql_preview is a serialized content field with no skip_serializing (record.rs:67) and FileAuditSink::append writes the whole record to each JSON line via `serde_json::to_string(record)` (sink.rs:70). Doc comments assert coverage: record.rs:52-53 "entry_hash covers the seq and all content fields, so any edit or reorder breaks verification"; record.rs:79 "covers seq + content + prev_hash". Chain-verify test path (sink.rs:291-297) only calls hash_is_valid() + prev_hash linka
**Repro:** Minimal X→Y: let r = AuditRecord::chained(&draft(), 1, GENESIS_HASH, "t".into()); assert!(r.hash_is_valid()); then mutate ONLY the preview: r.sql_preview = "SELECT 1".to_owned(); — hash_is_valid() still returns TRUE because compute_entry_hash recomputes from self.{seq,timestamp,agent_identity,tool,sql_sha256,danger_level,decision,rows_affected,outcome,prev_hash} and never reads self.sql_preview. Contrast the existing tampering_breaks_the_hash test (record.rs:223-229), which mutates danger_level (a hashed field) and correctly fails verification. The closed proof: since sql_preview is never an argument to compute_entry_hash, the recomputed digest is invariant under any change to sql_preview, s
**Suggested fix:** Add a `sql_preview: &str` parameter to compute_entry_hash and append it to the length-prefixed field loop, e.g. `for field in [timestamp, agent_identity, tool, sql_sha256, sql_preview, danger_level]` (keep length-prefixing so adjacent fields can't be confused). Pass it at both call sites: in AuditRecord::chained pass `&sql_preview` (after it is computed at record.rs:111) and in hash_is_valid pass `&self.sql_preview`. Add a regression test alongside tampering_breaks_the_hash that mutates only sql_preview and asserts `!hash_is_valid()`. Since this changes the hash output, note that pre-existing on-disk chains need re-anchoring — acceptable for this pre-release crate. (Alternative, weaker fix: correct the doc comments at record.rs:52-53 and 79 to state sql_preview is explicitly NOT covered; hashing it is the stronger, more consistent choice given the stated forensic intent.)

### crates/oraclemcp-audit/src/record.rs:163 — verdict=keep, severity=low
**Impact:** An attacker with write access to the out-of-band audit file can rewrite a record's human-readable sql_preview (e.g. "DELETE FROM customers" -> "SELECT 1 FROM dual") and hash_is_valid()/the chain walk still pass, misleading any forensic or compliance reviewer who reads sql_preview and trusts chain verification without recomputing sql_sha256; the cryptographic anchor (sql_sha256) stays intact, so the deception is bounded to the display field only.
**Evidence:** record.rs:177 field loop omits sql_preview:
  for field in [timestamp, agent_identity, tool, sql_sha256, danger_level] {
      hasher.update((field.len() as u64).to_be_bytes());
      hasher.update(field.as_bytes());
  }
compute_entry_hash (record.rs:163-192) takes seq, timestamp, agent_identity, tool, sql_sha256, danger_level, decision, rows_affected, outcome, prev_hash — no sql_preview param. Both chained (line 112) and hash_is_valid (line 144) call it without sql_preview.
sql_preview is a persisted serialized field (record.rs:67, no serde skip), populated at line 111 (sql.chars().take(PREVIEW_LEN)), and read by humans (token_security.rs:134 asserts no token leaks into it).
Doc contract record.rs:51-53: "entry_hash covers the seq and all content fields, so any edit or reorder breaks verification"; line 79: "covers seq + content + prev_hash". sql_preview is a stored content field NOT co
**Repro:** Isomorphic proof harness mirroring compute_entry_hash's exact field set (record.rs:176-184, sans sql_preview):
  preview-only tamper -> hash_is_valid() = true
  danger_level tamper -> hash_is_valid() = false
Triggering input: take an AuditRecord auditing sql="DELETE FROM customers" (sql_preview="DELETE FROM customers"), set r.sql_preview = "SELECT 1 FROM dual", leave sql_sha256/danger_level/all hashed fields unchanged. compute_entry_hash recomputes the identical digest (it never reads sql_preview), so hash_is_valid() returns true -> tamper undetected. Mirror of the existing tampering_breaks_the_hash test (record.rs:223) but mutating sql_preview instead of danger_level would fail to assert fa
**Suggested fix:** Add a `sql_preview: &str` parameter to compute_entry_hash and include it in the length-prefixed string loop (e.g. `for field in [timestamp, agent_identity, tool, sql_sha256, sql_preview, danger_level]`). Pass &sql_preview from AuditRecord::chained (already computed at line 111) and &self.sql_preview from hash_is_valid (line 143). Add a regression test mirroring tampering_breaks_the_hash that mutates r.sql_preview and asserts !r.hash_is_valid(). No pre-existing audit files exist (fresh project), so the chain-format change needs no migration; length-prefixing gives the preview the same domain separation as the other string fields.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Root-cause fix in production code; regression test fails before / passes after.
- [ ] `cargo test -p <crate>` + `cargo clippy -p <crate> --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; fmt only oraclemcp-*.
- [ ] No regression on existing tests / adversarial corpus.
close_reason: [done] implemented via parallel worktree workflow (commit c8fd58d173), re-verified on main. Root cause: compute_entry_hash (crates/oraclemcp-audit/src/record.rs) never received sql_preview, so the only operator-legible record field was outside the tamper-evidence hash. An actor with write access to the append-only log could rewrite sql_preview (e.g. 'DELETE FROM payroll WHERE ...' -> 'SELECT 1') without touching sql_sha256/danger_level and chain verification still reported the log as untampered, contradicting the documented 'any edit breaks verification' invariant. Fix (stronger of the

title: PLSQL-MCP-LIVE-005 — Implement `list_objects` with structured type / name-pattern / schema filters; pages results with cursor token for large estates
description: Imported from `plan.md` v0.11 on 2026-05-12.
close_reason: Added crates/plsql-mcp/src/list_objects.rs with run_list_objects(conn, request) -> ListObjectsResponse. Request shape: ListObjectsRequest { object_type, name_pattern, schema, page_size, cursor } all serde-Option. Response: ListObjectsResponse { entries: Vec<ListObjectsEntry { owner, name, object_type, status, last_ddl_time }>, next_cursor: Option<String>, issued_sql }. (1) Cursor paging: opaque OWNER\u0001NAME tuple; build_query fetches page_size + 1 rows to detect overflow + emits a stable seek predicate (owner > or (owner = and object_name >) for cross-schema; object_name > only for single-schema USER_OBJECTS). (2) Schema-aware dictionary selection: ALL_OBJECTS when caller specifies a schema, USER_OBJECTS otherwise. (3) MAX_PAGE_SIZE = 500 cap + DEFAULT_PAGE_SIZE = 100; page_size clamped via .clamp(1, MAX_PAGE_SIZE). (4) Parameterized bind generation via OracleBind for every filter — no string interpolation of user input. (5) ListObjectsError::InvalidCursor for malformed cursors. issued_sql surfaced so an operator can audit. 6 new unit tests cover: page fits (no cursor), overflow returns cursor with encoded (owner,name), malformed cursor rejection, page_size clamp + empty backend = empty response, schema filter routes to all_objects vs user_objects, name_pattern + object_type filters appear in issued SQL. 55/55 plsql-mcp tests pass; clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings clean.
